topics.title: Classification of Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Energy Source** is any physical phenomenon, material, or natural resource from which **useful mechanical work, electrical energy, or thermal energy** can be derived, either directly through conversion (e.g., photovoltaic effect) or indirectly through a chain of intermediate transformations (e.g., fossil fuel → steam → turbine → generator).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Approved):**
> *Energy sources are classified as the naturally occurring or artificially processed reservoirs of energy that can be harnessed by humans to perform productive work, categorized primarily by their replenishment rate, environmental impact, and stage of utilization in the energy supply chain.*

The **two master classifications** adopted by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

1. **Conventional Energy Sources** — Traditional sources exploited since the First Industrial Revolution (e.g., coal, petroleum, natural gas, large-scale hydroelectricity, nuclear fission).
2. **Non-Conventional (Alternative / Renewable) Energy Sources** — Sources that are either perpetually replenished by nature (solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, ocean thermal) or represent emerging technologies with low carbon footprints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" Model

Imagine Earth's energy system as a **family bank account**:

- **Renewable Energy Sources** behave like a **monthly salary deposited into the account**. As long as the sun shines, the wind blows, and the tides rise, money (energy) keeps coming in. You can withdraw, but the balance is **continuously refilled**.
- **Non-Renewable Energy Sources** behave like a **fixed inheritance** — a one-time deposit of coal, oil, and gas left by ancient biological and geological processes over millions of years. The more you spend, the **closer you get to zero**, and there is no way to refill the account on a human timescale.
- **Non-Conventional Energy Sources** are essentially **new investment instruments** (solar panels, wind turbines) that tap into the salary stream rather than the inheritance, ensuring the account survives indefinitely.

## 1.3 The Tri-Axial Classification Framework

KTU 2024 evaluates energy sources along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Question Answered | Categories |
|------|-------------------|------------|
| **Replenishment Rate** | Can it be refilled by nature? | Renewable / Non-Renewable |
| **Adoption Maturity** | Is it commercially proven & widespread? | Conventional / Non-Conventional |
| **Supply-Chain Position** | At what stage is it used? | Primary / Secondary / Tertiary / Final |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** *Conventional* and *Non-Renewable* are **NOT synonyms**. For example, **large hydro** is *conventional* but *renewable*, while **nuclear** is *conventional* but *non-renewable*. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Energy Units

The following **fundamental constants and unit conventions** are essential for every numerical or descriptive answer in this module:

- **Energy Unit (SI):** Joule (J) — defined as $1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}$.
- **Power Unit (SI):** Watt (W) = 1 J/s.
- **Industrial Energy Unit:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.
- **Thermal Energy Unit:** $1 \text{ cal} = 4.184 \text{ J}$.
- **Solar Constant (AM1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (extraterrestrial irradiance).
- **Standard Earth Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> All energy sources discussed in this module will be quantitatively compared using **Joules (J)** or **kilowatt-hours (kWh)** as the SI reference. The conversion $1 \text{ toe}$ (tonne of oil equivalent) = $41.868 \text{ GJ}$ is also frequently used in board numericals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Tree (Hierarchical Breakdown)

The classification of energy sources can be systematically decomposed into a four-tier hierarchical tree:

### **Tier 1 — Primary vs. Secondary Energy Sources**
- **Primary Energy Source:** Energy obtained directly from nature *before* any human-induced conversion. Examples: Crude petroleum, raw sunlight, wind kinetic energy, coal in-situ, geothermal brine heat.
- **Secondary Energy Source:** Energy derived *after* one or more conversion stages from a primary source. Examples: Electricity (converted from coal, hydro, or solar), gasoline (refined from crude), hydrogen (electrolysed from water using electricity).
- **Final Energy:** The form in which energy reaches the consumer (e.g., electricity at a wall socket, LPG at a burner).
- **Useful Energy:** The energy that actually performs the desired work (e.g., light from a lamp, mechanical motion from a motor).

### **Tier 2 — Commercial vs. Non-Commercial Energy**
- **Commercial Energy:** Sources that are bought and sold in a regulated market with a measurable price. Examples: Coal, electricity, LNG, petrol.
- **Non-Commercial Energy:** Sources gathered for subsistence, often unpriced and used in rural/off-grid contexts. Examples: Firewood, agricultural residue, cow-dung cakes, straw.

### **Tier 3 — Renewable vs. Non-Renewable**
- **Renewable:** Replenished at a rate **faster than or equal to** its consumption rate.
- **Non-Renewable:** Replenished at a rate **vastly slower** than its consumption rate.

### **Tier 4 — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional**
- **Conventional:** Established, large-scale, technologically mature.
- **Non-Conventional:** Emerging, modular, often smaller-scale, typically (but not always) renewable.

## 2.2 Detailed Source Inventory (Bulleted Logical Breakdown)

### **A. Conventional Non-Renewable Sources**
1. **Fossil Fuels (Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas)** — formed from anaerobic decomposition of biomass over 100–400 million years.
2. **Nuclear Fission Energy** — uses Uranium-235 ($^{235}U$) or Plutonium-239 ($^{239}Pu$) as fuel; classified non-renewable due to finite ore reserves.
3. **Fossil-fuel-based Thermal Power** — combustion-based electricity generation.

### **B. Conventional Renewable Sources**
1. **Large Hydroelectric Power (HEP)** — dams exceeding **25 MW** (as per KTU classification); uses gravitational potential energy of stored water.
2. **Traditional Biomass Combustion** — firewood, charcoal for cooking/heating.

### **C. Non-Conventional Renewable Sources** *(Primary Focus of this Course)*
1. **Solar Energy** — photovoltaic (PV) and solar thermal.
2. **Wind Energy** — kinetic-to-mechanical-to-electrical conversion.
3. **Biomass (Modern)** — biogas, biodiesel, ethanol.
4. **Geothermal Energy** — heat from Earth's core.
5. **Tidal Energy** — gravitational potential/kinetic of seawater.
6. **Wave Energy** — surface ocean wave motion.
7. **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — temperature gradient between surface and deep seawater.
8. **Fuel Cells & Hydrogen Energy** — electrochemical conversion.
9. **Magneto-Hydro-Dynamic (MHD) Power** — ionized gas → direct electricity.

### **D. Non-Conventional Non-Renewable**
1. **Nuclear Fusion Energy** — experimental; D-T reaction not yet commercially realized.
2. **Tar Sands / Oil Shale** — unconventional fossil reserves.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Quantification & Classification Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` in all LaTeX expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

| **Formula / Metric** | **LaTeX Form** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Content of Fuel | $Q = m \cdot CV$ | $Q$ = heat released (J); $m$ = mass (kg); $CV$ = calorific value (J/kg) |
| Energy in Falling Water (Hydro) | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | $E$ = potential energy; $h$ = head (m) |
| Mass Flow Rate (Hydro) | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s; $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ for water |
| Wind Power Available | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot v^3$ | $P_{wind}$ in W; $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225$ kg/m³ |
| Solar Irradiance on Surface | $G = G_{sc} \cdot \cos(\theta_z)$ | $\theta_z$ = zenith angle |
| Replenishment Ratio | $R_r = \frac{E_{replenished}}{E_{consumed}}$ | $R_r \geq 1$ ⇒ Renewable; $R_r \ll 1$ ⇒ Non-Renewable |
| Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_{output}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in % |
| Energy Payback Time (EPBT) | $EPBT = \frac{E_{invested}}{E_{annual\_output}}$ | Years to recover embodied energy |
| Carbon Footprint | $C_f = \frac{CO_2 \text{ emitted}}{E_{generated}}$ | kgCO₂/kWh |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding energy source classification is **foundational** to every subsequent engineering decision:

- **Power System Design:** Grid planners must balance firm (dispatchable) capacity from conventional sources against intermittent renewable generation.
- **Policy & Economics:** Government subsidies (e.g., India's PM-KUSUM, PM-Surya Ghar Yojana) flow toward **non-conventional renewable** sources.
- **Industrial Project Selection:** Engineers select sources based on local availability — a geothermal plant makes engineering sense in Iceland but not Kerala, while a tidal plant makes sense at Gujarat's Gulf of Kutch but not in landlocked states.
- **Environmental Compliance:** Carbon-credit accounting under ISO 14064 depends entirely on whether a source is renewable or fossil.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation — Solar Energy Available at a Surface

This derivation is foundational, as it mathematically demonstrates *why* solar is classified as **renewable and non-conventional**, and it sets up all PV/thermal calculations in later modules.

**Problem:** Determine the solar power per unit area incident on a flat surface tilted at an angle equal to the local latitude.

### **Step 1 — Define the Extraterrestrial Solar Constant**
The Sun delivers energy at a flux $G_{sc}$ at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere:

$$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$$

This is a **measured physical constant** (NASA WMO standard, 1981).

### **Step 2 — Apply the Cosine Law for a Tilted Surface**
When the sun's rays strike a surface at a zenith angle $\theta_z$ (measured from the vertical), the effective irradiance $G_T$ on the surface is:

$$G_T = G_{sc} \cdot \cos(\theta_z)$$

### **Step 3 — Apply Atmospheric Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Model)**
After passing through the atmosphere, the beam is attenuated by absorption and scattering. The direct normal irradiance at ground level is:

$$G_{bn} = G_{sc} \cdot \tau^{m}$$

where $\tau$ is the atmospheric transmittance (typical clear-sky value $\tau \approx 0.75$) and $m$ is the air mass ratio.

### **Step 4 — Air Mass Definition**
The air mass ratio is the ratio of the slant path length to the vertical path length:

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

For sun directly overhead, $\theta_z = 0°$ and $m = 1$ (AM1 condition — KTU's reference for solar calculations).

### **Step 5 — Combine into Final Ground-Level Irradiance**
Substituting $m$ into the transmittance equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{bn} &= G_{sc} \cdot \tau^{\frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}} \\
&= 1367 \times 0.75^{\sec(\theta_z)} \quad \text{(in W/m}^2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation at KTU-Standard Conditions**
For $\theta_z = 48.2°$ (typical for India at AM1.5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sec(\theta_z) &= \frac{1}{\cos(48.2°)} = \frac{1}{0.6665} \approx 1.5 \\
G_{bn} &= 1367 \times 0.75^{1.5} \\
&= 1367 \times 0.6495 \\
&\approx 887.7 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This $887.7 \text{ W/m}^2$ is the **standard test condition (STC)** irradiance used worldwide for rating PV modules — directly traceable to the energy-source classification principles established here.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Classification (KTU Exam Favourite)

The matrix below is a **high-yield summary** that maps every major source against its classification axes. This table alone is worth **4–6 marks** in descriptive answers.

| **Energy Source** | **Renewable?** | **Conventional?** | **Primary/Secondary** | **Commercial?** | **Global Capacity (2024)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coal | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~2000 GW |
| Petroleum | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Primary | ✅ Yes | N/A (transport fuel) |
| Natural Gas | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~1800 GW |
| Nuclear Fission | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~410 GW |
| Large Hydro | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~1410 GW |
| Solar PV | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~2000 GW |
| Wind | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~1018 GW |
| Biomass (modern) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~150 GW |
| Geothermal | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ✅ Yes | ~16 GW |
| Tidal | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ✅ Yes (pilot) | <0.5 GW |
| OTEC | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Primary | ❌ No | <0.01 GW |
| Firewood | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (traditional) | Primary | ❌ No | ~2 billion users |
| Electricity (grid) | Depends on mix | — | Secondary | ✅ Yes | — |
| Hydrogen (green) | ✅ Yes (indirect) | ❌ No | Secondary | ✅ Yes (emerging) | ~50 Mt/yr |

## 3.3 Logical Derivation of the "Why" Behind Classification

**Step 1 — Why distinguish Renewable vs Non-Renewable?**
Because replenishment rate determines **sustainability horizon**. Non-renewables have a finite lifetime measured in centuries; renewables have an effectively infinite lifetime.

**Step 2 — Why distinguish Conventional vs Non-Conventional?**
Because the **maturity of technology and infrastructure** dictates cost and deployment speed. Conventional sources have mature supply chains; non-conventional sources require innovation-driven cost reduction.

**Step 3 — Why distinguish Primary vs Secondary?**
Because **efficiency analysis** must be applied at every conversion stage. A combined-cycle gas plant may be 60% efficient *from primary fuel*, but only 38% efficient *from primary well-head gas*.

**Step 4 — Why distinguish Commercial vs Non-Commercial?**
Because **energy-access policy** (SDG-7 of UN) requires separate metrics. India's 100% electrification target (achieved 2018–2024) addresses commercial energy, while the Ujjwala Yojana addresses non-commercial LPG substitution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete hierarchical classification of energy sources as a **block-level functional architecture**, suitable for a 14-mark descriptive answer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENERGY SOURCES] --> B[REPLENISHMENT AXIS]
    A --> C[MATURITY AXIS]
    A --> D[SUPPLY CHAIN AXIS]
    A --> E[COMMERCE AXIS]

    B --> F[RENEWABLE]
    B --> G[NON RENEWABLE]

    F --> F1[Solar Energy]
    F --> F2[Wind Energy]
    F --> F3[Hydro Energy]
    F --> F4[Biomass]
    F --> F5[Geothermal]
    F --> F6[Tidal and Wave]
    F --> F7[Hydrogen]

    G --> G1[Coal]
    G --> G2[Petroleum]
    G --> G3[Natural Gas]
    G --> G4[Nuclear Fission]
    G --> G5[Oil Shale]

    C --> H[CONVENTIONAL]
    C --> I[NON CONVENTIONAL]

    H --> H1[Coal Thermal]
    H --> H2[Large Hydro above 25 MW]
    H --> H3[Nuclear Fission]
    H --> H4[Traditional Biomass]

    I --> I1[Solar PV and Thermal]
    I --> I2[Wind Turbines]
    I --> I3[Small Hydro below 25 MW]
    I --> I4[Modern Biomass and Biogas]
    I --> I5[Fuel Cells]
    I --> I6[MHD Generators]
    I --> I7[OTEC Systems]
    I --> I8[Tidal Barrages]

    D --> J[PRIMARY]
    D --> K[SECONDARY]
    D --> L[FINAL]
    D --> M[USEFUL]

    J --> J1[Crude Oil, Coal, Sunlight, Wind]
    K --> K1[Electricity, Refined Fuels, Hydrogen]
    L --> L1[Wall Socket Power, LPG at Burner]
    M --> M1[Light, Heat, Mechanical Motion]

    E --> N[COMMERCIAL]
    E --> O[NON COMMERCIAL]

    N --> N1[Grid Electricity, Petrol, LNG]
    O --> O1[Firewood, Cow Dung, Agricultural Waste]
```

## 4.2 Energy Conversion Chain — From Primary to Useful

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a single primary energy source flows through successive conversion stages, reinforcing the **Primary → Secondary → Final → Useful** chain introduced in Section 2.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[SUNLIGHT PRIMARY] --> S1[Photovoltaic Cell]
    P2[COAL PRIMARY] --> S2[Boiler Steam]
    P3[WIND PRIMARY] --> S3[Turbine Rotor]
    P4[URANIUM PRIMARY] --> S4[Reactor Heat Exchanger]

    S1 --> SEC1[DC ELECTRICITY SECONDARY]
    S2 --> SEC2[HIGH PRESS STEAM SECONDARY]
    S3 --> SEC3[MECHANICAL SHAFT SECONDARY]
    S4 --> SEC4[STEAM SECONDARY]

    SEC1 --> FIN1[AC AT WALL SOCKET FINAL]
    SEC2 --> FIN1
    SEC3 --> FIN1
    SEC4 --> FIN1

    FIN1 --> US1[LIGHT FROM LED USEFUL]
    FIN1 --> US2[MOTOR MOTION USEFUL]
    FIN1 --> US3[HEAT FROM HEATER USEFUL]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Notes

- **Block (P1–P4)** = Primary sources from nature.
- **Block (SEC1–SEC4)** = Secondary forms after one conversion.
- **Block FIN1** = A **single, universal secondary form** — electricity — illustrating why modern grids standardize on AC electricity regardless of primary source.
- **Block (US1–US3)** = Final useful outcomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The classification tree is *not* a hierarchy of value; it is a hierarchy of **state** and **maturity**. A 1 MW rooftop solar system and a 1000 MW coal plant are both equally valid energy sources — they are simply optimized for different deployment contexts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Conventional** and **Non-Conventional** energy sources. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Sources used for a long time with established technology | Newer, emerging sources often with cleaner footprints |
| Maturity | High commercial penetration | Still in growth phase |
| Examples | (1) Coal-based thermal power; (2) Large hydroelectric dams | (1) Solar photovoltaic; (2) Wind turbines |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 0.5 mark for each correct example = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Renewable** and **Non-Renewable** energy sources. Justify why **nuclear fission energy** is classified as non-renewable.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Renewable Energy Source:** A source that is naturally replenished at a rate equal to or greater than the rate of consumption. *(1 mark)*
- **Non-Renewable Energy Source:** A source whose replenishment rate is negligible compared to consumption, leading to eventual depletion. *(1 mark)*
- **Justification for Nuclear Fission being Non-Renewable:** Uranium-235 ore deposits are finite and take hundreds of millions of years to form geologically. At current global consumption, known reserves are projected to last only ~80–120 years. Since the consumption rate vastly exceeds the natural replenishment rate, it is classified as non-renewable despite being low-carbon. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Classify energy sources based on **(i) Replenishment rate** and **(ii) Commercial availability**. Provide **two examples** for each sub-category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **primary → secondary → final → useful** energy conversion chain for a **coal-fired thermal power plant**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Classification (7 Marks)**

**(i) Classification based on Replenishment Rate (4 Marks):**

1. **Renewable Energy Sources** — replenished continuously by natural processes at a rate faster than or equal to human consumption. *(1 mark)*
   - Example 1: Solar energy (the sun has a remaining lifespan of ~5 billion years). *(0.5 mark)*
   - Example 2: Wind energy (atmospheric circulation is continuously driven by solar heating). *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Non-Renewable Energy Sources** — replenished at a geological timescale (millions of years), negligible relative to consumption. *(1 mark)*
   - Example 1: Coal (formed from Carboniferous-era biomass, ~300 million years ago). *(0.5 mark)*
   - Example 2: Petroleum (formed from marine sediments under heat & pressure). *(0.5 mark)*

**(ii) Classification based on Commercial Availability (3 Marks):**

1. **Commercial Energy** — bought and sold in formal markets with measurable price. *(1 mark)*
   - Example: Grid electricity, LPG, petrol. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Non-Commercial Energy** — collected informally, typically unpriced, used in rural settings. *(1 mark)*
   - Example: Firewood, cow-dung cakes. *(0.5 mark)*

#### **Part (b) — Coal Power Plant Energy Chain (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **Primary Source (1 mark):** Coal in the mine (chemical energy bound in C–C and C–H bonds).
2. **Combustion → Secondary (1 mark):** In the boiler furnace, coal is burned in the presence of oxygen. Chemical energy → Thermal energy of high-temperature, high-pressure steam.
3. **Steam Turbine → Mechanical (1 mark):** Steam expands through turbine blades; thermal → mechanical rotational energy on the shaft.
4. **Generator → Electrical (1 mark):** Rotating shaft drives an electromagnetic generator (Faraday's Law); mechanical → electrical energy (AC).
5. **Final Energy (1 mark):** Stepped-up transformer raises voltage; electricity enters the transmission grid at 220 kV / 400 kV.
6. **Useful Energy (1 mark):** At the consumer end, electricity drives lights, fans, motors — converted to light, motion, and heat.
7. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Every conversion stage incurs losses (boiler efficiency ~88%, turbine ~45%, generator ~98%); overall plant efficiency is only ~33–40%, illustrating why **classification into conversion stages** matters for system design.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain why **solar energy** is classified as a **non-conventional renewable** source. Discuss its **primary, secondary, final, and useful** forms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal town in Kerala proposes to install a **500 kW wind turbine**. Given $v = 12 \text{ m/s}$, $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$ swept area, and $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate the **(i)** available wind power and **(ii)** actual electrical power assuming an overall conversion efficiency of 35%. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Solar Energy Classification (7 Marks)**

1. **Renewable — Justification (2 marks):** Solar energy is continuously supplied by the Sun's nuclear fusion reactions. The Sun has an estimated remaining lifetime of ~5 billion years, so on human timescales, the flux is effectively inexhaustible. The replenishment rate far exceeds consumption, satisfying the renewable criterion.

2. **Non-Conventional — Justification (2 marks):** Large-scale solar harvesting (PV and Concentrated Solar Power) became commercially viable only in the last 20–25 years. Compared to coal and large-hydro (exploited for >100 years), solar is still in the technology-maturity growth phase, hence *non-conventional*.

3. **Form Classification (3 marks):**
   - **Primary:** Direct beam and diffuse sunlight at the Earth's surface (irradiance).
   - **Secondary:** DC electricity (from PV cell) or thermal energy of a heat-transfer fluid (from solar collector).
   - **Final:** AC electricity at the grid (after inverter + transformer).
   - **Useful:** Light from LED, mechanical motion from motor, heat from heater.

#### **Part (b) — Wind Turbine Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Swept area $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$
- Wind velocity $v = 12 \text{ m/s}$
- Air density $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Overall efficiency $\eta = 35\% = 0.35$

**(i) Available Wind Power (4 Marks):**

The fundamental kinetic-flux equation is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot v^3$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 200 \times (12)^3 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 200 \times 1728 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 345600 \\
&= 0.5 \times 423360 \\
&= 211680 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 211.68 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual Electrical Power Output (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical} &= \eta \times P_{wind} \\
&= 0.35 \times 211.68 \text{ kW} \\
&= 74.09 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The turbine generates approximately **74.09 kW** of electrical power — well below the nameplate 500 kW rating, illustrating why capacity factor and Betz limit must always be considered in wind-energy system design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Conventional with Non-Renewable:** Many students incorrectly equate the two. Large hydro is conventional BUT renewable. Nuclear is conventional BUT non-renewable. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the units in numericals:** Wind power in $\text{W}$, then convert to $\text{kW}$ only at the final step. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 3. **Applying Betz limit:** Wind turbines can extract a maximum of $\frac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%$ of the kinetic power. A 35% efficiency is realistic, but exceeding 59.3% is physically impossible. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Missing the Primary → Useful chain:** Always identify all four stages in descriptive answers. Skipping one stage costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Using outdated data:** If citing global capacity figures, use the most recent available data; KTU penalizes stale numbers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition (1-liner):** Energy source = natural/artificial reservoir convertible to useful work.
- [ ] **Two master categories:** Conventional vs Non-Conventional; Renewable vs Non-Renewable — **NOT synonyms**.
- [ ] **Four-tier hierarchy:** Primary → Secondary → Final → Useful (state transformations).
- [ ] **Two commerce types:** Commercial (priced, traded) vs Non-Commercial (subsistence).
- [ ] **Key constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (AM1.5 ground value ≈ 1000 W/m² under STC).
- [ ] **Wind power formula:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — note the $v^3$ cubic dependence.
- [ ] **Hydro power formula:** $P = \rho g Q h$ — derived from $E = mgh$ and $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.
- [ ] **Solar transmittance:** $G_{bn} = G_{sc} \cdot \tau^{\sec(\theta_z)}$.
- [ ] **Renewability criterion:** $R_r = \frac{E_{replenished}}{E_{consumed}} \geq 1$.
- [ ] **Betz limit:** Wind turbines extract at most **59.3%** of available kinetic power.
- [ ] **Conversion efficiency chains:** Coal plant ≈ 33–40%; Combined cycle gas ≈ 60%; Solar PV module ≈ 18–22%.
- [ ] **Examples to memorize:** Solar (non-conv, renew), Coal (conv, non-renew), Large hydro (conv, renew), Nuclear (conv, non-renew), Firewood (conv, renew, non-commercial).
- [ ] **KTU-favourite trick question:** *"Is geothermal renewable?"* — **Yes**, because Earth's internal heat is continuously produced by radioactive decay (replenishment rate ≈ 30 TW globally vs <0.1 TW human extraction).
- [ ] **Kerala-specific context:** Kerala has excellent solar (5–6 kWh/m²/day), moderate wind (Western Ghats), and emerging tidal potential in Gulf of Kutch region (Gujarat, but relevant for comparative questions).
- [ ] **Global 2024 capacity to remember:** Solar ~2000 GW, Wind ~1018 GW, Hydro ~1410 GW, Nuclear ~410 GW.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Importance of Non-Conventional Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Importance of Non-Conventional Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Conventional Energy (NCE)** refers to energy sources that are **renewable**, **sustainable**, **environmentally benign**, and which are **continuously replenished by natural processes** at a rate equal to or faster than their rate of consumption. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEBBT837 – Non-Conventional Energy Systems*, these are also termed **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** or **Alternative Energy Sources**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Non-conventional energy sources are the energy sources which are continuously replenished by natural processes and do not get exhausted by their use. Examples include solar, wind, biomass, tidal, geothermal, and ocean thermal energy."*

In contrast, **Conventional Energy Sources** (coal, petroleum, natural gas, nuclear fission fuels like uranium) are:
- **Finite** (depletable / exhaustible)
- Formed over **millions of years** of geological processes
- Often termed **non-renewable** because their replenishment rate is vanishingly small compared to human consumption rate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bank Account vs. Daily Salary" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you have **two sources of money**:
> 
> 1. **Conventional Energy = A Fixed Bank Account** — You have ₹10 lakh deposited decades ago. Every time you spend money, the balance drops. There is no fresh inflow. Eventually, the account becomes **empty** (resource depletion).
> 
> 2. **Non-Conventional Energy = Your Daily Salary** — Every morning, ₹5,000 is credited to your account. As long as you spend less than (or equal to) what gets credited daily, **the account never empties**. This is the essence of *sustainability* — consumption ≤ replenishment.
> 
> The **Sun** is the cosmic "employer" who pays the Earth this daily energy salary in the form of solar radiation. Every form of non-conventional energy (wind, hydro, biomass, wave) is simply a **re-packaged form of this daily solar deposit**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constant (Solar Energy Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar Constant (I₀) ≈ 1361 W/m²** — This is the rate at which solar energy strikes the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere per unit area, perpendicular to the solar beam. It is the foundational metric for almost all NCE calculations.
> 
> **Terrestrial Peak Irradiance (AM 1.5) ≈ 1000 W/m²** — After atmospheric attenuation, the maximum solar power available on Earth's surface at noon.

### 1.4 Why "Non-Conventional" vs "Renewable"?

The two terms are often used **synonymously** in KTU examinations, but a subtle distinction exists:

| Terminology | Emphasis | KTU Context |
|-------------|----------|-------------|
| **Non-Conventional** | Contrast against fossil/nuclear | Older terminology, still common in KTU QPs |
| **Renewable** | Self-replenishing nature | Modern, preferred in NEP 2020 framework |
| **Alternative** | Substitute for fossil fuels | Policy/industry usage |

For **examination purposes**, treat them as **interchangeable**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Energy Sources — Structured Logic

The classification follows a **decision-tree** logic based on two questions:
1. **Is it replenished naturally within a human timescale?**
2. **Does its use cause significant environmental degradation?**

#### A. Based on Replenishment Rate

1. **Renewable / Non-Conventional Sources** (Replenishment rate ≥ Consumption rate)
   - **Solar Energy** — Direct radiant energy from the Sun (photovoltaic + thermal)
   - **Wind Energy** — Kinetic energy of moving air masses
   - **Hydel / Hydroelectric Energy** — Potential + kinetic energy of water
   - **Biomass Energy** — Chemical energy stored in organic matter
   - **Geothermal Energy** — Heat from Earth's interior
   - **Tidal Energy** — Gravitational pull of Moon/Sun on seawater
   - **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — Temperature gradient between surface and deep ocean
   - **Wave Energy** — Surface ocean wave motion
   - **Hydrogen Energy** (via electrolysis using renewables)
   - **Fuel Cells** (powered by green hydrogen)

2. **Non-Renewable / Conventional Sources** (Replenishment rate ≪ Consumption rate)
   - **Fossil Fuels** — Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas (formed over ~300 million years)
   - **Nuclear (Fission)** — Uranium-235, Plutonium-239 (reserves last ~100 years at current use)

#### B. Based on Environmental Impact

1. **Green / Clean Sources** — Solar, Wind, Tidal, Geothermal, Hydro (low emissions)
2. **Brown / Polluting Sources** — Coal, Oil, Gas (high CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ emissions)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Significance |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $I_0 \approx 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Energy flux at top of atmosphere |
| 2 | Terrestrial Solar Irradiance (peak) | $I_{sc} \approx 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Standard test condition (STC) for PV |
| 3 | Total Solar Power Intercepted by Earth | $P_{\oplus} = I_0 \times \pi R_{\oplus}^2$ | W | Where $R_{\oplus} = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m |
| 4 | Numerical Value of $P_{\oplus}$ | $P_{\oplus} \approx 1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W | W | ~ 174 Petawatts |
| 5 | Captured by Earth Surface (~ 70%) | $\approx 1.22 \times 10^{17}$ W | W | Available for harvesting |
| 6 | Global Primary Energy Demand (2024) | $\approx 6 \times 10^{13}$ W = **60 TW** | W | Only ~ 0.05% of available solar! |
| 7 | Wind Power Density | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ | W/m² | Cubic dependence on velocity |
| 8 | Hydroelectric Power | $P = \rho g Q H$ | W | $\rho$ = water density, $Q$ = flow rate, $H$ = head |
| 9 | Tidal Power (barrage) | $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2 \omega$ | W | Function of tidal range $R$ |
| 10 | Geothermal Gradient | $\approx 25$–$30^\circ$C/km | °C/km | Earth's internal heat rises with depth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The cubic relationship $P \propto v^3$ for wind energy means that **doubling wind speed increases available power by 8 times**. This is a frequent exam question — examiners love testing this non-linear dependence.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-conventional energy systems power:
- **Decentralized rural electrification** (solar home lighting systems in Kerala's tribal hamlets)
- **Grid-scale renewable integration** (e.g., India's 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030)
- **Transportation electrification** (EVs charged by solar/wind)
- **Green hydrogen production** for hard-to-abate sectors (steel, ammonia, shipping)
- **Space applications** (satellite PV, solar-thermal propulsion)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Energy

| Parameter | Conventional Energy | Non-Conventional Energy |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Nature** | Finite, exhaustible | Infinite (on human timescale), renewable |
| **Formation Time** | Millions of years | Continuous / instantaneous |
| **Environmental Impact** | High CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, particulates | Negligible / zero emissions |
| **Carbon Footprint** | High (e.g., coal ≈ 820 g CO₂/kWh) | Near-zero |
| **Capital Cost** | Low–Medium | High (declining rapidly) |
| **Operating Cost** | High (fuel cost) | Low (free "fuel") |
| **Maintenance** | Complex | Generally simpler (no combustion) |
| **Reliability** | Dispatchable, on-demand | Variable (intermittent) — needs storage |
| **Geographic Dependence** | Portable (oil/gas can be shipped) | Site-specific (solar needs sun, wind needs wind) |
| **Examples** | Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal |
| **Reserves Lifetime (at current use)** | Coal ~ 130 yrs, Oil ~ 50 yrs, Gas ~ 50 yrs | Practically infinite |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Total Solar Power Available on Earth

This derivation is **highly probable** in KTU Module 1 questions. Let us compute the total solar power intercepted by Earth from the Sun.

**Given:**
- Solar Constant: $I_0 = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$
- Mean Earth Radius: $R_{\oplus} = 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$
- The Earth intercepts sunlight over a **circular disk** of radius $R_{\oplus}$ (not the full sphere).

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of the Earth facing the Sun**

The Earth presents a **circular cross-section** to the parallel solar rays. This is because the Sun is so far away that its rays are essentially parallel by the time they reach Earth.

$$
A_{\text{disk}} = \pi R_{\oplus}^2
$$

**Step 2: Express the area numerically**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{disk}} &= \pi \times (6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m})^2 \\
&= \pi \times (4.059 \times 10^{13}) \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 1.275 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> The square of $6.371 \times 10^6$ is computed as $6.371^2 \approx 40.59$, hence $40.59 \times 10^{12} = 4.059 \times 10^{13}$. Multiplying by $\pi \approx 3.1416$ gives the disk area.

**Step 3: Multiply by the Solar Constant to get total power intercepted**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\oplus} &= I_0 \times A_{\text{disk}} \\
&= 1361 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 1.275 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 1735.3 \times 10^{14} \text{ W} \\
&\approx 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Multiplication step: $1361 \times 1.275 = 1735.275$, hence $1735.275 \times 10^{14} = 1.735 \times 10^{17}$ W.

**Step 4: Result in Petawatts**

$$
P_{\oplus} \approx 174 \text{ Petawatts (PW)} = 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with global human energy consumption**

$$
\text{Global demand (2024)} \approx 60 \text{ TW} = 6 \times 10^{13} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the ratio**

$$
\frac{\text{Human Demand}}{\text{Available Solar}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{13}}{1.74 \times 10^{17}} \approx 3.45 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.034\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Humanity currently uses only **~0.034%** of the solar energy that strikes Earth. Capturing even **0.1%** of available solar would meet global demand **3 times over**. This mathematically proves the *immense importance* of non-conventional energy.

### 3.3 Energy Availability Hierarchy (Descended from Sun)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Sun}} &\rightarrow E_{\text{Solar Direct (PV/thermal)}} \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{Wind (atmospheric circulation)}} \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{Hydro (water cycle)}} \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{Biomass (photosynthesis)}} \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{Wave (wind-ocean coupling)}} \\
&\rightarrow E_{\text{Ocean Thermal (sun-heated surface)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: **Geothermal** and **Tidal** are **non-solar** in origin — they derive from Earth's internal heat (radiogenic decay) and Earth-Moon gravitational interaction respectively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Energy Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Sources] --> B{Conventional or Non-Conventional?}
    B --> C[Conventional Non-Renewable]
    B --> D[Non-Conventional Renewable]
    C --> C1[Fossil Fuels]
    C --> C2[Nuclear Fission]
    C1 --> C1a[Coal]
    C1 --> C1b[Petroleum]
    C1 --> C1c[Natural Gas]
    D --> D1[Solar Based]
    D --> D2[Non-Solar Based]
    D1 --> D1a[Photovoltaic]
    D1 --> D1b[Solar Thermal]
    D2 --> D2a[Wind]
    D2 --> D2b[Hydro]
    D2 --> D2c[Biomass]
    D2 --> D3[Planetary Based]
    D3 --> D3a[Tidal]
    D3 --> D3b[Geothermal]
    D3 --> D3c[Wave and OTEC]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Importance of NCE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sun Daily Energy Deposit] --> PV[Solar PV]
    SUN --> ST[Solar Thermal]
    SUN --> WIND[Wind Turbine]
    SUN --> HYDRO[Hydro Plant]
    SUN --> BIO[Biomass Reactor]
    EARTH[Earth Internal Heat] --> GEO[Geothermal]
    MOON[Moon Gravity] --> TIDAL[Tidal Barrage]
    PV --> GRID[Smart Grid Distribution]
    ST --> GRID
    WIND --> GRID
    HYDRO --> GRID
    BIO --> GRID
    GEO --> GRID
    TIDAL --> GRID
    GRID --> LOAD1[Domestic Load]
    GRID --> LOAD2[Industrial Load]
    GRID --> LOAD3[EV Charging]
    GRID --> LOAD4[Green H2 Production]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Importance Pillars of Non-Conventional Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Importance of NCE] --> P1[Environmental Sustainability]
    P --> P2[Energy Security]
    P --> P3[Economic Development]
    P --> P4[Social Equity]
    P --> P5[Technological Innovation]
    P1 --> P1a[Reduces CO2 Emissions]
    P1 --> P1b[Combats Climate Change]
    P2 --> P2a[Reduces Import Dependency]
    P2 --> P2b[Decentralized Generation]
    P3 --> P3a[Green Jobs Creation]
    P3 --> P3b[Long-Term Low Cost]
    P4 --> P4a[Rural Electrification]
    P4 --> P4b[Energy Access for All]
    P5 --> P5a[Drives Innovation]
    P5 --> P5b[Supports Space and Defense]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

Define non-conventional energy sources. Give any four examples.

**Model Answer:**

Non-conventional energy sources are those energy sources which are **continuously replenished by natural processes** and **do not get exhausted** by their continuous use. They are renewable, sustainable, and environment-friendly.

**Four examples:**
1. Solar Energy
2. Wind Energy
3. Biomass Energy
4. Tidal Energy

*(Alternative valid examples: Geothermal, OTEC, Wave, Small Hydro)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

State any three differences between conventional and non-conventional energy sources.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Conventional Energy | Non-Conventional Energy |
|---|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Finite / exhaustible (formed over millions of years) | Inexhaustible / renewable (replenished continuously) |
| 2 | High CO₂ and pollutant emissions | Zero / negligible emissions, eco-friendly |
| 3 | Examples: coal, oil, natural gas, uranium | Examples: solar, wind, hydro, biomass, tidal, geothermal |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme ESE regulations, students answer **one full question from a pair** (Choice-based). Both alternatives provided below are independent and complete 14-mark answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Modified from past patterns]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the importance of non-conventional energy sources. Discuss their role in mitigating climate change and ensuring energy security. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat derivation, compute the total solar power intercepted by the Earth. Given solar constant $= 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ and Earth radius $= 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Non-conventional energy sources are of **strategic, environmental, and economic importance** to any nation. The key reasons are:

**1. Environmental Sustainability**
- Conventional fossil fuels emit **~ 33 Gt CO₂/year** globally.
- Solar PV emits only **~ 40 g CO₂/kWh** over its lifetime (vs. coal's ~ 820 g CO₂/kWh).
- Reduces greenhouse gases, slowing global warming.

**2. Energy Security**
- India imports **~ 85% of its crude oil** and **~ 50% of its natural gas**, causing forex drain.
- NCE sources are **indigenous and inexhaustible** → reduces import dependence.
- Provides **decentralized generation** — critical for remote/rural areas.

**3. Economic Development**
- Creates **green jobs** (solar installers, wind technicians, biomass operators).
- After initial capital cost, **fuel cost is zero** → predictable long-term tariffs.
- India's NCE target: **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030**.

**4. Climate Change Mitigation**
- Paris Agreement (2015) commitments require **deep decarbonization**.
- NCE is the **only scalable pathway** to net-zero by 2070.

**5. Social Equity**
- Schemes like **Saubhagya** (rural electrification via solar home systems) bring electricity to unconnected households.

**Conclusion:** NCE is not just an alternative — it is the **inevitable future** of the global energy economy.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** The Earth intercepts sunlight over a circular disk of radius equal to Earth's radius.

$$
A_{\text{disk}} = \pi R_{\oplus}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $R_{\oplus} = 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{disk}} &= \pi \times (6.371 \times 10^6)^2 \\
&= \pi \times (40.59 \times 10^{12}) \\
&= 127.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 1.275 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total intercepted power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\oplus} &= I_0 \times A_{\text{disk}} \\
&= 1361 \times 1.275 \times 10^{14} \\
&= 1.735 \times 10^{17} \text{ W} \\
&\approx 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W} = 174 \text{ PW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The Earth intercepts approximately **$1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W (174 Petawatts)** of solar power.

**Comparison:** Global demand is ~ 60 TW. Hence, solar alone is ~ **2900 times** the global demand — proving the **enormous potential** of non-conventional solar energy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model, Adapted]*

**(a)** Classify non-conventional energy sources. Explain any two solar-based and two non-solar-based sources with one-line applications each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Why is wind energy called an indirect form of solar energy? Explain with the wind power density formula and discuss the cubic velocity dependence. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification:**

Non-conventional energy sources are classified based on their origin:

**(i) Solar-based sources** — originate from the Sun's radiation:
- **Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** — direct conversion of sunlight to electricity using semiconductors. *Application: rooftop solar panels, solar farms.*
- **Solar Thermal** — sunlight heats a working fluid to produce steam. *Application: solar water heaters, concentrated solar power (CSP) plants.*
- **Wind Energy** — driven by differential solar heating of land/sea. *Application: grid-connected wind farms.*
- **Biomass Energy** — chemical energy stored via photosynthesis. *Application: biogas plants, biodiesel.*
- **Hydroelectric Energy** — water cycle driven by solar heating. *Application: large dams like Idukki, run-of-river small hydro.*
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — uses warm surface vs. cold deep water. *Application: tropical island power plants.*
- **Wave Energy** — surface waves caused by wind. *Application: coastal wave-energy converters.*

**(ii) Non-solar-based sources** — originate from planetary/earth processes:
- **Tidal Energy** — gravitational pull of Moon/Sun on seawater. *Application: tidal barrage at Gulf of Kutch (proposed).*
- **Geothermal Energy** — heat from Earth's core (radioactive decay). *Application: Puga Valley (Ladakh) geothermal pilots.*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Why Wind is "Indirect Solar":**

Wind is caused by **uneven heating of Earth's surface** by the Sun. The equator receives more solar radiation than the poles, causing warm air to rise at the equator and cooler air to rush in from higher latitudes. This convection, combined with the **Coriolis effect** from Earth's rotation, generates global wind patterns (trade winds, westerlies, etc.). Since the Sun drives this thermal differential, wind energy is essentially **stored solar kinetic energy**.

**Wind Power Density Formula:**

The power available per unit swept area of a wind turbine is:

$$
\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density $\approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level
- $v$ = wind speed in m/s
- $A$ = swept area of blades in m²

**Derivation of cubic dependence:**

The kinetic energy of an air mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. The mass flow rate through area $A$ is $\dot{m} = \rho A v$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m} v^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance of $v^3$ dependence:**

- If wind speed **doubles**, available power **increases 8-fold**.
- This is why wind farms are sited in **high-wind corridors** (e.g., Western Ghats in Kerala, Muppandal in Tamil Nadu).
- Even a **small increase in turbine height** (where wind is stronger due to less surface friction) yields large power gains — hence the modern trend of **taller towers + longer blades**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Defining NCE without mentioning "renewable/replenished"** — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the *renewable* keyword. Omitting it = −1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up "renewable" with "clean/green"** — they overlap but are not identical. Be precise: renewable = replenishable; clean = low-emission.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical derivations** — Always write W/m², m², W. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark per KTU valuation key.
> 
> 4. **In the solar intercepted-power derivation**, students often use $4\pi R^2$ (full sphere area) instead of $\pi R^2$ (cross-sectional disk). This is a **classic KTU trap** — the Earth intercepts over a disk, not the full sphere. Getting this wrong = −2 marks.
> 
> 5. **In wind power questions**, students forget to mention that the formula gives **maximum available** power (Betz limit ≈ 59.3% is the theoretical maximum extractable). A complete answer must include the Betz limit context for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Definition & Importance of NCE**

- ✅ **Definition:** NCE = renewable + sustainable + inexhaustible on human timescale.
- ✅ **Solar Constant:** $I_0 \approx 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ (top of atmosphere).
- ✅ **Peak Terrestrial Irradiance:** $\approx 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ (AM 1.5, STC).
- ✅ **Total solar power intercepted by Earth:** $\approx 1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W = **174 PW**.
- ✅ **Disk area formula** (NOT sphere area): $A = \pi R_{\oplus}^2$.
- ✅ **Classification:** Solar-based (PV, thermal, wind, hydro, biomass, OTEC, wave) vs. Non-solar (tidal, geothermal).
- ✅ **Wind Power Density:** $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ — note **cubic dependence on velocity**.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** Maximum extractable wind power = **59.3%** (16/27).
- ✅ **Hydro Power:** $P = \rho g Q H$ — depends on **head** ($H$) and **flow rate** ($Q$).
- ✅ **Importance pillars:** Environmental, Energy Security, Economic, Social, Technological.
- ✅ **Numerical fact to memorize:** Humanity uses only ~ **0.034%** of available solar — huge untapped potential.
- ✅ **India context:** **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030** (COP26 commitment).
- ✅ **Examples:** Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal, Wave, OTEC, Hydrogen, Fuel Cells.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global and National Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global and National Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Global Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive quantitative and qualitative study of worldwide energy resources, their availability, production rates, consumption patterns, distribution infrastructure, reserves-to-production ratios, and future demand projections across all primary energy carriers (fossil, nuclear, and renewable).

> [!NOTE]
> **National Energy Scenario** is the country-specific (India-centric) analysis of energy resources, installed capacities across generation technologies, per capita energy consumption, sector-wise end-use distribution (industrial, domestic, agricultural, transport), energy security challenges, and policy frameworks governing conventional and non-conventional energy sources.

**Key Nomenclature Used by KTU Board:**
- **Primary Energy** – Energy extracted directly from nature (coal, crude oil, natural gas, sunlight, wind).
- **Final Energy** – Energy delivered to the end-user after conversion (electricity, gasoline, LPG).
- **Useful Energy** – Energy actually performing the desired service (light, mechanical work, heat).
- **Commercial Energy** – Energy bought and sold commercially (electricity, coal, petroleum).
- **Non-Commercial Energy** – Traditional fuels gathered free of cost (firewood, dung, crop residue).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of the Global Energy Scenario as a giant "family budget" for Planet Earth.** Just like a household tracks its monthly income (resources available), expenses (consumption), and savings (reserves), the world monitors its fossil fuel bank balance, energy spending habits, and future sustainability. When the budget gets tight (oil reserves running low), the family (humanity) must find alternative income streams — these are the **Non-Conventional Energy Sources** (solar, wind, tidal, biomass, geothermal, hydrogen).

For a KTU student reading this for the first time:
- **Energy Scenario = Resource Availability + Consumption Pattern + Future Demand Forecast + Policy Response**
- It is essentially a **"snapshot + forecast"** of where humanity stands in its energy journey.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (KTU Board Favourite Values)

The following metrics are **routinely tested** in KTU examinations:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Global Value (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy Consumption | PEC | Total energy used in a year | **~606 EJ/year** (2022) |
| Per Capita Energy Consumption | PCEC | Energy used per person per year | **~75 GJ/person** (World Avg.) |
| Installed Power Capacity | IC | Sum of all generation capacities | **~8.5 TW** (Global, 2023) |
| Reserve-to-Production Ratio | R/P | Years of reserve left at current rate | **~50 yrs (Oil)**, **~50 yrs (Gas)** |
| Energy Mix Share (Renewable) | — | % of renewables in total mix | **~30%** (Global Electricity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The board frequently asks students to recall **per capita consumption values**: India ≈ **25 GJ/person**, USA ≈ **~300 GJ/person**, World Average ≈ **~75 GJ/person**. Memorize these three numbers — they are guaranteed KTU marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of the Global Energy Scenario

The global energy scenario is built on **four pillars**, each addressed by structured analysis:

**Pillar 1: Resource Inventory**
- Proven reserves of fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas)
- Theoretical and technical potential of renewables (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, tidal, OTEC, biomass)
- Uranium reserves (for nuclear)

**Pillar 2: Production & Consumption Pattern**
- Annual extraction rates
- Sector-wise consumption split (Industry → ~37%, Transport → ~29%, Buildings → ~21%, Agriculture → ~2%, Other → ~11%)
- Geographic distribution (China, USA, India, EU, Russia = top 5 consumers)

**Pillar 3: Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio**
- Time horizon for which current reserves can sustain current production
- Computed as: $R/P = \dfrac{\text{Recoverable Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$

**Pillar 4: Environmental & Geopolitical Constraints**
- CO₂ emissions and climate change (Paris Agreement targets)
- Energy import dependency (India imports ~85% of its crude oil)
- Geopolitical volatility of fossil fuel supply

### 2.2 Structure of the Indian (National) Energy Scenario

India occupies a unique position characterized by:

1. **High Population, Low Per Capita Consumption** — 5th largest energy producer, but per capita consumption is one-fourth the world average.
2. **Coal-Dominant Generation Mix** — Coal accounts for ~50% of installed capacity and ~70% of actual electricity generation.
3. **Heavy Import Dependency** — ~85% of crude oil, ~50% of natural gas, and significant coking coal is imported.
4. **Aggressive Renewable Targets** — 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 (Panchamrit commitment at COP26).
5. **Sectoral Demand Drivers** — Industrialization, urbanization, rising middle-class lifestyle, EV penetration.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below have appeared in KTU previous year papers (2018–2024) and are predicted to recur under the 2024 Scheme.**

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Energy Consumption | $PCEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumption}}{\text{Population}}$ | GJ/person or kWh/person | Always use **annual** values |
| Annual Electricity Generation | $E = P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760$ | kWh or MWh | $CF$ = Capacity Factor (0 to 1) |
| Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Maximum Possible Energy}}$ | Dimensionless | Solar ≈ 0.15–0.25, Wind ≈ 0.20–0.40 |
| Reserve-to-Production Ratio | $R/P = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Years | Statutory meaning: years of reserve remaining |
| Plant Load Factor (PLF) | $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times 8760}$ | % | Used for thermal power plants |
| Energy Demand Growth (CAGR) | $D_n = D_0 \times (1+r)^n$ | kWh or MW | $r$ = growth rate, $n$ = years |
| Primary Energy → Electricity Conversion | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | % | Typical thermal plant: **30–40%** |
| Renewable Penetration Limit (Grid) | Grid stability breaks ~30–40% variable RE | % | Beyond this, storage mandatory |
| Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{E}{m}$ or $\dfrac{E}{V}$ | Wh/kg or Wh/L | For batteries / fuels |
| Energy Density (Fuel) | $\rho_E = \dfrac{Q_{HV}}{V}$ or $\dfrac{Q_{HV}}{m}$ | MJ/L or MJ/kg | Hydrogen ≈ 120 MJ/kg vs Petrol ≈ 44 MJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever a question gives you a population and total consumption, the first thing to calculate is **Per Capita Consumption**. Board examiners allocate 2–3 marks for this alone.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Study

Understanding the energy scenario is **not academic trivia** — it directly drives:

- **Power plant design decisions** (whether to invest in coal vs solar for the next 25 years).
- **Government policy** (subsidies, taxes on fossil fuels, renewable purchase obligations).
- **Industrial investment** (capacity addition, fuel choice, emission compliance).
- **Research priorities** (storage R&D, hydrogen economy, carbon capture).
- **Energy security strategies** (strategic petroleum reserves, diversification of supply).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)

**Problem Setup:**
Given a country with total annual primary energy consumption $E_{total}$ (in joules or kWh) and population $N$, derive the per capita energy consumption.

**Step 1 — Define the variable.**
Let $PCEC$ denote per capita energy consumption, measured in energy units per person per year.

**Step 2 — Express the definition mathematically.**

$$
PCEC \equiv \frac{E_{total}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert units to validate dimensional consistency.**

$$
\text{Units of PCEC} = \frac{\text{Joules (or kWh)}}{\text{persons}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style).**
Suppose India's total primary energy consumption ≈ **35,000 TWh** (≈ 35,000 × 3.6 PJ = **126,000 PJ**) and population ≈ **1.4 × 10⁹**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 35{,}000 \text{ TWh/year} \\
N &= 1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ persons} \\
PCEC &= \frac{35{,}000 \text{ TWh}}{1.4 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{35{,}000 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wh}}{1.4 \times 10^9} \\
&= 25{,}000 \text{ Wh/person} \\
&= 25 \text{ kWh/person per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to GJ/year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PCEC &= 25 \text{ kWh/day} \times 365 \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} \\
&= 32{,}850 \text{ MJ/year} \\
&\approx 32.85 \text{ GJ/person/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Note:** Always show **both** unit forms (kWh/person/day and GJ/person/year) when answering such questions. KTU examiners award 1 extra mark for unit conversion discipline.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

**Step 1 — Define R/P ratio.**
$R/P$ represents the number of years the proven reserves would last if production continued at the current annual rate.

**Step 2 — Write the equation.**

$$
R/P = \frac{R}{P_{annual}}
$$

where $R$ is the recoverable reserve at the start of the year and $P_{annual}$ is the production rate during that year.

**Step 3 — Worked Example (Crude Oil).**
Suppose proven oil reserves $R$ = **1.6 trillion barrels** and annual production $P$ = **~30 billion barrels/year**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R/P &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ barrels}}{30 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels/year}} \\
&\approx 53.3 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using cumulative production instead of **annual** production in the denominator. This error changes the answer by orders of magnitude. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this.

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Demand Forecast (CAGR Method)

**Step 1 — Define CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate).**
If demand today is $D_0$ and grows at a constant rate $r$ per annum, after $n$ years it becomes $D_n$.

**Step 2 — Write the governing equation.**

$$
D_n = D_0 \times (1+r)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example.**
India's electricity demand in 2024 ≈ **1,600 TWh**. Assume growth rate $r = 5\%$ per annum. Forecast demand in 2030 ($n = 6$ years).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= 1{,}600 \text{ TWh} \\
r &= 0.05 \\
n &= 6 \text{ years} \\
D_n &= 1600 \times (1.05)^6 \\
(1.05)^6 &= 1.3401 \\
D_n &= 1600 \times 1.3401 \\
&= 2144.16 \text{ TWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the projected demand in 2030 ≈ **2,144 TWh**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Global vs National Comparison (KTU Favorite Table)

| Parameter | Global | India (National) |
|---|---|---|
| Total Primary Energy Use | **~606 EJ/yr** | **~36 EJ/yr (≈ 6% of world)** |
| Per Capita Consumption | **~75 GJ/yr** | **~25 GJ/yr (1/3 of world avg.)** |
| Coal Share in Mix | **~27%** | **~50%** |
| Renewable Share in Electricity | **~30%** | **~25% (and rising fast)** |
| CO₂ Emissions (Annual) | **~37 GtCO₂** | **~2.7 GtCO₂ (3rd largest emitter)** |
| Crude Oil Import Dependency | Various | **~85%** |
| R/P Ratio (Oil) | **~50 years** | **~5–7 years (domestic fields)** |
| Installed Power Capacity | **~8.5 TW** | **~430 GW (May 2024)** |
| Per Capita Electricity Use | **~3,500 kWh/yr** | **~1,300 kWh/yr** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board examiners **love** asking students to fill in such comparison tables in Part A (3-mark) and Module Internal Choice questions. Memorize the bolded values.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of the Energy Scenario

Below is a **functional architecture flow** mapping how the four pillars of the energy scenario interlink (adapted for Mermaid rendering safety):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INPUT LAYER Energy Resources] --> nodeB[Identification and Quantification of Reserves]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Production Extraction and Conversion]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Primary Energy Supply EJ per year]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Transformation to Secondary Energy Refineries and Power Plants]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Final Energy Distribution Grid and Fuel Network]
    nodeF --> nodeG[END USE SECTORS Industry Transport Domestic Agriculture]
    nodeG --> nodeH[FEEDBACK LAYER Demand Growth and Reserve Depletion]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Policy and Planning Response]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Investment in Renewables and Efficiency]
    nodeJ --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeK[Environmental Constraints CO2 and Climate]
    nodeK --> nodeI
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **INPUT LAYER** → All primary energy sources enter the system here.
- **RESERVE → PRODUCTION → CONVERSION → DISTRIBUTION → END-USE** is the linear flow.
- **FEEDBACK LAYER** is the key non-linear element: depletion and emissions drive policy responses, which feed back to alter the input mix (e.g., adding more renewables).

### 4.2 Block Diagram — India's Energy Landscape (National Scenario)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1[COAL ~50 percent of capacity] --> nodeMIX[India Generation Mix 2024]
    nodeR2[NATURAL GAS ~6 percent] --> nodeMIX
    nodeR3[NUCLEAR ~2 percent] --> nodeMIX
    nodeR4[HYDRO ~10 percent] --> nodeMIX
    nodeR5[SOLAR ~14 percent and rising] --> nodeMIX
    nodeR6[WIND ~10 percent] --> nodeMIX
    nodeR7[BIOMASS and OTHERS ~8 percent] --> nodeMIX
    nodeMIX --> nodeLOAD[Demand Sectors Industry 41 percent Domestic 24 percent Agriculture 17 percent Commercial 9 percent Others 9 percent]
    nodeLOAD --> nodeGAP[Generation Demand Gap managed by Imports and Storage]
    nodeGAP --> nodePOLICY[POLICY FRAMEWORK National Electricity Plan 500 GW Non Fossil Target by 2030]
    nodePOLICY --> nodeR5
    nodePOLICY --> nodeR6
```

**Interpretation:**
- Coal dominates India's grid today, but **solar and wind are the fastest-growing segments**.
- Industrial demand dominates, but **domestic demand is rising rapidly** due to lifestyle changes and cooling needs.

### 4.3 Schematic: Energy Reserve Depletion Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1[Year 2024] --> nodeT2[Year 2030 Fossil fuel R P reduces by 15 percent]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Year 2040 Renewable share crosses 50 percent globally]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[Year 2050 Net Zero Targets under Paris Agreement]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Year 2070 Conventional Fossil Reserves effectively exhausted for economic use]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Year 2100 Energy Economy dominated by Solar Wind Hydrogen and Storage]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**
**Define "Global Energy Scenario" and list any four key parameters used to characterize it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Global Energy Scenario refers to the systematic study of worldwide energy resources, their availability, consumption patterns, production rates, and future demand projections across all primary energy sources including fossil fuels, nuclear, and renewables.

**Four key parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Total Primary Energy Consumption** (EJ/year) — overall scale of energy use.
2. **Per Capita Energy Consumption** (GJ/person/year) — standard of living indicator.
3. **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)** — sustainability indicator in years.
4. **Energy Mix Share** (%) — diversification across sources.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Installed Power Capacity (TW) and CO₂ emissions (GtCO₂/year).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**What is the current per capita energy consumption of India? Compare it with the world average and USA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Country / Region | Per Capita Energy Consumption |
|---|---|
| **India** | **~25 GJ/person/year (~25 kWh/day)** |
| **World Average** | **~75 GJ/person/year** |
| **USA** | **~300 GJ/person/year** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** India's per capita energy consumption is **about one-third of the world average** and **one-twelfth of the USA**, indicating significant untapped demand potential as the country develops.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **global energy scenario** with reference to primary energy consumption pattern, dominant energy sources, and the share of renewables. Highlight the major reasons for the shift toward non-conventional energy sources. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With relevant data, describe the **national energy scenario of India**. Explain why India is heavily dependent on coal and imported crude oil despite the abundance of renewable potential. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**1. Global Energy Consumption Pattern (2 Marks):**
- Global primary energy consumption ≈ **606 EJ/year (2022)**.
- Top consumers: China (~25%), USA (~15%), India (~7%), EU (~10%), Russia (~5%).
- Sector-wise split: Industry (37%), Transport (29%), Buildings (21%), Agriculture (2%).

**2. Dominant Energy Sources (2 Marks):**
- **Fossil fuels still dominate** with ~82% of primary energy mix:
  - Oil: ~31%
  - Coal: ~27%
  - Natural Gas: ~24%
- Renewables (incl. hydro) ≈ 13%, Nuclear ≈ 4%.

**3. Share of Renewables (1 Mark):**
- Renewables account for ~30% of global electricity generation, but only ~13% of primary energy (due to low conversion efficiency and intermittency).

**4. Reasons for Shift to Non-Conventional Sources (2 Marks):**
- **Depletion of fossil reserves** (R/P ratio of oil ≈ 50 years).
- **Climate change concerns** (CO₂ from fossil fuels = ~75% of GHG emissions).
- **Geopolitical risks** and import dependency.
- **Falling costs of solar and wind** (solar PV cost ↓ 90% in last decade).
- **Energy access and rural electrification** needs in developing nations.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**1. Indian Energy Scenario Data (3 Marks):**
- Installed capacity ≈ **430 GW (May 2024)**.
- Coal-based capacity ≈ 50%, but generates ~70% of actual electricity.
- Renewable capacity ≈ 200 GW (Solar ~85 GW, Wind ~47 GW).
- Per capita electricity consumption ≈ 1,300 kWh/year.
- Annual CO₂ emissions ≈ 2.7 GtCO₂ (3rd largest globally).

**2. Why Coal Dependency Persists (2 Marks):**
- **Abundant domestic reserves** (~360 billion tonnes, enough for 100+ years).
- **Low cost** of coal-based generation (~₹3–4/kWh).
- **Existing infrastructure** — legacy thermal power plants with 25–40 year lifetimes.
- **Base-load reliability** — coal plants provide 24×7 dispatchable power unlike solar/wind.

**3. Why Crude Oil is Imported (2 Marks):**
- India has only ~0.3% of global oil reserves but ~18% of global population.
- Domestic crude oil production ≈ **30 MT/year** vs consumption ≈ **240 MT/year** → ~85% import.
- Strategic location near major shipping lanes (Strait of Hormuz, Malacca) creates both opportunity and vulnerability.
- Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) maintained at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, Padur to mitigate supply shocks.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio**. Compute the R/P ratio for crude oil given that reserves = 1.6 trillion barrels and annual production = 30 billion barrels. Comment on the implication of this value. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the **major challenges** faced by India in meeting its energy demand. Suggest **five policy interventions** to address them. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**1. Definition of R/P Ratio (2 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio is defined as the ratio of the remaining recoverable reserves at the beginning of a year to the production (extraction) rate during that year. It indicates the number of years the reserves would last if production continued at the current rate.

**Mathematical form:**

$$
R/P = \frac{R}{P_{annual}}
$$

**2. Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ barrels} \\
P &= 30 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels/year} \\
R/P &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{12}}{30 \times 10^{9}} \\
&= \frac{1600}{30} \\
&= 53.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Implication (2 Marks):**
- At current production rates, proven oil reserves will last **~53 years**.
- This does **not** mean the world will "run out" of oil in 2077 — reserves are dynamic and increase with new discoveries and improved extraction technology.
- However, it signals **urgent need** for transitioning to alternative sources, since demand is projected to grow and ease of extraction declines over time.
- Also, oil prices will rise as harder-to-reach reserves are exploited (tar sands, deep-sea drilling), making renewable substitutes economically attractive earlier.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**1. Major Challenges (4 Marks):**

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Import Dependency** | ~85% crude oil, ~50% natural gas imported — exposes India to price shocks and currency risks |
| **Coal-Dominant Generation** | ~50% capacity is coal → high CO₂ emissions, ageing fleet, stranded asset risk |
| **Per Capita Gap** | At 1,300 kWh/person, India lags global avg of 3,500 kWh/person — significant unmet demand |
| **Grid Integration of Renewables** | Variable solar/wind output creates grid stability challenges beyond 30% penetration |
| **Energy Access** | Although ~100% electrification achieved, ~24-hour reliable supply remains a goal |
| **Financing Gap** | ~$150 billion investment needed annually for clean energy transition |

**2. Five Policy Interventions (3 Marks):**

1. **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)** — Mandate DISCOMs to source a minimum % from renewables.
2. **Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme** — Boost domestic manufacturing of solar cells, modules, EV batteries, electrolysers.
3. **National Hydrogen Mission** — Target 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030.
4. **PM-KUSUM and Saubhagya Schemes** — Decentralized solar for agriculture pumps and rural household electrification.
5. **Energy Storage Obligations** — Mandate 4-hour storage capacity for new RE projects to ensure round-the-clock supply.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Energy Scenario Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Primary Energy with Electricity Generation** — Always state clearly whether the value is in EJ (primary) or TWh (electricity). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed.
> 2. **Outdated Data** — Using pre-2022 statistics will cost 1 mark. Always cite the **most recent fiscal year** data.
> 3. **Missing Units** — Writing "India uses 1,300 kWh" without "/person/year" loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why"** — Board examiners award marks for explaining **why** India depends on coal, **why** R/P ratio matters — not just listing numbers.
> 5. **No Diagram** — A 14-mark question **must** have at least one flowchart or pie-chart-style representation. Absence costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Calculation Mistakes** — In CAGR and R/P problems, students often invert the formula. Memorize: $R/P = R \div P$, $D_n = D_0 \times (1+r)^n$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Energy Scenario (Module 1)**

- **Global primary energy consumption ≈ 606 EJ/year; fossil fuels ≈ 82% share.**
- **India's per capita energy consumption ≈ 25 GJ/person/year; world avg ≈ 75; USA ≈ 300.**
- **India's installed capacity ≈ 430 GW; renewable share growing rapidly toward 500 GW target by 2030.**
- **R/P ratio of crude oil globally ≈ 50 years — a key driver for renewable transition.**
- **India imports ~85% of crude oil — energy security is a national strategic priority.**
- **Five pillars of energy scenario:** Resource Inventory → Production → Consumption → R/P ratio → Policy.
- **Formulas to memorize cold:**
  - $PCEC = E_{total} / N$
  - $R/P = R / P_{annual}$
  - $E = P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760$
  - $CF = E_{actual} / E_{max}$
  - $D_n = D_0 (1+r)^n$
- **India's "Panchamrit" climate commitments:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity, 50% renewable share, 45% emissions intensity reduction, net-zero by 2070.
- **Always cite a year** for every statistical value — KTU examiners deduct marks for vague statements like "India uses a lot of coal".
- **Draw at least one diagram** for every 14-mark answer — flowcharts, pie charts, or block diagrams are all acceptable.
- **Sector-wise energy consumption order (Global):** Industry > Transport > Buildings > Agriculture.
- **India's sector-wise electricity use order:** Industry > Domestic > Agriculture > Commercial.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impacts of Conventional Energy Sources
India's Non-Conventional Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** India's Non-Conventional Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Non-conventional energy sources (NCES)**, also termed **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** or **Alternative Energy Sources**, are naturally replenishing energy flows that exist independently of finite fossil-fuel reserves. In the Indian context, the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) formally classifies them as energy derived from solar, wind, biomass, small hydro, geothermal, tidal, wave, ocean-thermal, and hydrogen-based pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Module 1):**
> *"Non-conventional energy sources are inexhaustible, indigenous, eco-friendly, and sustainable energy resources that can be harnessed without depleting the natural capital of a nation. For India, they represent the strategic pathway to bridge the energy-demand gap while honouring the commitments made at the COP summits."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Kitchen Pantry"

Imagine your family kitchen. Conventional energy (coal, oil, gas) is like a **packed lunchbox bought from a shop**: you consume it once, and when it is gone, you must buy another. It also generates "trash" (CO₂, ash, pollutants). Non-conventional energy is like a **kitchen garden** at the back of your house — sunlight, wind, flowing water, agricultural waste — that keeps producing fresh "ingredients" every day, free of cost, and with zero waste. A wise family uses the garden for daily meals and keeps the packed lunchbox only for emergencies. This is exactly the strategic logic India is applying to its national energy mix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - *Conventional sources* → Stock-type, finite, polluting (coal, lignite, petroleum, natural gas, nuclear).
> - *Non-conventional sources* → Flow-type, infinite (within human time-scales), non-polluting, indigenous.

## 1.3 The Indian Energy Backdrop — Why NCES is Critical

India is the **third-largest primary energy consumer in the world** and **imports nearly 40–45 % of its crude-oil requirement**. The geography of India — lying between latitudes 8°4′ N and 37°6′ N — gives it a tropical advantage: roughly **300–330 sunny days per year** and a **solar insolation of 4–7 kWh/m²/day** over most regions. Coupled with a **7,516 km coastline**, the Western Ghats wind corridor, vast agricultural biomass, and geothermal hot springs, India is uniquely endowed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Compelling Drivers (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Energy Security** — Reduces import dependence of fossil fuels.
> 2. **Environmental Obligation** — India has committed to **Net-Zero by 2070** at COP-26 (Glasgow, 2021).
> 3. **Rural Electrification & Decentralization** — Distributed generation suits India's 600,000+ villages.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Indian Energy Metrics

| Metric | Value (Bold) |
|---|---|
