| Solar constant (extra-terrestrial) | **1367 W/m²** |
| Peak Solar Insolation over India (avg.) | **5.5 kWh/m²/day** |
| Annual Wind Power Potential (MNRE assessed @ 80 m hub) | **~302 GW** |
| Theoretical Small-Hydro Potential (≤ 25 MW) | **21,000 MW** |
| Biomass Surplus (agricultural residues) | **~500 million tonnes/year** |
| Tidal Energy (Estimated) | **~9,000 MW** |
| Wave Energy (Estimated) | **~40,000–60,000 MW** |
| Geothermal (Identified sites) | **~10,000 MW** |
| Coastline length | **7,516 km** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Non-Conventional Energy Sources in India

The MNRE classification is the de-facto academic classification used by KTU. Every source can be grouped by the **physical phenomenon** that produces the energy.

### A. Solar Energy (The Crown Jewel of India)
- **Direct Conversion** → Photovoltaic (PV) cells, solar thermal collectors, Concentrated Solar Power (CSP).
- **Indirect Conversion** → Solar-induced wind, biomass, ocean thermal.

### B. Wind Energy
- Kinetic energy of moving air converted by horizontal-axis wind turbines (HAWT) or vertical-axis machines (VAWT).
- India is the **4th largest wind-power producer globally**.

### C. Biomass & Biogas
- Agricultural residue, municipal solid waste (MSW), bagasse, cattle dung.
- Conversion routes: combustion, gasification, anaerobic digestion, fermentation to ethanol.

### D. Small Hydro Power (SHP) — ≤ 25 MW
- Run-of-river, mini/micro hydro projects.
- Largely **decentralized**, ideal for hilly states.

### E. Geothermal Energy
- Heat from the earth's interior. Identified sites: Manikaran (HP), Puga Valley (Ladakh), Surajkund (Jharkhand), Unai (Maharashtra).

### F. Ocean Energy (Three Sub-Categories)
- **Tidal Energy** — Gujarat (Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Cambay) has the largest potential.
- **Wave Energy** — Kerala and Tamil Nadu coastlines.
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — Andaman & Nicobar, Lakshadweep (ΔT ≥ 20 °C).

### G. Hydrogen Energy & Fuel Cells
- Green hydrogen (electrolysis powered by renewables) is India's bet for the next decade.

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet for India-Specific Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value and pipe symbols are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown-table breakage.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Application in India |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Power Available | $P = A \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | $A$ = area (m²), $G$ = irradiance (W/m²), $\eta$ = efficiency | Sizing PV plants (Bhadla, Pavagada) |
| 2 | Daily Energy Yield (PV) | $E_d = A \cdot H_t \cdot \eta_{sys} \cdot PR$ | $H_t$ = peak sun hours, $PR$ = performance ratio (0.75–0.85) | MNRE plant-design norm |
| 3 | Wind Power Density | $P/_{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^{3}$ | $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³), $V$ = wind speed (m/s) | Muppandal wind farm, Tamil Nadu |
| 4 | Betz Limit (Maximum extractable) | $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical cap on any wind turbine |
| 5 | Tidal Power (Barrage) | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h^{2}$ | $h$ = tidal range (m), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $A$ = basin area | Gulf of Kutch feasibility studies |
| 6 | Wave Power per metre crest | $P/_{L} = \tfrac{\rho \cdot g^{2}}{64 \pi} \cdot H^{2} \cdot T$ | $H$ = wave height, $T$ = period | Vizhinjam (Kerala) experimental plant |
| 7 | OTEC Efficiency (Carnot) | $\eta_{OTEC} = 1 - T_{c}/T_{h}$ | $T$ in Kelvin | Lakshadweep pilot feasibility |
| 8 | Biogas Yield (Boyle's equation) | $V_{CH_4} = V_{digester} \cdot S_0 \cdot (Y/_{CH_4})$ | $S_0$ = substrate loading (kg VS/m³·d) | KVIC / DEEP NGO designs |
| 9 | Capacity Utilisation Factor | $CUF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$ | $E$ in kWh, dimensionless ratio | Performance benchmarking |
| 10 | Levelised Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \Sigma(C_t/(1+r)^{t}) \; / \; \Sigma(E_t/(1+r)^{t})$ | $C_t$ = cost, $E_t$ = energy, $r$ = discount rate | SECI tender evaluations |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Source — Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Solar PV in Rajasthan/Gujarat** — Ultra-high direct normal irradiance (DNI) enables some of the lowest LCOE in the world (~₹2/kWh in recent SECI auctions).
- **Wind in Tamil Nadu** — The Aralvaimozhi Pass creates a natural venturi accelerating the south-west monsoon winds, giving 6–8 m/s at 80 m hub height year-round.
- **Biomass in Punjab & UP** — Rice and wheat straw, otherwise burnt (causing the north-Indian smog), can power 1–10 MW plants.
- **Small hydro in the Western Ghats & Himalayas** — High rainfall, perennial streams, and steep gradients make run-of-river plants viable at the village scale.
- **Tidal at Gulf of Kutch** — Tidal range of **5–7 m**, among the highest in the world (comparable to the Bay of Fundy, Canada).
- **OTEC near Lakshadweep** — Surface (~28 °C) and deep-water (~6 °C) temperature difference exceeds the **20 °C minimum** required for viable OTEC operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Why NCES is suited for India?" always combine a **geographic reason** (latitude, coastline, biomass surplus) with a **policy reason** (MNRE subsidy, REC mechanism, government of India targets). A mono-dimensional answer loses marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Wind Power Density Over an Indian Site

The wind-power equation is one of the **most-tested derivations** in KTU Module 1. We derive it from first principles of fluid dynamics.

### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of a Moving Air Parcel

A mass of air $m$ moving with velocity $V$ has kinetic energy:

$$E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot V^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Express Mass in terms of Air Density and Volume

Mass = density × volume:

$$m = \rho \cdot \text{Vol}$$

The "volume" of air passing through the rotor swept area $A$ per unit time is $A \cdot V$. So mass-flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$

### Step 3 — Power Available in the Wind

Power = kinetic energy per unit time:

$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Power Density (Power per unit area)

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$\frac{P_{wind}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^{3}$$

This is the **Wind Power Density (WPD)**. For an Indian site with $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $V = 7$ m/s (typical of Muppandal, Tamil Nadu):

$$\frac{P_{wind}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 7^{3}$$

$$= 0.6125 \cdot 343 \;\; \text{W/m}^{2}$$

$$= 210.09 \;\; \text{W/m}^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Betz Limit (Maximum extractable)

Betz proved that no turbine can extract more than **16/27 ≈ 59.26 %** of the kinetic energy, otherwise the air would reverse direction.

$$P_{max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3} = 0.5926 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3}$$

Numerically, for our 7 m/s site with rotor area 100 m²:

$$P_{max} = 0.5926 \cdot 0.6125 \cdot 100 \cdot 343 \approx 12{,}447 \;\; \text{W} \approx 12.45 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Logic Explained:** Every step here used either dimensional substitution (Step 1→2) or a direct physical principle (Betz limit, Step 5). A KTU full-mark answer must annotate **each** step with a one-line "why" — examiners award 1 mark per logical step.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Energy Demand vs Supply in India

A frequently-asked analytical problem in KTU Module 1 is the **energy-gap analysis**. We model it step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

- $D$ = projected energy demand in year $Y$ (in TWh).
- $C$ = installed conventional capacity (GW).
- $R$ = installed renewable capacity (GW).
- $CF_c$, $CF_r$ = capacity factors of conventional and renewable plants respectively.
- $H$ = hours in a year = 8760.

### Step 2 — Express Energy Supply

$$E_{supply} = (C \cdot CF_c + R \cdot CF_r) \cdot 8760$$

For India (typical planning values):
- $C = 230$ GW, $CF_c = 0.65$ (coal thermal).
- $R = 125$ GW, $CF_r = 0.25$ (mixed solar+wind).

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation

$$E_{conventional} = 230 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 8760 = 1{,}309{,}620 \;\; \text{GWh} = 1309.62 \;\; \text{TWh}$$

$$E_{renewable} = 125 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 8760 = 273{,}750 \;\; \text{GWh} = 273.75 \;\; \text{TWh}$$

$$E_{supply,total} = 1309.62 + 273.75 = 1583.37 \;\; \text{TWh}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to Demand

The Central Electricity Authority (CEA) projects demand of ~**1900 TWh** by 2030. The gap is:

$$\Delta E = 1900 - 1583.37 = 316.63 \;\; \text{TWh}$$

This gap (~17 % shortfall) is the strategic justification for the MNRE's **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030**.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — Selection of NCES for Different Indian Regions

| Indian Region | Dominant NCES | Reason (Why) | Typical Capacity Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thar Desert (Rajasthan) | Solar PV & CSP | Low rainfall, highest DNI (~6.5 kWh/m²/d) | 100 MW – 2 GW |
| Western Ghats (Tamil Nadu, Kerala) | Wind | Venturi-accelerated monsoon winds (6–8 m/s) | 50 MW – 300 MW |
| Punjab, Haryana, UP (Indo-Gangetic plain) | Biomass (rice straw) | Surplus 50 Mt/year; reduces stubble burning | 5 – 20 MW |
| Himalayan foothills | Small Hydro | Steep gradients, perennial rivers | 1 – 25 MW |
| Gulf of Kutch (Gujarat) | Tidal | Tidal range 5–7 m; narrow bay | 50 – 250 MW |
| Lakshadweep, Andamans | OTEC & Solar | Deep-ocean ΔT ≥ 20 °C; islanded grids | 1 – 10 MW (island) |
| Puga Valley (Ladakh) | Geothermal | Identified hot springs (~150 °C) | Pilot scale |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Recap (KTU 2024):** Part A = 3 marks (short answers, no choice). Part B = 14 marks (choice-based, two sub-parts of 7 marks each). Module-1 weightage is typically **15–20 %** of the entire paper.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"List any four non-conventional energy sources available in India with one geographical region of high potential for each."**

**Model Answer (12–15 lines):**
1. **Solar Energy** → High potential in Thar Desert (Rajasthan) and Rann of Kutch (Gujarat) due to high DNI of 5.5–6.5 kWh/m²/day and 300+ sunny days.
2. **Wind Energy** → Tamil Nadu (Muppandal, Aralvaimozhi pass) and Gujarat (Kutch, Saurashtra) with wind speeds 6–8 m/s at 80 m hub.
3. **Tidal Energy** → Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay (Gujarat) with tidal range of 5–7 m, among the highest in the world.
4. **Small Hydro** → Western Ghats and Himalayan foothills, with estimated 21,000 MW potential in India.
5. **Geothermal** → Puga Valley (Ladakh), Manikaran (Himachal Pradesh) and Surajkund (Jharkhand).
6. **Biomass** → Indo-Gangetic plains (Punjab, UP, Haryana) due to surplus agricultural residue.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Differentiate between conventional and non-conventional energy sources with two examples each."**

**Model Answer (Tabular form, 10–12 lines):**

| Parameter | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| Renewability | Exhaustible (finite stock) | Inexhaustible (continuous flow) |
| Environmental impact | High CO₂, SO₂, particulate matter | Near-zero emissions |
| Examples | Coal, lignite, petroleum, natural gas, nuclear | Solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal |
| Time to replenish | Millions of years | Continuous / seconds to months |
| Capital vs operating cost | Low capex, high opex | High capex, low opex |
| Geographic concentration | Localised (mines, ports) | Distributed (rooftop, rural) |
| Strategic concern | Import dependence (40–45 % oil) | Indigenous and self-reliant |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

> **"With reference to India's non-conventional energy scenario:**  
> **(a) Discuss the salient features of the National Solar Mission (JNNSM). Why is India considered a global leader in solar deployment? (7 marks)**  
> **(b) Estimate the annual energy output of a 10 MW solar PV plant located in Bhadla, Rajasthan, given the following: average daily insolation 5.8 kWh/m²/day, module efficiency 18 %, system efficiency (inverter + losses) 90 %, performance ratio 0.78, and total module area 55,500 m². Comment on the result. (7 marks)"**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Genesis & Targets (2 marks):**
The Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM) was launched in **2010** under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). It set an initial target of **20,000 MW by 2022** which was later revised to **100,000 MW by 2022** and subsequently to **280 GW of solar capacity by 2030** as part of the Panchamrit announcements at COP-26 (Glasgow, 2021).

**2. Salient Features (3 marks):**
- **Phase-wise scaling:** Phase-I (2010–13) target 1,100 MW, Phase-II 9,000 MW, Phase-III 20 GW+ ultra-mega projects.
- **Bundled & reverse-auction model:** Conducted by SECI through VGF (Viability Gap Funding) of up to 30 % of project cost.
- **Deemed-Approved "Must-Run" status:** Solar plants given priority dispatch on the grid.
- **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO):** Obligates DISCOMs to procure a minimum % of solar.
- **Solar Parks Scheme:** Development of 50 ultra-mega solar parks with plug-and-play infrastructure (e.g., Bhadla, Pavagada, Rewa).
- **CPSU Scheme:** Government producer 12 GW of solar for own consumption.

**3. Why India is a Global Leader (2 marks):**
- Lowest solar tariffs globally: ₹2.00–2.50/kWh in 2020–21 SECI auctions.
- Largest single-location solar park: Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan, 2.25 GW).
- Massive manufacturing push: PLI scheme for ₹45,000 crore.
- Major investor-friendly reforms and a single-window clearance mechanism.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Plant capacity $P_{rated}$ = 10 MW
- Daily insolation $H_t$ = 5.8 kWh/m²/day
- Module efficiency $\eta_m$ = 0.18
- System efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ = 0.90
- Performance ratio $PR$ = 0.78
- Module area $A$ = 55,500 m²

**Step 1 — Daily Energy Output:**

$$E_d = A \cdot H_t \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_{sys} \cdot PR$$

$$E_d = 55{,}500 \cdot 5.8 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.78$$

**Step 2 — Multiply sequentially:**

$$E_d = 55{,}500 \cdot 5.8 = 321{,}900 \;\; \text{kWh/day equivalent}$$

$$E_d = 321{,}900 \cdot 0.18 = 57{,}942 \;\; \text{kWh/day}$$

$$E_d = 57{,}942 \cdot 0.90 = 52{,}147.8 \;\; \text{kWh/day}$$

$$E_d = 52{,}147.8 \cdot 0.78 = 40{,}675.28 \;\; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Energy Output:**

$$E_{annual} = E_d \cdot 365 = 40{,}675.28 \cdot 365 = 14{,}846{,}477.6 \;\; \text{kWh/year}$$

$$E_{annual} \approx 14.85 \;\; \text{GWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF):**

$$CUF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760} = \frac{14{,}846{,}477.6}{10{,}000 \cdot 8760} = \frac{14.85}{87.6} \approx 0.1695$$

$$CUF \approx 16.95 \;\%$$

**Step 5 — Comments (1 mark):**
- The CUF of ~17 % is **typical for Indian solar plants** and is below the ideal 20 % but reasonable for Bhadla.
- Possible improvements: bifacial modules, single-axis tracking, robotic cleaning, and DC-side optimisers.
- Annual generation of **14.85 GWh** can power ~10,000 rural households at average consumption of 1,500 kWh/year.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Confusing "module area" with "plant area" — if the question gives plant area, you must divide by packing density (~0.5) to get module area.
> 2. Forgetting to multiply by 365 for annual energy. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for missing the annualisation step.
> 3. Mixing up efficiency values — module efficiency (η) and system efficiency (η_sys) are **different** and must be multiplied separately.
> 4. Not stating units at the end of every step — strict SI conformity earns bonus marks.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] (ALTERNATIVE)**

> **"With respect to India's non-conventional energy landscape:**  
> **(a) Explain the four major challenges in the large-scale integration of renewable energy into the Indian grid and outline MNRE's mitigation strategies. (7 marks)**  
> **(b) Calculate the maximum extractable wind power from a 1.5 MW turbine having rotor diameter 80 m, hub-height wind speed 12 m/s, air density 1.18 kg/m³, and power coefficient 0.45. Compare this with the Betz-limit value. (7 marks)"**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**The four integration challenges (4 marks):**
1. **Intermittency & Variability** — Solar output drops to zero at night; wind fluctuates within minutes. Grid balancing becomes difficult.
2. **Low Capacity Factor** — Solar CUF 15–22 %, Wind CUF 25–35 % (combined < 30 %) versus coal CUF of 60–70 %.
3. **Inertia & Stability** — RE plants use power-electronic inverters, providing little rotational inertia → frequency instability.
4. **Transmission & Curtailment** — RE-rich states (TN, Rajasthan, Gujarat) far from load centres; evacuation infrastructure is constrained, leading to curtailment of up to 5–7 %.

**MNRE mitigation strategies (3 marks):**
- **Round-the-Clock (RTC) tenders** — bundled solar + storage to ensure 24/7 supply.
- **Pumped Storage & BESS** — National Mission on Advanced Storage with 50 GWh target.
- **Green Energy Corridors** — dedicated transmission lines (₹41,000 crore scheme).
- **Forecasting & Scheduling** — Vidyut Pravah portal + AI-based RE forecasting by NIWE & IMD.
- **Ancillary services market** — Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) to provide virtual inertia.
- **National Smart Grid Mission** — Distribution automation, AMI rollouts.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $D$ (rotor diameter) = 80 m → radius $r = 40$ m
- $V$ = 12 m/s
- $\rho$ = 1.18 kg/m³
- $C_p$ = 0.45 (actual)
- $C_{p,max}$ = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (Betz)

**Step 1 — Rotor Swept Area:**

$$A = \pi \cdot r^{2} = \pi \cdot 40^{2} = 1600 \pi \approx 5026.55 \;\; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Available Wind Power:**

$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3} = 0.5 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 12^{3}$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 8{,}685{,}958.4 \approx 5{,}124{,}635 \;\; \text{W} \approx 5.12 \;\; \text{MW}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Power Output (with $C_p = 0.45$):**

$$P_{actual} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.45 \cdot 5.12 \approx 2.31 \;\; \text{MW}$$

**Step 4 — Betz-Limit Power (with $C_{p,max} = 0.5926$):**

$$P_{Betz} = 0.5926 \cdot 5.12 \approx 3.03 \;\; \text{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison & Interpretation:**

The turbine is operating at $C_p/C_{p,max} = 0.45/0.5926 = 0.7595 \approx 76\%$ of the Betz limit, which is **good aerodynamic design** (modern utility turbines operate at 75–85 % of Betz).

The **rated capacity** of 1.5 MW is **less than the calculated available power (2.31 MW)**, which is normal — turbines are pitch-controlled to cap output and prevent over-speed damage during high-wind conditions. This is called "**derating**".

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Confusing "rotor diameter" with "radius" — a diameter of 80 m means radius is 40 m, not 80 m. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. Forgetting the **½** factor in the wind-power equation — a frequent error.
> 3. Not stating the Betz coefficient explicitly (16/27 ≈ 0.593) — losing half-mark on "compare" sub-question.
> 4. Failing to comment on the **derating** concept in the final step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **India's three strategic reasons** for NCES push → Energy Security, Climate Commitment, Rural Electrification.
- **MNRE** is the apex nodal ministry; **SECI**, **IREDA**, **NIWE**, **NISE**, **NIBE** are key technical/implementation agencies.
- **Solar potential** is highest in Rajasthan/Gujarat (Thar, Rann of Kutch). India targets **280 GW solar + 140 GW wind by 2030** (Panchamrit pledge).
- **Wind potential** in India is ~302 GW at 80 m hub; **Tamil Nadu** remains the leading wind-state followed by Gujarat and Karnataka.
- **Betz limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926** is the universal upper bound on wind-turbine efficiency.
- **CUF** for solar is **15–22 %**; for wind is **25–35 %**; these are critical for KTU design problems.
- **Three ocean-energy variants:** Tidal (Gulf of Kutch), Wave (Kerala coast), OTEC (Lakshadweep).
- **Small hydro** is defined as **≤ 25 MW** and is the most successful decentralised RE in the Indian Himalayas.
- **Biomass** in India is dominated by **agricultural residue (rice/wheat straw)** in the Indo-Gangetic plain.
- **Geothermal** is still in pilot stage; Puga Valley and Manikaran are the most promising sites.
- **Green Hydrogen Mission (₹19,744 crore)** targets **5 MMT by 2030**.
- **Net-Zero target year for India = 2070** (announced at COP-26, Glasgow).
- **Formulas to memorise** (in order of KTU frequency): Wind Power, Betz Limit, Tidal Power, Solar Daily Energy, CUF, LCOE.
- **Common exam errors:** mixing up efficiencies, forgetting to convert diameter→radius, missing the "annualisation" step (multiply by 365), not stating units after every step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked "Why India needs NCES?", always structure the answer in three pillars — (i) Geographic endowment, (ii) Policy/legislation, (iii) International commitments. Examiners reward structured, multi-pillar answers with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Energy: Solar Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Energy: Solar Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ☀️ Solar Energy: Solar Radiation

## 1.1 Definition & Fundamental Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Radiation** is the electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun (chiefly in the wavelength band **0.2 µm to 3.0 µm**) that reaches the Earth after passing through the atmosphere. It is the primary driver of all renewable energy systems — photovoltaic, solar thermal, wind, biomass, and ocean thermal.

The Sun is essentially a **gaseous sphere of hot plasma** undergoing continuous **thermonuclear fusion** (hydrogen → helium), at a core temperature of approximately **15 million K** and a surface (photosphere) temperature of about **5778 K**. By **Stefan–Boltzmann's law**, a black body at this temperature radiates enormous power per unit area.

**Spectral Breakdown of Solar Radiation (at the Earth's surface):**

| Spectral Region | Wavelength Band | Approx. % of Total Energy |
|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Ultraviolet (UV) | < 0.38 µm | ~ 3 % |
| Visible Light    | 0.38 – 0.78 µm | ~ 47 % |
| Infrared (IR)    | > 0.78 µm       | ~ 50 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Solar Constant (Gsc)** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received on a unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the **mean distance of the Earth from the Sun** (1 Astronomical Unit), outside the atmosphere. **Gsc = 1367 W/m²** (standard value adopted by WMO and ASHRAE; some older texts use 1353 W/m²).

### 🌞 Intuitive Analogy: "The Cosmic Light Bulb"

Imagine the Sun as a giant, eternal light bulb placed 149.6 million km (1 AU) away. Even at this distance, every square metre of the Earth's outer atmosphere intercepts enough of that light to power a small electric heater (1367 W). By the time the light reaches the ground, it is **dimmed, scattered, and filtered** by the atmosphere — analogous to sunlight passing through a dusty/foggy window pane. What engineers need to know is: **how much usable solar energy actually strikes a collector on a particular day, at a particular location, at a particular tilt?**

---

### 1.2 Classification of Solar Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test the distinction between the following three radiation components. Memorize the definitions.

1. **Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI / I_bn):** Solar radiation received from the Sun's disk **without change of direction**, on a surface held **perpendicular** to the Sun's rays. Measured with a **Pyrheliometer**.

2. **Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DHI / I_d):** Solar radiation received from the **entire sky dome** (except the solar disk) after scattering by air molecules, dust, water vapour, and aerosols. Measured with a **Pyranometer** (with shadow ring/ball).

3. **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI / I_g):** The **total** solar radiation on a horizontal surface = Direct + Diffuse.
$$I_g = I_{bn} \cos\theta_z + I_d$$
where $\theta_z$ is the **zenith angle**.

4. **Extraterrestrial Radiation (I_o or H_o):** Solar radiation received on a horizontal surface at the **top of the atmosphere** (no atmospheric attenuation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Solar Geometry — The Mathematical Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar geometry** is the *heart* of every solar energy design calculation. The location of the Sun in the sky at any instant depends on three coordinates: the **observer's latitude (φ)**, the **Sun's declination (δ)**, and the **hour angle (ω)**. KTU problems almost always combine these to find the angle of incidence on a tilted surface.

## 2.1 Key Solar Angles — Conceptual Explanation

| # | Angle | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|-------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Latitude** | $\phi$ | Angular position North (+) or South (−) of the Equator (−90° ≤ φ ≤ +90°). |
| 2 | **Declination** | $\delta$ | Angular position of the Sun North (+) or South (−) of the Equator (due to Earth's axial tilt of 23.45°). |
| 3 | **Hour Angle** | $\omega$ | Angular displacement of the Sun **East (+) or West (−** of the local meridian, due to Earth's rotation. **1 hour = 15°**. |
| 4 | **Zenith Angle** | $\theta_z$ | Angle between the **vertical** (zenith) and the Sun's rays. |
| 5 | **Altitude (Solar Elevation) Angle** | $\alpha$ or $\beta_s$ | Angle between the **horizontal plane** and the Sun's rays. $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$. |
| 6 | **Azimuth Angle** | $\gamma_s$ | Horizontal angle measured from **South (Northern Hemisphere)** or **North (Southern Hemisphere)** to the projection of Sun's rays on the horizontal plane. |
| 7 | **Inclination / Tilt Angle** | $\beta$ | Angle between the **plane of the collector** and the horizontal. |
| 8 | **Surface Azimuth** | $\gamma$ | Horizontal angle of the **collector normal** measured from South (N. Hemisphere). |
| 9 | **Angle of Incidence** | $\theta$ | Angle between the Sun's rays and the **normal** to the collector surface. |
| 10 | **Wall Azimuth** | $\gamma_w$ | Azimuth of a vertical surface (wall). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (Northern Hemisphere, KTU standard):**
> - Declination: $\delta > 0$ in summer, $\delta < 0$ in winter.
> - Hour angle: $\omega < 0$ before solar noon, $\omega > 0$ after solar noon, $\omega = 0$ at solar noon.
> - Azimuth: $\gamma_s > 0$ towards West, $\gamma_s < 0$ towards East (when measured from South).

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** These are the **10 Golden Formulas** that appear in 90% of KTU numerical questions from this module. Memorize them in SI units; **declination in degrees, hour angle in degrees, lengths in metres, radiation in W/m² or MJ/m²-day**.

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Cooper's Equation (Declination) | $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$ | degrees | Day-of-year $n$ (1 Jan = 1). |
| 2 | Day Angle (radians) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi (n-1)}{365}$ | rad | Used in trigonometric sun-position algorithms. |
| 3 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$ | degrees | $t_s$ = solar time in hours. |
| 4 | Sunset / Sunrise Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan\phi \tan\delta\right]$ | degrees | When $\alpha = 0$ (Sun on horizon). |
| 5 | Day Length (N) | $N = \dfrac{2}{15}\omega_s$ | hours | Total sunshine hours per day. |
| 6 | Air Mass | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ (for $\theta_z \le 90°$) | unitless | Kasten & Young: $AM = 1/[\cos\theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995°-\theta_z)^{-1.6364}]$ |
| 7 | Altitude Angle | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | degrees | Spherical-trigonometric fundamental equation. |
| 8 | Cosine of Incidence on Tilted Surface | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$ | degrees | Universal incidence formula (Hottel–Whillier). |
| 9 | Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface | $I_{b,\beta} = I_{bn}\cos\theta$ | W/m² | Only when $\cos\theta > 0$. |
| 10 | Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation (H_o) | $H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600 \, G_{sc}}{\pi}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{2\pi\,\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | J/m²/day | For daily-averaged analysis. |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where These Equations Are Used

- **Architectural Design:** Sizing south-facing windows, designing sun-shading devices, and daylighting studies for energy-efficient buildings.
- **Photovoltaic Systems:** Determining optimum **tilt angle** ($\beta_{opt} \approx \phi - 15°$ for winter, $\beta_{opt} \approx \phi$ for year-round), orientation, and inverter sizing.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Calculating **cosine losses**, heat gain, and storage capacity.
- **Agriculture & Solar Drying:** Predicting solar insolation for crop growth and food preservation.
- **Satellite & Spacecraft Power:** Designing solar arrays for low-Earth orbit satellites based on orbital geometry.

---

## 2.4 Solar Time vs. Local Clock Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar time** is the time measured by a sundial. **Local Standard Time** is the time shown by a clock. They differ because of two effects: (1) the **Equation of Time** (EoT) due to Earth's elliptical orbit, and (2) the **longitude correction** within a time zone.

**Equation of Time (in minutes):**
$$B = \dfrac{360°}{365}(n - 81) \quad \text{(in degrees)}$$

$$E_oT = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos B - 1.5 \sin B \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

**Solar Time:**
$$t_s = LST + E_oT + 4(\text{Longitude}_{std} - \text{Longitude}_{local}) \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

where $\text{Longitude}_{std}$ is the reference meridian of the time zone (e.g., 82.5°E for IST = UTC+5:30).

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **4 minutes per degree** arises because the Earth rotates 360° in 24 h, so 1° ≡ 4 min of time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Demonstrations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-grade**, written out in full with every algebraic step. Reproducing this in a KTU answer book will fetch **full marks**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Altitude, Zenith, and Azimuth Angles

Starting from the **spherical law of cosines** applied to the celestial triangle formed by the **zenith**, the **pole star**, and the **Sun**:

**Step 1 — Identify the spherical triangle sides.**
The angle at the zenith is the azimuth $\gamma_s$ (between the meridian and the Sun's vertical circle). The side opposite the Sun is the co-altitude (i.e., $90° - \alpha$). The pole is at co-latitude $(90° - \phi)$ from the zenith, and the Sun is at co-declination $(90° - \delta)$ from the pole.

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine rule for sides.**
$$\cos(90° - \alpha) = \cos(90° - \phi)\cos(90° - \delta) + \sin(90° - \phi)\sin(90° - \delta)\cos\omega$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using $\cos(90°-x) = \sin x$.**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

This is the **fundamental altitude-angle equation** (also obtainable from the vector dot product of the Sun's unit vector with the horizontal).

**Step 4 — Zenith angle.**
$$\theta_z = 90° - \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Step 5 — Azimuth angle** (using the sine rule of spherical trigonometry):
$$\sin\gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

$$\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle

At **sunrise/sunset**, the altitude $\alpha = 0°$, so $\sin\alpha = 0$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $\alpha = 0$ into the altitude equation.**
$$0 = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\cos\omega_s$.**
$$\cos\omega_s = -\dfrac{\sin\phi\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta} = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$

**Step 3 — Take inverse cosine.**
$$\boxed{\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)}$$

**Step 4 — Day length (in hours).** Since the Sun travels $15°/\text{h}$, the total hours from sunrise to sunset is:
$$N = \dfrac{2 \times \omega_s}{15°/\text{h}} = \dfrac{2\,\omega_s}{15}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation (H_o)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous beam intensity on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere.**
$$I_o(t) = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the daylight period from $-\omega_s$ to $+\omega_s$** (in radians, convert degrees → radians):
$$H_o = \int_{-t_s}^{t_s} I_o(t)\,dt$$

Since $\omega = 15°/\text{h} \times t$, $d\omega = 15°/\text{h} \times dt \Rightarrow dt = \dfrac{24}{2\pi}\,d\omega = \dfrac{12}{\pi}\,d\omega$ (in hours).

**Step 3 — Convert intensity to energy.**
$$H_o = G_{sc}\!\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right) \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\cos\theta_z \dfrac{12 \times 3600}{\pi}\,d\omega$$

Converting to **J/m²/day** (multiplying by 3600 s/h) and noting the limits in degrees (multiplying by $\pi/180$), the standard result is:

$$\boxed{\,H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600 \, G_{sc}}{\pi}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \dfrac{2\pi\,\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]\,}$$

The first bracketed term is the **eccentricity correction factor** (Earth-Sun distance), and the second is the **geometric sunset integral**.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style, 14-mark type)

> **Question:** A location in Kerala has latitude $\phi = 10°\text{N}$, longitude $76°\text{E}$, and standard time-zone meridian $82.5°\text{E}$. For **n = 100** (10 April):
> 1. Compute the **declination angle** using Cooper's equation.
> 2. Find the **sunrise hour angle** and **day length**.
> 3. Calculate the **hour angle at 2:00 PM** solar time.
> 4. Find the **altitude and azimuth angles** at that instant.
> 5. Determine the **air mass**.

### Step 1 — Declination
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + 100)\right] = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}\times 384\right]$$

Compute the argument: $\dfrac{360 \times 384}{365} = \dfrac{138240}{365} = 378.74°$

Reduce modulo 360°: $378.74° - 360° = 18.74°$

$$\delta = 23.45° \sin(18.74°) = 23.45° \times 0.3214 = 7.54°$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\delta \approx 7.54°$ (Sun in the Northern hemisphere — consistent with April).

### Step 2 — Sunrise hour angle and day length
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan(10°)\tan(7.54°)\right] = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-(0.1763)(0.1322)\right] = \cos^{-1}(-0.02331)$$

$$\omega_s = 91.34°$$

> ✅ **Sunrise hour angle** = $91.34°$ (just slightly more than 90° since location is in tropics with positive declination).

$$N = \dfrac{2 \times 91.34}{15} = 12.18 \text{ hours}$$

> ✅ **Day length** = **12 h 11 min**.

### Step 3 — Hour angle at 2:00 PM solar time
$$\omega = 15°(14 - 12) = 30°$$

### Step 4 — Altitude and Azimuth
$$\sin\alpha = \sin(10°)\sin(7.54°) + \cos(10°)\cos(7.54°)\cos(30°)$$

$$\sin\alpha = (0.1736)(0.1312) + (0.9848)(0.9913)(0.8660)$$

$$\sin\alpha = 0.02277 + 0.8453 = 0.8681$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8681) = 60.34°$$

> ✅ **Altitude** $\alpha = 60.34°$.

**Zenith:** $\theta_z = 90° - 60.34° = 29.66°$

**Azimuth:**
$$\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi} = \dfrac{(0.8681)(0.1736) - 0.1312}{(0.4964)(0.9848)} = \dfrac{0.1507 - 0.1312}{0.4889} = \dfrac{0.01950}{0.4889}$$

$$\cos\gamma_s = 0.0399 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_s = \pm 87.71°$$

To resolve the sign, use the **sine rule for azimuth**:
$$\sin\gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha} = \dfrac{(0.9913)(0.5)}{0.4964} = 0.9986$$

$$\gamma_s = 87.08° \approx 87°\text{ West of South (afternoon)}$$

### Step 5 — Air Mass
$$AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \dfrac{1}{\cos(29.66°)} = \dfrac{1}{0.8687} = 1.151$$

> ✅ **Air Mass ≈ 1.15** (AM 1.15 — typical clear afternoon value).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Air Mass 1.5 (AM1.5) spectrum** is the international standard used for rating PV modules and solar cells. The above calculation shows that 2 PM at this tropical location is slightly above AM1.5 — quite efficient solar geometry.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation

For the same location (Kerala, $n=100$, $\phi=10°$), compute $H_o$ in **MJ/m²-day**.

**Inputs:**
- $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.367 \text{ kW/m}^2$
- $n = 100$
- $\delta = 7.54°$
- $\omega_s = 91.34°$
- $\phi = 10°$

**Eccentricity factor:**
$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360 \times 100}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(98.63°) = 1 + 0.033(-0.1504) = 0.9950$$

**Convert $\omega_s$ to radians:** $\omega_s = 91.34° \times \pi/180 = 1.5944$ rad

**Compute bracket term:**
$$\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = \cos(10°)\cos(7.54°)\sin(91.34°)$$
$$= (0.9848)(0.9913)(0.9996) = 0.9757$$

$$\dfrac{2\pi\,\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta = \dfrac{2\pi \times 91.34}{360}(0.1736)(0.1312)$$
$$= (1.5941)(0.02277) = 0.0363$$

Sum = $0.9757 + 0.0363 = 1.0120$

**Compute H_o:**
$$H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi} \times 0.9950 \times 1.0120 \;\text{J/m}^2\text{-day}$$

$$H_o = \dfrac{118,108,800}{\pi} \times 1.0069 = 37,602,800 \times 1.0069 = 37,861,500 \;\text{J/m}^2\text{-day}$$

$$H_o \approx 37.86 \text{ MJ/m}^2\text{-day}$$

> ✅ **$H_o = 37.86$ MJ/m²-day** — this is the upper bound of solar energy available; actual terrestrial value will be lower due to atmospheric attenuation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Schematic & Functional Architecture Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> Since solar radiation is a *physical/astronomical* concept that does not lend itself to a single neat Mermaid diagram, the following two block diagrams illustrate (a) the **cosine-of-incidence geometry** as a labelled functional flow, and (b) the **solar radiation measurement instrumentation chain**.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Solar Geometry — Angle Relationship Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Observer Location phi deg N"] --> B["Day of Year n Jan 1 to Dec 31"]
    B --> C["Declination Angle delta Cooper Eqn"]
    A --> D["Local Time Correction EoT plus Longitude"]
    D --> E["Solar Hour Angle omega 15 deg per hr"]
    C --> F["Spherical Triangle Solver"]
    A --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Altitude Angle alpha"]
    F --> H["Zenith Angle theta_z 90 minus alpha"]
    F --> I["Solar Azimuth gamma_s South Reference"]
    G --> J["Air Mass AM 1 by cos theta_z"]
    H --> J
    J --> K["Tilted Surface Incidence Angle theta"]
    I --> K
    A --> K
    C --> K
    E --> K
    K --> L["Beam Radiation on Tilted I_b_beta = I_bn cos theta"]
    L --> M["Collector Output Thermal or PV"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with observer latitude $\phi$ and day-of-year $n$ (inputs). These feed the *geometry solver* along with hour angle $\omega$. The solver produces three intermediate angles ($\alpha$, $\theta_z$, $\gamma_s$). These, combined with the **tilt $\beta$** and **surface azimuth $\gamma$** of the collector, yield the **incidence angle $\theta$** which directly determines beam radiation on the collector via $\cos\theta$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Solar Radiation Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ATMOS["Atmosphere Layer"]
        SC["Solar Constant Gsc 1367 W per m2"]
        SC --> ATT["Attenuation by Air Dust Clouds"]
        ATT --> SURFACE["Earth Surface Radiation Pool"]
    end

    SURFACE --> R1["Beam Normal I_bn Pyrheliometer"]
    SURFACE --> R2["Diffuse Sky I_d Pyranometer with Shadow Ring"]
    SURFACE --> R3["Global Horizontal I_g Pyranometer"]

    R1 --> SUMM["Summer I_g = I_bn cos theta_z plus I_d"]
    R2 --> SUMM
    R3 --> VAL["Validation I_g approx equal to I_bn cos theta_z plus I_d"]

    SUMM --> DATA["Data Logger plus Integrator"]
    VAL --> DATA
    DATA --> OUT1["Hourly Average W per m2"]
    DATA --> OUT2["Daily Total MJ per m2 day"]
    DATA --> OUT3["Monthly Mean kWh per m2 day"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** The relationship $I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d$ is a useful **self-consistency check** for measured data. A well-calibrated station will have the global pyranometer reading match the sum of the pyrheliometer (× cos θz) and shaded pyranometer (diffuse) within ±5%.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Extraterrestrial to Terrestrial Radiation Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Extraterrestrial H_o at Top of Atmosphere"] --> T2["Atmospheric Transmittance tau approx 0.75 Tropical India"]
    T2 --> T3["Direct Beam H_b on Horizontal"]
    T1 --> T4["Scattering by Aerosols and Molecules"]
    T4 --> T5["Diffuse H_d"]
    T3 --> T6["Ground Reflection Albedo approx 0.2"]
    T6 --> T7["Reflected H_r onto Tilted Surface"]
    T5 --> T8["Total Terrestrial H_g on Tilted"]
    T3 --> T8
    T7 --> T8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🟢 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]**
**Define the term "Solar Constant". What is its accepted numerical value?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$)** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface held **perpendicular to the Sun's rays**, at the Earth's **mean distance from the Sun**, **outside the atmosphere**.
$$\boxed{G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2}$$
It represents the **solar flux density at the top of the atmosphere (TOA)** under AM 0 conditions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between Beam, Diffuse, and Global solar radiation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Origin | Direction | Measuring Instrument |
|------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Beam (DNI, $I_{bn}$)** | Sun's direct disk | Collimated from Sun's position | Pyrheliometer |
| **Diffuse (DHI, $I_d$)** | Sky scattering | All directions from sky dome | Pyranometer (shaded) |
| **Global (GHI, $I_g$)** | Sum of beam + diffuse | All directions onto horizontal surface | Pyranometer (unshaded) |

**Relation:** $\;I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d$

---

## 5.2 🟡 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (with Internal Choice)

> *Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each. KTU typically allows the student to pick **one full question** of 14 marks, so the two alternatives below simulate this.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the various **solar angles** used in solar energy engineering. Derive the expressions for **declination angle** and **sunrise hour angle**. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A location in Trivandrum has latitude $\phi = 8.5°\text{N}$. Calculate the **declination angle, day length, and altitude angle at 11:00 AM solar time** on **22 February** ($n = 53$). Take hour angle $\omega = -15°$. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to (a)

**Solar angles** (refer to the schematic in Section 4.1): declination $\delta$, hour angle $\omega$, altitude $\alpha$, zenith $\theta_z$, azimuth $\gamma_s$, inclination $\beta$, surface azimuth $\gamma$, and angle of incidence $\theta$.

**Derivation of declination (Cooper's equation):**
Starting from the fact that the Earth's axis is tilted at $23.45°$ and the day-of-year $n$ governs Earth's position in orbit, the empirical relation is:
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$
(For derivation, Cooper fitted the astronomical declination curve with a single sinusoid; maximum on June 21 ≈ $n = 172$, minimum on Dec 21 ≈ $n = 355$.)

**Derivation of sunrise hour angle:** (see Section 3.2)
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan\phi \tan\delta\right]$$

#### ✅ Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Declination ($n = 53$):**
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + 53)\right] = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360 \times 337}{365}\right]$$

$$= 23.45° \sin(332.38°) = 23.45° \sin(-27.62°) = 23.45° \times (-0.4636)$$

$$\boxed{\delta = -10.87°}$$

(Negative → Sun is in the Southern Hemisphere — as expected in late February.)

**Step 2 — Day length:**
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan(8.5°)\tan(-10.87°)\right] = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-(0.1495)(-0.1921)\right]$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(0.02871) = 88.36°$$

$$N = \dfrac{2 \times 88.36}{15} = 11.78 \text{ hours} \approx 11 \text{ h } 47 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Altitude at $\omega = -15°$ (11:00 AM):**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin(8.5°)\sin(-10.87°) + \cos(8.5°)\cos(-10.87°)\cos(-15°)$$

$$= (0.1478)(-0.1885) + (0.9890)(0.9821)(0.9659)$$

$$= -0.02786 + 0.9383 = 0.9104$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 65.55°}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Pyranometer** and a **Pyrheliometer** for solar radiation measurement. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** The daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at a location $\phi = 13°\text{N}$ on $n = 172$ (summer solstice) is to be computed. Using $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m², determine $H_o$ in **MJ/m²-day**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to (a)

**Pyranometer:**
- A pyranometer measures **global (beam + diffuse) solar radiation** on a planar surface.
- It uses a **thermoelectric (thermopile) sensor** with a blackened absorbing surface protected by **two concentric hemispherical glass domes** (to filter IR and reduce convection).
- The temperature difference between the hot (sunlit) junctions and the cold (shaded) junctions produces a **Seebeck EMF** proportional to irradiance.
- When used with a **shadow ring or shading disc**, it measures only **diffuse radiation**.

**Pyrheliometer:**
- A pyrheliometer measures **direct normal irradiance (DNI)** from the Sun's disk only.
- It is mounted on a **solar tracker** (equatorial mount driven by clock mechanism).
- The incoming beam enters through a **long collimator tube** (field of view ~ 5°) that excludes sky and ground radiation.
- The beam falls on a **blackened thermopile** at the bottom; the EMF is calibrated against $G_{sc}$ in W/m².
- Reference instrument: **Angström compensation pyrheliometer** (primary standard); **Eppley NIP** (working standard).

#### ✅ Model Solution to (b)

**Inputs:**
- $\phi = 13°\text{N}$, $n = 172$, $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²
- $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45° \sin(449.42°) = 23.45° \sin(89.42°) \approx 23.44° \approx 23.45°$ (Summer solstice, max declination ✓)

**Eccentricity factor:**
$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360 \times 172}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(169.64°) = 1 + 0.033(-0.9841) = 0.9675$$

**Sunrise hour angle:**
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-\tan(13°)\tan(23.45°)\right] = \cos^{-1}\!\left[-(0.2309)(0.4335)\right] = \cos^{-1}(-0.1001) = 95.75°$$

**Convert $\omega_s$ to radians:** $\omega_s = 95.75° \times \pi/180 = 1.6713$ rad

**Bracket term:**
$$\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = \cos(13°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(95.75°)$$
$$= (0.9744)(0.9178)(0.9950) = 0.8897$$

$$\dfrac{2\pi\,\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta = \dfrac{2\pi \times 95.75}{360}(0.2250)(0.3979) = (1.6713)(0.0895) = 0.1496$$

Sum = $0.8897 + 0.1496 = 1.0393$

**Compute $H_o$:**
$$H_o = \dfrac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi} \times 0.9675 \times 1.0393$$

$$= \dfrac{118{,}108{,}800}{\pi} \times 1.0056 = 37{,}602{,}800 \times 1.0056$$

$$= 37{,}813{,}600 \;\text{J/m}^2\text{-day} \approx 37.81 \;\text{MJ/m}^2\text{-day}$$

> ✅ **$H_o \approx 37.81$ MJ/m²-day** — slightly higher than the April value computed in Section 3.5 because the Sun is closer to overhead at this lower latitude during summer solstice (longer day + higher declination).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Solar Radiation problems — and how to avoid them:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in $\omega$:** Hour angle must be in **degrees** for the trig functions to give correct answers. A common mistake is using $\omega$ in **radians** without conversion.
> 2. **Declination sign error:** KTU expects you to use **Cooper's equation** (n = 1 for Jan 1) — the result is **positive** in Northern summer (Apr–Sep) and **negative** in winter. Some students use the wrong sign of the argument.
> 3. **Day length integer value:** $N$ must be between **0 and 24 h**. If your formula gives a negative or value > 24, your $\omega_s$ has the wrong sign.
> 4. **Equation of Time direction:** $E_oT$ is **added** to local time in some conventions and **subtracted** in others. KTU's standard textbook (Sukhatme/Pangulo/A.G. Ter-Gazarian) convention must be used — write it explicitly in the answer.
> 5. **Pyrheliometer vs Pyranometer mix-up:** KTU *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you confuse the two. Remember: **Pyran-o-meter = sky (Global)**; **Pyrheli-o-meter = direct sun (DNI)**.
> 6. **Forgetting eccentricity factor:** The term $1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ in $H_o$ is often forgotten, leading to ~3% error.
> 7. **Latitude/Longitude sign convention:** Northern latitudes and Eastern longitudes are **positive** (KTU convention). State this clearly in the answer.
> 8. **Air Mass at sunrise/sunset:** AM → ∞. Don't use the formula $1/\cos\theta_z$ at $\theta_z = 90°$ (gives 0) — use the **Kasten & Young** formula for $\theta_z > 90°$.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solar Energy: Solar Radiation**

- ⭐ **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²** (WMO standard, 1981).
- ⭐ **Spectral range** of solar radiation at Earth's surface: **0.2 – 3.0 µm**; 50% IR, 47% visible, 3% UV.
- ⭐ **Cooper's equation** for declination: $\delta = 23.45°\sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$.
- ⭐ **Hour angle:** $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$; **+ve** in afternoon, **−ve** in forenoon.
- ⭐ **Sunrise hour angle:** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$.
- ⭐ **Day length:** $N = 2\omega_s / 15$ hours.
- ⭐ **Altitude:** $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- ⭐ **Zenith:** $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$.
- ⭐ **Air Mass (AM):** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (valid for $\theta_z \le 90°$).
- ⭐ **Beam on tilted surface:** $I_{b,\beta} = I_{bn}\cos\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle of incidence.
- ⭐ **Global horizontal:** $I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d$.
- ⭐ **Extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$** involves an **eccentricity factor** $(1 + 0.033\cos 360n/365)$ and an **integral over daylight hours**.
- ⭐ **Equation of Time (EoT):** $E_oT = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$ minutes, where $B = 360°(n-81)/365$.
- ⭐ **Solar time correction:** 4 minutes per degree of longitude difference from time-zone standard meridian.
- ⭐ **Pyrheliometer** = direct beam; **Pyranometer** = global (or diffuse with shade ring); both use **thermopile** principle.
- ⭐ For **Kerala** (φ ≈ 8–13° N), the Sun is **nearly overhead** twice a year (zenith passage around Apr 14 and Sep 1) — giving excellent solar geometry.
- ⭐ For **PV module rating**, the standard reference spectrum is **AM 1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C cell temperature** (STC).
- ⭐ KTU **most-asked** sub-topics: (i) declination + day length; (ii) hourly radiation computation on tilted surface; (iii) Pyranometer / Pyrheliometer working; (iv) $H_o$ numerical with full formula.

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When solving a 14-mark question, **always draw the celestial triangle / surface angle diagram**. Even a hand-drawn sketch in the answer book earns you 1–2 "method" marks before you start the calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement and Estimation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement and Estimation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Measurement and Estimation Techniques** in Non-Conventional Energy Systems refer to the standardized instrumentation, methodologies, and mathematical models used to **quantify**, **record**, and **predict** the availability, intensity, and energy potential of renewable energy resources such as **solar radiation**, **wind speed/direction**, **biomass yield**, and **hydrological data**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**  
> Measurement refers to *direct instrumental data acquisition* (using sensors/transducers), while Estimation refers to *predictive mathematical modeling* of energy resources when direct measurement is impractical, costly, or for long-term forecasting and feasibility analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you want to know **how much rain will fall next month**:
- **Measurement** = Holding a rain gauge *today* and reading the actual water collected.
- **Estimation** = Using *past rainfall patterns, cloud cover, and humidity data* with a mathematical formula to *predict* next month's rainfall.

In the same way, to design a solar power plant in Kerala, an engineer must **measure** today's solar intensity using instruments (pyranometer) and **estimate** the annual solar energy potential using empirical models (Angstrom-Prescott equation) based on sunshine hours.

## 1.3 Classification of Measurement & Estimation Techniques

| Resource | Measurement Instrument | Estimation Models |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Solar Radiation | Pyranometer, Pyrheliometer, Sunshine Recorder | Angstrom-Prescott, Hargreaves-Samani, Liu-Jordan |
| Wind | Cup Anemometer, Vane, Sonic Anemometer | Weibull, Rayleigh, Power Law |
| Biomass | Weighing balance, Moisture meter | Energy crop yield models |
| Hydel/Tidal | Flow meters, Tide gauges | Run-off models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:**  
> - Global Solar Radiation is measured in **W/m²** or **kWh/m²/day** (the **Daily Solar Insolation** H̄).  
> - Wind speed standard reference height is **10 meters** (WMO Standard).  
> - Solar Constant (I_sc) = **1367 W/m²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Radiation Measurement

### 2.1.1 Types of Solar Radiation
1. **Direct (Beam) Radiation (I_b):** Travels in straight line from the sun.
2. **Diffuse Radiation (I_d):** Scattered by atmosphere, clouds, aerosols.
3. **Global Radiation (I_g):** Total on a horizontal surface = **I_b + I_d**.
4. **Terrestrial (Extra-terrestrial) Radiation (I_o):** Radiation received at the top of the atmosphere.

### 2.1.2 Measurement Instruments

**A. Pyranometer (Measures Global Solar Radiation):**
- A thermopile-based device using black and white sensor surfaces.
- Output is in **W/m²** (instantaneous) or integrated as **kWh/m²/day**.
- Spectral range: **0.3 µm to 3 µm**.
- Standards: **WMO Class 1 (Secondary Standard)**, ISO 9060.

**B. Pyrheliometer (Measures Direct Beam Radiation):**
- Mounted on a **sun-tracker** with a 5° aperture collimator.
- Only captures direct beam; diffuse is blocked.

**C. Sunshine Recorder (Campbell-Stokes):**
- A glass sphere focuses sunlight onto a calibrated paper card.
- Records **sunshine duration (hours/day)** — used as input to estimation models.

## 2.2 Solar Radiation Estimation Models

### 2.2.1 Angstrom-Prescott Model (Linear Model)
The **first and most widely used** empirical correlation.

$$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_0}} = a + b \left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right)$$

Where:
- $\bar{H}$ = Monthly average daily global radiation on horizontal surface (MJ/m²/day)
- $\bar{H_0}$ = Monthly average daily **extra-terrestrial radiation** (MJ/m²/day)
- $\bar{n}$ = Monthly average daily **actual sunshine hours** (hours)
- $\bar{N}$ = Monthly average daily **maximum possible sunshine hours** (day length, hours)
- $a, b$ = Empirical regression constants (region-specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of a and b:**
> - $a$ = Fraction of radiation reaching Earth's surface on a fully overcast day (diffuse fraction on cloudy day).
> - $a + b$ = Fraction of radiation on a clear-sky day.
> - Typical range: $0.20 \le a \le 0.30$, $0.45 \le b \le 0.55$.

### 2.2.2 Extra-Terrestrial Radiation (H₀) Computation

The monthly mean daily extra-terrestrial radiation is given by:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times I_{sc}}{\pi} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right) \left(\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s\right)$$

Where:
- $I_{sc}$ = **1367 W/m²** (Solar Constant)
- $n$ = Day number of the year (Jan 1 = 1)
- $\omega_s$ = Sunrise hour angle (radians)
- $\phi$ = Latitude of the location (radians)
- $\delta$ = Solar declination (radians)

### 2.2.3 Solar Declination and Hour Angle

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$

$$\bar{N} = \frac{2}{15} \omega_s \quad \text{(in hours)}$$

### 2.2.4 Hargreaves-Samani Model (Temperature-Based)

Used when sunshine data is unavailable:

$$\bar{H} = K_{RS} \cdot \sqrt{T_{max} - T_{min}} \cdot \bar{H_0}$$

Where $K_{RS}$ = adjustment coefficient (≈ 0.16 for interior regions, 0.19 for coastal).

## 2.3 Wind Resource Measurement & Estimation

### 2.3.1 Measurement
- **Cup Anemometer** (most common): 3-cup rotating device, accuracy ±0.5 m/s.
- **Vane / Wind Direction Sensor**: Monitors direction.
- **Data logger**: Records **10-minute averages** at 10 m height (WMO standard).

### 2.3.2 Wind Speed Estimation — Weibull Distribution

The **probability density function (PDF)** of wind speed:

$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right]$$

Where:
- $v$ = Wind speed (m/s)
- $k$ = **Shape parameter** (dimensionless), typically 1.5–3.0
- $c$ = **Scale parameter** (m/s)

**Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**

$$F(v) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right]$$

**Mean Wind Speed (Weibull Mean):**

$$\bar{v} = c \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)$$

Where $\Gamma$ is the Gamma function.

### 2.3.3 Weibull Parameters from Data

Given mean $\bar{v}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_v$:

$$k = \left(\frac{\sigma_v}{\bar{v}}\right)^{-1.086}$$

$$c = \frac{\bar{v}}{\Gamma(1 + 1/k)}$$

### 2.3.4 Power Density of Wind

$$P/v = \frac{1}{2} \rho \bar{v}^3 \quad \text{(per unit area)}$$

$$\text{Mean Power Density} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \int_0^\infty v^3 f(v) \, dv = \frac{1}{2} \rho c^3 \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$

Where $\rho$ = **air density ≈ 1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15°C.

### 2.3.5 Wind Power Law (Height Extrapolation)

$$\frac{v}{v_0} = \left(\frac{h}{h_0}\right)^\alpha$$

Where $\alpha$ = terrain coefficient (1/7 ≈ 0.143 for open terrain).

## 2.4 Biomass Energy Estimation

$$\text{Biomass Energy} = M \times C_v \times \eta_c \times \eta_{conv}$$

Where $M$ = dry mass (kg), $C_v$ = calorific value (~18 MJ/kg for agro-residue), $\eta_c$ = collection efficiency, $\eta_{conv}$ = conversion efficiency.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angstrom-Prescott | $\bar{H}/\bar{H_0} = a + b(\bar{n}/\bar{N})$ | Estimate solar radiation |
| 2 | Extra-terrestrial $\bar{H_0}$ | $\frac{24 \times 3600 \times I_{sc}}{\pi}(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365})(\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta+\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s)$ | Reference solar input |
| 3 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | Sun's angular position |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Sunrise/sunset |
| 5 | Max Sunshine Hours | $\bar{N} = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s$ | Day length |
| 6 | Hargreaves-Samani | $\bar{H} = K_{RS}\sqrt{T_{max}-T_{min}}\;\bar{H_0}$ | Temp-based estimation |
| 7 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = \frac{k}{c}(v/c)^{k-1}\exp[-(v/c)^k]$ | Wind speed distribution |
| 8 | Weibull Mean Speed | $\bar{v} = c\,\Gamma(1+1/k)$ | Average wind speed |
| 9 | Shape parameter | $k = (\sigma_v/\bar{v})^{-1.086}$ | From measured data |
| 10 | Wind Power Law | $v/v_0 = (h/h_0)^\alpha$ | Height correction |
| 11 | Wind Power Density | $\frac{1}{2}\rho\,c^3\,\Gamma(1+3/k)$ | Energy per area |
| 12 | Clear Sky Index | $K_T = \bar{H}/\bar{H_0}$ | Atmospheric transmission |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Solar PV Plant Design:** Estimation of annual energy yield (kWh).
- **Wind Farm Siting:** Power density determines turbine selection.
- **Hybrid Systems:** Match renewable supply profiles to load demand.
- **Government Policy:** India's MNRE uses these metrics for renewable energy potential mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Extra-Terrestrial Daily Radiation ($\bar{H_0}$)

### Starting Point — Instantaneous Extra-Terrestrial Irradiance

The solar irradiance on a surface perpendicular to the beam at the top of the atmosphere, accounting for Earth's elliptical orbit:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc}\left[1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right]$$

The irradiance on a horizontal surface:

$$I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z$$

where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle.

**Step 1:** Express $\cos\theta_z$ in terms of hour angle $\omega$, declination $\delta$, latitude $\phi$:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Step 2:** Integrate $I_o$ over the day from sunrise ($\omega_s$) to sunset ($-\omega_s$), where the total sunshine duration in radians is $2\omega_s$:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} I_{on}(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega)\,d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Because $I_{on}$ is approximately constant over one day, take it outside the integral:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi}\cdot I_{on}\int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s}(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega)\,d\omega$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral. Note that $\sin\phi\sin\delta$ is constant and $\cos\omega$ is an even function:

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s}(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega)\,d\omega = 2\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + 2\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back and simplify the coefficient $\frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} = \frac{86400}{2\pi}$:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\,I_{on}\,(\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s)$$

**Step 6 (Final):** Substitute $I_{on}$ to obtain the full expression (in **MJ/m²/day** when $I_{sc}$ is in **MW/m²**):

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times I_{sc}}{\pi}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\!\left(\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right)\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Weibull Mean Wind Speed

We want $\bar{v} = \int_0^\infty v\,f(v)\,dv$.

**Step 1:** Start with PDF:

$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1}e^{-(v/c)^k}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = (v/c)^k$, which gives $v = c\,x^{1/k}$ and $dv = \frac{c}{k}x^{(1/k)-1}dx$:

$$\bar{v} = \int_0^\infty c\,x^{1/k}\cdot e^{-x}\cdot dx = c\int_0^\infty x^{(1+1/k)-1}e^{-x}\,dx$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the integral as the Gamma function:

$$\Gamma(s) = \int_0^\infty x^{s-1}e^{-x}\,dx$$

With $s = 1 + 1/k$:

$$\bar{v} = c\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem Walkthrough

### Worked Example: Solar Radiation Estimation (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Estimate the monthly average daily global radiation for Trivandrum ($\phi = 8.5^\circ$N) in the month of March. Given:
- Average sunshine hours/day $\bar{n} = 8.5$ h
- Angstrom constants: $a = 0.27$, $b = 0.52$
- Take representative day: $n = 80$ (mid-March)

**Step 1: Solar Declination**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ\sin(35.84^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times 0.586 = 13.74^\circ$$

**Step 2: Sunrise Hour Angle** ($\phi = 8.5^\circ$)

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 8.5^\circ \tan 13.74^\circ) = \cos^{-1}(-0.0362)$$

$$\omega_s = 92.07^\circ = 1.6071 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Sunshine Hours**

$$\bar{N} = \frac{2}{15}\times 92.07 = 12.28 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4: Extra-Terrestrial $\bar{H_0}$**

Eccentricity correction factor:

$$1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360\times 80}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(78.9^\circ) = 1.00645$$

Bracket term:

$$\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s$$

$$= (1.6071)(\sin 8.5^\circ)(\sin 13.74^\circ) + (\cos 8.5^\circ)(\cos 13.74^\circ)(\sin 92.07^\circ)$$

$$= (1.6071)(0.1478)(0.2375) + (0.9890)(0.9714)(0.9994)$$

$$= 0.0564 + 0.9600 = 1.0164 \text{ rad}$$

Now:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi}\times 1.00645 \times 1.0164$$

$$\bar{H_0} = 37.59 \times 1.00645 \times 1.0164 = 38.45 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 5: Apply Angstrom-Prescott**

$$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_0}} = 0.27 + 0.52\left(\frac{8.5}{12.28}\right) = 0.27 + 0.52 \times 0.6922 = 0.6300$$

$$\bar{H} = 0.6300 \times 38.45 = 24.22 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{\;\bar{H} \approx 24.22 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} = 6.73 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value closely matches the **MNRE Solar Atlas** estimate for Trivandrum (~5.5–6.5 kWh/m²/day annually), confirming the validity of the model for Kerala's tropical climate.

---

### Worked Example: Weibull Wind Analysis

**Problem:** At a coastal site, mean wind speed $\bar{v} = 7.2$ m/s and $\sigma_v = 3.0$ m/s. Estimate the shape parameter, scale parameter, and mean power density.

**Step 1: Shape parameter**

$$k = \left(\frac{3.0}{7.2}\right)^{-1.086} = (0.4167)^{-1.086}$$

Taking logarithm: $\ln k = -1.086 \times \ln(0.4167) = -1.086 \times (-0.8755) = 0.9508$

$$k = e^{0.9508} = 2.59$$

**Step 2: Scale parameter**

Need $\Gamma(1+1/k) = \Gamma(1+0.386) = \Gamma(1.386)$.

From standard gamma tables: $\Gamma(1.386) \approx 0.8895$

$$c = \frac{7.2}{0.8895} = 8.094 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Power density**

$$\Gamma(1+3/k) = \Gamma(1+1.158) = \Gamma(2.158) \approx 1.158 \times \Gamma(1.158) \approx 1.158 \times 0.937 = 1.085$$

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(8.094)^3(1.085) = 0.6125 \times 530.2 \times 1.085$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Mean Power Density} \approx 352.4 \text{ W/m}^2\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Conversion / Logic Summary Table

| Input Data | Intermediate Step | Output | Engineering Use |
|------------|-------------------|--------|-----------------|
| Latitude $\phi$ + Day $n$ | Compute $\delta, \omega_s, \bar{N}$ | Geometry inputs | Astronomical position |
| Sunshine hours $\bar{n}$ | Ratio $\bar{n}/\bar{N}$ | Clearness fraction | Regression input |
| Angstrom $a, b$ | Multiply | Clearness index $K_T$ | Daily global radiation |
| Wind speed time series | Compute $\bar{v}, \sigma_v$ | Weibull $k, c$ | Site characterization |
| Weibull $k, c$ | Integrate $v^3 f(v)$ | Power density W/m² | Turbine sizing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solar Radiation Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sunA[Sun Source] --> beamA[Direct Beam Radiation Ib]
    sunA --> diffA[Diffuse Radiation Id]
    beamA --> pyrhA[Pyrheliometer + Sun Tracker]
    diffA --> pyrhA
    beamA --> pyraA[Pyranometer Shaded]
    diffA --> pyraD[Pyranometer Unshaded]
    pyraA --> dataA[Data Logger]
    pyrhA --> dataA
    pyraD --> dataA
    ssrA[Sunshine Recorder Campbell Stokes] --> dataA
    dataA --> procA[Processing Unit]
    procA --> ibOut[Beam Radiation W per m2]
    procA --> igOut[Global Radiation W per m2]
    procA --> idOut[Diffuse Radiation W per m2]
    procA --> sdOut[Sunshine Hours]
```

## 4.2 Wind Resource Assessment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Site Selection] --> measA[Install Anemometer at 10m and 30m]
    measA --> logA[Data Logger 10 Min Averages]
    logA --> statA[Compute Mean and Std Deviation]
    statA --> distA[Fit Weibull Distribution k and c]
    distA --> pdA[Calculate Power Density]
    pdA --> ttypeA[Select Turbine Class]
    pdA --> arrA[Determine Array Layout]
    arrA --> eestA[Annual Energy Estimation]
    eestA --> decA[Investment Decision]
```

## 4.3 Solar Estimation Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    solA[Need Solar Resource Data] --> q1A[Is sunshine hour data available]
    q1A -- Yes --> angA[Use Angstrom Prescott Model]
    q1A -- No --> q2A[Only temperature data available]
    q2A -- Yes --> harA[Use Hargreaves Samani Model]
    q2A -- No --> q3A[Use satellite derived data]
    q3A -- Yes --> satA[Use NASA SSE or MNRE Atlas]
    angA --> houtA[Daily Global Radiation H]
    harA --> houtA
    satA --> houtA
    houtA --> tiltA[Apply Tilt Angle Adjustment]
    tiltA --> planeA[Radiation on Tilted Plane]
    planeA --> yieldA[PV Yield Calculation]
```

## 4.4 Instrumentation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    transA[Transducer Sensor] --> ampA[Signal Amplifier]
    ampA --> adcA[ADC Conversion]
    adcA --> logA[Microcontroller Data Logger]
    logA --> memA[Memory Storage]
    memA --> txA[Communication GSM or Wi-Fi]
    txA --> cloudA[Cloud Database]
    cloudA --> anaA[Analysis Software]
    anaA --> repA[Reports and Dashboards]
```
