# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms:**
**(a)** Pyranometer  
**(b)** Pyrheliometer

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Pyranometer:** An electro-thermal instrument based on a thermopile that measures **global (total) solar radiation** received from the entire hemisphere on a horizontal surface. It consists of a black absorbing surface in contact with a thermopile, covered by a glass dome to block longwave radiation. Output is calibrated in **W/m²** (instantaneous) or integrated as **kWh/m²/day**. Compliant with **ISO 9060** and **WMO Class 1** standards.

**(b) Pyrheliometer:** An instrument that measures **direct beam solar radiation** from the sun only, by mounting on a sun-tracking platform with a 5° aperture collimator tube. It blocks diffuse and reflected radiation, isolating only the line-of-sight solar beam. Used to determine the **direct normal irradiance (DNI)** critical for concentrated solar plants.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the Angstrom-Prescott equation for estimating solar radiation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Angstrom-Prescott equation is an empirical linear regression model that estimates monthly mean daily global solar radiation ($\bar{H}$) on a horizontal surface using sunshine duration data:

$$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_0}} = a + b\left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right)$$

Where $\bar{H_0}$ is the monthly average daily extra-terrestrial radiation, $\bar{n}$ is actual sunshine hours, $\bar{N}$ is maximum possible sunshine hours, and $a$, $b$ are location-specific empirical constants. The constant $a$ represents the diffuse fraction on overcast days and $(a+b)$ represents the clear-sky transmission. This equation is widely used because it requires only easily available sunshine duration data.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions

### 🎯 Choice Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the monthly mean daily extra-terrestrial radiation $\bar{H_0}$ on a horizontal surface. State clearly all assumptions and define each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A location at latitude $\phi = 13^\circ$N has measured mean wind speed $\bar{v} = 6.5$ m/s and standard deviation $\sigma_v = 2.8$ m/s. Calculate the Weibull parameters ($k$, $c$) and the mean wind power density per unit area. Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Choice A:

### Part (a): Derivation of $\bar{H_0}$

**Assumptions:**
1. Earth-Sun distance variation causes a $\pm 3.3\%$ change in irradiance, modeled by the eccentricity factor.
2. The day-of-year average is treated as instantaneous (over the day, $I_{on}$ is constant).
3. The sun rises and sets symmetrically about solar noon.

**Derivation:** See the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Final result:

$$\bar{H_0} = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times I_{sc}}{\pi}\left[1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right]\left[\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right]$$

with

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$$

**Term definitions:** $I_{sc}=1367$ W/m², $n$ = day-of-year, $\phi$ = latitude, $\delta$ = solar declination, $\omega_s$ = sunrise hour angle.

---

### Part (b): Weibull Wind Power Density Calculation

**Given:** $\bar{v} = 6.5$ m/s, $\sigma_v = 2.8$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\phi = 13^\circ$N (latitude not actually used in Weibull calculation; included as extra data).

**Step 1: Shape parameter $k$**

$$k = \left(\frac{\sigma_v}{\bar{v}}\right)^{-1.086} = \left(\frac{2.8}{6.5}\right)^{-1.086} = (0.4308)^{-1.086}$$

Compute $\ln(0.4308) = -0.8416$. Thus $\ln k = -1.086 \times (-0.8416) = 0.9140$.

$$\boxed{k = e^{0.9140} = 2.494}$$

**Step 2: Scale parameter $c$**

Need $\Gamma(1 + 1/k) = \Gamma(1 + 1/2.494) = \Gamma(1.401)$.

From gamma tables / interpolation: $\Gamma(1.40) = 0.8873$, $\Gamma(1.41) = 0.8889$.

Interpolating: $\Gamma(1.401) \approx 0.8874$

$$c = \frac{\bar{v}}{\Gamma(1+1/k)} = \frac{6.5}{0.8874}$$

$$\boxed{c = 7.324 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Mean power density**

Need $\Gamma(1 + 3/k) = \Gamma(1 + 3/2.494) = \Gamma(2.203)$.

For $\Gamma(2.203) = 1.203 \times \Gamma(1.203)$. Now $\Gamma(1.203) \approx 0.918$ (interpolation between $\Gamma(1.20) = 0.9182$).

So $\Gamma(2.203) \approx 1.203 \times 0.918 = 1.104$.

$$\text{Mean Power Density} = \frac{1}{2}\rho\,c^3\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{3}{k}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(7.324)^3(1.104)$$

$$= (0.6125)(392.74)(1.104)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Mean Power Density} \approx 265.6 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often confuse $\Gamma(1+1/k)$ with $\Gamma(1/k)$. Always remember: **the argument is $1 + \frac{1}{k}$**, NOT $\frac{1}{k}$.
> - Power density must be computed using **$v^3 f(v)$** integration, NOT $\bar{v}^3$ (the latter would give the wrong answer because wind speed distribution is non-Gaussian).
> - Air density **at altitude** must be corrected using $\rho = \rho_0 \exp(-h/8500)$.

---

### 🎯 Choice Question B — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Campbell-Stokes sunshine recorder. With a neat diagram, describe its main components. Compare its accuracy with modern electronic sunshine sensors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at $20^\circ$N latitude, on day number $n = 105$ (mid-April), compute:
  (i) Solar declination  
  (ii) Sunrise hour angle and maximum possible sunshine hours  
  (iii) Extra-terrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface

Given: $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Choice B:

### Part (a): Campbell-Stokes Sunshine Recorder

**Working Principle:**  
A solid glass sphere of ~10 cm diameter acts as a **converging lens**, focusing parallel rays of sunlight onto a specially calibrated paper card held in a metal bowl. As the sun's position changes through the day, the focused spot traces a **burn mark** along the card. The length of the burn mark is directly proportional to the duration of bright sunshine (radiation intensity ≥ 210 W/m² is considered "sunshine").

**Main Components:**
1. **Glass sphere** — focal length ~5 cm
2. **Metal bowl** — supports sphere, holds card, equatorially mounted
3. **Calibrated card** — divided into hourly segments
4. **Mounting frame** — aligned to local latitude and true north

**Comparison with electronic sensors:**
| Feature | Campbell-Stokes | Electronic (e.g., pyrheliometer threshold) |
|---------|-----------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Accuracy | ±0.1 h, manual reading | ±0.01 h, automated |
| Response | Intense sunlight only (threshold) | Programmable threshold |
| Maintenance | Daily card replacement | Quarterly calibration |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |

---

### Part (b): Solar Geometry Calculation

**Given:** $\phi = 20^\circ$N, $n = 105$, $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².

**Step (i): Solar Declination**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin\!\left(\frac{360(284+105)}{365}\right) = 23.45^\circ\sin(383.34^\circ)$$

Note: $383.34^\circ \equiv 23.34^\circ$ (subtract 360°)

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ\sin(23.34^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \times 0.3961$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 9.29^\circ}$$

**Step (ii): Hour Angle and $\bar{N}$**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 20^\circ \tan 9.29^\circ) = \cos^{-1}(-0.36397 \times 0.16366)$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(-0.0596) = 93.42^\circ = 1.6305 \text{ rad}$$

$$\bar{N} = \frac{2}{15}\times 93.42 = 12.46 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_s = 93.42^\circ, \quad \bar{N} = 12.46 \text{ h}}$$

**Step (iii): Extra-Terrestrial Radiation**

Eccentricity correction:

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360 \times 105}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(103.56^\circ) = 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.2314) = 0.9924$$

Bracket term:

$$\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s$$

$$= (1.6305)(\sin 20^\circ)(\sin 9.29^\circ) + (\cos 20^\circ)(\cos 9.29^\circ)(\sin 93.42^\circ)$$

$$= (1.6305)(0.3420)(0.1614) + (0.9397)(0.9869)(0.9982)$$

$$= 0.0900 + 0.9261 = 1.0161 \text{ rad}$$

Now convert: $\frac{24 \times 3600 \times I_{sc}}{\pi} = \frac{86400 \times 1367 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi} = \frac{118.1088}{\pi} = 37.596$ (in MJ/m²/day units, with $I_{sc}$ as 1367 W/m²).

$$\bar{H_0} = 37.596 \times 0.9924 \times 1.0161 = 38.03 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

Convert to kWh: $1$ kWh $= 3.6$ MJ

$$\boxed{\bar{H_0} \approx 38.03 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} = 10.56 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> - **Unit Conversion:** $I_{sc}$ in W/m² gives $\bar{H_0}$ in **kJ/m²/day**; multiply by $10^{-3}$ for **MJ/m²/day**, and divide by 3.6 for **kWh/m²/day**.
> - **Sign Errors:** $-\tan\phi\tan\delta$ must be **negative** for sunrise/sunset to exist; check sign convention of $\delta$.
> - **Day Number:** Jan 1 = 1, not 0. Feb 28 = 59 (or 60 in leap years).
> - **Hour Angle Convention:** $\omega_s$ in **radians** inside the trig functions (degrees must be converted).
> - **Manual Reading Error:** Campbell-Stokes burn marks are subjective; examiners expect students to acknowledge this limitation.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Solar Constant** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² — always remember this value for KTU problems.
- **Measurement vs. Estimation:** Measurement = real instruments; Estimation = models (Angstrom, Weibull).
- **Angstrom-Prescott:** $\bar{H}/\bar{H_0} = a + b(\bar{n}/\bar{N})$ — linear, requires sunshine data.
- **Hargreaves-Samani:** Temperature-based alternative when sunshine data missing.
- **Extra-terrestrial radiation** depends only on **latitude** and **day-of-year**, NOT weather.
- **Solar declination** varies from $-23.45^\circ$ to $+23.45^\circ$.
- **Weibull distribution** = standard model for wind speed frequency analysis.
- **Shape parameter $k$** (1.5–3.0 typical); higher $k$ = more uniform wind.
- **Scale parameter $c$** (m/s) — close to mean wind speed.
- **Wind power density** uses $v^3$ — mean of $v^3$ ≠ mean$^3$ of $v$ (Be careful!).
- **Wind Power Law** $(h/h_0)^\alpha$ used to extrapolate to hub height.
- **Air density** at sea level = 1.225 kg/m³.
- **Pyranometer** measures **global** radiation; **Pyrheliometer** measures **direct**; **Campbell-Stokes** measures **sunshine duration**.
- **WMO standard** measurement height = **10 m** for wind.
- **KTU frequently asked:** Be ready for derivation of $\bar{H_0}$, Weibull mean speed, and calculation of $\bar{H}$ given Angstrom constants.

### 🎯 Common Unit Conversions
- $1$ kWh = $3.6$ MJ = $3600$ kJ
- $1$ MJ/m²/day = $0.2778$ kWh/m²/day
- $1$ W/m² = $0.0864$ MJ/m²/day (integrated over 24 h)
- $1$ kcal/m²/day = $4.1868$ kJ/m²/day
- $1$ Langley = $1$ cal/cm² = $0.041868$ MJ/m²

### 🧠 Memory Mnemonics
- **A**ngstrom = **A**ctual sunshine (uses $\bar{n}$).
- **H**argreaves = **H**eat (uses temperature max/min).
- **W**eibull = **W**ind (PDF $f(v)$).
- **C**ampbell-**S**tokes = **C**ard-**S**phere (CS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When solving estimation problems, always **draw a flow diagram** of your calculation steps before writing equations. Examiners reward methodical solutions with **step marks** even if the final numerical answer is incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal Systems: Flat Plate Collectors
Concentrating Collectors: Parabolic Trough, Dish, and Solar Tower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concentrating Collectors: Parabolic Trough, Dish, and Solar Tower

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Concentrating Solar Collectors (CSC)?

A **Concentrating Solar Collector (CSC)** is a special class of solar thermal device that uses optical elements (reflectors or refractors) to focus a large area of incoming solar radiation onto a small receiver area, thereby increasing the **flux intensity (intensity)** of the solar energy at the absorber. The fundamental purpose of concentration is to achieve higher working temperatures than what is possible with conventional flat-plate collectors, enabling efficient thermal-to-mechanical or thermal-to-electrical energy conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A concentrating collector is a device that concentrates solar radiation from a large collecting aperture onto a smaller receiver, achieving flux concentration ratios typically ranging from **1×** (no concentration) to **10,000×** (extreme point-focus systems).

### 1.2 The Need for Concentration — Physical Motivation

Flat-plate collectors are limited to temperatures below ~**100 °C** because of inevitable thermal losses (convection and radiation) from the absorber surface. As the absorber temperature rises, the re-radiation loss grows according to the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** ($Q \propto T^4$). To push temperatures higher (200 °C to >1500 °C) for power generation or industrial process heat, the absorbed flux must be **amplified**, otherwise losses dominate the gain.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Physical Constant — Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

### 1.3 Classification of Concentrating Collectors

Concentrating collectors are classified based on the **shape of the focusing optics** and the **number of tracking axes**:

| Type | Focusing Geometry | Concentration Ratio (C) | Tracking |
|------|------------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Parabolic Trough (PTC)** | Linear (2D parabola) | 30 – 100 | Single-axis |
| **Parabolic Dish** | Point (3D parabola) | 1000 – 10000 | Two-axis |
| **Solar Tower (Central Receiver)** | Array of heliostats → point | 300 – 1500 | Two-axis (heliostats) |
| **Fresnel Reflector (linear)** | Flat segmented mirrors | 10 – 40 | Single-axis |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun-Magnifying Glass"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine using a magnifying glass on a sunny day to burn a piece of paper. The lens (or curved mirror) gathers sunlight spread over a large lens area and focuses it onto a tiny spot, raising the temperature enough to scorch paper. A parabolic trough is like an *elongated magnifying glass* (focusing along a *line*), while a dish or solar tower focuses it to a *point* — just like a real magnifying glass does. The smaller the focused spot, the hotter it gets — this is the essence of *solar concentration*.

### 1.5 Acceptance Angle and Concentration Ratio

Every concentrating collector has a finite **acceptance angle (θₐ)** — the maximum angular deviation of the incoming beam at which the radiation still falls entirely on the receiver. A smaller acceptance angle demands more precise sun-tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **maximum theoretical concentration ratio** for a 2D concentrator (trough) is $\frac{1}{\sin(\theta_a)}$, and for a 3D point-focus concentrator (dish/tower) it is $\frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_a)}$. For the Sun, $\theta_s \approx 0.265°$ (half-angle), giving theoretical maxima of **~216×** (2D) and **~46,500×** (3D).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Major CSC Technologies — Operating Principles

#### 2.1.1 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)

- **Geometry:** A cylindrical parabolic mirror focuses parallel beam radiation onto a linear focal line.
- **Receiver:** A blackened absorber tube (typically carrying **synthetic oil, water, or molten salt**) placed along the focal line, enclosed in a glass envelope to reduce convective losses.
- **Tracking:** **Single-axis tracking** — the trough rotates about one axis (typically N–S or E–W) to follow the Sun.
- **Operating Temperature:** 150 °C – 400 °C (with oil); up to **565 °C** with molten salt.
- **Practical Concentration Ratio:** **30 – 100**.
- **Commercial Examples:** SEGS (Solar Energy Generating Systems) plants in California, AndaSol in Spain.

**Why parabolic?** A parabola has the unique property that all rays parallel to its axis reflect to a single point (focal point). For a 2D parabolic cross-section, all parallel rays reflect to a single **line** (focal line). This geometric perfection is why parabolic reflectors are used universally.

#### 2.1.2 Parabolic Dish (Dish-Stirling System)

- **Geometry:** A 3D paraboloid (dish-shaped reflector) focuses sun rays onto a single point — the focal point.
- **Receiver:** A cavity receiver placed at the focal point, often coupled directly to a **Stirling engine** for power generation.
- **Tracking:** **Two-axis tracking** — both altitude and azimuth are continuously adjusted.
- **Operating Temperature:** 650 °C – 1500 °C.
- **Practical Concentration Ratio:** **1000 – 10,000** (the highest of all CSCs).
- **Efficiency:** 30 – 40% (overall solar-to-electric).

**Real-World Analogy:** A satellite dish antenna is essentially the same geometry — both parabolic dishes and TV dishes reflect electromagnetic waves to/from a single point. The difference is that a solar dish uses *reflection* of sunlight inward, while a TV dish uses *reflection* of radio waves inward.

#### 2.1.3 Solar Tower (Central Receiver System)

- **Geometry:** A large field of independently tracked flat mirrors (**heliostats**) reflects sunlight onto a single receiver mounted atop a tall tower.
- **Receiver:** Cavity or external receiver; working fluid is **molten salt, water/steam, air, or liquid sodium**.
- **Tracking:** **Two-axis tracking** for each heliostat.
- **Operating Temperature:** 500 °C – 1400 °C.
- **Practical Concentration Ratio:** **300 – 1500**.
- **Capacity:** 10 MW – 200 MW (largest among CSCs).

**Analogy:** Imagine thousands of soldiers with mirrors individually aimed at a single castle tower — every mirror reflects the sun onto one point. As more mirrors are added or focused more precisely, the temperature at the tower rises dramatically.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU exams. Memorize the units and physical significance.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$ | Geometric concentration ratio; $A_a$ = aperture area, $A_r$ = receiver area | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{max,2D} = \dfrac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)}$ | Max concentration for line-focus (2D); $\theta_s$ = half Sun angle | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_{max,3D} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$ | Max concentration for point-focus (3D) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_o = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ | Optical efficiency; $\rho$ = reflectivity, $\tau$ = glass transmittance, $\alpha$ = absorptivity | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_u = A_a \cdot S \cdot \eta_o - A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)$ | Useful heat gain (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation for CSC) | W (Watts) |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_a \cdot S}$ | Thermal efficiency of collector | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{r}{2 \cdot \sin^2(\phi/2)}$ or $\dfrac{r}{4 \tan(\phi/2)}$ for parabola | Focal length of parabolic trough; $r$ = rim radius, $\phi$ = rim angle | m |
| 8 | $\eta_{cycle} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{sink}}{T_{source}}$ | Carnot efficiency (theoretical max); $T$ in Kelvin | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{optical} \times \eta_{thermal} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech/elec}$ | Overall plant efficiency | Dimensionless |

### 2.3 The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation for Concentrators

The useful energy collected by a CSC per unit time is:

$$Q_u = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \rho \tau \alpha - A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)$$

Where:
- $A_a$ = **Aperture area** (reflecting surface area), m²
- $I_b$ = **Direct (beam) normal solar irradiance**, W/m²
- $\rho$ = **Mirror reflectivity** (≈ 0.90 – 0.94 for silvered glass)
- $\tau$ = **Glass envelope transmittance** (≈ 0.92 – 0.96)
- $\alpha$ = **Absorber surface absorptivity** (≈ 0.90 – 0.96 for selective coating)
- $A_r$ = **Receiver (absorber) area**, m²
- $U_L$ = **Overall heat loss coefficient**, W/(m²·K)
- $T_r$ = **Receiver temperature**, K
- $T_a$ = **Ambient temperature**, K

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why $I_b$ and not $I$?** Concentrators can ONLY use **direct (beam) radiation**, because diffuse radiation arrives from all directions and cannot be focused. This is why CSCs require clear-sky conditions and are unsuitable for cloudy/hazy climates.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PTC** → Grid-scale solar thermal power (Andasol, Genesis, Noor-Ouarzazate).
- **Dish-Stirling** → Distributed power generation in remote areas; high-efficiency modular units.
- **Solar Tower** → Largest-scale solar thermal plants (Ivanpah in USA; Gemasolar in Spain — provides **24-hour electricity** with molten salt thermal storage).

The **higher the operating temperature**, the higher the **Carnot efficiency** of the heat engine — so dish and tower systems can theoretically achieve higher conversion efficiencies than trough systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Geometric Concentration Ratio

**Problem Setup:** Consider a parabolic trough of aperture width $W$ and a tubular receiver of diameter $D_r$ placed at the focal line.

**Step 1:** Define the aperture area (projected area perpendicular to incoming beam):
$$A_a = W \cdot L$$

Where $L$ = length of the trough. We work per unit length, so $A_a = W$.

**Step 2:** Define the receiver area:
$$A_r = \pi \cdot D_r \cdot L$$

Per unit length: $A_r = \pi D_r$.

**Step 3:** The geometric concentration ratio is the ratio:
$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{W}{\pi D_r}$$

**Step 4:** For perfect imaging, the rim angle $\phi$ (angle between the optical axis and the line from focus to rim) relates $W$ to $D_r$. In the limit of small $D_r$ (no rim shading):
$$W \approx 2 f \tan(\phi/2)$$

And the receiver diameter is approximately:
$$D_r \approx \frac{f}{\cos(\phi/2) - 1} \quad \text{(simplified for marginal rays)}$$

**Step 5:** The maximum theoretical concentration occurs when the receiver just intercepts all rays at the acceptance angle equal to the solar half-angle $\theta_s$:
$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)} \approx \frac{1}{\sin(0.00463)} \approx 216$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum 3D Concentration Ratio

**Step 1:** For a 3D point-focus concentrator (dish), the ratio becomes area-based (m² to m²):

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{A_{aperture,circle}}{A_{receiver,circle}}$$

**Step 2:** For an ideal point-focus concentrator with circular symmetry and acceptance half-angle $\theta_a$, étendue (optical invariant) conservation gives:

$$A_a \cdot \sin^2(\theta_a) = A_r \cdot \sin^2(\theta_s)$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $C = A_a / A_r$:

$$C = \frac{\sin^2(\theta_a)}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 4:** The maximum value occurs when $\theta_a = 90°$ (hemispherical receiver, the largest possible):
$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(0.00463)} \approx 46{,}500$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of a PTC

**Starting point:** The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:
$$Q_u = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_o - A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)$$

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by $A_a \cdot I_b$:
$$\frac{Q_u}{A_a \cdot I_b} = \eta_o - \frac{A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)}{A_a \cdot I_b}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $A_r / A_a = 1/C$:
$$\frac{Q_u}{A_a \cdot I_b} = \eta_o - \frac{U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)}{C \cdot I_b}$$

**Step 3:** By definition, thermal efficiency is:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{A_a \cdot I_b}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \eta_o - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{C \cdot I_b}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Physical Interpretation:** The second term is the *fractional loss*. As $C$ increases, the loss term shrinks — meaning **higher concentration yields higher efficiency**, allowing higher operating temperatures. This is the fundamental engineering reason for building dish and tower systems.

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Parabolic Trough Sizing

**Given:**
- Rim radius of parabola, $r = 2.5$ m
- Rim angle, $\phi = 70°$
- Receiver tube outer diameter, $D_r = 0.07$ m
- Working fluid: Therminol VP-1 oil

**Find:** (a) Focal length, (b) Aperture width, (c) Geometric concentration ratio.

**Step 1:** Focal length using formula $f = \dfrac{r}{4 \tan(\phi/2)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{2.5}{4 \tan(35°)} \\
  &= \frac{2.5}{4 \times 0.7002} \\
  &= \frac{2.5}{2.8008} \\
  &\approx 0.8926 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Aperture width $W = 2r = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0$ m.

**Step 3:** Concentration ratio (per unit length):
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{W}{\pi D_r} \\
  &= \frac{5.0}{\pi \times 0.07} \\
  &= \frac{5.0}{0.2199} \\
  &\approx 22.7
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Sanity check:** Typical PTC systems have $C \approx 30 - 80$. The value 22.7 is somewhat low because of the wide aperture; commercial designs choose smaller rim angles for higher concentration.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Focusing** | Line (2D) | Point (3D) | Point (3D) |
| **C (practical)** | 30 – 100 | 1000 – 10,000 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Tracking axes** | 1 | 2 | 2 (each heliostat) |
| **Operating T** | 150 – 565 °C | 650 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1400 °C |
| **Working fluid** | Oil / water / salt | Air / He-H₂ | Salt / air / steam |
| **Conversion** | Steam Rankine | Stirling engine | Steam/gas turbine |
| **Solar-to-electric η** | 15 – 25 % | 30 – 40 % | 20 – 35 % |
| **Storage friendly** | Yes (2-tank salt) | Limited | Yes (large-scale salt) |
| **Land area** | Moderate | Small per unit | Very large |
| **Commercial maturity** | High (proven) | Demo stage | High (proven) |

### 3.6 Derivation of the Overall Plant Efficiency

For a solar thermal power plant using any CSC, the overall efficiency is:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{optical} \times \eta_{receiver} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech/elec}$$

**Numerical example (Solar Tower):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{optical} &= 0.85 \\
\eta_{receiver} &= 0.90 \\
\eta_{cycle} &= 0.40 \text{ (Carnot at 800°C/40°C = 65%, realistic = 40%)} \\
\eta_{mech/elec} &= 0.95
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.40 \times 0.95 \\
               &= 0.2907 \\
               &\approx 29\%
\end{aligned}$$

This matches typical operating data for plants like Gemasolar and Crescent Dunes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Parabolic Trough Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sun Beam Radiation Ib] --> AP[Parabolic Reflector Aperture]
    AP --> REF[Reflection onto Focal Line]
    REF --> REC[Receiver Tube with Oil]
    REC --> HX[Heat Exchanger]
    HX --> STM[Steam Generator]
    STM --> TURB[Steam Turbine]
    TURB --> GEN[Electrical Generator]
    GEN --> GRID[Power Grid]
    REC -.->|Hot Oil| HX
    STM -.->|Cold Oil| REC
    TURB --> COND[Condenser]
    COND --> PUMP[Feedwater Pump]
    PUMP --> STM
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Dish-Stirling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUNB[Sun Beam] --> DISH[Paraboloidal Dish Reflector]
    DISH --> FOC[Focused Beam at Focal Point]
    FOC --> CAV[Cavity Receiver]
    CAV --> HEAT[Heated Working Gas He or H2]
    HEAT --> STI[Stirling Engine Hot Cylinder]
    STI --> COLD[Stirling Engine Cold Cylinder]
    STI --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft Rotation]
    SHAFT --> ALT[Alternator]
    ALT --> LOAD[Electrical Load]
    COLD -->|Reject Heat| CSK[Cold Sink]
```

```mermaid

    SUNR[Sun Beam] --> HEL[Field of Heliostats 2 axis Tracked]
    HEL --> REF[Reflected Beam to Tower Top]
    REF --> RCVR[Central Receiver Cavity or External]
    RCVR --> SALT[Heated Molten Salt 565 C]
    SALT --> HOTS[Hot Salt Storage Tank]
    HOTS --> GEN[Steam Generator Superheater Reheater]
    GEN --> ST[Steam Turbine]
    ST --> GE[Generator]
    GE --> GRD[Power Grid]
    ST --> CON[Condenser]
    CON --> FP[Feed Pump]
    FP --> GEN
    RCVR -.->|Cold Salt 290 C| COLDS[Cold Salt Storage Tank]
    COLDS --> RCVR
```

### 4.4 Functional Block — Optical Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Beam Irradiance Ib W per m2] --> B[Mirror Reflectance rho]
    B --> C[Geometric Concentration C]
    C --> D[Glass Transmittance tau]
    D --> E[Receiver Absorptivity alpha]
    E --> F[Useful Heat Qu]
    F --> G[Conversion to Steam]
    G --> H[Steam Turbine Work]
    H --> I[Electrical Output]
```

        Incoming Sun Rays (parallel)

            ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼

       │   ┌───────────────┐  │
       │   │ PARABOLIC     │  │

         ╲ └───────┬───────┘ ╱
          ╲        │focus   ╱
           ╲       │       ╱
            ╲      ▼      ╱        Focal Length f

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`  
**Define a concentrating solar collector. Why can't flat-plate collectors operate efficiently above 100 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

A **concentrating solar collector (CSC)** is a device that uses reflecting or refracting optical elements to concentrate direct beam solar radiation from a large aperture area onto a smaller receiver area, increasing the flux intensity and thereby the working temperature.

Flat-plate collectors cannot operate efficiently above ~100 °C because of **thermal losses** (convection and radiation) from the absorber. As temperature rises, radiative loss grows as $T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann law). Since flat-plate collectors have $C = 1$ (no concentration), the absorbed flux equals the solar flux (~1000 W/m²), which is insufficient to overcome the rapidly increasing thermal losses at higher temperatures. Concentrators overcome this by raising the absorbed flux to several thousand W/m².

**(3 Marks — Award 1 for definition, 1 for flux argument, 1 for T⁴ loss reference.)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`  
**Distinguish between line-focusing and point-focusing concentrators. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Line-Focusing | Point-Focusing |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Geometry | Cylindrical parabola — focus along a line | Paraboloid — focus at a point |
| Concentration ratio | 30 – 100 | 300 – 10,000 |
| Tracking | Single-axis | Two-axis |
| Operating temperature | 150 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C |
| Example | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish / Solar Tower |

The **parabolic trough** is a line-focusing collector, while the **parabolic dish** and **solar tower** are point-focusing collectors.

**(3 Marks — Award 1 for distinction, 1 for tracking/temperature contrast, 1 for examples.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often (i) confuse **concentration ratio** with **optical efficiency**, (ii) forget that CSCs require **direct beam radiation only**, (iii) use flat-plate thermal-loss formulas without dividing by C, and (iv) neglect the units when reporting efficiency. Always show the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation in its CSC form explicitly. Marks are deducted heavily for missing the $\eta_o = \rho \tau \alpha$ clarification and for wrong concentration-ratio units.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Parabolic Trough Deep Dive

**Q.A (a)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`  
**Explain the construction and working of a Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) with a neat sketch. Discuss its concentration ratio, tracking method, and typical applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

1. **Parabolic Reflector:** Made of silvered glass mirrors or polished aluminum, shaped as a cylindrical paraboloid. The cross-section is a 2D parabola defined by $y^2 = 4 f x$, where $f$ is the focal length.
2. **Receiver Tube:** A blackened metal tube placed along the focal line, coated with a **selective absorber coating** (e.g., cermet) that has high absorptivity ($\alpha \approx 0.95$) for solar wavelengths but low emissivity ($\varepsilon \approx 0.10$) for infrared.
3. **Glass Envelope:** A glass tube concentric with the receiver, evacuated or filled with inert gas to suppress convective losses. It transmits ~96% of incoming radiation.
4. **Tracking Mechanism:** A single-axis drive (hydraulic or motor) rotates the trough about its longitudinal axis to follow the Sun's daily motion.
5. **Support Structure:** Steel framework supporting reflector, receiver, and tracking drive.

**Working Principle:**

Parallel sun rays incident on the parabolic reflector are reflected and converged onto the focal line, where the receiver tube absorbs the concentrated radiation. The working fluid (oil, water, or molten salt) flowing inside is heated to 150 °C – 400 °C and used either for direct process heat or to generate steam in a Heat Exchanger (HXG).

**Concentration Ratio:** $C = W / (\pi D_r)$. For typical commercial PTC, $C \approx 30 - 80$.

**Tracking:** Single-axis (N–S axis troughs track E–W; E–W axis troughs track altitude).

**Applications:**
- Solar thermal power generation (SEGS-type plants).
- Industrial process heat (food, textile, chemical).
- Desalination (MED, MSF).
- Enhanced oil recovery.

**(7 Marks: 2 construction, 2 working, 1 concentration, 1 tracking, 1 application.)**

---

**Q.A (b)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`  
**A parabolic trough has a rim angle of 80° and a rim radius of 3 m. The receiver tube has an outer diameter of 0.08 m. Calculate: (i) the focal length, (ii) the aperture width, (iii) the geometric concentration ratio. If the optical efficiency is 0.72 and the beam radiation is 850 W/m², compute the useful heat gain per metre length, assuming overall heat-loss coefficient $U_L = 4.5$ W/(m²K), fluid temperature 300 °C, and ambient 25 °C.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Focal length:**
$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{r}{4 \tan(\phi/2)} \\
  &= \frac{3.0}{4 \times \tan(40°)} \\
  &= \frac{3.0}{4 \times 0.8391} \\
  &= \frac{3.0}{3.3564} \\
  &\approx 0.8938 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Aperture width:**
$$W = 2 r = 2 \times 3.0 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Geometric concentration ratio (per metre length):**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{W}{\pi D_r} \\
  &= \frac{6.0}{\pi \times 0.08} \\
  &= \frac{6.0}{0.2513} \\
  &\approx 23.87
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Useful heat gain per metre length:**

Given:
- $\eta_o = 0.72$
- $I_b = 850$ W/m²
- $U_L = 4.5$ W/(m²·K)
- $T_r = 300 + 273 = 573$ K
- $T_a = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $A_a = W \times 1 = 6.0$ m²
- $A_r = \pi D_r \times 1 = 0.2513$ m²

Using Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_o - A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a) \\
    &= (6.0 \times 850 \times 0.72) - (0.2513 \times 4.5 \times (573 - 298)) \\
    &= 3672 - (0.2513 \times 4.5 \times 275) \\
    &= 3672 - 311.0 \\
    &= 3361 \text{ W per metre}
\end{aligned}$$

**Thermal efficiency check:**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{A_a \cdot I_b} = \frac{3361}{6.0 \times 850} = 0.659 = 65.9\%$$

**(7 Marks: 2 focal length, 1 aperture, 1 concentration ratio, 3 useful heat gain with substitution.)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Dish & Solar Tower Alternative

**Q.B (a)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`  
**Describe the construction and working principle of a Solar Tower (Central Receiver) power plant. Explain the role of heliostats, the working fluid options, and the merits of integrating thermal energy storage.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A solar tower plant has three major components:

1. **Heliostat Field:** A large array of flat, sun-tracking mirrors (heliostats). Each heliostat is typically 1 – 120 m² in area and is mounted on a **two-axis pedestal** that continuously tracks the Sun. Each heliostat independently aims reflected light at the top of the tower.
2. **Central Tower:** A tall concrete/steel tower (50 – 250 m) supporting the receiver and related equipment at its apex.
3. **Receiver:** Mounted at the top of the tower. Two types: **cavity receiver** (radiation enters through an aperture, absorbed on inner walls) and **external receiver** (a cylindrical boiler-like surface directly exposed). Cavity receivers are more efficient at lower solar inputs; external receivers scale better for large plants.

**Working Principle:**

Each heliostat reflects and concentrates direct beam solar radiation toward the receiver. The total concentration at the receiver is the sum of contributions from thousands of heliostats. A working fluid (molten salt, water/steam, liquid sodium, or air) absorbs this concentrated flux and is heated to high temperatures (500 °C – 1400 °C). The hot fluid drives a heat engine (typically a steam Rankine cycle, sometimes a Brayton gas turbine).

**Working Fluid Options:**

- **Water/Steam:** Mature technology, temperature ~550 °C, used in early plants like Solar One (USA).
- **Molten Salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃):** Operates 290 °C – 565 °C; favored for storage capability (used in Gemasolar, Crescent Dunes).
- **Liquid Sodium:** Very high temperatures, but corrosive and hazardous.
- **Air:** Used with Brayton cycle (Solar Tower Jülich, Germany); no phase-change issues.

**Merits of Thermal Energy Storage (TES):**

1. **Dispatchability:** Stored heat allows electricity generation **24/7** (not just when sun shines).
2. **Smoothing:** Buffers cloud transients, ensuring steady turbine output.
3. **Capacity Factor Increase:** Raises annual capacity factor from ~25% (no storage) to ~65% (with 15-hour TES).
4. **Grid Stability:** Predictable power output eases grid integration.
5. **Economic:** Higher capacity factor → lower Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE).

**(7 Marks: 2 construction, 2 working, 1 fluid options, 2 storage merits.)**

---

**Q.B (b)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`  
**Compare the parabolic dish and solar tower systems with respect to: (i) concentration ratio, (ii) tracking method, (iii) working temperature, (iv) working fluid, (v) typical efficiency, (vi) land requirement. State two merits and two limitations of each.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| (i) Concentration Ratio | 1000 – 10,000 | 300 – 1500 |
| (ii) Tracking | Two-axis (per dish) | Two-axis (per heliostat) |
| (iii) Working T | 650 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1400 °C |
| (iv) Working Fluid | Air or He-H₂ (closed) | Molten salt / water / air |
| (v) Solar-to-Electric η | 30 – 40% | 20 – 35% |
| (vi) Land Area | Smallest per MW | Largest per MW |

**Merits of Dish-Stirling:**
1. Highest concentration ratio and efficiency among CSCs.
2. Modular — capacities from 5 kW to 50 kW per dish, suitable for remote/standalone use.

**Limitations of Dish-Stirling:**
1. Requires very precise two-axis tracking — expensive.
2. Limited thermal storage capability (small receiver).

**Merits of Solar Tower:**
1. Best suited for very large capacities (10 – 200 MW).
2. Excellent compatibility with large-scale molten-salt thermal storage → 24-hour generation.

**Limitations of Solar Tower:**
1. Very large land area for heliostat field.
2. High initial capital cost; atmospheric attenuation (haze) reduces efficiency of distant heliostats.

**(7 Marks: 3 table content, 2 merits/limits of dish, 2 merits/limits of tower.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorize**

- ✅ **Concentration ratio** is $C = A_{aperture}/A_{receiver}$, dimensionless.
- ✅ **Max 2D concentration:** $C_{max} = 1/\sin(\theta_s) \approx 216$.
- ✅ **Max 3D concentration:** $C_{max} = 1/\sin^2(\theta_s) \approx 46{,}500$.
- ✅ **Optical efficiency:** $\eta_o = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ (reflectivity × glass transmittance × absorptivity).
- ✅ **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss for CSC:** $Q_u = A_a I_b \eta_o - A_r U_L (T_r - T_a)$.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \eta_o - \dfrac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{C \cdot I_b}$.
- ✅ **CSCs use ONLY direct beam radiation**, NOT diffuse — they cannot work under clouds.
- ✅ **Parabolic Trough:** line focus, single axis, $C \approx 30 - 100$, oil/salt, 150 – 400 °C.
- ✅ **Parabolic Dish:** point focus, two axis, $C \approx 1000 - 10{,}000$, Stirling engine, 650 – 1500 °C.
- ✅ **Solar Tower:** point focus via heliostats, two axis per heliostat, $C \approx 300 - 1500$, molten salt, 500 – 1400 °C.
- ✅ **Focal length formula:** $f = r / (4 \tan(\phi/2))$.
- ✅ **Aperture width:** $W = 2r$.
- ✅ **Higher $C$ → higher efficiency → higher operating temperature → higher Carnot efficiency.**
- ✅ **Thermal storage (molten salt) is the major advantage of solar tower plants.**
- ✅ **Solar constant** at Earth's surface (AM 1.5) ≈ **1000 W/m²**.
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴.

> [!TIP]  
> **Exam Strategy:** Always begin numerical answers with the relevant formula; substitute values with units; write the final answer in bold with units. Use schematic sketches wherever possible (even a simple line diagram fetches 1–2 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications: Solar Water Heaters, Solar Dryers, Solar Ponds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Water Heaters (SWH)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Solar Water Heater (SWH)** is a device that converts solar radiation into thermal energy and transfers it to a working fluid (typically water) for domestic, commercial, or industrial hot water applications. The system essentially comprises a **solar collector** (the heat-gathering component), a **storage tank** (insulated reservoir), interconnecting piping, and auxiliary backup heating.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Garden Hose on a Sunny Day:**
Imagine leaving a black garden hose filled with water out in the Kerala summer sun. By afternoon, the water inside becomes noticeably warm. This is exactly what an SWH does — but in a *controlled, optimized, and engineered* way. The black surface (absorber plate) captures sunlight, the glass cover traps heat (greenhouse effect), and the insulated tank stores the warm water for later use (bathing, washing, cooking).

### Principal Sub-Types of SWH (KTU 2024 High-Yield):
1. **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** — Most common. Uses a flat absorber plate with a transparent glass cover. Suitable for temperatures up to **80 °C**.
2. **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)** — Uses parallel rows of double-walled borosilicate glass tubes with vacuum between them. Suitable for temperatures up to **120 °C**; excellent in cloudy/cold climates.
3. **Concentrating Collector (Parabolic Trough)** — Used for high-temperature industrial applications (>150 °C).

| System Configuration | Storage Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermosiphon SWH** | Integral (in-tank) | Domestic, Kerala climate |
| **Forced Circulation SWH** | Separate (with pump) | Large commercial/industrial |
| **Direct (Open Loop)** | Water itself heated | Non-freezing climates only |

---

## 1.2 Solar Dryers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Solar Dryer** is an enclosed or semi-enclosed device that uses solar radiation to remove moisture from agricultural products, food materials, timber, fish, or industrial goods by controlled evaporation. The drying medium (heated air) carries away the evaporated moisture, preserving the product and extending shelf life.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Sun-Dried Clothes Phenomenon:**
When you hang wet clothes on a clothesline on a sunny Kerala afternoon, they dry up within a few hours. A solar dryer is essentially a *premium, accelerated, and hygienic version* of this natural process — protected from rain, dust, insects, and birds, while channeling the heated air efficiently through the product bed.

### Principal Sub-Types of Solar Dryers:
1. **Direct Solar Dryer** — Sunlight falls *directly* on the product (e.g., cabinet/tunnel dryers).
2. **Indirect Solar Dryer** — Air is pre-heated in a separate solar air heater collector and then passed *over* the product (protects color, vitamins).
3. **Mixed-Mode (Hybrid) Dryer** — Combines both: pre-heated air + direct radiation. **Most efficient** and most asked in KTU exams.
4. **Greenhouse Dryer** — Tunnel-type structure; ideal for bulk agricultural produce like coconut, copra, banana chips.

---

## 1.3 Solar Ponds

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Solar Pond** is a large, shallow body of saline water in which a deliberate **salt-concentration gradient** (heavier salt at the bottom, lighter fresh water on top) suppresses natural convection. This artificial stratification allows the bottom layer to reach temperatures of **90–100 °C** and act as a long-term thermal storage medium for electricity generation, process heat, and desalination.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Invisible Glass Box in a Lake:**
Normally, when sunlight heats the bottom of a lake, the warm water rises (convection) and heat is lost to the surface. Now imagine a lake where you dissolved salt only at the bottom. The salty (heavier) bottom water cannot rise even when hot — it is gravitationally trapped. The pond now behaves *as if* an invisible glass box traps heat in the lower zone. You can literally "tap" hot water from the bottom for industrial use!

### The Three Zones of a Salt-Gradient Solar Pond:
- **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ):** Fresh/near-fresh water; acts as transparent insulating blanket.
- **Middle Non-Convective Zone (NCZ):** Salt gradient (transparent); suppresses convection — **the heart of the pond**.
- **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ):** Saturated brine; acts as the **thermal storage** layer (60–100 °C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, focus on **principle of operation, design features, advantages, limitations, and Indian applications** of each of these three systems. Numerical problems typically appear on Solar Water Heater efficiency (Hottel-Whillier equation) and Solar Pond performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Water Heater — Operational Physics

### Step-by-Step Working Principle of a Flat Plate Collector (FPC):
- **Step 1 — Solar Transmittance:** Solar radiation passes through the glass cover (transmissivity, $\tau$).
- **Step 2 — Absorption:** The black-coated absorber plate absorbs ~95% of incident radiation (absorptivity, $\alpha$). Selective coatings (e.g., black nickel, black chrome) further reduce re-radiation losses.
- **Step 3 — Heat Transfer to Fluid:** Tubes bonded to the plate carry water/glycol which carries away absorbed heat (via conduction → convection).
- **Step 4 — Heat Retention:** Glass cover and insulation (back + sides) minimize top and edge losses through the greenhouse effect.
- **Step 5 — Delivery:** Pump or thermosiphon action delivers heated water to storage tank.

### Key Design Parameters:

- **Loss Coefficient, $U_L$** — Total heat loss from absorber to surroundings (top + bottom + edge losses combined). Units: **W/m²K**. Typical value: **3.5–6.0 W/m²K** for a well-designed FPC.
- **Heat Removal Factor, $F_R$** — Ratio of actual useful heat gain to the maximum possible if the entire plate were at fluid inlet temperature. Ranges between **0.6 and 0.9**.
- **Optical Efficiency, $(\tau\alpha)_e$** — Effective transmittance–absorptance product. Typical: **0.75–0.85**.

---

## 2.2 Solar Dryer — Psychrometric & Drying Theory

### Moisture Content Definitions (KTU Favourite!):
- **Moisture Content on Wet Basis (w.b.):**
$$w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100\%$$
Where $m_w$ = mass of water in product (kg), $m_d$ = mass of dry solid (kg).

- **Moisture Content on Dry Basis (d.b.):**
$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_d} \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

### Drying Rate Equation:
The fundamental mass-transfer equation for a solar dryer:
$$\frac{dm_w}{dt} = -h_m \, A_s \, (P_s - P_a)$$

Where:
- $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $A_s$ = surface area of drying product (m²)
- $P_s$ = vapor pressure of water at product surface temperature (Pa)
- $P_a$ = partial pressure of water vapor in bulk air (Pa)

### Energy Required for Moisture Removal:
$$Q_{evap} = m_w \, h_{fg}$$

Where $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 100 °C; ≈ **2.43 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 60 °C.

---

## 2.3 Solar Pond — Stratification Physics

### Operating Principle:
In a normal pond, **Rayleigh number > Critical Rayleigh Number** → free convection → thermal mixing.
In a salt-gradient solar pond, the **density gradient counteracts thermal buoyancy** → convection is suppressed → **bottom temperature can build up to boiling point**.

### Governing Non-Convection Criterion (Density Gradient Requirement):
$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} \geq 0$$

Where $\rho$ = density of brine solution. This must hold at every depth $z$ throughout the NCZ for stability.

### Solar Pond Efficiency:
$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{A_{pond} \times I_{solar}}$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}_{useful}$ = useful heat extracted from LCZ (W)
- $A_{pond}$ = pond surface area (m²)
- $I_{solar}$ = incident solar radiation on pond surface (W/m²)

---

## KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Note:** All vertical bars in formulas are rendered with `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax. Use this as your one-stop reference for KTU Part A and Part B numericals.

| # | Application | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SWH (FPC) | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]$ | Useful heat gained | W | — |
| 2 | SWH (FPC) | $F_R = \dfrac{F'}{1 + \dfrac{F' \, U_L}{\dot{m} \, C_{pf}}}$ | Heat removal factor | — | 0.6–0.9 |
| 3 | SWH (FPC) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \, S}$ | Instantaneous collector efficiency | — | 0.40–0.70 |
| 4 | SWH (FPC) | $\eta_c = (\tau\alpha)_e - \dfrac{U_L (T_{in} - T_a)}{S}$ | Linear efficiency equation (vs $T_{in}/S$) | — | — |
| 5 | Dryer (w.b.) | $w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | kg/kg or % | 0 to 1 |
| 6 | Dryer (d.b.) | $X = m_w / m_d$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg/kg | 0.1–5 |

| 10 | Pond | $\rho_{LCZ} > \rho_{UCZ}$ | Density gradient stability | kg/m³ | $\Delta\rho \geq 20$ kg/m³ |
| 11 | Pond | $Q_{loss} = U_b \, A \, (T_b - T_g)$ | Ground heat loss from LCZ | W | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SWH:** Globally, **> 500 GW_th** is installed. India targets **20 million m² of SWH collector area** under Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM). Domestic payback: **3–5 years** in Kerala.
- **Solar Dryers:** Kerala's **spice industry** (cardamom, pepper, turmeric), **coconut copra drying**, fish drying in coastal areas, and **rubber sheet drying** are major beneficiaries.
- **Solar Ponds:** Demonstrated 5 MWe plant at **Bet Ha'Arava, Israel**; experimental ponds at **Bhavnagar, Gujarat (Gujarat Energy Research & Management Institute)** in India for process heat and electricity generation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (for FPC-SWH)

The **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** is the *single most important* formula in solar thermal engineering. KTU expects a full derivation in Part B.

### Starting Point — Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate:

For a steady-state FPC, the absorbed solar radiation equals the sum of useful heat gain and heat losses (top + bottom + edge). In differential form over an infinitesimal area $dA$:

$$\text{Energy In} = \text{Energy Out} + \text{Losses}$$

$$(\tau\alpha)_e \, S \, dA = q_{useful} \, dA + U_L (T_p - T_a) \, dA$$

Where:
- $(\tau\alpha)_e \, S$ = effective absorbed solar flux (W/m²)
- $T_p$ = local absorber plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C)
- $U_L$ = overall top/bottom loss coefficient (W/m²K)

### Step 1: Define Useful Heat Flux

The useful heat flux transferred to the fluid locally is:
$$q_{useful} = F' \left[ (\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_p - T_a) \right]$$

Where $F'$ = **collector efficiency factor** (ratio of useful heat to heat that would be transferred if entire plate were at fluid temperature).

### Step 2: Introduce Heat Removal Factor $F_R$

Since the fluid enters at $T_{in}$ and exits at $T_{out}$, we want an expression in terms of the **inlet fluid temperature** $T_{in}$ instead of $T_p$. Define:
$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} C_{pf} (T_{out} - T_{in})}{A_c \left[(\tau\alpha)_e S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]}$$

Solving for $F_R$ in terms of $F'$:
$$F_R = \frac{F'}{1 + \dfrac{F' \, U_L}{\dot{m} \, C_{pf} / A_c}}$$

Where $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), $C_{pf}$ = specific heat of fluid (J/kgK).

### Step 3: Final Useful Heat Gain Equation

By integrating the local equation over the entire collector area and using the definition of $F_R$:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]}$$

This is the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation** — a cornerstone of KTU numerical problems.

### Step 4: Derive Efficiency Equation

Divide both sides by incident solar power $A_c \, S$:
$$\boxed{\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, S} = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e - \frac{F_R \, U_L (T_{in} - T_a)}{S}}$$

**Key Insight:** This is a *linear equation* in $(T_{in} - T_a)/S$. If we plot $\eta_c$ on Y-axis against $(T_{in} - T_a)/S$ on X-axis, we get a **straight line** with:
- **Y-intercept** = $F_R (\tau\alpha)_e$ = *optical efficiency at zero loss*
- **Slope** = $-F_R \, U_L$ = *negative of total heat loss coefficient*

### Step 5: Worked Numerical (KTU-Style Example)

> **Given:** An FPC-SWH has $A_c = 2.0$ m², $F_R = 0.80$, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.78$, $U_L = 4.5$ W/m²K. Water enters at $T_{in} = 30$ °C, ambient $T_a = 25$ °C, solar flux $S = 800$ W/m². Find $Q_u$ and $\eta_c$.

**Solution:**

$$T_{in} - T_a = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ °C}$$

Apply the formula:
$$Q_u = (2.0)(0.80) \left[ (0.78)(800) - (4.5)(5) \right]$$

Computing inside the bracket:
- $(0.78)(800) = 624$ W/m²
- $(4.5)(5) = 22.5$ W/m²
- Bracket value: $624 - 22.5 = 601.5$ W/m²

Therefore:
$$Q_u = (2.0)(0.80)(601.5) = 962.4 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, S} = \frac{962.4}{(2.0)(800)} = 0.6015 \approx 60.15\%$$

✅ **Sanity Check:** This is within the typical range of 40–70%, confirming the calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Pond Useful Heat Extraction Rate

### Step 1: Solar Energy Absorbed in LCZ

The LCZ (lower convective zone) absorbs solar radiation as it passes down through the pond. Considering Beer-Lambert absorption:
$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu z}$$

Where:
- $I(z)$ = solar intensity at depth $z$ (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = solar intensity at pond surface (W/m²)
- $\mu$ = extinction coefficient of brine (m⁻¹)
- $z$ = depth (m)

### Step 2: Energy Stored in LCZ

For a pond of depth $h$ and considering steady state with no ground loss:
$$\dot{Q}_{LCZ} = A \cdot I_0 \left(1 - e^{-\mu \, h_{LCZ}}\right) - \dot{Q}_{ground}$$

### Step 3: Include Ground Heat Loss

Heat loss to ground beneath the LCZ:
$$\dot{Q}_{ground} = U_b \, A \, (T_b - T_g)$$

Where $U_b$ = bottom heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $T_b$ = LCZ temperature (°C), $T_g$ = ground temperature (°C).

### Step 4: Net Useful Heat Extracted

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{useful} = A \, I_0 \left(1 - e^{-\mu \, h_{LCZ}}\right) - U_b \, A \, (T_b - T_g)}$$

### Step 5: Define Pond Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{A \, I_0} = \left(1 - e^{-\mu \, h_{LCZ}}\right) - \frac{U_b (T_b - T_g)}{I_0}}$$

**Key Insight:** To maximize $\eta_{pond}$, we must:
- Use clear brine (low $\mu$)
- Make LCZ deeper (until $e^{-\mu h} \to 0$)
- Minimize ground heat loss (use insulation under pond)
- Maintain strong salt gradient to prevent convection

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solar Dryer — Drying Efficiency

### Step 1: Total Moisture Removed
During a drying cycle from initial moisture $X_i$ (d.b.) to final $X_f$ for product of dry mass $m_d$:
$$m_w = m_d (X_i - X_f)$$

### Step 2: Total Energy Required (Sensible + Latent)
For each kg of water removed:
$$q = h_{fg} + C_{pv} (T_{air,in} - T_{initial})$$

For simplification, neglecting sensible heating of vapor:
$$Q_{evap} \approx m_d (X_i - X_f) \cdot h_{fg}$$

### Step 3: Solar Energy Input Over Time
$$Q_{solar} = A_c \, I \, t$$

### Step 4: Drying Efficiency
$$\boxed{\eta_d = \frac{m_d (X_i - X_f) \, h_{fg}}{A_c \, I \, t}}$$

**Note:** $A_c$ here is the **collector aperture area**, which differs from the **product tray area**. KTU questions often give one and ask for the other.

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Flat Plate Collector (FPC) and an Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) used in solar water heating systems.**

**Model Answer (Concise):**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector (FPC) | Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Flat, rectangular panel | Parallel cylindrical vacuum tubes |
| **Insulation** | Glass cover + back insulation | Vacuum between inner & outer glass |
| **Temperature Range** | Up to 80 °C | Up to 120 °C (or higher) |
| **Efficiency in Cold/Cloudy Climate** | Lower | Higher (vacuum eliminates convection loss) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (~1.5× FPC) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (single glass replaceable) | Tubes individually replaceable |
| **Best Application** | Tropical climate like Kerala | Cold climates, high-temperature needs |

**(3 marks: 1 for difference in geometry, 1 for insulation, 1 for temperature range)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a "mixed-mode solar dryer"? Mention two advantages over a direct solar dryer.**

**Model Answer:**
A **mixed-mode solar dryer** is a hybrid drying system in which the product receives **both** (a) direct solar radiation falling on it through a transparent cover, and (b) **pre-heated air** supplied by a separate solar air-heater collector. This combination ensures higher drying temperature and faster, more uniform moisture removal.

**Two Advantages over Direct Solar Dryer:**
1. **Better product quality** — Pre-heated air reduces direct UV exposure, preserving color, aroma, and nutrients (especially useful for spices, herbs, and medicinal plants).
2. **Faster drying rate** — Air temperature can be controlled independently of solar intensity, enabling drying even during partial cloud cover.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 per advantage)**

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

#### (a) **Derive the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation for a flat plate solar collector and explain the significance of each term. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy Balance Setup:**
For a steady-state FPC, on a differential area $dA$, the absorbed solar energy equals the sum of useful heat removed and the various losses:
$$(\tau\alpha)_e \, S \, dA = q_u \, dA + U_L (T_p - T_a) \, dA$$

**Step 2 — Local Heat Flux Using Efficiency Factor $F'$:**
$$q_u = F' \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_p - T_a)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Integration Over Whole Plate & Inlet Temperature Substitution:**
By defining $F_R$ such that the equation is referenced to fluid inlet temperature $T_{in}$ instead of varying plate temperature:
$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss):**
$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]}$$

**Significance of Each Term:**

| Symbol | Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $A_c$ | Collector gross area | Area intercepting solar radiation (m²) |
| $F_R$ | Heat removal factor | Effectiveness of transferring absorbed heat to fluid; dimensionless, 0.6–0.9 |
| $(\tau\alpha)_e$ | Effective transmittance–absorptance product | Fraction of solar radiation retained after transmission & absorption; 0.75–0.85 |
| $S$ | Incident solar flux | Solar radiation on tilted collector (W/m²) |
| $U_L$ | Overall heat loss coefficient | Combined top, bottom, and edge losses (W/m²K) |
| $T_{in}$ | Fluid inlet temperature | Temperature of water entering the collector (°C) |
| $T_a$ | Ambient temperature | Surrounding air temperature (°C) |

**(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for explanation table, 2 for correct final form)**

---

#### (b) **A flat plate collector has the following specifications: $A_c = 3.0$ m², $F_R = 0.78$, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.80$, $U_L = 5.0$ W/m²K. Water enters at 35 °C when ambient is 28 °C and solar flux is 900 W/m². Compute: (i) Useful heat gained per hour, (ii) Outlet water temperature if mass flow rate is 0.02 kg/s. Take $C_p = 4180$ J/kgK. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $A_c = 3.0$ m², $F_R = 0.78$, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.80$
- $U_L = 5.0$ W/m²K, $T_{in} = 35$ °C, $T_a = 28$ °C
- $S = 900$ W/m², $\dot{m} = 0.02$ kg/s, $C_p = 4180$ J/kgK

**Part (i) Useful Heat Gained per Hour:**

Apply Hottel-Whillier-Bliss:
$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[(\tau\alpha)_e \, S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)\right]$$

Step 1: Compute absorbed radiation:
$$(\tau\alpha)_e \, S = (0.80)(900) = 720 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Step 2: Compute loss term:
$$U_L (T_{in} - T_a) = (5.0)(35 - 28) = (5.0)(7) = 35 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Step 3: Compute net absorbed minus loss:
$$720 - 35 = 685 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Step 4: Apply $F_R$ and area:
$$Q_u = (3.0)(0.78)(685) = 1603.6 \text{ W}$$

Step 5: Convert to hourly basis:
$$Q_{u,hour} = 1603.6 \times 3600 = 5{,}772{,}960 \text{ J/hour} \approx 5.77 \text{ MJ/hour}$$

**Part (ii) Outlet Water Temperature:**

Energy balance on water:
$$Q_u = \dot{m} \, C_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Rearranging:
$$T_{out} - T_{in} = \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} \, C_p} = \frac{1603.6}{(0.02)(4180)} = \frac{1603.6}{83.6} = 19.18 \text{ °C}$$

Therefore:
$$T_{out} = T_{in} + 19.18 = 35 + 19.18 = 54.18 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q_u = 1603.6$ W ≈ **5.77 MJ/hour**
- (ii) $T_{out} = 54.18$ °C

**(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for calculation, 1 for hourly conversion, 1 for outlet temperature)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **Explain the construction and working principle of a Salt-Gradient Solar Pond. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A salt-gradient solar pond is a man-made shallow basin (typically 1–3 m deep) filled with water in three distinct layers:
1. **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ):** 0.2–0.4 m thick, near-fresh water, low salinity.
2. **Middle Non-Convective Zone (NCZ):** 0.6–1.5 m thick, transparent salt concentration increasing linearly from top (low) to bottom (high).
3. **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ):** 0.3–1.0 m thick, fully saturated brine (saturated NaCl ≈ 26% by weight), acts as the heat storage layer.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
Sunlight penetrates the UCZ and NCZ with minimal absorption. In the LCZ, radiation is absorbed and converted to heat. Normally, the hot brine would rise (convection), but the density gradient (heavier salt below) counteracts thermal buoyancy. Hence, convection is suppressed. Heat accumulates in the LCZ, raising its temperature to **60–100 °C**. This hot brine is pumped through an **in-pond heat exchanger**, transferring heat to a secondary working fluid for power generation or process heat.

**Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Lowest-cost thermal energy storage (no expensive battery)
- Long-term heat storage (days to months)
- Dual use — electricity + desalination + greenhouse heating

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Large land area required
- Salt diffusion destabilizes gradient over time → requires periodic recharging
- Low conversion efficiency (~15–20%)

**(7 marks distribution as above)**

---

#### (b) **A solar pond has surface area 1000 m² and depth 1.5 m. Solar radiation of 800 W/m² is incident on the surface. The extinction coefficient of brine is 0.4 m⁻¹, and bottom heat loss coefficient is 0.5 W/m²K. If LCZ temperature is 75 °C and ground temperature is 25 °C, compute (i) the solar energy absorbed in LCZ, (ii) the useful heat extracted, and (iii) the pond efficiency. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $A = 1000$ m², depth = 1.5 m
- $I_0 = 800$ W/m², $\mu = 0.4$ m⁻¹
- $U_b = 0.5$ W/m²K, $T_b = 75$ °C, $T_g = 25$ °C

**Part (i): Solar Energy Absorbed in LCZ**

Assuming LCZ occupies the lower 1.5 m (full depth for simplicity):
$$\mu \, h = 0.4 \times 1.5 = 0.6$$

Fraction absorbed:
$$1 - e^{-\mu h} = 1 - e^{-0.6} = 1 - 0.5488 = 0.4512$$

Solar power absorbed in LCZ:
$$\dot{Q}_{abs} = A \, I_0 \left(1 - e^{-\mu h}\right) = (1000)(800)(0.4512) = 361{,}000 \text{ W} = 361 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (ii): Useful Heat Extracted**

Ground heat loss:
$$\dot{Q}_{ground} = U_b \, A \, (T_b - T_g) = (0.5)(1000)(75 - 25) = (0.5)(1000)(50) = 25{,}000 \text{ W} = 25 \text{ kW}$$

Useful heat:
$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = \dot{Q}_{abs} - \dot{Q}_{ground} = 361{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 336{,}000 \text{ W} = 336 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (iii): Pond Efficiency**

$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{A \, I_0} = \frac{336{,}000}{(1000)(800)} = \frac{336{,}000}{800{,}000} = 0.42 \text{ or } 42\%$$

✅ **Sanity Check:** Typical solar pond efficiency is 10–25% in real life; our 42% indicates this problem assumes optimistic conditions (clear brine, low $\mu$).

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Solar energy absorbed in LCZ = **361 kW**
- (ii) Useful heat extracted = **336 kW**
- (iii) Pond efficiency = **42%**

**(7 marks: 2 setup, 3 calculation, 1 efficiency formula, 1 numerical answer)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting between W and kW arbitrarily — always write the unit explicitly. Penalty: **0.5 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Multiply by Area ($A_c$):** The most common error in Part B is computing $Q_u/m^2$ and forgetting to multiply by collector area. Penalty: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing Up $F'$ and $F_R$:** $F'$ depends only on the collector geometry; $F_R$ additionally depends on flow rate. Use $F_R$ in the final equation.
> 4. **Forgetting the Inlet Temperature Difference:** Using $T_a$ instead of $T_{in}$ in the loss term reduces accuracy. Penalty: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Solar Pond NCZ Definition:** Students often skip defining why the NCZ is "non-convective" — write "because density gradient counteracts thermal buoyancy."
> 6. **Not Labeling Diagrams:** Diagrams without labels fetch 0 marks even if technically correct.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solar Water Heaters, Dryers & Ponds (Module 1)**

### 🔹 Solar Water Heaters (SWH)
- Two main types: **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** and **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)**.
- FPC uses selective coating + glass cover + insulation to trap heat via greenhouse effect.
- Key formula: **$Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)_e S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a)]$** ← Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation.
- Linear efficiency equation: **$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a)/S$** (slope and intercept have physical meaning).
- Typical FPC efficiency: 40–70%; typical operating temperature: 50–80 °C.
- Indian domestic SWH payback period: **3–5 years**.

### 🔹 Solar Dryers
- Four main types: **Direct, Indirect, Mixed-Mode (Hybrid), Greenhouse**.
- Mixed-mode dryers combine **direct radiation + pre-heated air** — best for product quality.
- Moisture content: **wet basis ($w$)** and **dry basis ($X$)** — KTU commonly tests the conversion between them.
- Drying efficiency formula: **$\eta_d = m_d (X_i - X_f) h_{fg} / (A_c I t)$**.
- Applications in Kerala: **spices, coconut copra, fish, rubber sheets, banana chips**.

### 🔹 Solar Ponds
- Three zones: **UCZ (transparent insulator) → NCZ (salt gradient barrier) → LCZ (heat storage)**.
- Working principle: salt gradient suppresses natural convection → heat trapped in LCZ.
- Bottom LCZ can reach **90–100 °C** in well-designed ponds.
- Pond efficiency: **$\eta_p = \dot{Q}_{useful} / (A I)$** — typically 10–25% in practice.
- Major limitation: salt diffusion destabilizes gradient → periodic maintenance required.
- Famous installations: **Bet Ha'Arava (Israel) — 5 MWe**; **Bhavnagar (Gujarat)** for process heat.

### 🔹 Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- Don't confuse **$F_R$** (heat removal factor) with **$F'$** (collector efficiency factor).
- Don't use **$T_a$** when **$T_{in}$** is required in the loss term.
- Don't forget to **multiply by $A_c$** when calculating total useful heat.
- Always **label diagrams** — unlabeled sketches score zero.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Applications: Solar Water Heaters, Solar Dryers, Solar Ponds**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Photovoltaic Systems: PV Cell Principles and Materials (Silicon, Thin-Film, Organic)
PV Modules and Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PV Modules and Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Photovoltaic (PV) Module?

A **Photovoltaic (PV) Module** is an assembly of electrically interconnected solar cells encapsulated within a weather-proof, mechanically robust frame that converts incident solar irradiance directly into direct current (DC) electricity through the **photovoltaic effect** at the semiconductor p–n junction. A single crystalline silicon cell typically produces only about **0.5 V to 0.7 V** under open-circuit conditions; hence, multiple cells are series-connected inside a module to obtain a usable DC voltage (commonly 12 V, 24 V, or higher).

A **PV Array** is a larger, field-deployed configuration formed by electrically and mechanically integrating several PV modules in series and/or parallel to satisfy the voltage, current, and power requirements of the intended load or grid-tie inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot**
> *A PV module is a pre-wired, encapsulated panel consisting of series-connected solar cells; a PV array is a combination of modules interconnected to deliver a specified electrical output.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade"

Imagine a row of water buckets, each capable of scooping **1 litre** of water per second. If you place them in a line and tip each bucket's contents into the next, the **flow rate (current)** stays the same, but the **pressure (voltage)** builds up. That is exactly what happens when solar cells are **connected in series** inside a module.

Now imagine another line of buckets placed **side-by-side**; their water streams merge into a wider pipe. The **pressure stays the same**, but the **flow rate (current) doubles**. That is the **parallel** connection of modules inside an array.

- A **cell** = 1 bucket (≈ 0.5 V, a few amps)
- A **module** = the row of buckets in series (≈ 30–40 cells, giving 18–24 V)
- An **array** = a network of such rows, both in series (for higher voltage) and parallel (for higher current)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Practical Constants to Remember**
> - Standard Test Conditions (STC) = **1000 W/m²** irradiance, **25 °C** cell temperature, **AM 1.5** spectrum.
> - Bandgap of crystalline silicon ≈ **1.12 eV**.
> - Open-circuit voltage of a single Si cell ≈ **0.6 – 0.7 V**.
> - Short-circuit current density of a typical Si cell ≈ **30 – 40 mA/cm²**.

---

## 1.3 Building-Block Hierarchy

| Level | Typical Voltage | Typical Power | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar Cell** | 0.5 – 0.7 V | 1 – 3 W | Basic photovoltaic unit |
| **PV Module** | 18 – 48 V | 50 – 400 W | Encapsulated, usable unit |
| **PV String** | 200 – 1000 V | 1 – 10 kW | Modules in series |
| **PV Array** | Any V, any I | kW – MW | Strings in series/parallel |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PV Cell — Operating Principle

A PV cell is fundamentally a **large-area p–n junction diode** with a thin, optically transparent top contact. When photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the semiconductor:

1. They are absorbed, generating **electron–hole pairs**.
2. The built-in electric field at the depletion region **sweeps the photo-generated carriers** to opposite sides of the junction.
3. The separated carriers produce a **photo-current $I_L$** that flows through the external load.

Because the cell is essentially a current source in parallel with a diode, its behaviour is described by the **single-diode equivalent circuit**.

---

## 2.2 The Single-Diode Equivalent Model

The standard single-diode model of a PV cell consists of:

- A **photo-generated current source** $I_L$ (proportional to irradiance).
- A **diode** representing the p–n junction, with saturation current $I_0$ and ideality factor $a$ (typically 1 – 2).
- A **series resistance $R_S$** capturing ohmic losses in contacts, current-collecting fingers, and the emitter sheet.
- A **shunt (parallel) resistance $R_{SH}$** capturing manufacturing-induced leakage paths near the cell edges.

The **characteristic equation of the PV cell** is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_S}{a} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_S}{R_{SH}}$$

Under the **idealised assumption** ($R_S = 0$, $R_{SH} \rightarrow \infty$), this reduces to the simpler **Shockley diode equation**:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT} \right) - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $q$ = electronic charge $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- $n$ = diode ideality factor (≈ 1 – 2)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- $T$ = absolute cell temperature in **K**

---

## 2.3 Key Performance Parameters

- **Short-circuit current ($I_{SC}$)** — current when the terminals are shorted ($V = 0$). Approximately equals the photo-generated current $I_L$.
- **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$)** — terminal voltage when current is zero:
  $$V_{OC} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$
- **Maximum power point ($P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$)** — the knee of the I–V curve where the product of $V$ and $I$ is maximum.
- **Fill Factor (FF)** — the "squareness" of the I–V curve:
  $$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$
- **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)** — ratio of electrical power output to incident solar power:
  $$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$
  where $G$ = irradiance (W/m²) and $A$ = active cell area (m²).

---

## 2.4 Interconnection Laws

- **Series Connection (String):** Currents equal, voltages add.
  $$I_{string} = I_{module}, \qquad V_{string} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_s} V_k$$
- **Parallel Connection:** Voltages equal, currents add.
  $$V_{array} = V_{string}, \qquad I_{array} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_p} I_k$$
- **Total Array Power:**
  $$P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{module}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V+IR_S}{a}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V+IR_S}{R_{SH}}$ | Single-diode model | A, V, Ω |
| 2 | $I = I_{SC} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Idealised cell equation | A, V |
| 3 | $V_{OC} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{SC}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V |
| 4 | $FF = \frac{V_m I_m}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | Fill Factor | dimensionless (0.5 – 0.85) |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{G \cdot A}$ | Cell/Module efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{max} = \eta \cdot G \cdot A$ | Output power at MPP | W |
| 7 | $V_{array} = N_s \cdot V_{module}$ | Series array voltage | V |
| 8 | $I_{array} = N_p \cdot I_{module}$ | Parallel array current | A |
| 9 | $P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{module}$ | Total array power | W |
| 10 | $\text{Temp coeff of } V_{OC} \approx -2.2 \text{ mV/°C per cell}$ | Voltage vs. temperature | mV/°C |
| 11 | $\text{Temp coeff of } I_{SC} \approx +0.06 \text{ %/°C}$ | Current vs. temperature | %/°C |

> [!NOTE]
> The thermal voltage $\frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K is a frequently-tested number in KTU derivations. Remember it!

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PV modules and arrays are the building blocks of:
- **Rooftop solar plants** (1 – 10 kW) for residential buildings.
- **Utility-scale solar farms** (1 MW – 600 MW) feeding the grid.
- **Stand-alone / off-grid systems** with battery storage for remote electrification.
- **Solar water pumping** systems for agriculture.
- **Spacecraft power systems** (high-efficiency multi-junction cells).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module-level design choices — series/parallel arrangement, bypass diode placement, and MPPT integration — directly determine the **string voltage, fault tolerance, and overall plant generation**. KTU examiners expect students to be able to choose the right configuration for a given DC load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Open-Circuit Voltage of a PV Cell

**Starting point:** Apply the single-diode equation with $I = 0$ and the open-circuit condition $V = V_{OC}$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Rearrange the equation to isolate the exponential term:

$$I_L = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Divide both sides by $I_0$:

$$\frac{I_L}{I_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}\right) - 1$$

Add 1 to both sides:

$$\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 = \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}\right)$$

Apply the natural logarithm on both sides to remove the exponential:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) = \frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}$$

Multiply both sides by $\frac{nkT}{q}$:

$$V_{OC} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Interpretation:** $V_{OC}$ increases logarithmically with light-generated current (and hence irradiance), and decreases strongly with increasing reverse saturation current $I_0$ — which itself doubles for every ~10 °C rise in temperature. This is why hot cells lose voltage rapidly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fill Factor Expression (Empirical)

A widely used empirical relation for silicon cells (Green, 1981) is:

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{OC} - \ln(v_{OC} + 0.72)}{v_{OC} + 1}$$

where $v_{OC} = \frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}$ is the normalised open-circuit voltage. To obtain the **real FF** including series-resistance effects, the empirical correction is:

$$FF = FF_0 \left( 1 - 1.02 r_S \right) \left( 1 - \frac{r_{SH}^{-1}}{v_{OC} + 0.72} \right)$$

where $r_S = \frac{R_S I_{SC}}{V_{OC}}$ and $r_{SH} = \frac{R_{SH} I_{SC}}{V_{OC}}$ are the normalised parasitic resistances.

**Step-by-step intuition:**
- $FF_0$ is the "ideal" fill factor depending only on $v_{OC}$.
- The term $(1 - 1.02\,r_S)$ penalises series resistance — every extra ohm of $R_S$ shaves off FF roughly in proportion to $I_{SC}/V_{OC}$.
- The third term penalises finite shunt resistance — leakage across the junction straightens the I–V curve near short-circuit and reduces FF.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numerical problems, a simpler but still valid form is:
> $$FF \approx \frac{V_{OC} - \ln(V_{OC} + 0.72)}{V_{OC} + 1} \times \left(1 - R_S \cdot \frac{I_{SC}}{V_{OC}}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Series/Parallel Interconnection on Module Output
