**Setup:** A module has $N_s$ cells connected in **series** and $N_p$ such series strings connected in **parallel**. Each cell has individual $V_{OC,cell}$ and $I_{SC,cell}$.

**Series connection — voltage:**

$$V_{OC,module} = N_s \cdot V_{OC,cell}$$

This is because, in a series string, the same current must flow through every cell, so their terminal voltages add.

**Series connection — current:**

$$I_{SC,module} = I_{SC,cell}$$

All cells share a common current path; hence the string current equals the individual cell current.

**Parallel connection — current:**

$$I_{SC,array} = N_p \cdot I_{SC,module}$$

The output currents of all parallel strings add at the common node.

**Parallel connection — voltage:**

$$V_{OC,array} = V_{OC,module}$$

All parallel strings must operate at the same terminal voltage.

**Total Array Power:**

$$P_{array} = V_{OC,array} \cdot I_{SC,array} \cdot FF_{array}$$

Substituting the interconnection laws:

$$P_{array} = \left( N_s \cdot V_{OC,cell} \right) \cdot \left( N_p \cdot I_{SC,cell} \right) \cdot FF_{array}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{cell} \cdot \frac{FF_{array}}{FF_{cell}}}$$

For a well-matched array, $FF_{array} \approx FF_{cell}$, and the array power is simply the cell power multiplied by the number of cells.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Temperature & Irradiance Corrections (Important for KTU Numericals)

**Temperature effect on $V_{OC}$:**

$$\Delta V_{OC} = K_V \cdot (T - 25) \quad \text{with } K_V \approx -2.2 \text{ mV/°C per cell}$$

**Temperature effect on $I_{SC}$:**

$$\Delta I_{SC} = K_I \cdot I_{SC,STC} \cdot (T - 25) \quad \text{with } K_I \approx +0.06 \text{ %/°C}$$

**Irradiance effect:**

$$I_{SC}(G) = I_{SC,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{1000}, \qquad V_{OC}(G) = V_{OC,STC} + \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{G}{1000}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Current is **linearly proportional to irradiance**; voltage changes only **logarithmically**. This is why cloudy skies cut current drastically but barely touch voltage.

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A PV module has the following specifications at STC:
$V_{OC} = 22 \, \text{V}$, $I_{SC} = 8 \, \text{A}$, $V_m = 17.5 \, \text{V}$, $I_m = 7.2 \, \text{A}$, $A = 0.5 \, \text{m}^2$.

**Find (a) Fill Factor, (b) Efficiency, (c) Total array power for 10 series × 4 parallel configuration.**

**(a) Fill Factor:**

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \frac{17.5 \times 7.2}{22 \times 8} = \frac{126}{176} = 0.716$$

**(b) Module Efficiency:**

$$P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m = 17.5 \times 7.2 = 126 \, \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{126}{1000 \times 0.5} = \frac{126}{500} = 0.252 = 25.2\%$$

**(c) Array Power (assuming no mismatch losses):**

$$P_{array} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{module} = 10 \times 4 \times 126 = 5040 \, \text{W} = 5.04 \, \text{kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — From Cell to Array

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cellA["SOLAR CELL\nSi PN Junction\n0.5 to 0.7 V, 3 to 8 A"] --> modA["PV MODULE\n36 or 72 cells in series\n18 V or 36 V, 50 to 400 W"]
    cellB["SOLAR CELL\nSi PN Junction\n0.5 to 0.7 V, 3 to 8 A"] --> modA
    cellC["SOLAR CELL\nSi PN Junction\n0.5 to 0.7 V, 3 to 8 A"] --> modA
    modA --> strA["PV STRING\nModules in series\nHigher DC voltage"]
    modB["PV MODULE\n36 or 72 cells in series\n18 V or 36 V, 50 to 400 W"] --> strA
    modC["PV MODULE\n36 or 72 cells in series\n18 V or 36 V, 50 to 400 W"] --> strA
    strA --> arrA["PV ARRAY\nStrings in series and parallel\nkW to MW scale output"]
    strB["PV STRING\nModules in series\nHigher DC voltage"] --> arrA
    strC["PV STRING\nModules in series\nHigher DC voltage"] --> arrA
    arrA --> load["DC LOAD OR\nGRID TIE INVERTER\nvia MPPT controller"]
```

## 4.2 Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["IL\nPhoto-current source"] --> nodeA((A))
    diode["DIODE\nSaturation current I0\nIdeality factor n"] --- nodeA
    nodeA --- nodeB((B))
    rsh["RSH\nShunt resistance"] --- nodeA
    nodeA --- nodeB
    rs["RS\nSeries resistance"] --- nodeB
    nodeB --> vout["V = Terminal voltage\nacross the cell"]
    nodeA --> vout
    nodeA --> iload["I = Load current"]
```

## 4.3 Interconnection — Series vs Parallel Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["SERIES STRING"]
        s1a["Module 1\n+ V"] --> s1b["Module 2\n+ V"] --> s1c["Module Ns\n+ V"]
    end
    subgraph S2["PARALLEL BANK"]
        p1a["String 1\n+ I"] --> comb1((Combiner Node))
        p2a["String 2\n+ I"] --> comb1
        p3a["String Np\n+ I"] --> comb1
    end
    S1 --> S2
    comb1 --> out["ARRAY TERMINALS\nV array = Ns x Vmodule\nI array = Np x Imodule"]
```

## 4.4 PV System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sun["SUN\nG = 1000 W per m square STC"] --> pvarr["PV ARRAY\nNs series x Np parallel\nOutputs DC at MPP"]
    pvarr --> jb["JUNCTION BOX\nBypass and blocking diodes\nFuses, SPD"]
    jb --> mppt["MPPT CHARGE CONTROLLER OR\nINVERTER FRONT END\nTracks Vm and Im"]
    mppt --> bess["BATTERY BANK\nOR DC BUS\nStores energy for off grid"]
    mppt --> inv["INVERTER\nDC to AC 50 Hz\n230 V single phase\n415 V three phase"]
    inv --> load["AC LOAD\nOR UTILITY GRID\nNet metering point"]
    bess --> mppt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In KTU theory questions, examiners often draw a PV system block and ask the student to label each block. Memorise the order: **PV Array → Junction Box (with bypass diodes) → MPPT/Inverter → Load/Grid**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Fill Factor and state its typical range for a silicon PV cell. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell is defined as the ratio of the **maximum obtainable power** to the product of the open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$) and short-circuit current ($I_{SC}$). It is given by:

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$

Mathematically, it represents the **"squareness"** of the I–V characteristic curve of the cell. A perfectly rectangular I–V curve would have FF = 1, while a poor cell (with high series resistance or low shunt resistance) will have a much smaller FF.

**Typical Range for Crystalline Silicon:** $FF \approx 0.65$ to $0.82$ (often cited as ~0.7 to 0.8 for commercial modules).

> [3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for the typical value range.]

---

### Q2. Distinguish between a PV Module and a PV Array. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PV Module | PV Array |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | An encapsulated, weather-proof panel containing series-connected cells | A collection of several modules interconnected in series and/or parallel |
| **Typical Output** | 50 W to 400 W (single unit) | kW to MW scale |
| **Voltage** | 12 V / 24 V / 48 V DC | Hundreds to thousands of volts DC |
| **Packaging** | Factory-sealed unit | Field-assembled |
| **Usage** | Stand-alone (with battery) | Utility, large commercial, or grid-tied |

> [3 marks: 1 for module definition + feature, 1 for array definition + feature, 1 for the difference.]

---

## 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. A(a)** With a neat sketch of the single-diode equivalent circuit, derive the **characteristic I–V equation** of a PV cell. Explain the role of $R_S$ and $R_{SH}$. [7 marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**Q. A(b)** A PV module has $V_{OC} = 21 \, \text{V}$, $I_{SC} = 7.5 \, \text{A}$, $V_m = 17 \, \text{V}$, $I_m = 6.8 \, \text{A}$, and active area $0.45 \, \text{m}^2$. Calculate (i) Fill Factor, (ii) Module efficiency at STC, (iii) Array power if **12 such modules are connected in series** to feed a battery bank. [7 marks]

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q. B(a)** Explain in detail the **effect of series and parallel interconnection** of solar cells on module voltage and current. Why is it essential to use **bypass and blocking diodes** in a PV array? [7 marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. B(b)** Discuss the **effect of cell temperature and solar irradiance** on PV module performance. Show mathematically how $V_{OC}$ and $I_{SC}$ change with temperature and irradiance. [7 marks]

---

## ✅ Model Solutions

### 🔹 Q. A(a) — Single-Diode Model Derivation

**Circuit elements:**
- **Current source $I_L$** — represents light-generated carriers.
- **Diode D** — represents the p–n junction with reverse saturation current $I_0$ and ideality factor $a$.
- **Series resistance $R_S$** — ohmic losses in metal contacts, finger busbars, and the thin emitter layer.
- **Shunt resistance $R_{SH}$** — leakage current paths near cell edges and crystal defects.

**Applying KCL at the output node:**

The total current delivered to the external load $I$ equals the photo-current $I_L$ minus the diode current $I_D$ minus the shunt current $I_{SH}$:

$$I = I_L - I_D - I_{SH}$$

The diode current (Shockley equation):

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_S}{a}\right) - 1 \right]$$

The shunt current (Ohm's law across $R_{SH}$):

$$I_{SH} = \frac{V + I R_S}{R_{SH}}$$

**Substituting:**

$$\boxed{I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_S}{a}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_S}{R_{SH}}}$$

**Role of $R_S$:** A high $R_S$ tilts the I–V curve, reducing FF and $P_{max}$, particularly near the voltage axis. Caused by poor front-side metallisation or back-contact resistance.

**Role of $R_{SH}$:** A low $R_{SH}$ provides a leakage path that drags the I–V curve down near the short-circuit region, reducing $I_{SC}$ and FF. Caused by manufacturing defects and edge shunts.

> [7 marks: 2 for circuit sketch description, 3 for derivation, 2 for roles of $R_S$ and $R_{SH}$.]

---

### 🔹 Q. A(b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Fill Factor:**

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \frac{17 \times 6.8}{21 \times 7.5} = \frac{115.6}{157.5} = 0.734$$

**(ii) Module Efficiency:**

$$P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m = 17 \times 6.8 = 115.6 \, \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{115.6}{1000 \times 0.45} = \frac{115.6}{450} = 0.2569 = 25.69\%$$

**(iii) Array Power (12 modules in series):**

In a **series connection**, currents are equal and voltages add:

$$V_{OC,array} = 12 \times 21 = 252 \, \text{V}$$

$$I_{SC,array} = 7.5 \, \text{A}$$

$$P_{array} = V_m \cdot I_m \cdot 12 = 115.6 \times 12 = 1387.2 \, \text{W} \approx 1.39 \, \text{kW}$$

> [7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 for the three sub-parts.]

---

### 🔹 Q. B(a) — Interconnection & Diodes

**Series connection:**
- Same current flows through all cells; voltages add.
- Used to obtain a higher DC link voltage.
- Disadvantage: a single shaded or faulty cell **forces the string current to bypass** through the cell, creating a "hot spot" that may destroy the cell.

**Parallel connection:**
- Same voltage across all strings; currents add.
- Used to obtain a higher output current.
- Disadvantage: a faulty module can drain current from the rest of the array, and a reverse-biased module can become a load.

**Bypass Diodes:**
- Connected in **anti-parallel** across groups of cells (typically 18–24 cells per diode).
- Under normal operation, the diode is reverse-biased and inactive.
- When a cell string is shaded, the bypass diode conducts, **shunting the current around** the shaded section and preventing hot-spot damage.

**Blocking Diodes:**
- Connected in **series** with the string output, with the cathode towards the positive terminal.
- Prevents **reverse current flow** from the battery or healthy strings into a faulty (shaded) string at night.
- Essential in off-grid battery-backed systems.

> [7 marks: 2 + 2 for series/parallel, 1.5 + 1.5 for bypass/blocking diodes.]

---

### 🔹 Q. B(b) — Temperature & Irradiance Effects

**Effect of Temperature:**

- **$V_{OC}$ decreases linearly** with temperature, at a rate of ~2.2 mV/°C per cell (negative temperature coefficient).
- **$I_{SC}$ increases slightly** with temperature (~0.06 %/°C, positive coefficient).
- **Net effect:** Power output **decreases** with rising cell temperature (~0.4–0.5 %/°C for silicon).

Mathematically:

$$V_{OC}(T) = V_{OC,STC} + K_V (T - 25)$$

$$I_{SC}(T) = I_{SC,STC} \left[ 1 + K_I (T - 25) \right]$$

**Effect of Irradiance:**

- **$I_{SC}$ is linearly proportional** to irradiance $G$ (W/m²):
  $$I_{SC}(G) = I_{SC,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{1000}$$
- **$V_{OC}$ changes logarithmically** with irradiance:
  $$V_{OC}(G) = V_{OC,STC} + \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{G}{1000}\right)$$
- **Power output** is approximately linearly proportional to $G$.

> [7 marks: 2 for temperature, 2 for irradiance, 2 for the formulae, 1 for concluding remark.]

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the unit of $V_{OC}$/cell:** Single cell = ~0.5–0.7 V; module = ~18–72 V. Do not confuse!
> - **Confusing $R_S$ and $R_{SH}$ roles:** A high $R_S$ tilts the I–V curve at the **voltage** knee; a low $R_{SH}$ tilts it at the **current** knee.
> - **Misapplying series/parallel laws:** Series adds **voltage**, parallel adds **current**. Mixing them is the #1 error in KTU numericals.
> - **Wrong temperature coefficient sign:** $V_{OC}$ **decreases** with temperature (negative coefficient).
> - **Omitting "STC" qualifier:** Always state $G = 1000$ W/m² and $T = 25$ °C when quoting PV performance.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 A **solar cell** is a p–n junction; **module = cells in series**; **array = modules in series + parallel**.
- 🔑 The single-diode model: $I = I_L - I_0[\exp((V+IR_S)/a) - 1] - (V+IR_S)/R_{SH}$.
- 🔑 **Fill Factor (FF)** = squareness of the I–V curve; ~0.7 for c-Si.
- 🔑 **Efficiency** $\eta = (V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF) / (G \cdot A)$.
- 🔑 **Series connection → voltages add**, **parallel connection → currents add**.
- 🔑 $V_{OC}$ **drops with temperature** (~2.2 mV/°C per cell); $I_{SC}$ **rises slightly**.
- 🔑 $I_{SC}$ is **linear in irradiance**; $V_{OC}$ changes **logarithmically**.
- 🔑 **Bypass diodes** protect against hot-spot damage from partial shading.
- 🔑 **Blocking diodes** prevent reverse current flow into shaded strings (off-grid systems).
- 🔑 STC = **1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5** — always mention when quoting PV data.
- 🔑 Thermal voltage $kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — handy for derivation problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Farms and Off-Grid Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Farms and Off-Grid Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solar Farm (Utility-Scale PV Power Plant):**
A Solar Farm is a large-scale, ground-mounted photovoltaic (PV) electricity generation facility, typically rated between **1 MW to several hundred MW**, that converts sunlight directly into electrical energy using interconnected arrays of PV modules and feeds this power into the medium/high-voltage distribution or transmission grid through power-conditioning and step-up transformer units.

**Off-Grid PV System (Stand-Alone PV System):**
An Off-Grid PV System is a self-contained solar photovoltaic installation that operates independently of the utility grid. It incorporates local energy storage (typically battery banks) and is designed to supply electrical loads in remote, isolated, or grid-inaccessible locations. It must autonomously balance generation, storage, and demand on a **24-hour diurnal cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Grid-Tied (On-Grid)** solar farms and **Off-Grid (Stand-Alone)** systems, identify all balance-of-system (BOS) components, and perform sizing calculations including array sizing, battery bank sizing, and capacity utilization factor (CUF) estimation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rainwater Harvesting Twin"

Think of a **Solar Farm exactly like a Rainwater Harvesting Tank**:
- The **sky/cloud (sunlight)** is the free, infinite source.
- The **rooftop catchment area (PV modules)** collects the resource.
- The **storage tank (battery bank)** holds the resource for use when it is not actively falling.
- The **pipes and taps (inverter, charge controller, wiring)** deliver it to your appliances.

In a **Solar Farm (On-Grid)**, you have a HUGE catchment area connected to the municipal water pipeline — the moment you produce more than you consume, it goes *back into the grid*, and when you need more, you draw from the grid. In an **Off-Grid System**, you have NO municipal pipeline — so your storage tank (battery) MUST be big enough to survive **several consecutive dry days (days of low sunlight, called "autonomy days")**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Peak Sun Hours (India average) | PSH | **4.5 to 6.0 kWh/m²/day** |
| Standard Test Conditions irradiance | G_STC | **1000 W/m²** |
| Module efficiency (commercial mono-Si) | η_mod | **18% to 22%** |
| Inverter efficiency | η_inv | **95% to 98%** |
| Battery round-trip efficiency | η_batt | **80% to 90%** |
| Hours in a year | h_year | **8760 hours** |
| State of Charge (typical operating range) | SOC | **20% to 100%** |
| Depth of Discharge (recommended limit) | DoD | **50% to 80%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** The solar constant (irradiance at the outer atmosphere) is **G_sc ≈ 1361 W/m²**. After atmospheric attenuation, the maximum terrestrial value at noon is roughly **1000 W/m²** (AM 1.5 spectrum), which is the reference Standard Test Condition (STC) used by every PV module manufacturer for rating.

### 1.4 Classification of PV Power Systems

```
PV POWER SYSTEMS
│
├── 1. GRID-CONNECTED (ON-GRID) ───► e.g., Solar Farms, Rooftop Net-Metered
│       ├── Without battery storage
│       └── With battery storage (hybrid)
│
├── 2. OFF-GRID (STAND-ALONE) ─────► e.g., Solar Farms AND Remote Electrification
│       ├── DC-coupled systems
│       └── AC-coupled systems
│
└── 3. HYBRID SYSTEMS ─────────────► Solar + Wind/Diesel/Biomass + Battery
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction:** A "Solar Farm" can technically be either **on-grid (utility-scale)** OR **off-grid (remote microgrid with diesel + battery + PV)**. The term "Solar Farm" primarily refers to SCALE (multi-MW), while "On/Off-Grid" refers to the OPERATING TOPOLOGY. Both must be clearly understood.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Solar Farm — Component Breakdown

A Solar Farm is NOT just solar panels. It is an orchestrated ecosystem of subsystems:

**A. PV Array Subsystem (Power Generation Core)**
- **PV Modules** — usually 400 Wp to 700 Wp mono-crystalline silicon
- **Module Mounting Structures (MMS)** — fixed-tilt or single-axis tracker
- **Foundation** — pile-driven or ballasted (depending on soil)
- **String formation** — series connection raises voltage, parallel raises current

**B. Power Conditioning Subsystem (PCU)**
- **String Inverters** OR **Central Inverters** — DC to AC conversion
- **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** — electronic algorithm to extract peak power under varying irradiance and temperature
- **Combiner Boxes** — DC string aggregation with DC fuses/SPDs

**C. Medium/High Voltage Subsystem**
- **Step-Up Transformer** (LV → 11 kV / 33 kV / 132 kV)
- **HT Switchgear** — VCBs, isolators
- **Pooling Substation** — collects multiple inverter outputs

**D. Balance of System (BOS)**
- DC and AC cables
- Earthing & Lightning protection
- SCADA / Data Acquisition System
- Roads, drainage, security fencing

**E. Grid Interface (On-Grid Farm)**
- Net meter / Bi-directional meter
- Grid synchronization equipment
- Protection relays (over/under voltage, frequency)

### 2.2 Off-Grid System — Additional Components

An off-grid system adds three critical blocks that a grid-tied farm does not need:

1. **Battery Bank** — stores excess daytime energy for night/cloudy use (lead-acid, lithium-ion, or flow batteries)
2. **Charge Controller** — protects batteries from overcharge/over-discharge (PWM or MPPT type)
3. **Backup Source (often)** — diesel generator set or wind turbine to extend autonomy

### 2.3 Why "Why & How" — The Operating Logic

**Why do we need MPPT?**
Because a PV module's V-I curve shifts with temperature and irradiance. Without an MPPT algorithm, the module would rarely operate at its true peak power point, losing **20% to 30%** of available energy.

**How does a Solar Farm handle night?**
On-Grid Solar Farm: simply stops generating; the grid takes over (no battery needed).
Off-Grid System: battery bank discharges to feed the load; if battery drains, load is shed (disconnected) or a backup generator starts.

**Why do off-grid systems need an "Autonomy Day" buffer?**
Because we cannot guarantee sunshine. To survive **3–5 consecutive cloudy days**, the battery bank must be sized large enough to feed the full daily load for that duration without any solar input.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the absolute high-yield set for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Memorize the table thoroughly.**

| # | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous PV Array Power | $P_{pv} = A \cdot \eta_{mod} \cdot G \cdot PR$ | Watts |
| 2 | Daily Energy from Array | $E_{day} = P_{pv,rated} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{sys}$ | kWh/day |
| 3 | Capacity Utilization Factor | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | Specific Yield | $Y_{f} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}}$ | kWh/kWp/year |
| 5 | Number of Modules (Series String) | $N_{s} = \dfrac{V_{system,DC}}{V_{module,mpp}}$ | count |
| 6 | Number of Parallel Strings | $N_{p} = \dfrac{I_{required}}{I_{module,mpp}}$ | count |
| 7 | Total Modules | $N_{tot} = N_{s} \times N_{p}$ | count |
| 8 | Battery Bank Capacity | $E_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{day} \times N_{aut}}{DoD \times \eta_{batt} \times \eta_{inv}}$ | kWh |
| 9 | Charge Controller Current Rating | $I_{cc} = 1.25 \times \dfrac{P_{array}}{V_{batt}}$ | Amps |
| 10 | Land Area Required | $A_{land} = \dfrac{P_{plant}}{G_{STC} \times \eta_{sys} \times PR \times d_{util}}$ | m² |
| 11 | Performance Ratio | $PR = \dfrac{Y_{f}}{Y_{r}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 12 | Array to Load Ratio | $ALR = \dfrac{E_{pv,day}}{E_{load,day}}$ | dimensionless (1.1–1.4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Legend for the table:**
> $A$ = Array area (m²), $G$ = Irradiance (W/m²), $PR$ = Performance Ratio (typically **0.75–0.85**), $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours, $\eta_{sys}$ = System efficiency, $V_{mpp}$ = Module max-power-point voltage, $DoD$ = Depth of Discharge (use **0.5** for lead-acid, **0.8** for lithium), $N_{aut}$ = Autonomy days, $d_{util}$ = Land utilization factor (typically **0.4–0.5** for fixed-tilt, considering row spacing).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Farm design engineers** use the CUF formula to forecast annual revenue and bid in government tenders (SECI, NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam).
- **Off-grid system integrators** (e.g., for telecom towers, rural electrification under Saubhagya scheme) use the battery sizing formula to ensure 3-day autonomy.
- **Land acquisition officers** use the land area formula to determine how many acres are needed for a 50 MW solar park.
- **Investors and banks** use Specific Yield and Performance Ratio to assess financial viability and recover loan repayments through PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) tariffs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Instantaneous PV Array Power

**Starting Principle:** A PV module converts incident solar radiation into electrical power. The electrical power produced is proportional to the product of three quantities:
1. The **incident irradiance** $G$ (energy per unit area per unit time)
2. The **module efficiency** $\eta_{mod}$ (fraction of light energy converted to electricity)
3. The **array area** $A$ (total light-collecting surface)

**Step 1:** Write the total incident solar power on the array.

$$
P_{incident} = A \cdot G
$$

**Step 2:** The PV module converts only a fraction $\eta_{mod}$ of this incident power into DC electricity at the cell level.

$$
P_{DC,cell} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{mod}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the system-level losses which are lumped into a single factor called the **Performance Ratio (PR)**. PR captures temperature losses, wiring losses, mismatch, soiling, inverter efficiency, and transformer losses.

$$
P_{DC,array} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{mod}
$$

$$
P_{AC,output} = P_{DC,array} \cdot PR
$$

**Step 4:** Combining steps 2 and 3 into a single expression:

$$
\boxed{P_{pv} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{mod} \cdot PR}
$$

**Real-world Example:** A 1 m² mono-Si module ($\eta_{mod} = 0.20$) at STC ($G = 1000$ W/m²) with PR = 0.80 produces:

$$
P_{pv} = 1 \times 1000 \times 0.20 \times 0.80 = 160 \text{ W}
$$

This is why a "200 Wp" module only ever delivers ~160 W of usable AC output in field conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Bank Sizing (Off-Grid)

**Starting Principle:** The battery must store enough energy to feed the load for $N_{aut}$ autonomy days, accounting for the fact that we can only use a fraction $DoD$ of its nominal capacity, and there are round-trip losses in both battery and inverter.

**Step 1:** Compute the daily load energy consumption in kWh.

$$
E_{day} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{load,i} \times t_{use,i}
$$

where $P_{load,i}$ is the wattage of the $i$-th appliance and $t_{use,i}$ is its daily usage in hours.

**Step 2:** Total energy required from battery over the autonomy period.

$$
E_{total} = E_{day} \times N_{aut}
$$

**Step 3:** The battery's *usable* energy is only $E_{batt} \times DoD$. To find the *nominal* (nameplate) battery capacity, we must divide by $DoD$.

$$
E_{batt,nom} = \dfrac{E_{day} \times N_{aut}}{DoD}
$$

**Step 4:** Now account for battery round-trip efficiency $\eta_{batt}$ (energy lost as heat during charge/discharge) and inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv}$ (DC-to-AC conversion loss).

$$
\boxed{E_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{day} \times N_{aut}}{DoD \times \eta_{batt} \times \eta_{inv}}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
- Daily load: $E_{day} = 10$ kWh
- Autonomy: $N_{aut} = 3$ days
- Depth of Discharge: $DoD = 0.5$ (lead-acid, conservative)
- Battery efficiency: $\eta_{batt} = 0.85$
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{10 \times 3}{0.5 \times 0.85 \times 0.95}
$$

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{30}{0.40375}
$$

$$
E_{batt} \approx 74.30 \text{ kWh}
$$

So a 48 V battery bank would need:

$$
\text{Capacity in Ah} = \dfrac{74{,}300 \text{ Wh}}{48 \text{ V}} \approx 1548 \text{ Ah}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)

**Starting Principle:** CUF expresses the ratio of actual energy produced over a year to the *theoretical* maximum if the plant ran at full rated capacity 24/7/365.

**Step 1:** The theoretical maximum annual energy from an installed capacity $P_{inst}$ is:

$$
E_{max} = P_{inst} \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2:** Actual annual energy is $E_{annual}$ (measured from the meter at grid interface).

**Step 3:** Take the ratio.

$$
\boxed{CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{inst} \times 8760}}
$$

**Example:** A 10 MW solar farm in Rajasthan generates 18.5 million kWh per year.

$$
CUF = \dfrac{18{,}500{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 8760} = \dfrac{18{,}500{,}000}{87{,}600{,}000} \approx 0.2112
$$

$$
CUF \approx 21.12\%
$$

This is **typical** for India. Kerala/Karnataka average CUF is 17–19%; Rajasthan/Gujarat is 20–24%.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Land Area for a Solar Farm

**Starting Principle:** The total land area must accommodate the PV modules *plus* the spacing between rows (to prevent inter-row shadowing) *plus* internal roads and inverter stations.

**Step 1:** Theoretical area if modules covered the ground perfectly:

$$
A_{theoretical} = \dfrac{P_{plant}}{G_{STC} \times \eta_{mod} \times PR}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by a **land utilization factor** $d_{util}$ (typically 1.8–2.5, i.e., you need 2 to 2.5× more land than the module footprint due to row spacing).

$$
\boxed{A_{land} = \dfrac{P_{plant}}{G_{STC} \times \eta_{mod} \times PR \times d_{util}}}
$$

**Example:** 50 MW plant, $\eta_{mod} = 0.20$, $PR = 0.80$, $d_{util} = 0.45$:

$$
A_{land} = \dfrac{50{,}000{,}000}{1000 \times 0.20 \times 0.80 \times 0.45}
$$

$$
A_{land} = \dfrac{50{,}000{,}000}{72} \approx 694{,}444 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{land} \approx 69.4 \text{ hectares} \approx 171.6 \text{ acres}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exams:** Approximately **1 acre per MW** for fixed-tilt plants in India, and **1.5 acres per MW** for single-axis tracker plants.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Grid-Connected Solar Farm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sunlight Irradiance G] --> A2[PV Array Field MWp]
    A2 --> A3[DC Combiner Boxes]
    A3 --> A4[Central Inverter Station with MPPT]
    A4 --> A5[AC LV Switchgear 415V]
    A5 --> A6[Step Up Transformer LV to HT]
    A6 --> A7[HT Switchgear 11kV or 33kV]
    A7 --> A8[Pooling Substation]
    A8 --> A9[Evacuation Line to Grid]
    A9 --> A10[Utility Grid Network]
    A4 -.SCADA Monitoring.-> A11[Control Room SCADA]
    A8 -.Revenue Meter.-> A12[Net Meter ABT Meter]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Off-Grid Stand-Alone PV System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Sunlight] --> B2[PV Array with Mounting Structure]
    B2 --> B3[MPPT Charge Controller]
    B3 --> B4[Battery Bank DC Bus 48V]
    B4 --> B5[Battery Management System BMS]
    B3 -.MPPT Control Signal.-> B3
    B4 --> B6[DC Bus Distribution]
    B6 --> B7[DC Loads LED Lights Fans]
    B6 --> B8[Off Grid Inverter DC to AC]
    B8 --> B9[AC Distribution Panel]
    B9 --> B10[AC Loads Pumps Refrigerator]
    B9 --> B11[Optional Diesel Generator Backup]
    B4 -.State of Charge Data.-> B12[System Controller and Display]
    B3 -.Telemetry.-> B12
    B8 -.Telemetry.-> B12
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow — On-Grid vs Off-Grid Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Start Site Selection] --> C2{Is Grid Available and Reliable?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3{Is Net Metering Allowed?}
    C2 -- No --> C4[Choose Off Grid System]
    C3 -- Yes --> C5[Design On Grid Solar Farm with PPA]
    C3 -- No --> C6[Design On Grid with Battery Hybrid]
    C4 --> C7[Calculate Daily Load and Autonomy Days]
    C7 --> C8[Size PV Array]
    C7 --> C9[Size Battery Bank]
    C8 --> C10[Select MPPT Charge Controller]
    C9 --> C11[Select Off Grid Inverter]
    C11 --> C12[Final System Integration]
    C10 --> C12
    C5 --> C13[Submit to DISCOM for Approval]
    C6 --> C13
    C13 --> C14[Commissioning and Synchronization]
    C12 --> C15[Commissioning and Performance Test]
```

### 4.4 Component Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[SOLAR FARM COMPLETE POWER PLANT] --> D2[PV Array Subsystem]
    D1 --> D3[Power Conditioning Subsystem]
    D1 --> D4[HT Evacuation Subsystem]
    D1 --> D5[Balance of System BOS]
    D1 --> D6[Control and Monitoring]
    D2 --> D2a[PV Modules]
    D2 --> D2b[Module Mounting Structure MMS]
    D2 --> D2c[Foundations and Civil Works]
    D3 --> D3a[String or Central Inverters]
    D3 --> D3b[DC and AC Combiner Boxes]
    D3 --> D3c[MPPT Charge Controller Off Grid Only]
    D3 --> D3d[Battery Bank Off Grid Only]
    D4 --> D4a[Step Up Transformer]
    D4 --> D4b[HT Panels and VCBs]
    D4 --> D4c[Pooling Substation]
    D5 --> D5a[Cables DC AC HT]
    D5 --> D5b[Earthing and Lightning Arrestors]
    D5 --> D5c[Roads Drainage Fencing]
    D6 --> D6a[SCADA System]
    D6 --> D6b[Weather Station Pyranometer]
    D6 --> D6c[ABT Meter Revenue Meter]
```

### 4.5 Daily Operating Profile — Off-Grid System (ASCII Schematic)

```
   ENERGY FLOW OVER 24 HOURS (Off-Grid 10 kWp System)

   Power (kW)
   10 |       ┌─────┐
    8 |      ╱│     │
    6 |     ╱ │     │
    4 |    ╱  │     │      ┌──Battery──┐
    2 |   ╱   │     │      │  Discharge │
    0 |──╱────┴─────┴──────┴───────────┴──── Time
      00   06   12   18   24

      [PV Generation]   [Battery Discharges to Load]
      Morning ramp-up   Sunset → Battery takes over
      Peak at noon      Until next sunrise
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Solar Farm. List any four major balance-of-system (BOS) components required for a 10 MW grid-connected solar PV plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Solar Farm is a large-scale, ground-mounted photovoltaic electricity generation facility, typically rated from 1 MW to several hundred MW, that feeds generated electricity into the medium or high-voltage utility grid.

**Four major BOS components:**
1. **Module Mounting Structure (MMS)** — fixed-tilt or single-axis tracker supporting the PV modules at optimal tilt.
2. **DC and AC Combiner Boxes** — aggregate output from multiple module strings and provide DC-side protection with fuses and surge protection devices.
3. **Step-up Transformer (LV → HT)** — raises inverter output voltage (415 V AC) to grid voltage (11 kV / 33 kV / 132 kV) for transmission.
4. **SCADA and Monitoring System** — provides real-time data on plant performance, irradiance, module temperature, and alarms.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 components = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between grid-connected and off-grid solar PV systems. Mention any two applications each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Grid-Connected | Off-Grid (Stand-Alone) |
|---|---|---|
| Battery storage | Not mandatory | Mandatory |
| Grid synchronization | Required | Not required |
| Power during outage | System shuts down (anti-islanding) | Continues to supply loads |
| Cost | Lower (no battery) | Higher (battery adds 30–40% cost) |

**Applications:**
- *Grid-connected:* Utility-scale solar farms, rooftop net-metered systems in urban buildings. (1 mark)
- *Off-grid:* Telecom tower power supply, remote village electrification under Saubhagya scheme. (1 mark)

*(Table = 1 mark, applications = 2 marks = 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Module 1 Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a 5 MW grid-connected solar PV farm. List and briefly explain the function of each subsystem. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 50 kWp solar farm is proposed at a site in Kerala receiving an average of 4.8 peak sun hours/day. The module efficiency is 19%, system performance ratio is 0.82, and the inverter efficiency is 96%. Calculate:
1. The daily AC energy generation in kWh.
2. The expected annual energy output in kWh.
3. The capacity utilization factor (CUF). **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Block diagram and explanation [7 marks]:**

Refer to **Section 4.1 Block Architecture — Grid-Connected Solar Farm** for the diagram.

**Subsystem explanations (7 × 1 mark each):**

1. **PV Array Subsystem** — converts sunlight into DC electricity. Made of series-connected modules forming strings; multiple strings form the array. Rating: 50 kWp at STC.

2. **DC Combiner Box** — aggregates DC output from multiple strings; contains DC fuses, surge protection device (SPD), and DC isolator for maintenance safety.

3. **String/Central Inverter with MPPT** — converts DC to 415 V three-phase AC using IGBT/MOSFET bridge. Embedded MPPT algorithm continuously adjusts operating point to harvest maximum available power (typically Perturb & Observe or Incremental Conductance method).

4. **AC Switchgear (LT Panel)** — houses AC circuit breakers, isolators, and protection relays for over-current, earth-fault, and short-circuit on the LV side.

5. **Step-Up Transformer** — converts 415 V AC to 11 kV (or 33 kV) AC using Dyn11 vector group to feed into the local substation.

6. **Pooling Substation / HT Panel** — aggregates power from multiple inverter-transformer units, with Vacuum Circuit Breakers (VCB), CTs, PTs, and an ABT (Availability Based Tariff) meter.

7. **SCADA System** — monitors real-time plant parameters: irradiance (via pyranometer), module temperature, string currents, inverter status, and feeds data to the control room and remote operators.

---

**(b) Numerical solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $P_{inst} = 50$ kWp
- $PSH = 4.8$ h/day
- $\eta_{mod} = 0.19$ (already embedded in $P_{inst}$ rating)
- $PR = 0.82$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$

**Note:** For a 50 kWp plant, the "50 kWp" already includes module efficiency at STC. So we use it directly with PSH and PR.

**Part 1 — Daily AC energy generation [2 marks]:**

$$
E_{day} = P_{inst} \times PSH \times PR \times \eta_{inv}
$$

$$
E_{day} = 50 \times 4.8 \times 0.82 \times 0.96
$$

$$
E_{day} = 50 \times 4.8 \times 0.7872
$$

$$
E_{day} = 240 \times 0.7872 = 188.93 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Part 2 — Annual energy output [2 marks]:**

$$
E_{annual} = E_{day} \times 365
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 188.93 \times 365 = 68{,}958 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 68{,}960 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 68.96 \text{ MWh/year}}
$$

**Part 3 — Capacity Utilization Factor [3 marks]:**

$$
CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{inst} \times 8760}
$$

$$
CUF = \dfrac{68{,}958}{50 \times 8760}
$$

$$
CUF = \dfrac{68{,}958}{438{,}000}
$$

$$
CUF = 0.1574
$$

$$
\boxed{CUF \approx 15.74\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A very common mistake students make in Part 1 is multiplying by module efficiency $\eta_{mod}$ AGAIN, even though the plant rating $P_{inst}$ is already the *output* rating under STC (which inherently accounts for module efficiency). Double-counting $\eta_{mod}$ will yield an answer that is **5× lower** than the correct one, costing you **3 marks**. Do not multiply $P_{inst}$ by $\eta_{mod}$ again.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Module 1 Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the architecture of an off-grid stand-alone PV system used for a remote village electrification. Discuss the role of each block in maintaining continuous power supply through day-night cycles. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A remote hamlet has a daily electrical load demand of 30 kWh. Design the battery bank (in kWh and Ah at 48 V) for 3 days of autonomy assuming DoD = 50%, battery round-trip efficiency = 85%, and inverter efficiency = 95%. Also calculate the charge controller current rating for a 10 kWp PV array. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Block diagram and explanation [7 marks]:**

Refer to **Section 4.2 Block Architecture — Off-Grid Stand-Alone PV System** for the diagram.

**Role of each block (7 × 1 mark each):**

1. **PV Array (10 kWp)** — converts sunlight to DC; sized so that daytime generation exceeds daily load (ALR = 1.2–1.4 to ensure full recharge of batteries).

2. **MPPT Charge Controller** — extracts peak power from PV; regulates battery charging; prevents overcharge; provides reverse-current protection at night.

3. **Battery Bank (48 V, lead-acid)** — stores excess daytime energy. During day, batteries are charged by PV. During night or cloudy weather, batteries discharge through the inverter to feed AC loads.

4. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — monitors State of Charge (SOC), temperature, cell balancing; prevents deep discharge (cuts off load below 50% DoD); prevents overcharge.

5. **Off-Grid Inverter (DC → AC)** — converts 48 V DC battery output to 230 V single-phase AC for household appliances; must be pure sine wave for sensitive electronics.

6. **AC Distribution Panel** — routes AC power to individual household feeders with MCBs/ELCBs for safety.

7. **Optional Diesel Genset Backup** — for extended cloudy periods (>3 days), an auto-start genset charges the battery bank and feeds critical loads, extending effective autonomy indefinitely.

**Day-Night Cycle Logic:** During 06:00–18:00, PV powers loads AND charges batteries. During 18:00–06:00, batteries discharge via inverter to power loads. If SOC < 20%, BMS sheds non-critical loads and signals the diesel genset to start.

---

**(b) Numerical solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $E_{day} = 30$ kWh
- $N_{aut} = 3$ days
- $DoD = 0.50$
- $\eta_{batt} = 0.85$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$
- $P_{array} = 10$ kWp
- $V_{batt} = 48$ V

**Part 1 — Battery Bank Capacity in kWh [3 marks]:**

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{day} \times N_{aut}}{DoD \times \eta_{batt} \times \eta_{inv}}
$$

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{30 \times 3}{0.50 \times 0.85 \times 0.95}
$$

$$
E_{batt} = \dfrac{90}{0.40375}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{batt} \approx 222.91 \text{ kWh}}
$$

**Part 2 — Battery Bank Capacity in Ah at 48 V [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{Capacity (Ah)} = \dfrac{E_{batt} \text{ (Wh)}}{V_{batt}}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity (Ah)} = \dfrac{222{,}910}{48}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Capacity} \approx 4644 \text{ Ah at 48 V}}
$$

This can be realized, for example, as 24 cells of 2 V each (48 V string) of ~4644 Ah capacity, or as 4 parallel strings of 1161 Ah each.

**Part 3 — Charge Controller Current Rating [2 marks]:**

$$
I_{cc} = 1.25 \times \dfrac{P_{array}}{V_{batt}}
$$

$$
I_{cc} = 1.25 \times \dfrac{10{,}000}{48}
$$

$$
I_{cc} = 1.25 \times 208.33
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{cc} \approx 260.42 \text{ A}}
$$

A standard 300 A MPPT charge controller would be selected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly forget the **1.25 safety factor** in the charge controller formula, costing 1 mark. Also, do not confuse $P_{array}$ (DC peak power) with AC load power — the controller sits on the DC side, so use $P_{array}$ in Watts and divide by battery DC voltage, NOT AC voltage. Using 230 V (AC) instead of 48 V (DC) will give an absurdly small current and lose 2 full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Solar Farms and Off-Grid Systems**

- [ ] A **Solar Farm** is a **utility-scale** (multi-MW) ground-mounted PV plant that feeds electricity into the **grid**.
- [ ] An **Off-Grid PV System** operates **without grid connection** and **must include battery storage** to survive nights and cloudy days.
- [ ] The seven major subsystems of a solar farm are: **PV Array, Inverter (PCU), Transformer, HT Switchgear, Pooling Substation, SCADA, and BOS (cables/earthing/civil)**.
- [ ] The **two extra blocks** in off-grid systems (compared to on-grid) are: **Battery Bank** and **Charge Controller (MPPT type)**.
- [ ] **Performance Ratio (PR)** captures ALL system losses (temperature, wiring, soiling, inverter, transformer); typical range is **0.75–0.85**.
- [ ] **CUF for India** averages **17%–22%** depending on location; Rajasthan high, Kerala moderate.
- [ ] **Specific Yield** $Y_f$ is the **kWh generated per kWp installed per year**; financially critical for PPA revenue forecasting.
- [ ] **Battery Sizing Formula** MUST include ALL of: $DoD$, $\eta_{batt}$, $\eta_{inv}$. Forgetting any one gives an undersized battery.
- [ ] **Autonomy Days ($N_{aut}$)** for off-grid: typically **3 days** for residential, **5–7 days** for critical loads (hospitals, telecom).
- [ ] **Charge Controller Sizing:** Always include the **1.25 safety factor** and use **battery DC voltage** (not AC voltage).
- [ ] **Land Requirement Rule of Thumb:** ~**1 acre per MW** (fixed-tilt), ~**1.5 acres per MW** (single-axis tracker).
- [ ] **Anti-islanding protection** is mandatory in on-grid systems — the inverter must shut down within **2 seconds** of grid failure for safety of line workers.
- [ ] **MPPT** (Maximum Power Point Tracking) recovers **20%–30%** of energy that would otherwise be lost.
- [ ] **DoD for lead-acid batteries** = **50%**; **DoD for lithium-ion** = **80%** (lithium allows deeper discharge without damage).
- [ ] **System Voltage (48 V DC)** is the most common battery bus voltage for small-to-medium off-grid systems; below 48 V, currents become large and cable losses prohibitive.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Module 1** requires you to be able to **sketch, label, and explain** a block diagram of either a grid-connected OR off-grid system for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Power Plant** (also called a Solar Generating Station or Solar Farm) is a large-scale, grid-connected or stand-alone engineered installation that converts solar radiant energy directly into electrical energy using either **Photovoltaic (PV) technology** or **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) technology**, with a typical installed capacity ranging from **1 MW to 500 MW** for utility-scale deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A Solar Power Plant is an integrated assembly of solar collectors/modules, power conditioning units (PCU), mounting structures, cabling, protection devices, and grid-interfacing infrastructure designed to deliver electrical power either to a local load (off-grid) or to the utility grid (grid-tied) by harnessing the Sun's radiation.

There are two principal technological pathways:

| Pathway | Working Principle | Output Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar Photovoltaic (SPV)** | Photonic excitation of electrons in semiconductor p-n junctions | DC → AC (via inverter) |
| **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** | Sunlight focused onto a working fluid to produce steam | AC (via turbine-generator) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rooftop Water Tank vs. The Municipal Dam"**
>
> Think of a **rooftop solar PV system** like a small overhead water tank collecting rainwater: it stores energy for one house, works when isolated, and only needs a pipe (inverter + wiring) to deliver it. A **utility-scale Solar Power Plant**, in contrast, is like a **large hydroelectric dam** — it requires massive land, engineered spillways (inverters), switchyards, evacuation infrastructure (transmission lines), and operators, but it delivers enormous quantities of energy consistently to thousands of consumers via the national grid.

### 1.3 Standard Solar Metrics (Must Memorise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Constant (Gsc)** = **1367 W/m²** — the solar irradiance received on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the top of Earth's atmosphere.
>
> **Peak Sun Hour (PSH)** = **1000 W/m² for 1 hour** = **1 kWh/m²/day** — the engineering reference used to size PV systems.
>
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** for PV modules: Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Cell Temperature = **25 °C**, Air Mass = **AM 1.5**

### 1.4 Classification of Solar Power Plants

Solar Power Plants are broadly classified into three families:

1. **Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Power Plants** — Direct conversion
   - *Grid-Connected (Utility-Scale)*
   - *Stand-Alone / Off-Grid*
   - *Hybrid (PV + Battery / PV + Diesel)*
2. **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plants** — Thermal conversion
   - *Parabolic Trough*
   - *Solar Tower (Central Receiver)*
   - *Solar Dish (Stirling Engine)*
   - *Linear Fresnel Reflector*
3. **Solar Thermal Power Plants** (for process heat / steam generation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

#### A. Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Power Plant
When photons of sunlight strike a semiconductor p-n junction (typically crystalline silicon), electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band if the photon energy $E = h\nu$ exceeds the bandgap energy $E_g$ (≈ **1.12 eV** for Si). The built-in electric field at the junction separates these photo-generated electron-hole pairs, producing a DC current proportional to the incident irradiance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?**
> A solar cell behaves like a **current source** in parallel with a diode. The photo-current $I_L$ is generated by incident light, while the diode allows a small reverse saturation current. The net I-V characteristic is what we plot on the curve tracer.

#### B. Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plant
CSP systems use **lenses or mirrors** (concentrators) to focus a large area of sunlight onto a small receiver. The concentrated heat raises the temperature of a **Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF)** — such as synthetic oil, molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃), or water/steam — which is then used to drive a Rankine or Brayton cycle turbine.

### 2.2 Core Components of a Grid-Connected SPV Power Plant

1. **Solar PV Modules / Arrays** — series-parallel assembly of cells to obtain desired V, I
2. **Module Mounting Structures (MMS)** — fixed-tilt, seasonal-tilt, or single-axis / dual-axis trackers
3. **String Combiner Box (SCB)** — aggregates DC output from multiple strings; includes fuses, surge protection
4. **DC Distribution Box / Array Junction Box**
5. **Power Conditioning Unit (PCU)** — MPPT charge controller + inverter + step-up transformer
6. **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** — perturb & observe / incremental conductance algorithms
7. **Step-up Transformer** (e.g., 0.4 kV → 11 kV / 33 kV)
8. **Switchyard / Evacuation Infrastructure** — circuit breakers, isolators, CT/PT, lightning arresters
9. **Monitoring & SCADA System** — string-level monitoring, weather station, data logging
10. **Balance of System (BoS)** — cables, earthing, lightning protection, civil works

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favourites. Memorise the variable names and units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | W/m² |
| 2 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Air Mass (zenith angle $\theta_z$) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega)$ | Zenith angle | degrees |
| 4 | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\dfrac{360}{365}(284+n)\right)$ | Solar declination angle | degrees |
| 5 | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G_T$ | Power output of PV array | W (or kW) |
| 6 | $E_{gen} = P_{rated} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{sys}$ | Daily energy generated | kWh/day |
| 7 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor | % |
| 8 | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Utilisation Factor (Indian MNRE definition) | % |
| 9 | $PR = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{reference}}$ | Performance Ratio | % |
| 10 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV}\cdot\eta_{inv}\cdot\eta_{cable}\cdot\eta_{trans}$ | System efficiency (product of sub-efficiencies) | % |
| 11 | $N_s = \dfrac{V_{DC,array}}{V_{module}}$ | Number of modules in series per string | — |
| 12 | $N_p = \dfrac{I_{DC,array}}{I_{module}}$ | Number of parallel strings | — |
| 13 | $\text{Specific Yield} = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}}$ | kWh generated per kWp installed | kWh/kWp |
| 14 | $\eta_{CSP} = \eta_{optical}\cdot\eta_{receiver}\cdot\eta_{thermal}\cdot\eta_{turbine}\cdot\eta_{gen}$ | Overall CSP plant efficiency | % |
| 15 | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelised Cost of Energy | ₹/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Critical Notes**
> - Solar constant $G_{sc} = 1367$ **W/m²** (sometimes quoted as **1366** W/m²).
> - STC reference: $G = 1000$ **W/m²**, $T_{cell} = 25$ °C, AM 1.5.
> - Typical $\eta_{PV}$ for crystalline Si: **15–22%**; thin-film CdTe: **10–12%**; multijunction: **>40%**.
> - Indian grid-connected plants typically achieve **CUF = 15–22%**.
> - Power-temperature coefficient of Si modules ≈ **−0.4 to −0.5 %/°C** above 25 °C.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Solar Power Plants are deployed worldwide with cumulative global installed capacity exceeding **1 TW** as of 2024. In India, under the **MNRE Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM)**, the target is **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030**. The largest operational single-site PV plant is **Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan (2.25 GW)**. CSP plants are predominantly located in sun-belt regions: Mojave Desert (USA), Atacama (Chile), and increasingly in Rajasthan (e.g., the 100 MW NTPC-Reliance CSP-Daggre project).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Output of a Solar PV Array

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the instantaneous electrical power output of a flat-plate PV array receiving global solar irradiance $G_T$ on its surface of area $A$.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of efficiency.**
The electrical conversion efficiency $\eta_{PV}$ is defined as the ratio of useful electrical power output $P_{out}$ to the incident solar power on the module's aperture:

$$\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{G_T \cdot A}$$

where $G_T \cdot A$ is the total solar power incident on the array surface.

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for output power.**

$$P_{out} = \eta_{PV} \cdot G_T \cdot A$$

This is the fundamental PV power equation. It tells us the power scales **linearly** with both the irradiance $G_T$ and the array area $A$, modulated by the conversion efficiency.

**Step 3 — Incorporate temperature derating.**
In practice, cell temperature $T_c$ rises above the STC reference of 25 °C, reducing efficiency. The temperature-corrected power is:

$$P_{out}(T_c) = P_{STC} \cdot \left[1 + \gamma \cdot (T_c - 25)\right]$$

where $\gamma$ is the temperature coefficient of power (negative, typically $-0.004$ /°C to $-0.005$ /°C).

**Step 4 — Express the cell temperature via NOCT model.**

$$T_c = T_a + \dfrac{NOCT - 20}{0.8} \cdot G_T$$

where $T_a$ is ambient temperature (°C), NOCT is the Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (typically 45 °C for Si modules), and $G_T$ is in kW/m².

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Daily Energy Generation from a PV Plant

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the daily energy output $E_{day}$ of a PV plant.

**Step 1 — Start with the rated power and Peak Sun Hours.**
If the plant rated capacity is $P_r$ (kWp) and the daily Peak Sun Hours are $PSH$ (h/day), then:

$$E_{day,\text{ideal}} = P_r \cdot PSH$$

**Step 2 — Apply system derating factor.**
Real plants lose energy due to inverter inefficiency, cable losses, transformer losses, soiling, mismatch, and downtime. The **Performance Ratio (PR)** accounts for all of these:

$$PR = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_r \cdot PSH}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for actual daily energy.**

$$E_{day} = PR \cdot P_r \cdot PSH$$

**Step 4 — Extend to annual energy.**

$$E_{annual} = P_r \cdot PSH_{\text{annual}} \cdot PR = P_r \cdot 365 \cdot \overline{PSH} \cdot PR$$

where $\overline{PSH}$ is the daily average Peak Sun Hour value for the site (typically **4.5–6.0 kWh/m²/day** in India).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 100 kWp grid-connected solar PV plant is installed in Kerala. The annual average Peak Sun Hour is 4.8 h/day, and the expected Performance Ratio is 0.78. Calculate:
1. The expected annual energy generation.
2. The Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF).
3. The specific yield.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual energy generation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= P_r \cdot 365 \cdot \overline{PSH} \cdot PR \\
&= 100 \text{ kWp} \times 365 \times 4.8 \text{ h/day} \times 0.78 \\
&= 100 \times 365 \times 4.8 \times 0.78 \\
&= 136{,}656 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Utilisation Factor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
CUF &= \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760} \\
&= \dfrac{136{,}656}{100 \times 8760} \\
&= \dfrac{136{,}656}{876{,}000} \\
&= 0.1560 = \mathbf{15.60\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Specific yield.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific Yield} &= \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}} \\
&= \dfrac{136{,}656 \text{ kWh}}{100 \text{ kWp}} \\
&= \mathbf{1366.56 \text{ kWh/kWp}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** This is consistent with Indian PV plant statistics — Kerala plants typically yield **1300–1450 kWh/kWp** annually with a CUF in the **14–18%** range due to monsoon cloud cover.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Capacity Factor vs. Capacity Utilisation Factor

Both metrics describe plant utilisation but differ subtly:

$$\begin{aligned}
CF &= \dfrac{\text{Energy actually generated over time } T}{P_{rated} \times T} \\
CUF &= \dfrac{\text{Energy generated during specified period (e.g., a year)}}{P_{installed} \times \text{hours in that period}}
\end{aligned}$$

In Indian regulatory practice (CERC / MNRE), **CUF** is used. **CF** is more common in international literature. For a plant with no forced outages and no curtailment, $CF \approx CUF$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SPV vs. CSP

| Parameter | Solar PV | CSP (Parabolic Trough / Tower) |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion | Photonic → DC → AC | Thermal → Mechanical → AC |
| Moving parts | None (except trackers) | Turbine, pumps |
| Efficiency | 15–22% (module); 12–18% (system) | 30–40% thermal; 15–25% net electrical |
| Storage | Battery / thermal | Molten salt (6–15 h storage) |
| Land requirement | ~5 acres/MW | ~6–10 acres/MW (with storage) |
| Dispatchability | Non-dispatchable (unless battery) | Dispatchable (with TES) |
| Water use | Negligible | Significant (cooling, cleaning) |
| CAPEX (2024) | ~₹4–5 Cr/MWp | ~₹15–25 Cr/MW |
| Best suited for | Distributed + utility scale | Utility scale with storage |

---

### 3.6 Tracking Gain Derivation

A single-axis tracker increases effective irradiance compared to a fixed-tilt surface. The tracking gain $G_{track}/G_{fixed}$ can be approximated as:

$$\text{Tracking Gain} \approx \dfrac{\int_{sunrise}^{sunset} G_{track}(\omega)\, d\omega}{\int_{sunrise}^{sunset} G_{fixed}(\omega)\, d\omega}$$

Typical observed gains in India:
- **Single-axis tracking:** **+15 to +25%** energy gain over fixed tilt
- **Dual-axis tracking:** **+25 to +35%** gain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat for KTU:** Tracking increases CAPEX, O&M complexity, and land-area requirement. The cost-benefit ratio must justify the gain — for ground-mounted plants in India, single-axis tracking is typically **breakeven-positive**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Grid-Connected Solar PV Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sunlight Irradiance G_T] --> B[PV Array - Modules in Series and Parallel]
    B --> C[String Combiner Box - DC Aggregation]
    C --> D[DC Distribution Box]
    D --> E[MPPT Charge Controller - DC DC]
    E --> F[Inverter - DC to AC Conversion]
    F --> G[AC Distribution Panel]
    G --> H[Step Up Transformer 0.4 kV to 11 kV or 33 kV]
    H --> I[Switchyard - Circuit Breaker CT PT LA]
    I --> J[Utility Grid or Load]
    F --> K[SCADA Monitoring System]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Data Logger and Weather Station]

    classDef source fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mon fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A source
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H elec
    class I,J grid
    class K,L mon
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plant (Parabolic Trough with Thermal Storage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOL[Sunlight] --> REF[Parabolic Trough Mirrors - Concentrator]
    REF --> REC[Receiver Tube with Heat Transfer Fluid]
    REC --> HX[Heat Exchanger - Steam Generation]
    HX --> TURB[Steam Turbine - Rankine Cycle]
    TURB --> GEN[Synchronous Generator]
    GEN --> XFMR[Step Up Transformer]
    XFMR --> GRID[Utility Grid]
    REC -.-> HOTS[Hot Molten Salt Tank - 565 °C]
    HOTS -.-> REC
    HOTS --> COLDS[Cold Molten Salt Tank - 290 °C]
    HX -.-> COLDS
    TURB --> COND[Condenser]
    COND --> PUMP[Feed Water Pump]
    PUMP --> HX

    classDef solar fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SOL solar
    class REF,REC,HX thermal
    class TURB,GEN,XFMR,GRID,COND,PUMP power
    class HOTS,COLDS storage
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Standalone (Off-Grid) PV System with Battery Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[PV Array] --> CC[MPPT Charge Controller]
    CC --> BAT[Battery Bank - Lead Acid or Li-ion]
    BAT --> INV[Standalone Inverter]
    INV --> LOAD[AC Load]
    BAT --> DCL[DC Load]
    CC --> MON[System Monitoring]

    classDef src fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S,MON src
    class BAT storage
    class CC,INV,LOAD,DCL pwr
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Solar Plant Performance Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IRR[Irradiance G_T] --> MOD[Module Efficiency]
    MOD --> INV[Inverter Efficiency]
    INV --> CAB[Cable Losses]
    CAB --> XFMR[Transformer Efficiency]
    XFMR --> SOIL[Soiling and Mismatch Losses]
    SOIL --> DOWNT[Downtime Losses]
    DOWNT --> OUT[Net AC Output to Grid]

    classDef step fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class IRR,MOD,INV,CAB,XFMR,SOIL,DOWNT step
    class OUT final
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Power Plant. List any four major components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Solar Photovoltaic Power Plant** is an installation that converts sunlight directly into electricity using semiconductor photovoltaic cells, arranged in modules and arrays, typically ranging from a few kWp (rooftop) to several hundred MWp (utility-scale).

**Four major components:**
1. **PV Modules / Array** — converts sunlight into DC electricity.
2. **Power Conditioning Unit (PCU)** — includes MPPT controller + inverter that converts DC to grid-synchronised AC.
3. **Module Mounting Structure (MMS)** — supports and orients the modules at the optimal tilt angle (≈ latitude).
4. **Step-up Transformer and Switchgear** — raises the inverter output voltage to the grid interconnect voltage.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) and Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SPV | CSP |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion principle | Photonic → DC | Solar thermal → Mechanical → AC |
| Uses concentration | No | Yes (mirrors/lenses) |
| Output | DC, then inverted to AC | AC (directly from turbine-generator) |
| Storage | Requires batteries | Inherent thermal storage (molten salt) |
| Dispatchability | Non-dispatchable (without battery) | Dispatchable |
| Best suited for | Distributed & utility scale | Large utility scale with TES |
| Example plant | Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan) | Ivanpah (USA), Gemasolar (Spain) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Module-level choice is provided.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the various components and working of a **grid-connected solar PV power plant**. Discuss the function of the **MPPT** in the system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **50 kWp solar PV plant** is installed at a site with an annual average Peak Sun Hour of **5.2 h/day**. The system has an overall Performance Ratio of **0.80**. Calculate:
1. The expected **annual energy generation** in kWh.
2. The **Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF)**.
3. The **specific yield** in kWh/kWp.
4. Comment on whether this performance is acceptable for a plant in Kerala. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working:**

A grid-connected solar PV power plant consists of the following components (refer to diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **PV Array:** Series-parallel connected modules producing DC power. Modules are mounted on **MMS** at a tilt angle ≈ latitude of the site (for Kerala, ≈ 10°).
2. **String Combiner Box (SCB):** Aggregates DC from multiple strings; contains string fuses and DC disconnect switches.
3. **MPPT Charge Controller:** Continuously extracts the **maximum available power** from the PV array by adjusting the operating voltage to the peak of the I-V curve. Algorithms used include **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**.
4. **Inverter:** Converts DC to AC synchronised with grid voltage and frequency (50 Hz in India).
5. **Step-up Transformer:** Raises voltage from inverter output (typically 0.4 kV) to distribution level (11 kV or 33 kV).
6. **Switchyard:** Houses circuit breakers, isolators, CTs/PTs, and lightning arresters for protection.
7. **SCADA & Monitoring:** Real-time monitoring of plant parameters (string-level current, voltage, irradiance, temperature).

**Function of MPPT:**
The MPPT is essential because the PV array's operating point shifts continuously with irradiance, temperature, and load. The P-V curve has a single peak (MPP), and the MPPT controller perturbs the operating voltage and observes the resulting power change to converge on this peak. Without MPPT, the system could lose **20–30%** of available energy.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $P_r = 50$ kWp, $\overline{PSH} = 5.2$ h/day, $PR = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Annual energy generation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= P_r \times 365 \times \overline{PSH} \times PR \\
&= 50 \times 365 \times 5.2 \times 0.80 \\
&= 75{,}920 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Utilisation Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CUF &= \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760} \\
&= \dfrac{75{,}920}{50 \times 8760} \\
&= \dfrac{75{,}920}{438{,}000} \\
&= 0.1733 = \mathbf{17.33\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Specific yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific Yield} &= \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}} = \dfrac{75{,}920}{50} = \mathbf{1518.4 \text{ kWh/kWp}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
A CUF of 17.33% is **slightly above the Indian average** for Kerala (typically 14–18%) but is **reasonable** given the PSH of 5.2 h/day. Specific yield of 1518 kWh/kWp is on the **higher side**, indicating a well-designed plant. Performance is **acceptable**. Monsoon months (June–August) reduce generation, while dry months (January–March) boost it.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the working of a **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plant** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With necessary equations, derive the expression for **instantaneous electrical power output of a flat-plate PV array**. A **1 MWp** PV plant has a **module efficiency of 18%** and total array area of **5,556 m²**. If the incident irradiance is **850 W/m²**, calculate the actual DC power output. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — CSP Plant with TES:**

A Concentrated Solar Power plant (parabolic trough type) works on the Rankine cycle using solar thermal energy.

**Working (refer to diagram in Section 4.2):**

1. **Concentrator:** Parabolic trough mirrors track the Sun and concentrate direct normal irradiance (DNI) by a factor of **70–80 suns** onto the receiver tube.
2. **Receiver / Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF):** Synthetic oil (e.g., Therminol VP-1) or molten salt flows through the receiver, absorbing concentrated heat up to **400 °C** (oil) or **565 °C** (salt).
3. **Heat Exchanger / Steam Generator:** HTF transfers heat to water, producing superheated steam.
4. **Turbine-Generator:** Steam drives a Rankine turbine coupled to a synchronous generator producing AC power.
5. **Condenser & Pump:** Steam is condensed and recycled.

**Role of Thermal Energy Storage (TES):**

TES uses **two-tank molten salt storage**:
- **Hot Tank (565 °C):** Stores surplus thermal energy during peak sunshine.
- **Cold Tank (290 °C):** Receives cooled salt after heat extraction.

By routing stored hot salt through the heat exchanger during cloudy periods or after sunset, the plant can **extend generation by 6–15 hours**, achieving **dispatchability** — a key advantage over PV. This allows CSP to provide **firm, schedulable power** rather than variable output.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical:**

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Efficiency definition:} \quad \eta_{PV} &= \dfrac{P_{out}}{G_T \cdot A} \\
\text{Rearranging:} \quad P_{out} &= \eta_{PV} \cdot G_T \cdot A
\end{aligned}$$

This is the instantaneous DC power output from a flat-plate PV array of area $A$ under incident irradiance $G_T$ and module conversion efficiency $\eta_{PV}$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $P_r = 1$ MWp, $\eta_{PV} = 0.18$, $A = 5{,}556$ m², $G_T = 850$ W/m²

**Step 1 — Verify the rated capacity at STC (1000 W/m²):**

$$P_{STC} = 0.18 \times 1000 \times 5556 = 1{,}000{,}080 \text{ W} \approx 1 \text{ MWp} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Actual DC output at 850 W/m²:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{actual} &= \eta_{PV} \cdot G_T \cdot A \\
&= 0.18 \times 850 \times 5556 \\
&= 849{,}966 \text{ W} \\
&\approx \mathbf{850 \text{ kW (DC)}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The plant produces **~85% of its rated capacity** when irradiance is 850 W/m² — confirming the **linear relationship** between irradiance and power output in the absence of temperature derating.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Performance Ratio (PR)** in energy calculations. Many students compute $E = P_r \times 365 \times PSH$ but forget to multiply by PR — this gives **inflated values** and **loses 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Capacity Factor (CF) with Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF).** Indian regulatory exams (CERC/MNRE) use CUF; international literature uses CF. State which one you are computing.
> 3. **Not specifying the tilt angle** for Kerala (≈ 10°–11°). Always mention site-specific tilt ≈ latitude.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the numerical answer (kWh/year vs. MW).
> 5. **Confusing $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²** with $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m². These are different — one is extraterrestrial, the other is the engineering STC reference.
> 6. **Skipping the MPPT explanation** in block-diagram questions — it is a frequent 2-mark sub-question.
> 7. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** showing power/signal flow direction — full mark loss on the diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Power Plants — One-Glance Summary**

- ✅ **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1367$ **W/m²**; STC reference irradiance = **1000 W/m²**.
- ✅ **Peak Sun Hour (PSH)** is the **engineering substitute** for actual variable irradiance over a day.
- ✅ **Two technology families:** SPV (direct, solid-state) and CSP (thermal, mechanical).
- ✅ **Annual energy** $E_{annual} = P_r \times 365 \times \overline{PSH} \times PR$.
- ✅ **CUF for Indian solar plants** typically lies in the range **15–22%**.
- ✅ **Performance Ratio (PR)** captures ALL system losses (inverter, cable, transformer, soiling, mismatch, downtime) and ideally falls in **0.75–0.85** for well-designed Indian plants.
- ✅ **Specific yield** of a healthy Indian plant: **1200–1600 kWh/kWp/year**.
- ✅ **MPPT** is mandatory in modern PV systems — efficiency boost of **20–30%** without it.
- ✅ **Tilt angle** for fixed-mount systems in India ≈ **site latitude** (Kerala ≈ 10°).
- ✅ **Single-axis tracking** gives **+15–25%** energy gain; dual-axis gives **+25–35%**.
- ✅ **CSP advantage:** Inherent **thermal storage** → **dispatchable power**.
- ✅ **CSP limitation:** Requires high **DNI > 2000 kWh/m²/year** — limits site selection to desert regions.
- ✅ **PV cell temperature coefficient** ≈ **−0.4 %/°C** above 25 °C — hot climate reduces output.
- ✅ **MNRE target** India: **500 GW non-fossil by 2030**.
- ✅ **Largest PV plant:** Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan (**2.25 GW**).
- ✅ **Typical PV module efficiencies:** mono-Si **20–22%**, poly-Si **15–18%**, CdTe thin-film **10–12%**, multijunction lab cells **>40%**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conversion Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Conversion Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Energy Conversion Systems — KTU Module 2 Deep Dive

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Conversion System (ECS)** in the context of non-conventional energy refers to a complete engineered assembly that captures energy from a naturally replenishing or non-fossil source and transforms it into a usable form of energy—typically **electrical, thermal, or mechanical**—using direct or indirect conversion pathways while complying with the principles of thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, and electrochemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Framework:** Energy conversion efficiency is the dimensionless ratio of useful energy output to the total energy input, bounded mathematically by $0 \le \eta \le 1$. For non-conventional systems, the standard reference metric is **$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$**.

The discipline is broadly bifurcated into:

| Class | Pathway | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Energy Conversion (DEC)** | One-step conversion without rotating prime-mover | Photovoltaic, Fuel Cell, MHD, Thermoelectric |
| **Indirect Energy Conversion (IEC)** | Multi-step via mechanical intermediate | Wind Turbine, Biomass Steam Plant, OTEC |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Energy Translator

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an Energy Conversion System as a **currency exchange booth at an international airport**. The "raw" renewable energy (solar photon, kinetic wind gust, chemical biomass) is the foreign currency. The "useful" electrical energy is the local currency you need. The converter is the teller. The **exchange rate** is the **conversion efficiency**. Just as a real teller charges a small commission (losses like heat, friction, and irreversibility), every ECS loses some energy to entropy — never violating the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used Throughout

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Solar constant (AM1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$
- **Standard PV cell bandgap (Si):** $E_g = 1.12 \ \text{eV}$
- **Faraday constant:** $F = 96485 \ \text{C/mol}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Conversion Systems

The energy conversion chain in any non-conventional system follows a canonical 4-stage architecture:

1. **Collection / Capture Stage** — Harvesting raw energy (e.g., photovoltaic panel, rotor blades).
2. **Conversion Stage** — Transforming to a more usable energy form.
3. **Conditioning Stage** — Power electronics, inverters, transformers, charge controllers.
4. **Delivery / Storage Stage** — Grid injection, battery storage, or direct load.

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Breakdown

### A. Direct Energy Conversion (DEC) Methods

**A1. Photovoltaic (PV) Conversion**
- Operates on the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Becquerel (1839).
- A photon with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ is absorbed by a semiconductor, creating an electron-hole pair.
- The built-in electric field at the p-n junction separates the pair, generating DC current.
- **Output equation of a PV cell:**

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where $I_L$ is light-generated current, $I_0$ is reverse saturation current, $q$ is electron charge, $n$ is ideality factor, $k$ is Boltzmann constant, $R_s$ is series resistance, and $R_{sh}$ is shunt resistance.

**A2. Fuel Cell**
- Converts chemical energy of a fuel (commonly H₂) directly into electricity via an electrochemical reaction.
- **Net cell reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$
- **Theoretical efficiency** (from Gibbs free energy):

$$\eta_{\text{fuel cell}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \dfrac{T \cdot \Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

**A3. Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) Generator**
- A conducting fluid (ionized gas/plasma) is passed through a magnetic field, generating an EMF (Faraday's Law applied to fluid flow).
- **Induced EMF per electrode pair:** $\varepsilon = B \cdot v \cdot d$
- where $B$ is magnetic flux density, $v$ is fluid velocity, $d$ is electrode separation.

**A4. Thermoelectric Generator (Peltier-Seebeck)**
- Based on the **Seebeck effect**: temperature gradient across dissimilar metals produces voltage.
- **Output voltage:** $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- where $\alpha$ is Seebeck coefficient (V/K).

### B. Indirect Energy Conversion (IEC) Methods

**B1. Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)**
- Kinetic energy of moving air is converted to mechanical energy by rotor blades, then to electrical energy by a generator.
- **Power available in wind:**

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3$$

where $\rho$ is air density, $A$ is swept area, $v$ is wind velocity.

**B2. Biomass Conversion**
- Three primary routes: **Combustion, Gasification, Anaerobic Digestion**.
- **Combustion reaction:** $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$
- **Calorific value (CV)** is the key metric in $\text{MJ/kg}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| System | Key Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| PV Cell | $I = I_L - I_0(e^{qV/nkT} - 1)$ | $I$ in A, $V$ in V, $T$ in K | $T = 298$ K (STC) |
| Solar Collector | $Q_u = A \cdot F_R \cdot [S - U_L(T_i - T_a)]$ | $Q_u$ in W, $U_L$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | $F_R$ is heat removal factor |
| Wind Power | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $v$ in m/s | Cut-in speed $v_{\text{ci}} \ge 3$ m/s |
| Wind Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{rotor}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$ | Dimensionless | **Betz Limit: $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$** |
| Fuel Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Dimensionless | $\eta_{\text{H}_2} \approx 83\%$ theoretical |
