| MHD Generator | $\varepsilon = B v d$ | $B$ in T, $v$ in m/s, $d$ in m | $T_{\text{plasma}} \ge 2000$ K for ionization |
| Thermoelectric | $V = \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ in V/K | $\alpha_{\text{Bi}_2\text{Te}_3} \approx 200 \ \mu\text{V/K}$ |
| OTEC (Carnot) | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Absolute temps in K | $\Delta T \ge 20°\text{C}$ required |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Domains:** Energy conversion systems form the technological backbone of:
> - Utility-scale **solar farms** (GW-class PV installations in Rajasthan, Bhadla).
> - **Offshore wind** (Hornsea, North Sea — 1.2 GW capacity).
> - **Green hydrogen** production via electrolysis coupled with renewables.
> - **Spacecraft power** (PV arrays on satellites use direct conversion).
> - **Standalone rural electrification** in Kerala (KSEB rooftop solar initiative).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Extraction from Wind (Betz's Law)

**Problem Statement:** Determine the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy that can be extracted from a free-flowing wind stream by an ideal wind turbine rotor.

**Step 1 — Write the kinetic energy of an air mass moving at velocity $v$ through cross-section $A$ per unit time:**

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A v) \cdot v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

This is the **upstream** kinetic power available in the undisturbed wind.

**Step 2 — Apply the actuator disc model.** Let $v_1$ = upstream wind velocity, $v_2$ = velocity at the rotor disc, $v_3$ = downstream (wake) velocity. By continuity and momentum conservation in the ideal case:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \dot{m}(v_1 - v_3) \\
F &= \rho A v_2 (v_1 - v_3) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance across the disc.** The thrust force $F$ times the disc velocity $v_2$ equals the power extracted by the rotor:

$$P_{\text{rotor}} = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A v_2^2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

**Step 4 — Use the kinematic relation** $v_2 = \dfrac{v_1 + v_3}{2}$ (from Euler's equation on an ideal stream-tube):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{rotor}} &= \rho A \left( \dfrac{v_1 + v_3}{2} \right)^2 (v_1 - v_3) \\
&= \dfrac{\rho A}{4} (v_1^2 - v_3^2)(v_1 + v_3) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Form the power coefficient** $C_p$ by dividing by $P_{\text{wind}}$:

$$C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{rotor}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\rho A}{4}(v_1^2 - v_3^2)(v_1 + v_3)}{\dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$

**Step 6 — Define the velocity ratio** $b = \dfrac{v_3}{v_1}$ (induction factor) and simplify:

$$C_p = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - b^2)(1 + b)$$

**Step 7 — Maximize $C_p$ by differentiating with respect to $b$ and setting to zero:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC_p}{db} &= \dfrac{1}{2}[-2b(1+b) + (1-b^2)] = 0 \\
-2b - 2b^2 + 1 - b^2 &= 0 \\
1 - 2b - 3b^2 &= 0 \\
3b^2 + 2b - 1 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Solve the quadratic** $3b^2 + 2b - 1 = 0$:

$$b = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}}{6} = \dfrac{-2 \pm 4}{6}$$

Taking the positive root: $b = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

**Step 9 — Substitute $b = 1/3$ back into $C_p$:**

$$C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{8}{9} \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (Betz's Law):** The maximum theoretical efficiency of any wind turbine, regardless of design, is **$C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 59.3\%$**. Real-world turbines achieve $C_p$ values of 0.35–0.45 due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses.

## 3.2 Derivation: PV Cell Fill Factor and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define Fill Factor (FF):**

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

where $V_{mp}, I_{mp}$ are voltage and current at the maximum power point (MPP), $V_{oc}$ is open-circuit voltage, $I_{sc}$ is short-circuit current.

**Step 2 — Express cell efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (c-Si solar cell, STC):**
Given $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $FF = 0.78$, $G = 1000$ W/m², $A_{\text{cell}} = 0.0156$ m² (6 inch):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= 0.65 \times 3.5 \times 0.78 \\
&= 1.7745 \ \text{W} \\
\eta_{\text{cell}} &= \dfrac{1.7745}{1000 \times 0.0156} \\
&= \dfrac{1.7745}{15.6} \\
&\approx 0.1137 = 11.37\%
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Major ECS

| Conversion System | Theoretical $\eta_{\max}$ | Practical $\eta$ | Primary Loss Mechanism | Maturity (TRL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon PV Cell | 33% (Shockley-Queisser) | 18–24% | Thermalization + recombination | 9 (Commercial) |
| Wind Turbine | 59.3% (Betz) | 35–45% | Aerodynamic drag, gearbox | 9 (Commercial) |
| PEM Fuel Cell | 83% (H₂) | 50–60% | Activation + ohmic polarization | 8 (Deployed) |
| MHD Generator | ~60% | 10–20% | Plasma heat loss, electrode wear | 4 (Research) |
| Thermoelectric | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_H} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1+ZT}-1}{\sqrt{1+ZT}+T_C/T_H}$ | 5–8% | Conduction losses | 6 (Niche) |
| Biomass Combustion | 60% (Carnot limit) | 20–35% | Moisture, incomplete combustion | 9 (Commercial) |
| OTEC | 6–8% (real $\Delta T$) | 3–4% | Heat exchanger losses | 6 (Pilot) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Direct Energy Conversion (DEC). List any four examples of DEC systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Direct Energy Conversion (DEC) is the process of converting one form of energy directly into electrical energy without the intermediate step of mechanical rotation or a thermal cycle.

**Four examples of DEC systems:**
1. **Photovoltaic (PV) cell** – converts solar photon energy directly into DC electricity via the photovoltaic effect.
2. **Fuel cell** – converts chemical energy of fuel (typically hydrogen) directly into electricity through electrochemical reactions.
3. **Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) generator** – converts thermal energy of an ionized conducting gas into electricity via Faraday's law in a moving fluid.
4. **Thermoelectric generator** – converts a temperature gradient directly into voltage via the Seebeck effect.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and explain Betz's Law for wind energy conversion.**

**Model Answer:**
Betz's Law states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3%) of the kinetic energy** present in a free-flowing wind stream.

$$C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

The remaining ~40.7% of the kinetic energy must remain in the wake to keep the air mass moving away from the rotor. This is a direct consequence of conservation of mass and momentum applied to the actuator disc model. Practical wind turbines achieve $C_p$ values of 0.35–0.45 due to additional mechanical and electrical losses.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Photovoltaic (PV) energy conversion system. Derive the I-V characteristic equation of an ideal solar cell.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A PV cell is essentially a p-n junction diode fabricated from a semiconductor material (commonly crystalline silicon). When photons of energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ strike the cell, they are absorbed in the depletion region or the quasi-neutral region, generating electron-hole pairs. The built-in electric field at the junction sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side. When an external load is connected, the separated charges flow as a direct current.

**Block Diagram:** (See SECTION 4.3 Mermaid Flow)

**Derivation of I-V Equation:**

Consider the equivalent circuit of a PV cell comprising:
- A current source $I_L$ (light-generated current)
- A diode in parallel (representing the p-n junction)
- A series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$

Applying KCL at the output node:

$$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$

The diode current follows the Shockley equation:

$$I_D = I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q V_D}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$$

Shunt current: $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$

Substituting:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

For an ideal cell ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$$

This is the standard one-diode model of a solar cell.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters under STC: $I_L = 3.2$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300$ K. Calculate (i) Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, and (ii) Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given values:** $I_L = 3.2$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300$ K, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Step 2 — Compute the thermal voltage** $V_T = \dfrac{nkT}{q}$:

$$V_T = \dfrac{1.3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \dfrac{5.382 \times 10^{-21}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0336 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{oc}$** (set $I = 0$):

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{V_{oc}/V_T} - 1 \right)$$

$$e^{V_{oc}/V_T} = 1 + \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} = 1 + \dfrac{3.2}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 1 + 2.133 \times 10^{9}$$

$$V_{oc} = V_T \cdot \ln(2.133 \times 10^{9}) = 0.0336 \times \ln(2.133 \times 10^{9})$$

$$\ln(2.133 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.133) + 9 \cdot \ln(10) = 0.757 + 20.723 = 21.48$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.0336 \times 21.48 = 0.7217 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $I_{sc}$** (set $V = 0$):

$$I_{sc} \approx I_L = 3.2 \ \text{A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to multiply by the ideality factor $n$ when computing the thermal voltage $V_T$. Some also incorrectly set $I_0$ as the diode saturation at the operating point rather than the reverse saturation current. Verify all constants carefully. Marks are also lost when the logarithm's argument is not unitless (always normalize by $I_0$).

---

### 📗 Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS). State and prove Betz's Law.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.4 Mermaid Flow)

**Working of WECS:** A wind energy conversion system consists of:
1. **Rotor blades** – aerodynamic surfaces that capture wind kinetic energy.
2. **Hub & Nacelle** – mechanical housing connecting blades to the drivetrain.
3. **Gearbox** – steps up the low rotor RPM to generator-compatible RPM.
4. **Generator (PMSG / DFIG / SCIG)** – converts mechanical rotation to electrical AC.
5. **Power converter (AC–DC–AC)** – conditions frequency and voltage.
6. **Yaw & Pitch controllers** – optimize the angle of attack to the wind direction.
7. **Transformer & Grid** – delivers power at transmission voltage levels.

**Proof of Betz's Law:** (See complete derivation in SECTION 3.1 of these notes — all 9 steps)

**Final Result:** $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (i.e., 59.3%)

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. Wind speed is 12 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, and the power coefficient is 0.40. Calculate (i) The total power in the wind, and (ii) The actual power output of the turbine.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the swept area** $A$:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 60^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3600}{4} = 900\pi = 2827.43 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total wind power:**

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 12^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wind}} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 1728 \\
&= 0.6125 \times 4,885,799 \\
&\approx 2,992,551 \ \text{W} \\
&\approx 2.99 \ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the actual turbine power output:**

$$P_{\text{out}} = C_p \times P_{\text{wind}} = 0.40 \times 2.99 \ \text{MW} = 1.197 \ \text{MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Common mistakes in WECS numerical problems: (1) Forgetting to convert cm² to m² or km/h to m/s, (2) Using $C_p$ greater than 16/27 which is physically impossible, (3) Confusing "swept area" with "blade area" (always use swept area $= \pi D^2 / 4$). Always show unit conversions explicitly — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for unit consistency.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Module 2 Energy Conversion Systems**

- ✅ **Betz Limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.593** is the universal wind energy ceiling — never exceed it in any answer.
- ✅ **PV cell I-V equation** has two limiting cases: $V = 0 \Rightarrow I = I_L$ (short-circuit) and $I = 0 \Rightarrow V = V_{oc}$ (open-circuit).
- ✅ **Fill Factor (FF)** must lie in range **0.7 – 0.85** for a healthy c-Si cell; lower FF implies high series resistance.
- ✅ **Fuel cell theoretical efficiency** depends on $\Delta G / \Delta H$ — for H₂ this is ~83%.
- ✅ **MHD generator** requires plasma temperature > 2000 K — it is the only DEC system that needs thermal ionization.
- ✅ **Thermoelectric figure of merit $ZT$** governs practical efficiency — higher ZT = better material (Bi₂Te₃ is industrial standard).
- ✅ **Wind power scales with $v^3$** — doubling wind speed gives 8× more power. This is why site selection matters.
- ✅ **DEC systems avoid moving parts** — hence they have higher reliability and lower maintenance than IEC systems.
- ✅ **Shockley-Queisser limit** (33%) bounds a single-junction silicon PV cell. Multi-junction cells push this to 46%.
- ✅ **OTEC requires $\Delta T \ge 20°C$** between warm surface and cold deep ocean water for viable operation.
- ✅ **Always state units in numerical problems** — KTU awards partial marks for unit correctness.
- ✅ **Diagrams are mandatory** for 7-mark sub-questions — a missing block diagram can cost 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Extraction Principles: Hot Water and Dry Steam Systems
Wind Energy: Wind Characteristics and Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy: Wind Characteristics and Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wind Energy — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Wind Energy** is the form of **kinetic energy** possessed by air masses in motion due to differential solar heating of the Earth's surface, which can be converted into useful mechanical work (via aerodynamic lift/drag on rotor blades) and subsequently into electrical energy through a coupled generator system.

**Wind Characteristics** refer to the statistical, physical, and meteorological properties of the wind resource at a given site — including wind speed distribution, wind direction, wind shear, turbulence intensity, air density, and energy content — which determine the **site suitability, machine sizing, and annual energy yield** of a wind energy conversion system (WECS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to define wind power available in a stream tube, derive the **Betz Limit**, explain the **Power Law** and **Logarithmic Law** for vertical wind profile, describe the **Weibull and Rayleigh** statistical distributions, and list the instruments used for wind measurement (anemometers, wind vanes, SODAR, LIDAR).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Wind as a Flowing River of Air"

Imagine standing in a **fast-flowing river**. The faster the water moves and the larger the cross-section of the stream, the more energy the river carries. Wind behaves identically — except the "river" is invisible, made of air molecules colliding with rotor blades.

- A **slow breeze** is like a thin trickle of water — little energy to extract.
- A **strong gust** is like a flood — enormous kinetic energy is delivered.
- A **larger rotor area** intercepts more "flow" — just like a wider net catches more fish in a current.

Crucially, because the power in wind depends on the **cube of wind speed** ($v^3$), even a modest 10% increase in wind speed delivers nearly **33% more power**. This cubic dependency is the single most important concept in wind energy engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants (ISA — International Standard Atmosphere at sea level, 15 °C):**
> - **Air density, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Swept area of rotor: $A = \pi R^2$ (m²), where $R$ = rotor radius

### 1.3 Key Wind Characteristics to Be Measured

| Characteristic | Symbol | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Wind speed | $v$ | Determines available power ($v^3$ dependence) |
| Wind direction | $\theta$ | Decides orientation/yaw of turbine |
| Air density | $\rho$ | Directly proportional to power output |
| Wind shear | $\alpha$ | Affects hub-height selection |
| Turbulence Intensity | $I$ | Governs fatigue loads on blades |
| Wind power density | $P/A$ | Site ranking metric (W/m²) |
| Capacity factor | $C_f$ | Indicates true plant utilization |
| Energy pattern factor | $E_p$ | Accounts for cubic vs. square averaging |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Foundation — Why Wind Carries Energy

Air is a fluid with mass. When it moves with velocity $v$, each unit mass carries **kinetic energy** equal to $\tfrac{1}{2}v^2$. The mass flow rate through a circular cross-section of area $A$ perpendicular to the flow is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

Multiplying the mass flow rate by the kinetic energy per unit mass yields the **power available in the wind stream tube**:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Magic Cube" Rule:** Because of the $v^3$ term, doubling wind speed increases available power by **8 times**, and tripling increases it by **27 times**. This is why even marginal site-finding improvements (a few percent higher average wind speed) translate into major economic gains.

### 2.2 The Betz Limit — Theoretical Maximum Extractable Power

A wind turbine **cannot** extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy. If it did, the air would have to stop completely behind the rotor — which would prevent any new air from entering! German physicist **Albert Betz (1919)** proved that the absolute maximum fraction of wind power that an **ideal, frictionless, disc-shaped actuator** can extract is:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; (59.26\%)$$

This is the famous **Betz Limit** or **Betz Coefficient**. Real turbines achieve $C_p \approx 0.35$ to $0.45$ due to blade profile losses, tip losses, drag, and mechanical inefficiencies.

### 2.3 Wind Speed Statistics — Weibull & Rayleigh Distributions

Wind is **intermittent and variable** — it never blows at a single constant speed. Engineers describe it statistically:

- **Weibull Distribution** (most general, two-parameter):
$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right]$$
where $k$ = **shape parameter** (typically 1.5–3.0) and $c$ = **scale parameter** (m/s, related to mean wind speed).

- **Rayleigh Distribution** (special case of Weibull with $k = 2$):
$$f(v) = \frac{2v}{c^2}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^2\right]$$
Used when only the mean wind speed $\bar{v}$ is known; here $c = \bar{v} \cdot \sqrt{2/\pi}$.

### 2.4 Vertical Wind Profile — Power Law and Logarithmic Law

Wind speed **increases with height** above ground because surface friction slows the air near the ground.

**Power Law (Empirical, widely used):**

$$\frac{v(z)}{v(z_{ref})} = \left(\frac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ = **wind shear exponent** (≈ 1/7 over open flat land; 0.10–0.40 typical).

**Logarithmic Law (Theoretical, from boundary-layer theory):**

$$v(z) = \frac{v_{*}}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

where $v_{*}$ = friction velocity, $\kappa \approx 0.4$ (von Kármán constant), $z_0$ = surface roughness length.

### 2.5 Wind Measurement Instruments

| Instrument | Function | Measured Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cup Anemometer** | Measures horizontal wind speed | $v$ (m/s) |
| **Propeller / Vane Anemometer** | Measures speed + direction | $v, \theta$ |
| **Wind Vane** | Indicates wind direction | $\theta$ (degrees) |
| **Sonic Anemometer** | Ultrasonic pulse-time measurement | 3-D wind vector |
| **SODAR** | Sound Detection And Ranging | Vertical wind profile |
| **LIDAR** | Light Detection And Ranging | Remote wind profiling |
| **Pyranometer** | Solar irradiance (for hybrid plants) | $G$ (W/m²) |
| **Barometer / Thermometer** | Site air density correction | $\rho$ |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Expression | Units | Key Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Wind power in stream | $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | W | $A \perp v$ |
| 2 | Rotor swept area | $A = \pi R^2$ | m² | $R$ = radius |
| 3 | Power coefficient | $C_p = P_{turbine}/P_{wind}$ | — | $0 \le C_p \le 16/27$ |
| 4 | Betz limit | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27$ | — | Frictionless, ideal disc |
| 5 | Tip-speed ratio | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | — | Optimal $\lambda^* \approx 7$–9 |
| 6 | Power law profile | $v(z) = v_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | m/s | $z > z_{ref}$ |
| 7 | Logarithmic profile | $v(z) = (v_*/\kappa)\ln(z/z_0)$ | m/s | Neutral atmosphere |
| 8 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = (k/c)(v/c)^{k-1}\exp[-(v/c)^k]$ | s/m | $k>0,\;c>0$ |
| 9 | Rayleigh PDF | $f(v) = (2v/c^2)\exp[-(v/c)^2]$ | s/m | $k=2$ case |
| 10 | Mean wind speed (Rayleigh) | $\bar{v} = c\sqrt{\pi/4}$ | m/s | Rayleigh only |
| 11 | Turbulence Intensity | $I = \sigma_v / \bar{v}$ | — | $\sigma_v$ = std. dev. |
| 12 | Capacity factor | $C_f = E_{actual}/(P_{rated}\cdot 8760)$ | — | $0 < C_f \le 1$ |
| 13 | Air density (ideal gas) | $\rho = P/(R_{air}\cdot T)$ | kg/m³ | $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K) |
| 14 | Wind power density | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ | W/m² | Site assessment |
| 15 | Energy pattern factor | $E_p = \langle v^3\rangle/\bar{v}^{\,3}$ | — | $\ge 1$ always |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Site Assessment & Micro-siting:** Weibull/Rayleigh fits to measured $v$ data predict annual energy production (AEP) before installing a single turbine.
- **Hub-height Optimization:** Power-law extrapolation enables correct tower-height selection (e.g., going from 80 m to 100 m hub can increase $v$ by ~6–10% → ~20–30% more energy).
- **Turbine Class Selection (IEC 61400-1):** Turbulence intensity $I$ decides whether a turbine qualifies as Class I, II, or III.
- **Hybrid System Design:** Wind + Solar hybrid plants use coincident $v$ and $G$ data for storage sizing.
- **Grid Integration:** Capacity factor $C_f$ is used by power-system planners to estimate firm/infirm capacity of wind farms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Power Available in a Wind Stream Tube

**Starting Principle:** Consider a circular control volume of cross-sectional area $A$ perpendicular to an undisturbed wind of velocity $v_1$. Air passes through per unit time with **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through area $A$:**

The volume of air crossing $A$ per second is $A \cdot v_1$ (in m³/s). Since each m³ contains mass $\rho$ kg, the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v_1$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass of the incoming air:**

$$KE_{per\;kg} = \frac{1}{2} v_1^2$$

**Step 3 — Total kinetic energy flux (power) entering the stream tube:**

This is obtained by multiplying the mass flow rate by the specific kinetic energy:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times \frac{1}{2}v_1^2 = \rho \, A \, v_1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} v_1^2$$

**Step 4 — Final form (Wind Power Equation):**

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, A\, v_1^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is the *starting point* for every wind-energy calculation. The cubic dependence on $v$ dominates all design decisions — site selection, rotor sizing, tower height, and economic feasibility.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Betz Limit (Maximum Power Extraction Coefficient)

**Setup:** Consider an ideal actuator disc that extracts energy from the wind. Let the upstream velocity be $v_1$, the velocity at the disc plane be $v_d$, and the downstream (wake) velocity be $v_2$. For maximum extraction, $v_d = (v_1 + v_2)/2$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the disc:**

The control volume has the same mass flow in and out:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A_d \, v_d = \rho \, A_1 \, v_1 = \rho \, A_2 \, v_2$$

**Step 2 — Thrust force on the disc (from momentum change):**

Using Newton's second law on the air parcel:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho \, A_d \, v_d (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 3 — Power extracted by the disc (work done per unit time):**

The power extracted equals the thrust multiplied by the disc velocity $v_d$:

$$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v_d = \rho \, A_d \, v_d^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the axial induction factor $a$:**

Define $a$ as the fractional drop in wind speed at the disc:

$$v_d = v_1(1 - a) \quad \text{and} \quad v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

Substituting into the power equation:

$$P_{extracted} = \rho \, A_d \, [v_1(1-a)]^2 \cdot [v_1 - v_1(1-2a)]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$P_{extracted} = \rho \, A_d \, v_1^3 (1-a)^2 \cdot 2a$$

**Step 6 — Power coefficient (ratio to upstream wind power):**

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\rho A_d v_1^3 \cdot 2a(1-a)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_d v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$:**

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Solving: $a = 1/3$ (the non-trivial root).

**Step 8 — Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into $C_p$:**

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; (59.26\%)}$$

**Step 9 — Check the wake velocity condition:**

$$v_2 = v_1(1-2a) = v_1\left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{v_1}{3}$$

So the wake air still moves forward at $v_1/3$ — confirming the disc could not possibly stop the air completely (that would mean infinite $C_p$, which violates energy conservation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** No wind turbine — no matter how perfectly designed — can ever convert more than **59.26%** of the kinetic energy in the wind into useful mechanical/electrical energy. Real machines operate at 35–45% due to drag, blade-tip vortices, gearbox losses, and generator losses.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Mean Wind Speed from Rayleigh Distribution

**Starting Point:** The Rayleigh PDF (special case of Weibull with $k=2$):

$$f(v) = \frac{2v}{c^2}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 1 — Definition of the expected (mean) value:**

$$\bar{v} = \int_{0}^{\infty} v \cdot f(v)\,dv = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2v^2}{c^2}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^2\right]dv$$

**Step 2 — Use the standard integral** $\int_0^\infty x^2 e^{-x^2}dx = \sqrt{\pi}/4$:

Let $u = v/c \Rightarrow dv = c\,du$:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{2}{c^2}\int_{0}^{\infty} c^2 u^2 e^{-u^2}\cdot c\,du = 2c\int_{0}^{\infty} u^2 e^{-u^2}du = 2c \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{v} = c\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{4}} = c \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862\,c}$$

**Step 3 — Inversion** (most often used in practice): if $\bar{v}$ is known, find $c$:

$$c = \frac{2\bar{v}}{\sqrt{\pi}} \approx 1.1284\,\bar{v}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Power Law Vertical Profile from Logarithmic Law

The logarithmic law gives the cleanest derivation of the effective shear exponent. By definition:

$$\alpha = \frac{d(\ln v)}{d(\ln z)}\bigg|_{z}$$

Starting from the logarithmic profile $v(z) = (v_*/\kappa)\ln(z/z_0)$:

$$\ln v = \ln(v_*/\kappa) + \ln\!\left[\ln(z/z_0)\right]$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\ln z$ (using $d(\ln z) = dz/z$):

$$\frac{d(\ln v)}{d(\ln z)} = \frac{z}{v}\cdot\frac{dv}{dz} = \frac{z}{v}\cdot\frac{v_*}{(\kappa z)} = \frac{v_*}{(\kappa v)}$$

For typical hub heights where $v/v_* \approx 5$–$10$ and $\kappa = 0.4$:

$$\alpha \approx \frac{1}{0.4 \cdot (5\text{ to }10)} \approx 0.20\text{ to }0.14 \approx \frac{1}{7}$$

This is the famous **"one-seventh power law"** ($\alpha \approx 0.143$) widely used for open-terrain wind-resource estimation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Bandwidths

| Parameter | Low-Wind Site | Good Site | Excellent Site |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Mean wind speed $\bar{v}$ (m/s) | 4 – 5 | 6 – 7 | 8 – 10+ |
| Weibull shape $k$ | 1.2 – 1.5 | 1.8 – 2.2 | 2.5 – 3.5 |
| Weibull scale $c$ (m/s) | 4.5 – 5.5 | 6.5 – 7.5 | 9 – 11 |
| Power density (W/m²) | 100 – 200 | 300 – 500 | 600 – 1000+ |
| Capacity factor $C_f$ | 0.10 – 0.20 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.40 – 0.55 |
| Shear exponent $\alpha$ (offshore) | 0.05 – 0.08 | 0.08 – 0.10 | 0.10 – 0.12 |
| Shear exponent $\alpha$ (onshore) | 0.20 – 0.25 | 0.18 – 0.22 | 0.14 – 0.18 |
| Turbulence intensity $I$ | > 0.20 | 0.15 – 0.20 | < 0.12 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Wind Energy Conversion & Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Mass with Velocity v] --> B[Swept Rotor Area A = pi R squared]
    B --> C[Actuator Disc Extracts Kinetic Energy]
    C --> D[Gearbox Increases RPM]
    D --> E[Generator Converts to Electrical Power]
    E --> F[Power Electronics and Grid Interface]
    F --> G[End User Load or Utility Grid]

    H[Cup Anemometer at Hub Height] --> I[Data Logger Sampling at 1 Hz]
    J[Wind Vane] --> I
    K[Barometer and Thermometer] --> I
    L[Sonic Anemometer for Turbulence] --> I
    M[SODAR or LIDAR for Vertical Profile] --> I
    I --> N[Statistical Analysis: Weibull and Rayleigh Fit]
    N --> O[Annual Energy Production Forecast in MWh per year]
    O --> P[Turbine Class Selection per IEC 61400]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic — Site Wind-Resource Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Collect 1 Year of Wind Data at Site] --> S2[Compute Mean Wind Speed and Standard Deviation]
    S2 --> S3{Fit Weibull Distribution}
    S3 --> S4[Determine Shape k and Scale c Parameters]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Wind Power Density in W per square metre]
    S5 --> S6{Power Density Threshold Met}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Reject Site as Unsuitable]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Check Turbulence Intensity below IEC Limit]
    S8 --> S9[Check Wind Shear for Hub Height]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Capacity Factor and AEP]
    S10 --> S11[Economic Feasibility and Final Site Approval]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Anemometer Data Acquisition & Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Cup Anemometer Sensor] --> Q2[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    Q2 --> Q3[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    Q3 --> Q4[Microprocessor Based Data Logger]
    Q4 --> Q5[Timestamp and Storage on SD Card]
    Q5 --> Q6[Statistical Post Processing Module]
    Q6 --> Q7[Weibull Fit and Power Density Output]
    Q6 --> Q8[Hourly and Monthly Mean Speed Reports]
    Q6 --> Q9[Wind Rose and Direction Frequency Plot]
    Q7 --> Q10[Decision Support for Turbine Sizing]
    Q8 --> Q10
    Q9 --> Q10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture?** The block diagrams above translate the abstract equations ($P=\tfrac{1}{2}\rho Av^3$, Betz limit, Weibull fit) into a tangible **measurement-to-decision pipeline** — exactly what KTU examiners expect students to sketch in 14-mark theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Wind Power Density". Why is it considered a superior metric compared to mean wind speed alone for site assessment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Wind power density is the wind power available per unit area of the stream tube perpendicular to the wind direction, expressed as $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ in **W/m²**.
- **Superiority over mean wind speed (2 marks):** Mean wind speed alone does not capture the cubic dependence on $v$ or the influence of air density. Two sites with identical $\bar{v}$ but different distributions (one with many strong gusts, one with steady moderate wind) can have very different energy yields. Wind power density integrates the cube of $v$ and the local air density, providing a single, physically meaningful ranking metric. KTU-approved site classification (poor/good/excellent) is based on $P/A$ bands, not on $\bar{v}$ alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three instruments used for wind measurement and state the specific quantity each measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Cup Anemometer (1 mark):** Measures horizontal wind speed in m/s via the rotation rate of hemispherical cups mounted on horizontal arms.
2. **Wind Vane (1 mark):** Measures wind direction in degrees (0°–360°) by aligning a finned tail with the prevailing wind.
3. **SODAR / Sonic Anemometer (1 mark):** Measures vertical wind profile up to several hundred metres using acoustic (SODAR) or ultrasonic pulse transit-time (Sonic) techniques.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the power available in a wind stream tube. Hence, with neat reasoning, obtain the Betz coefficient ($16/27$) using the actuator disc theory.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Derivation of wind power equation (3 marks):**
Consider a stream tube of cross-sectional area $A$ perpendicular to an undisturbed wind of velocity $v_1$. The mass flow rate of air through the tube is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_1 \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

Each kilogram of air carries kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}v_1^2$ (J/kg). The power in the wind — i.e., the rate at which kinetic energy is convected downstream — is:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$$

**Part 2 — Actuator disc setup and Betz derivation (4 marks):**
Consider an ideal disc of area $A_d$ that extracts energy from the wind. Let $v_1, v_d, v_2$ be the upstream, disc-plane, and wake velocities. Define the induction factor $a$ such that:

$$v_d = v_1(1-a), \quad v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$$

The thrust (force) on the disc equals the rate of momentum loss:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A_d v_d(v_1 - v_2) = 2\rho A_d v_1^2 a(1-a)$$

The mechanical power extracted equals thrust times the disc velocity:

$$P_{ext} = F \cdot v_d = 2\rho A_d v_1^3 a(1-a)^2$$

The power coefficient is:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{ext}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A_d v_1^3 a(1-a)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

(using $A_d \approx A$ for an ideal disc). Differentiate to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{3}$$

Substituting:

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \boxed{\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$

---

**(b) A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. At a site where the mean wind speed is 9 m/s and the air density is 1.20 kg/m³, calculate: (i) the swept area, (ii) the total wind power available, and (iii) the maximum extractable mechanical power per the Betz limit. Take $C_p$ of the actual machine as 0.40 and find the actual electrical output assuming an overall generator + gearbox efficiency of 90%.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 80 \text{ m}$, $R = 40 \text{ m}$, $\bar{v} = 9 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,\text{actual}} = 0.40$, $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$.

**(i) Swept area (1 mark):**
$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Total wind power available (2 marks):**
$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.20)(5026.55)(9)^3$$
$$P_{wind} = 0.6 \times 5026.55 \times 729 = 0.6 \times 3,664,355 \approx 2,198,613 \text{ W} \approx 2.20 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Betz-limited maximum (2 marks):**
$$P_{Betz} = C_{p,\max} \cdot P_{wind} = \frac{16}{27} \times 2,198,613 \approx 1,302,734 \text{ W} \approx 1.30 \text{ MW}$$

**Actual electrical output (2 marks):**
$$P_{electrical} = C_{p,\text{actual}} \cdot P_{wind} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} = 0.40 \times 2,198,613 \times 0.90$$
$$P_{electrical} = 0.36 \times 2,198,613 \approx 791,501 \text{ W} \approx 0.79 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **Forgetting the cube of $v$:** Many students write $v$ instead of $v^3$ in the power equation. This drops your answer by an order of magnitude → **−2 marks**.
> - **Confusing $C_{p,\max}$ with actual $C_p$:** Betz gives the **theoretical ceiling** for an *ideal* disc. Real machines use 0.35–0.45. Mixing the two = **−1 mark**.
> - **Units:** Swept area MUST be in m² (not cm² or mm²). Mismatch in units is a very common **−1 mark** deduction.
> - **Number of decimals:** Show intermediate steps rounded to 2 decimal places minimum; final answer to 3 significant figures.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) What is the Weibull distribution? Explain the significance of the shape parameter $k$ and scale parameter $c$ in describing wind-speed frequency at a given site. A site has $k=2.0$ and $c=8$ m/s. Compute the most probable wind speed and the probability that the wind speed exceeds 12 m/s.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition of Weibull distribution (2 marks):**
The Weibull probability density function is:

$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right], \quad v \ge 0$$

**Significance of parameters (2 marks):**
- **Shape parameter $k$:** Controls the *peakedness* / *skewness* of the distribution. Small $k$ (~1) means a broad, exponential-like spread (highly variable wind). Large $k$ (~3) means a narrow, almost symmetric distribution (steady wind).
- **Scale parameter $c$:** Has units of m/s and is closely tied to the mean wind speed. Larger $c$ shifts the entire distribution to higher wind speeds.

**Most probable wind speed** (mode) — set $df/dv = 0$:

$$v_{mp} = c\left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^{1/k} = 8\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/2} = 8 \times 0.7071 = 5.66 \text{ m/s}$$

**Probability that $v > 12$ m/s** (1 mark):

The cumulative distribution function is $F(v) = 1 - \exp[-(v/c)^k]$. Therefore:

$$P(v > 12) = 1 - F(12) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{12}{8}\right)^2\right] = \exp[-(1.5)^2] = e^{-2.25} \approx 0.1054 \text{ or } 10.54\%$$

**Weibull/Rayleigh check (1 mark):** With $k=2$, the Weibull reduces to the Rayleigh distribution, used when only $\bar{v}$ is known from raw data — confirming consistency.

---

**(b) Explain the Power Law and Logarithmic Law for vertical wind speed variation. A reference anemometer at 10 m height records 6 m/s. Estimate the wind speed at hub heights of 50 m and 80 m using the power law with $\alpha = 0.20$. If the same site has roughness length $z_0 = 0.05$ m and friction velocity $v_* = 0.6$ m/s, verify your 80 m result using the logarithmic law. Discuss which model is preferred for engineering design.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Power Law statement (1.5 marks):**
$$v(z) = v_{ref}\left(\frac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

**Logarithmic Law statement (1.5 marks):**
$$v(z) = \frac{v_*}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right), \quad \kappa = 0.4$$

**Power Law calculation at 50 m (1 mark):**
$$v(50) = 6 \left(\frac{50}{10}\right)^{0.20} = 6 \times 5^{0.20} = 6 \times 1.3797 = 8.28 \text{ m/s}$$

**Power Law at 80 m (1 mark):**
$$v(80) = 6 \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.20} = 6 \times 8^{0.20} = 6 \times 1.5157 = 9.09 \text{ m/s}$$

**Logarithmic Law verification at 80 m (1 mark):**
$$v(80) = \frac{0.6}{0.4}\ln\!\left(\frac{80}{0.05}\right) = 1.5 \times \ln(1600) = 1.5 \times 7.378 = 11.07 \text{ m/s}$$

**Discussion (1 mark):**
- The **Power Law** is empirical and simple to apply; widely used in wind-resource mapping (e.g., WAsP, OpenFOAM preprocessors). It is the **industry default** for turbine design when only $v_{ref}$ and $\alpha$ are known.
- The **Logarithmic Law** is theoretically grounded in Monin–Obukhov similarity theory and is more accurate for **neutral atmospheric conditions** but requires $z_0$ and $v_*$, which are harder to measure.
- For hub-height extrapolation in **engineering design**, the Power Law is preferred due to its simplicity; the Logarithmic Law is used in research and offshore assessments where atmospheric stability matters.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Wrong base of exponentiation:** Some students compute $(z_{ref}/z)^{\alpha}$ — the *inverse* — giving the wrong (smaller) wind speed at higher altitudes. This is a classic **−2 mark** error.
> - **Forgetting the von Kármán constant $\kappa = 0.4$:** Often substituted as 1.0 by mistake. Causes logarithmic-law value to be **under-estimated by 60%** → **−1 mark**.
> - **Mixing Weibull $c$ and $k$:** In part (a), $c$ is *scale* (with velocity units), $k$ is *shape* (dimensionless). Swapping them in the formula = **−1 mark**.
> - **Not stating "neutral atmosphere" for logarithmic law:** Required to justify its use. Omission = **−0.5 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wind Power Equation:** $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — memorize in both directions (formula and units).
- **Betz Limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926** — the absolute theoretical ceiling, derived from $a = 1/3$ actuator disc theory.
- **Weibull parameters:** $k$ = shape (1.5–3.0 typical), $c$ = scale (m/s); Rayleigh = Weibull with $k=2$.
- **Power Law:** $v(z) = v_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha \approx 1/7$ over flat open terrain.
- **Logarithmic Law:** $v(z) = (v_*/\kappa)\ln(z/z_0)$ — use $\kappa = 0.4$ (von Kármán constant).
- **Tip-Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$; optimal $\lambda^* \approx 7$–9 for modern three-blade turbines.
- **Cup Anemometer** measures speed; **Wind Vane** measures direction; **SODAR/LIDAR** profile the wind vertically.
- **Turbulence Intensity** $I = \sigma_v/\bar{v}$ — must be below IEC 61400-1 class limits.
- **Capacity Factor** $C_f = E_{actual}/(P_{rated}\cdot 8760)$; typical 0.25–0.45 for good sites.
- **Air density** at site: $\rho = P/(R_{air}\cdot T)$, with $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K); standard sea-level value = **1.225 kg/m³**.
- **Cubic dominance:** Doubling wind speed → 8× more power. This single fact drives *all* wind-energy economics.
- **Site ranking metric** is wind power density (W/m²), NOT mean wind speed alone.
- **Hub-height gain:** Going from 50 m to 80 m hub (with $\alpha = 0.20$) gives ~10% higher wind speed and ~33% more power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy Conversion Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy Conversion Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: WIND ENERGY CONVERSION SYSTEMS (WECS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated electromechanical system that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into useful mechanical work, which is then transformed into electrical energy through a generator coupled via a power train. The complete chain comprises **aerodynamic rotor → gear box → generator → grid/load** with associated control electronics.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Wind is a moving river of air. To harvest its power, we just need to throw an obstacle in its path that turns. A windmill blade is that obstacle."**

Imagine you are standing in a fast-flowing stream. The water has energy because it is moving. If you hold a paddle against the flow, the water pushes the paddle, doing work on it. Wind is the same — it is *invisible water* of much lower density. A wind turbine blade acts like the paddle; the faster the stream (wind speed), and the larger the area swept by the paddle (swept area of the rotor), the more energy you can extract.

The system is essentially a **four-stage energy transformer**:
$$\text{Wind (Kinetic)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rotor (Mechanical)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Generator (Electrical)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Grid / Battery}$$

### 1.2 Why Wind Energy Matters

| Parameter | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| Global theoretical potential | ~ **1.3 × 10¹⁴ kWh/year** |
| Technically extractable (class III+ sites) | ~ **5.3 × 10¹² kWh/year** |
| Air density (at sea level, 15°C) | ρ = **1.225 kg/m³** |
| World installed capacity (2024) | ~ **950 GW** |
| Capacity factor (typical onshore) | **25 % – 40 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight – Physical Constant:**
> The **standard air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** at 15 °C and 1 atm is used in all KTU textbook problems unless otherwise specified. Always carry this value in numerical substitution steps.

### 1.3 The Wind Resource – Origin and Nature

Wind originates from the **unequal heating of the Earth's surface by the Sun**, which creates pressure gradients. Air flows from high-pressure zones to low-pressure zones; the greater the gradient, the higher the wind speed. Since the Sun is the ultimate source, wind energy is an **indirect form of solar energy**.

Wind is characterized by three main properties:
1. **Speed (v)** — measured in **m/s** at a standard **hub height of 10 m** (or 50 m / 100 m for modern turbines).
2. **Direction** — measured in degrees from true North.
3. **Turbulence intensity** — fluctuating component that reduces efficiency and increases fatigue loads.

The kinetic energy of a mass `m` of air moving at speed `v` is:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Since `m = ρ A v` (mass per second = density × swept area × velocity), the **power available in the wind** sweeping a cross-section of area `A` is:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

This `v³` dependence is the most striking feature — **doubling the wind speed gives 8 times the power.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a WECS

A modern WECS consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Rotor / Aerodynamic Assembly** — blades, hub, pitch mechanism.
2. **Drive Train** — main shaft, gear box (step-up ratio typically 1:50 to 1:100).
3. **Generator** — induction (Type 1, 2) or synchronous with power electronics (Type 3, 4).
4. **Yaw Mechanism** — orients the nacelle into the wind.
5. **Tower** — supports the nacelle; taller tower = higher, faster, more laminar wind.
6. **Control Electronics & Power Converter** — for variable speed operation and grid interfacing.
7. **Auxiliary Systems** — hydraulics, anemometer, wind vane, safety brakes.

## 2.2 Principle of Energy Conversion

The wind rotor extracts a fraction of the kinetic energy of the incoming wind stream. Let:
- `v₁` = upstream (far-field) wind speed
- `v` = wind speed at the rotor plane
- `v₂` = downstream (wake) wind speed

If the rotor extracts the maximum possible energy, the wake speed is reduced to:
$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} = \frac{2}{3} v_1$$

The **mass flow rate** of air through the rotor disk is:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A v = \rho A \left( \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \right)$$

The **force (thrust)** on the rotor and the **power extracted** are:
$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A \left( \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \right) (v_1 - v_2)$$

$$P = F \cdot v_{\text{avg}} = \rho A \left( \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \right)^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU WECS numerical.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Power in undisturbed wind | W |
| 2 | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ | Rotor swept area | m² |
| 3 | $P_{\text{rotor}} = C_p \cdot P_{\text{wind}}$ | Mechanical power extracted | W |
| 4 | $C_p^{\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ | Betz coefficient (max) | — |
| 5 | $P_{\max} = \tfrac{8}{27}\rho A v_1^3$ | Maximum extractable power | W |
| 6 | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | Tip speed ratio | — |
| 7 | $E = P \cdot T \cdot \text{CF}$ | Annual energy | kWh |
| 8 | $\text{CF} = E_{\text{actual}} / E_{\text{rated}}$ | Capacity factor | — |
| 9 | $v_{\text{alt}} = v_{\text{ref}}(h/h_{\text{ref}})^{\alpha}$ | Power law for hub height | m/s |
| 10 | $v_{\text{alt}} = v_{\text{ref}} \dfrac{\ln(h/z_0)}{\ln(h_{\text{ref}}/z_0)}$ | Logarithmic law | m/s |
| 11 | $E = \int_{v_{\text{in}}}^{v_{\text{out}}} P(v)\, f(v)\, dv$ | Annual energy using Weibull | kWh |

Where:
- `ρ` = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at STP)
- `α` = power-law exponent, **1/7 ≈ 0.143** (open terrain)
- `z₀` = surface roughness length (typical 0.001 – 0.1 m)
- `D` = rotor diameter (m), `R = D/2`
- `ω` = rotor angular velocity (rad/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on vertical bars:** Whenever absolute value or conditional expressions are required (e.g. when a velocity must be `≥ 0`), use `\vert v \vert` — never the unescaped pipe symbol — to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Wind Energy Economics & Capacity Factor

The **capacity factor (CF)** is the ratio of actual annual energy output to the energy that would be produced if the turbine ran at rated power continuously for a year (8760 h):

$$\text{CF} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy in a Year}}{\text{Rated Power} \times 8760 \, \text{h}}$$

For onshore Indian sites (Muppandal, Jaisalmer, Kayathar), typical CF values are **20 % – 30 %**. Offshore sites can achieve **40 % – 50 %**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid-scale generation:** Modern utility turbines are 3–15 MW with rotor diameters of 120–240 m (e.g., Vestas V236-15.0 MW, Siemens Gamesa SG-14-222 DD).
- **Distributed generation:** Small turbines (1–100 kW) for remote homes, telecom towers, water pumping.
- **Hybrid systems:** WECS coupled with solar PV, diesel, or battery storage (Wind-Diesel hybrid at Rameswaram, Lakshadweep).
- **Desalination & hydrogen production:** Emerging applications where WECS powers RO or electrolyzers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in the Wind

**Given:** A cylindrical air column of cross-sectional area `A` (m²), moving at uniform velocity `v` (m/s), with density `ρ` (kg/m³).

**Step 1 — Mass of air passing per second:**
The volume passing per second = `A × v` (m³/s). Multiplying by density gives mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass:**
$$E_k / m = \frac{1}{2} v^2 \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Power = rate of kinetic energy flow:**
$$P = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2} = \rho A v \cdot \frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad [\text{W}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Because of the `v³` term, a turbine in a 10 m/s wind produces 8× the power of the same turbine in a 5 m/s wind. This is why **wind site selection prioritizes high mean wind speed** over all other parameters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Betz Limit (Maximum Power Extraction)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams** — practice it step-by-step.

**Setup (control volume approach):**
Consider an ideal rotor of swept area `A` placed in a uniform stream. Use the *stream tube* model of Rankine–Froude:

- `v₁` = wind velocity far upstream (undisturbed)
- `v` = wind velocity at the rotor plane
- `v₂` = wind velocity far downstream (in the wake)
- Pressure at far upstream = atmospheric `p_a`; same downstream (flow is unbounded)

**Step 1 — Mass conservation (steady, incompressible flow):**
The mass of air entering the stream tube per second must equal the mass leaving. Since the stream tube has cross-section `A` at the rotor, the mass flow rate is the same upstream, at, and downstream:
$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v = \rho A_2 v_2$$

(We define `A₁`, `A`, `A₂` as stream-tube areas; for a slender stream tube `A ≈ A₁ ≈ A₂` is *not* assumed — instead the mass flow uses the actual area at that section.)

**Step 2 — Euler's momentum theorem (force on the rotor):**
The thrust on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum:
$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v \, (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 3 — Bernoulli's equation (outside the rotor):**
Since the air outside the rotor disk has no friction and no work transfer, we can apply Bernoulli's equation separately from far upstream to the rotor plane, and from the rotor plane to far downstream:

Upstream of rotor:
$$p_a + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = p^+ + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$

Downstream of rotor:
$$p^- + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_a + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Here `p⁺` and `p⁻` are the static pressures just upstream and just downstream of the rotor disk. For an ideal rotor, the pressure jump is assumed uniform across the disk, so the average pressure difference is:
$$p^+ - p^- = \frac{F}{A} = \rho v (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4 — Add the two Bernoulli equations:**
$$(p^+ - p^-) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_1^2 - v_2^2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$$

Equating the two expressions for the pressure drop:
$$\rho v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$$

Dividing both sides by `ρ(v₁ − v₂)` (non-zero, otherwise no extraction):
$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

> This is the **fundamental kinematic relation** of an ideal turbine: the velocity at the rotor is the arithmetic mean of upstream and downstream velocities.

**Step 5 — Power extracted by the rotor:**
Power = Force × Velocity at the rotor:
$$P = F \cdot v = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2) \cdot v$$

Substitute `v = (v₁ + v₂)/2`:
$$P = \rho A \left( \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \right)^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 6 — Express power in terms of `v₁` and induction factor `a`:**
Define the **axial induction factor** `a` such that:
$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a), \quad v = v_1 (1 - a)$$

**Step 7 — Express power in terms of `v₁` and `a`:**
$$P = \rho A \left[ v_1 (1 - a) \right] \left[ v_1 (1 - a) \right] \left[ v_1 (1 - a) - v_1 (1 - 2a) \right]$$

Simplify:
$$P = \rho A v_1^3 (1 - a) (1 - a) \cdot a = \rho A v_1^3 \, a (1 - a)^2$$

**Step 8 — Power available in the wind (undisturbed):**
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$$

**Step 9 — Power coefficient:**
$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{\rho A v_1^3 \, a (1 - a)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4 a (1 - a)^2$$

**Step 10 — Maximize `C_p` with respect to `a`:**
$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1 - a)(-1) \right] = 4 (1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

Setting `(1 − 3a) = 0`:
$$a = \frac{1}{3}$$

The other root `a = 1` gives `C_p = 0` (no flow).

**Step 11 — Substitute back to get Betz limit:**
$$C_p^{\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_p^{\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(or 59.26 \%)}}$$

This is the **Betz limit** — no ideal rotor can convert more than **59.26 %** of the kinetic energy in the wind into mechanical work.

**Step 12 — Maximum extractable power:**
$$P_{\max} = C_p^{\max} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3 = \frac{8}{27} \rho A v_1^3$$

The corresponding downstream velocity is:
$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a) = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{v_1}{3}$$

Hence at optimum: `v₂ = v₁/3` and `v = 2v₁/3` (a third of the upstream kinetic energy is carried away in the wake, a third is extracted, and a third remains at the rotor plane).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you state the *physical meaning* of the Betz limit — *"No turbine, however ideal, can extract more than ~59.3 % of the kinetic energy in the wind, because the remaining energy is needed to push the air out the back of the rotor disk."*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tip Speed Ratio and Design Selection

The **tip speed ratio (TSR or λ)** is the ratio of blade tip speed to free-stream wind speed:
$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D N}{60\, v}$$

where `N` is rotational speed in rpm. Different turbine designs have characteristic optimum λ values:

| Turbine Type | Number of Blades | Optimum λ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Savonius (drag) | — | λ ≈ 1 | Low-speed pumps |
| American farm (multiblade) | 18–24 | λ ≈ 1 | Water pumping |
| Three-blade HAWT (modern) | 3 | λ ≈ 6 – 8 | Grid electricity |
| Two-blade HAWT | 2 | λ ≈ 4 – 6 | Lightweight machines |
| Darrieus (lift, VAWT) | 2–3 | λ ≈ 3 – 5 | Urban, low wind |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Power vs. Wind Speed

A modern variable-speed turbine has a **power curve** with three regions:

| Region | Wind speed | Output | Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cut-in (`v_in`) | 3 – 4 m/s | Ramping up | Pitch + torque control |
| Partial load | `v_in < v < v_rated` | `P = ½ ρ A C_p v³` | Optimizing `λ` for max `C_p` |
| Rated (`v = v_rated`) | 12 – 14 m/s | `P = P_rated` | Pitch limits `C_p` |
| Cut-out (`v > v_out`) | 25 m/s (typical) | Zero (shut down) | Full feather, brake |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse "cut-in" with "cut-out." Remember — **cut-in is the *lower* speed** (machine starts), and **cut-out is the *upper* speed** (machine shuts down to protect itself).

---

## 3.5 Wind Speed Extrapolation to Hub Height

Wind data are often measured at 10 m. For hub height `h`:
$$v(h) = v(h_{\text{ref}}) \cdot \left( \frac{h}{h_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{\alpha}$$

For a typical open-terrain site, `α = 1/7 ≈ 0.143`. For example, with `v(10 m) = 5 m/s` and a 50 m hub:
$$v(50) = 5 \cdot (50/10)^{1/7} = 5 \cdot 5^{0.143}$$

Compute `5^{0.143} = e^{0.143 \ln 5} = e^{0.143 × 1.6094} = e^{0.2301} ≈ 1.259`:
$$v(50) \approx 5 \times 1.259 = 6.30 \, \text{m/s}$$

A 26 % increase in wind speed yields a `1.26³ ≈ 2.0×` increase in available power — justifying tall towers.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Numerical (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A three-blade horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s, air density 1.225 kg/m³, generator efficiency 90 %, gear-box efficiency 95 %, and the power coefficient is 0.40. Find (a) the power in the wind, (b) the actual electrical power output, and (c) the annual energy generated assuming a capacity factor of 0.30.

**Solution:**

(a) Swept area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

Power in the wind:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.5 \times 12^3$$

Compute step by step:
- `0.5 × 1.225 = 0.6125`
- `0.6125 × 5026.5 = 3079.8`
- `12³ = 1728`
- `3079.8 × 1728 = 5,321,894 W ≈ 5.32 MW`

$$P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.32 \, \text{MW}$$

(b) Mechanical power at rotor shaft:
$$P_{\text{mech}} = C_p \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.40 \times 5.32 = 2.128 \, \text{MW}$$

Electrical output (after gear-box and generator efficiencies):
$$P_{\text{elec}} = P_{\text{mech}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gb}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} = 2.128 \times 0.95 \times 0.90$$

Compute:
- `2.128 × 0.95 = 2.0216`
- `2.0216 × 0.90 = 1.819 MW`

$$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} \approx 1.82 \, \text{MW}}$$

(c) Annual energy with CF = 0.30:
$$E = \text{Rated power} \times 8760 \times \text{CF} = 1.82 \times 8760 \times 0.30$$

- `1.82 × 8760 = 15,943 MWh`
- `15,943 × 0.30 = 4,783 MWh`

$$\boxed{E \approx 4.78 \times 10^3 \, \text{MWh/year} = 4.78 \, \text{GWh/year}}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Major Components of a WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ATMOSPHERIC WIND KINETIC ENERGY] --> B[AERODYNAMIC ROTOR BLADES AND HUB]
    B --> C[MAIN SHAFT LOW SPEED]
    C --> D[GEAR BOX STEP UP]
    D --> E[HIGH SPEED SHAFT]
    E --> F[GENERATOR ELECTRICAL]
    F --> G[POWER CONVERTER AND TRANSFORMER]
    G --> H[GRID OR LOCAL LOAD]
    F --> I[CONTROL SYSTEM PITCH AND YAW]
    I --> B
    I --> F
    J[ANEMOMETER AND WIND VANE] --> I
    K[TOWER AND FOUNDATION] -.-> B
```

**Reading guide:** Wind enters the rotor; mechanical power flows downward through the drive train to the generator; electrical power flows to the grid. The control loop (right side) reads wind sensors and adjusts pitch/yaw to maximize `C_p` and protect the machine.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STAGE 1 WIND] --> S2[STAGE 2 ROTOR MECHANICAL]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 GENERATOR ELECTRICAL]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 GRID OR STORAGE]
    L1[BETZ LIMIT 16 OVER 27] -.-> S2
    L2[GEARBOX AND GENERATOR EFFICIENCIES] -.-> S3
    L3[CAPACITY FACTOR 20 TO 40 PERCENT] -.-> S4
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Classification of Wind Turbines

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WT[WIND TURBINES] --> AXIS[AXIS OF ROTATION]
    AXIS --> HAWT[HORIZONTAL AXIS HAWT]
    AXIS --> VAWT[VERTICAL AXIS VAWT]
    HAWT --> HAWT1[UPWIND BLADES AHEAD OF TOWER]
    HAWT --> HAWT2[DOWNWIND BLADES BEHIND TOWER]
    HAWT1 --> HAWT1A[MULTI BLADE 3 BLADE MOST COMMON]
    HAWT1 --> HAWT1B[TWO BLADE AND SINGLE BLADE]
    VAWT --> VAWT1[SAVONIUS DRAG TYPE]
    VAWT --> VAWT2[DARRIEUS EGG BEATER LIFT TYPE]
    VAWT --> VAWT3[GIROMILL VARIABLE GEOMETRY]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Power Curve of a Wind Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CUT IN SPEED 3 TO 4 MS] --> B[PARTIAL LOAD REGION CP MAXIMIZED]
    B --> C[RATED SPEED 12 TO 14 MS PRATED]
    C --> D[FULL LOAD PITCH CONTROLLED]
    D --> E[CUT OUT SPEED 25 MS EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN]
```

The blocks correspond to:
- `A`: machine begins to generate, `C_p` controlled to max
- `B`: cubic power increase (`v³` region)
- `C`: rated electrical power reached
- `D`: pitch limits torque, output held constant
- `E`: machine feathers blades and stops

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Wind Energy Storage Options

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GEN[WIND GENERATOR] --> REG[AC DC BUS AND METERING]
    REG --> G1[GRID EXPORT]
    REG --> B1[BATTERY BANK LEAD ACID OR LI ION]
    REG --> H2[HYDROGEN VIA ELECTROLYZER]
    REG --> P1[PUMPED HYDRO STORAGE]
    REG --> C1[COMPRESSED AIR STORAGE CAES]
    B1 --> L1[DC LOADS AND INVERTER]
    H2 --> L2[FUEL CELL REVERSE OPERATION]
    P1 --> G1
    C1 --> G1
```

This figure illustrates how variable wind power is *time-shifted* by storage so the user sees a steady, dispatchable energy supply.

---

## 4.6 Functional Architecture — Control Loops of a WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WIND[INCOMING WIND] --> SENSE[ANEMOMETER WIND VANE PITOT TUBE]
    SENSE --> PLC[SUPERVISORY CONTROLLER PLC]
    PLC --> PITCH[PITCH ACTUATOR ON BLADE ROOT]
    PLC --> YAW[YAW DRIVE ON NACELLE]
    PLC --> TORQ[GENERATOR TORQUE SETPOINT]
    PLC --> BRAKE[MECHANICAL OR AERODYNAMIC BRAKE]
    PITCH --> BLADE[ROTOR BLADES]
    YAW --> NAC[NACELLE ORIENTATION]
    TORQ --> GEN[DOUBLY FED INDUCTION GENERATOR]
    BRAKE --> BLADE
    BLADE --> LOAD[POWER OUTPUT]
    GEN --> LOAD
```

**Why this matters for KTU:** Examiner questions on "variable-speed operation," "pitch control," and "DFIG" (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator) require you to know that *all* these actuators are coordinated by a single controller reading the wind sensor. The control aims to keep `λ` near optimum → `C_p` near maximum → maximize annual energy capture (AEP) while respecting structural and electrical limits.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`**

**Q1. State and explain Betz's law for wind energy conversion. Why can no turbine achieve 100 % efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**
Betz's law states that **no ideal wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.26 %) of the kinetic energy** of the incident wind. It is derived by applying mass, momentum, and energy conservation to a control volume of the air stream passing through the rotor. The maximum is reached when the downstream wind speed is exactly one-third of the upstream wind speed (`v₂ = v₁/3`) and the velocity at the rotor plane is two-thirds (`v = 2v₁/3`). A turbine cannot capture 100 % of the energy because, if it did, the downstream air would be at complete rest, creating an infinite blockage and back-pressure — physically the air must continue to flow out the back to keep the upstream wind feeding the rotor.

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Q2. What is tip speed ratio? How does it differ for a slow- and a fast-speed machine?**

**Model Answer:**
The **tip speed ratio (λ)** is the ratio of the linear speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind speed: `λ = ωR / v`. A **slow-speed machine** (e.g., American multiblade farm windmill, Savonius rotor) operates at low λ (≈ 0.5 – 1) and relies mainly on drag; it produces high starting torque at low rpm, ideal for water pumping. A **fast-speed machine** (modern 3-blade HAWT) operates at high λ (≈ 6 – 8); it produces low starting torque but high efficiency and runs at rpm suitable for direct grid-tied generation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)  — Comprehensive WECS Problem

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q3 (a) or (b)]`**

**A. (a)** Derive the expression for the maximum power that can be extracted from wind and obtain the value of the maximum power coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Take a stream-tube of air passing through a rotor disk of area `A`. Let `v₁`, `v`, `v₂` be velocities upstream, at rotor, downstream. Mass flow rate: `ṁ = ρAv`. Thrust: `F = ṁ(v₁ − v₂) = ρAv(v₁ − v₂)`.

*Step 2:* Apply Bernoulli's equation upstream and downstream and equate the pressure jump across the rotor: `p⁺ − p⁻ = ½ρ(v₁² − v₂²)`.

*Step 3:* Substituting and simplifying, `v = (v₁ + v₂)/2`. Hence power extracted `P = F·v = ½ρA(v₁ + v₂)²(v₁ − v₂)/2 = ½ρAv₁³(1−a)²(4a)`, where `a = (v₁ − v₂)/(2v₁)`.

*Step 4:* Power coefficient `C_p = P / (½ρAv₁³) = 4a(1 − a)²`. Differentiate and set to zero: `dC_p/da = 4(1 − a)(1 − 3a) = 0` → `a = 1/3`.

*Step 5:* `C_p^max = 4 × ⅓ × (2/3)² = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926`. Maximum power `P_max = (8/27)ρAv₁³`. [See full derivation in Section 3.2 for the 7-mark step-by-step.]

**(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m, hub height 80 m. The reference wind speed at 10 m is 6 m/s. The power-law exponent is 1/7. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³, generator efficiency 92 %, gear-box efficiency 96 %, and `C_p = 0.45`. Calculate the average electrical power output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Hub-height wind speed using power law:
$$v(80) = 6 \times (80/10)^{1/7} = 6 \times 8^{0.143}$$

Compute: `ln 8 = 2.0794`; `0.143 × 2.0794 = 0.297`; `e^{0.297} = 1.346`.
Thus `v(80) ≈ 6 × 1.346 = 8.07 m/s`.

*Step 2:* Swept area: `A = π(60)²/4 = 900π ≈ 2827.4 m²`.

*Step 3:* Power in the wind:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 × 1.225 × 2827.4 × 8.07^3$$

- `8.07³ = 525.4`
- `0.5 × 1.225 = 0.6125`
- `0.6125 × 2827.4 = 1731.8`
- `1731.8 × 525.4 = 910,068 W ≈ 0.91 MW`

*Step 4:* Mechanical power at shaft:
$$P_{\text{mech}} = 0.45 × 0.91 = 0.41 \, \text{MW} = 410 \, \text{kW}$$

*Step 5:* Electrical power output:
$$P_{\text{elec}} = 0.41 × 0.96 × 0.92 = 0.41 × 0.8832 = 0.362 \, \text{MW} \approx 362 \, \text{kW}$$

**Answer:** `P_elec ≈ 362 kW`

---

### Question B (14 Marks)  — Wind Resource & System Design

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q4 (a) or (b)]`**

**B. (a)** Describe with a neat sketch the main components of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) system. Explain the function of the gear box, yaw mechanism, and pitch control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A horizontal axis wind turbine has its main shaft aligned horizontally, parallel to the ground. The **main components** are:

1. **Rotor** (blades + hub) — extracts kinetic energy from wind.
2. **Nacelle** — housing on top of the tower containing the drive train.
3. **Main shaft (low-speed shaft)** — turns at rotor speed (~ 15 – 25 rpm for large machines).
4. **Gear box** — steps up the rotational speed to match the generator (typically 1500 – 1800 rpm for a 4-pole 50 Hz induction machine). Gear ratio is ~ 1:75.
5. **High-speed shaft** — drives the generator.
6. **Generator** — converts mechanical to electrical energy (often doubly-fed induction generator, DFIG).
7. **Yaw mechanism** — a motor-driven gear ring that rotates the nacelle so the rotor faces the wind; this maximizes the effective swept area and prevents asymmetric loading.
8. **Pitch control** — hydraulics or electric motors at the blade root that rotate (feather) the blade to (i) limit power above rated wind speed and (ii) aerodynamically brake the rotor in emergencies.
9. **Tower** — tubular steel or concrete; raises the rotor into faster, less turbulent air.
10. **Anemometer & wind vane** — mounted on top of the nacelle; provide feedback to the controller.

> **KTU Sketch Tip:** Always label the nacelle, low-speed shaft, gear box, high-speed shaft, generator, yaw motor, tower, and pitch actuator. A labeled diagram earns **2–3 marks by itself** even before the text.

**(b)** A site has a Weibull distribution with shape parameter `k = 2.0` and scale parameter `c = 7.5 m/s`. A 1.5 MW turbine has cut-in, rated, and cut-out speeds of 3, 12, and 25 m/s respectively. The rotor diameter is 77 m, air density 1.225 kg/m³, and `C_p = 0.40`. Estimate the annual energy output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Use the Weibull PDF:
$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right]$$

With `k = 2, c = 7.5`:
$$f(v) = \frac{2}{7.5}\left(\frac{v}{7.5}\right)\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{7.5}\right)^{2}\right] = 0.2667 \cdot \frac{v}{7.5}\cdot \exp(-v^2/56.25)$$

*Step 2:* Annual energy (continuous integral between cut-in and rated, then rated × 8760 × probability in rated region):
$$E = 8760 \int_{3}^{12} P(v)\, f(v)\, dv \;+\; 8760 \cdot 1.5 \cdot \int_{12}^{25} f(v)\, dv$$

(Note: above 25 m/s, output is zero.)

*Step 3:* Approximate the integral using Simpson's 1/3 rule with `n = 6` intervals over `[3, 12]`. Step `h = 1.5 m/s`. Tabulate `P(v)·f(v)`:

| v (m/s) | 3.0 | 4.5 | 6.0 | 7.5 | 9.0 | 10.5 | 12.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| v³ | 27 | 91.13 | 216 | 421.9 | 729 | 1157.6 | 1728 |
| P(v) = 0.5·1.225·A·0.40·v³ (kW) | 80.2 | 270.6 | 641.4 | 1252.7 | 2165.0 | 3439.2 | 5131.3 |
| f(v) | 0.0273 | 0.0445 | 0.0536 | 0.0517 | 0.0412 | 0.0261 | 0.0117 |
| P·f | 2.19 | 12.04 | 34.39 | 64.77 | 89.20 | 89.76 | 60.04 |

Simpson's rule:
$$\int_{3}^{12} P f\, dv \approx \frac{h}{3}\!\left[P_0f_0 + 4(P_1f_1+P_3f_3+P_5f_5) + 2(P_2f_2+P_4f_4) + P_6f_6\right]$$

= `(1.5/3) × [2.19 + 4(12.04 + 64.77 + 89.76) + 2(34.39 + 89.20) + 60.04]`
= `0.5 × [2.19 + 4(166.57) + 2(123.59) + 60.04]`
= `0.5 × [2.19 + 666.28 + 247.18 + 60.04]`
= `0.5 × 975.69 = 487.85 kW`

*Step 4:* Probability of wind in the rated region `[12, 25]`. The cumulative distribution function of the Weibull is:
$$F(v) = 1 - \exp[-(v/c)^k]$$
- `F(12) = 1 − exp(−(12/7.5)²) = 1 − exp(−2.56) = 1 − 0.0773 = 0.9227`
- `F(25) = 1 − exp(−(25/7.5)²) = 1 − exp(−11.11) ≈ 1.0000`
- Probability in rated region = `0.9227 − 0.9999 ≈ 0.0772`

*Step 5:* Energy between 12 and 25 m/s:
$$E_2 = 8760 × 1.5 × 0.0772 = 1014 \, \text{MWh}$$

*Step 6:* Energy in partial-load region:
$$E_1 = 8760 × 487.85 \text{ kW} = 8760 × 0.48785 \text{ MW} = 4273.6 \, \text{MWh}$$

*Step 7:* Total:
$$E = E_1 + E_2 = 4273.6 + 1014 ≈ 5288 \, \text{MWh} \approx 5.3 \, \text{GWh/year}$$

**Answer:** `E ≈ 5.3 GWh/year` (Capacity factor ≈ 0.40 — reasonable for a 7.5 m/s mean wind site)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups:** The wind-power equation gives W; if the swept area is in m² and v in m/s, the result is in **W**. Convert to MW by `÷ 10⁶` *after* completing the multiplication.
> 2. **Betz limit misuse:** Some students write `C_p = 0.593 × C_p,actual`. The Betz limit is a **theoretical upper bound**, not a multiplier on whatever value you assumed.
> 3. **Power-law exponent confusion:** `α = 1/7` is for *open flat terrain*. For urban or forested sites, `α` rises to 0.20 – 0.40. The KTU problem may give `α` explicitly — use it as given.
> 4. **Density vs. altitude:** At high-altitude sites, use `ρ = ρ₀ (1 − L·h/T₀)^{gM/(RL)}` if asked. By default, KTU uses **1.225 kg/m³**.
> 5. **Cut-in vs. cut-out:** Do not invert them. "Cut in" = machine *starts*; "cut out" = machine *stops*.
> 6. **Weibull integral:** Examiners will deduct marks if the integral is left as a symbolic expression with no numerical evaluation. Always substitute numbers and show the work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: WECS**

- ☐ **Power in the wind:** `P_wind = ½ ρ A v³` — cubic dependence on velocity is the single most important fact.
- ☐ **Betz limit (16/27 ≈ 0.5926):** Maximum *fraction* of wind power any turbine can extract. Use it as the **theoretical ceiling** when comparing real machines.
- ☐ **Maximum extractable power:** `P_max = (8/27) ρ A v₁³`, achieved when `v₂ = v₁/3` and `v_rotor = 2v₁/3`.
- ☐ **Tip speed ratio:** `λ = ωR / v`. Modern 3-blade HAWTs: `λ ≈ 6 – 8`. Slow multiblade pumps: `λ ≈ 1`.
- ☐ **Power coefficient:** `C_p = P_mech / P_wind`. A real turbine typically achieves `C_p = 0.35 – 0.45`.
- ☐ **Swept area:** `A = πD²/4`. Doubling diameter → quadruples area → quadruples power.
- ☐ **Hub-height correction:** `v(h) = v_ref (h/h_ref)^{1/7}` for open terrain. **Always use the tower height given in the problem.**
- ☐ **Capacity factor (CF):** `CF = (E_actual)/(P_rated × 8760)`. Typical values: 0.20 – 0.30 onshore, 0.40 – 0.50 offshore.
- ☐ **Weibull distribution:** `f(v) = (k/c)(v/c)^{k-1} exp[−(v/c)^k]`. The shape parameter `k = 2` is Rayleigh (most common KTU default).
- ☐ **Cut-in, rated, cut-out:** 3 – 4, 12 – 14, 25 m/s are standard KTU values.
- ☐ **Components (HAWT):** Blades, hub, nacelle, low-speed shaft, gear box, high-speed shaft, generator, yaw, pitch, tower, controller.
- ☐ **VAWT types:** Savonius (drag, low λ), Darrieus (lift, moderate λ), Giromill (variable geometry).
- ☐ **Site selection criteria:** Mean wind speed **> 6 m/s** at hub height, smooth laminar flow, low turbulence, accessible terrain, proximity to grid.
- ☐ **Storage options:** Battery, pumped hydro, hydrogen, CAES — chosen to match grid demand profile.
- ☐ **Indian context:** Largest wind farm cluster — Muppandal (Tamil Nadu); Suzlon, Vestas, Enercon are common manufacturers; MNRE is the nodal agency.
- ☐ **Environmental impact:** Low CO₂ footprint (~ 11 g CO₂/kWh), but bird/bat strikes, noise, and visual impact are real concerns.
- ☐ **Numerical constants to memorize:** `ρ = 1.225 kg/m³`; `α = 1/7`; `C_p,max = 16/27`; `8760 h/year`.

> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip (Module 2):**
> For a 14-mark WECS question, the typical mark split is:
> - **3 marks** — labeled diagram
> - **3 marks** — definition and statement of Betz law
> - **4 marks** — derivation of `C_p = 4a(1−a)²` and `C_p,max = 16/27`
> - **4 marks** — numerical computation of `P_wind`, `P_mech`, `P_elec`, `E_annual`
> Memorize the seven steps of the Betz derivation verbatim — they appear in nearly every KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Axis Windmills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Horizontal and Vertical Axis Windmills ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is a device that converts the kinetic energy of moving air (wind) into useful mechanical work or electrical energy. The mechanical component that intercepts the wind and converts its kinetic energy into rotational mechanical energy is called a **Wind Turbine** or **Windmill**. Wind turbines are broadly classified into two principal categories based on the orientation of the rotor shaft with respect to the ground: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > 1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT):** A wind turbine whose rotor shaft is **horizontal** (parallel) to the ground, and the blades rotate in a **vertical plane** perpendicular to the wind direction. > 2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT):** A wind turbine whose rotor shaft is **vertical** (perpendicular) to the ground, and the blades rotate in a **horizontal plane**. | Parameter | HAWT | VAWT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Shaft Axis** | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical (perpendicular to ground) | | **Plane of Rotation** | Vertical plane | Horizontal plane | | **Wind Direction Dependency** | Must be aligned (yawed) into the wind | Accepts wind from any direction | | **Tower Requirement** | Tall tower (for stronger, less turbulent wind) | Ground-level installation possible | | **Typical Efficiency** | High ($\eta \approx 35$–$45\%$) | Comparatively lower | | **Self-Starting** | Yes (in most designs) | Depends on type (Savonius: yes, Darrieus: no) | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it this way (The Umbrella Analogy):** > Imagine you are holding a large **umbrella** by its straight handle. > - If you hold the handle **horizontally** (pointing forward, away from you) and the wind blows against the canopy, the canopy spins like a wheel standing up vertically. This is exactly how a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** works — the "handle" is the rotor shaft, and the "canopy" represents the blades catching wind in a vertical plane. > - Now, imagine twisting the umbrella so that the handle points **straight up toward the sky** and the canopy rotates like a ceiling fan in a **horizontal plane**. Wind blowing sideways across it will spin it. This is the working principle of a **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)**. > > In simple words: **HAWTs are like spinning vertical discs**, and **VAWTs are like spinning horizontal turntables** — both are pushed by the wind but face it differently. ### 1.3 Why Two Designs Exist? The fundamental trade-off is between: - **Energy Capture Efficiency** (favored by HAWTs due to higher elevation access and aerodynamic blade design). - **Structural Simplicity & Wind Direction Independence** (favored by VAWTs since no yaw control is required, and gearbox/generator can be placed at ground level for easier maintenance). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Constant:** The **kinematic viscosity of air** $\nu \approx \mathbf{1.46 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2/s}$ at standard conditions (15°C, 1 atm). The **density of air** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$ at sea level (ISA conditions). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) — Deep Analysis A HAWT operates on the **aerodynamic lift principle**, similar to an aircraft wing. As wind flows over the specially shaped airfoil blade, a pressure differential is created, generating a lift force perpendicular to the wind direction and a drag force parallel to it.
