The resultant force causes rotation. #### 2.1.1 Sub-classification of HAWTs | Sub-type | Description | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Upwind Turbine** | Rotor faces the wind; tower is downwind. Uses yaw mechanism to keep rotor facing the wind. | Most modern utility-scale turbines. | | **Downwind Turbine** | Rotor is placed downwind of the tower; wind shadow from the tower may cause cyclic loading. | Rare, used in some experimental designs. | | **Multi-blade HAWT** | 8–24 blades, low rotational speed, high torque. | Water pumping (traditional farm windmills). | | **Few-blade HAWT** | 2 or 3 blades, high rotational speed. | Electricity generation. | | **Dutch Four-Arm Type** | Traditional wooden structure, 4 blades with cloth sails. | Historical water pumping. | #### 2.1.2 Key Parameters Governing HAWT Performance - **Rotor Diameter ($D$):** The diameter of the circle swept by the blade tips, in meters. - **Swept Area ($A$):** $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ - **Hub Height ($H$):** The height of the rotor center above ground, governing the wind speed accessed. - **Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$):** Defined as the ratio of blade tip speed to free wind speed. - **Solidity ($\sigma$):** Ratio of total blade area to swept area. --- ### 2.2 Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) — Deep Analysis A VAWT has its rotor shaft oriented vertically, accepting wind from **any horizontal direction** without the need for a yaw system. #### 2.2.1 Major Sub-classification of VAWTs | Type | Working Principle | Characteristics | Self-Starting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Savonius Rotor** | **Drag-based** | Two semi-cylindrical scoops, S-shaped cross-section, low speed, high torque. | **Yes** | | **Darrieus Rotor** | **Lift-based** | Curved (troposkein) or straight blades, egg-beater shape, high speed. | **No** (requires external push) | | **Giromill** | Lift-based, variable pitch | Darrieus variant with straight blades and controllable pitch. | **Yes** (with pitch control) | | **H-Rotor** | Lift-based | Straight vertical blades in H-configuration; simple structure. | No (usually) | > [!NOTE] > **Why does Savonius self-start but Darrieus does not?** > A Savonius rotor uses **drag** — the concave scoop catches wind and is pulled forward by drag, while the convex side offers less resistance. This asymmetry always produces torque in the wind direction. > A Darrieus rotor relies purely on **lift**, which requires the blade to already be moving at an angle of attack. At rest, symmetric blades produce **zero net torque** — hence it needs a motor or Savonius to start. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exam — Wind Turbines** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Power in Wind** | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Total kinetic power of wind crossing swept area. | W (Watts) | | 2 | **Swept Area** | $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | Geometric area covered by blades. | $m^{2}$ | | 3 | **Power Extracted** | $P = C_{p}\,P_{wind} = C_{p}\left(\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}\right)$ | Actual mechanical power produced. | W | | 4 | **Power Coefficient** | $C_{p} = \frac{P}{P_{wind}}$ | Fraction of wind power captured. | Dimensionless | | 5 | **Betz Limit** | $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Theoretical maximum efficiency. | Dimensionless | | 6 | **Torque Coefficient** | $C_{T} = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{2} R}$ | Torque extraction efficiency. | Dimensionless | | 7 | **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D N}{60\,v}$ | Ratio of blade tip speed to wind speed. | Dimensionless | | 8 | **Angular Velocity** | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Rotor rotational speed. | rad/s | | 9 | **Solidity** | $\sigma = \frac{B\,c}{\pi R} = \frac{N_{b}\,c}{\pi D}$ | Ratio of blade chord area to swept area. | Dimensionless | | 10 | **Specific Speed** | $N_{s} = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Used to classify turbine (used with power $P$, head $H$). | — | | 11 | **Number of Blades (Design)** | $B = \frac{2\,\lambda_{r}}{\lambda_{d}}$ | Where $\lambda_r$ is design TSR, $\lambda_d$ is per-blade optimum. | — | | 12 | **Energy in Wind (per unit mass)** | $e = \frac{1}{2}v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy per kg of air. | J/kg | | 13 | **Wind Power Density** | $P/A = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^{3}$ | Power per unit swept area. | $W/m^{2}$ | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **HAWTs dominate the modern wind energy market** — over 95% of utility-scale wind farms use 3-bladed upwind HAWTs because of their superior aerodynamic efficiency and proven reliability. - **VAWTs are favored in niche applications** — building-integrated turbines, urban installations, rooftop systems, and remote off-grid applications where variable wind direction and ground-level maintenance are critical. - **Traditional multi-blade HAWTs** are still widely used for **mechanical water pumping** in rural India and African countries. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in the Wind The power available in the wind is the rate at which kinetic energy of an air mass crosses a given area. **Step 1:** Consider an air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ through a cross-sectional area $A$. **Step 2:** In time $dt$, the length of the air column that passes through area $A$ is $L = v \cdot dt$. **Step 3:** The volume of air passing through the area in time $dt$ is $V = A \cdot L = A \cdot v \cdot dt$. **Step 4:** The mass of this air is $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot dt$. **Step 5:** The kinetic energy of this mass is $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$. **Step 6:** Substituting the expression for $m$: $$E = \frac{1}{2}\,(\rho\,A\,v\,dt)\,v^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}\,dt$$ **Step 7:** Power is the time rate of change of energy, $P = \frac{dE}{dt}$: $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$$ This is the **fundamental wind power equation** — the foundation of all wind energy calculations. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical $C_p$) This derivation proves that **no wind turbine can extract more than 59.26% of the kinetic energy in the wind**. **Step 1:** Consider an ideal actuator disc model. Let the wind velocity at: - Far upstream (infinity): $v$ - At the disc plane: $v_d$ - Far downstream (wake): $v_w$ **Step 2:** By continuity, the mass flow rate through the disc is constant: $$\dot{m} = \rho A v_{d} = \rho A_{w} v_{w}$$ **Step 3:** The thrust force (axial force) on the disc equals the change in momentum of the air: $$F = \dot{m}\,(v - v_{w}) = \rho A v_{d}\,(v - v_{w})$$ **Step 4:** Using Bernoulli's equation between upstream and downstream (far-field, no pressure recovery): $$F = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v^{2} - v_{w}^{2})$$ **Step 5:** Equating the two force expressions: $$\rho A v_{d}\,(v - v_{w}) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v^{2} - v_{w}^{2})$$ **Step 6:** Simplifying using $(v - v_{w})(v + v_{w}) = v^{2} - v_{w}^{2}$: $$v_{d} = \frac{1}{2}(v + v_{w})$$ **Step 7:** The power extracted by the turbine equals the thrust force times the velocity at the disc: $$P = F \cdot v_{d} = \rho A v_{d}\,(v - v_{w})\cdot v_{d}$$ **Step 8:** Substituting $v_d = \frac{1}{2}(v + v_w)$: $$P = \frac{1}{4}\rho A (v + v_{w})^{2}(v - v_{w})$$ **Step 9:** Define the induction factor $a = \frac{v - v_d}{v}$, which gives $v_d = v(1-a)$ and $v_w = v(1-2a)$. **Step 10:** Substituting into the power equation: $$P = \frac{1}{4}\rho A \left[2v - 2av\right]^{2}\left[2av\right]$$ $$P = \frac{1}{4}\rho A \cdot 4v^{2}(1-a)^{2} \cdot 2av$$ $$P = 2\rho A v^{3}\,a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 11:** The power coefficient is: $$C_{p} = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A v^{3}\,a(1-a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 12:** To find the maximum, differentiate $C_p$ with respect to $a$ and set to zero: $$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right]$$ $$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ **Step 13:** Solving $1 - 3a = 0$ gives $a = \frac{1}{3}$ (rejecting $a=1$). **Step 14:** Substituting $a = \frac{1}{3}$ into $C_p$: $$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$ $$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\;(\text{59.26\%})}$$ This is the celebrated **Betz Limit** (Albert Betz, 1919). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) Relationship **Step 1:** The linear velocity of the blade tip is given by $v_{tip} = \omega R = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot R = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$. **Step 2:** The TSR is defined as the ratio of blade tip speed to free wind speed: $$\lambda = \frac{v_{tip}}{v} = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D N}{60\,v}$$ **Step 3:** Rearranging for rotational speed in terms of TSR: $$N = \frac{60\,v\,\lambda}{\pi D}$$ **Step 4:** For multi-blade HAWTs (low TSR, $\lambda \approx 1$), high torque is achieved at low RPM — ideal for water pumping. For 3-blade HAWTs (high TSR, $\lambda \approx 6$–$8$), high RPM is achieved — ideal for electricity generation. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A wind turbine with 3 blades has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind blows at 12 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, and the power coefficient is 0.4. Calculate: > (a) The total power available in the wind. > (b) The actual power extracted. > (c) The torque produced at the shaft (assume radius $R = 40$ m). **Solution:** **(a) Swept Area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55\ m^{2}$$ **Total Power in Wind:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^{3}$$ $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(1728)$$ $$P_{wind} \approx 5.319\ MW$$ **(b) Power Extracted:** $$P = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.4 \times 5.319\ MW \approx 2.128\ MW$$ **(c) Torque Produced:** Using $P = T \cdot \omega$, and we can compute torque from the torque coefficient formulation or simply: $$T = \frac{P}{\omega}$$ Assuming the rotor spins at the design TSR of $\lambda = 7$: $$\omega = \frac{\lambda \cdot v}{R} = \frac{7 \times 12}{40} = 2.1\ rad/s$$ $$T = \frac{2.128 \times 10^{6}}{2.1} \approx 1.013 \times 10^{6}\ N\cdot m$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 1.013\ MN\cdot m}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HAWT vs VAWT Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Wind Source
Kinetic Energy] --> nodeB[Direction of Wind Flow] nodeB --> nodeC{Turbine Type} nodeC -->|Horizontal Axis| nodeD[HAWT System] nodeC -->|Vertical Axis| nodeE[VAWT System] nodeD --> nodeD1[Rotor Hub
at Top of Tower] nodeD1 --> nodeD2[3 Blades
Aerofoil Airfoils] nodeD2 --> nodeD3[Yaw Mechanism
Aligns Rotor to Wind] nodeD3 --> nodeD4[Gearbox in Nacelle] nodeD4 --> nodeD5[Generator
Electricity Output] nodeE --> nodeE1[Vertical Shaft
at Ground Level] nodeE1 --> nodeE2a[Savonius
Drag Type] nodeE1 --> nodeE2b[Darrieus
Lift Type] nodeE2a --> nodeE3[Ground-Based
Gearbox and Generator] nodeE2b --> nodeE3 nodeE3 --> nodeE4[Electricity Output] nodeD5 --> nodeF[Power Conditioning
Inverter and Transformer] nodeE4 --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG[Grid or Load] style nodeA fill:#ffe0b3 style nodeC fill:#ffcc99 style nodeD fill:#cce5ff style nodeE fill:#d4edda style nodeF fill:#fff3cd style nodeG fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow in a Wind Turbine System ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Wind at Velocity v] -->|Kinetic Energy| nodeB[Rotor Blades] nodeB -->|Aerodynamic Conversion| nodeC[Rotational Mechanical Power] nodeC -->|Torque Transmission| nodeD[Gearbox] nodeD -->|Speed Step-Up| nodeE[Generator Rotor] nodeE -->|Electromagnetic Conversion| nodeF[Electrical Power Output] nodeF -->|Conditioning| nodeG[Power Electronics] nodeG -->|Step-Up| nodeH[Grid Connection] nodeA -.->|Cp less than 16/27| nodeB nodeB -.->|Mechanical Losses| nodeC nodeC -.->|Gearbox Efficiency| nodeD nodeD -.->|Generator Efficiency| nodeE nodeE -.->|Converter Losses| nodeF style nodeA fill:#ffd9b3 style nodeH fill:#b3d9ff ``` ### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — HAWT Cross-Section View ``` WIND DIRECTION ===> Blade 1 /\ / \ / \ / /\ \ Hub =====/==/====\==\====> Yaw Motor \ \/ / \ / \ / \/ Tower | (Tubular Steel) | | [Foundation] [Gearbox + Generator in Nacelle] ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Savonius VAWT Cross-Section ``` Top View (Looking Down from Above) WIND FROM ANY DIRECTION | v ___________________ / \ | ________________ | | / \ | ___ | | ______________ || / \ | | / \ || \ / | | | S - SHAPE | || \ / | | \ BLADES / || / \ | | \____________/ || / \ | \ / | \___/ | \______________/ | \ / \_________________/ | Vertical Shaft | [Ground Level Gearbox and Generator] ``` ### 4.5 Comparison Matrix | Design Feature | HAWT | VAWT (Savonius) | VAWT (Darrieus) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Blade Count** | 2, 3, or many | 2 (scoops) | 2, 3 (curved) | | **Rotor Size (typical)** | 40–200 m diameter | 1–5 m | 10–30 m | | **Wind Access** | Elevated (above buildings) | Ground level | Ground level | | **Yaw Control** | Required | Not required | Not required | | **Power Output** | High (MW range) | Low (W to kW) | Moderate (kW) | | **Efficiency** | 35–45% | 10–15% | 25–35% | | **Maintenance** | Difficult (at height) | Easy (ground level) | Easy (ground level) | | **Self-Starting** | Yes | Yes | No | | **Noise** | Moderate (blade tip noise) | Low | Low | | **Visual Impact** | High (tall tower) | Low | Moderate | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, Module 2]** > **Q:** Differentiate between Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT). List any three advantages of HAWT over VAWT. **Model Answer:** | S.No. | HAWT | VAWT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1. | Shaft axis is **horizontal** (parallel to ground). | Shaft axis is **vertical** (perpendicular to ground). | | 2. | Blade rotation occurs in a **vertical plane**. | Blade rotation occurs in a **horizontal plane**. | | 3. | **Yaw mechanism** is required to align rotor with wind. | **No yaw** required — accepts wind from any direction. | | 4. | Generator and gearbox placed in **nacelle on top of tower**. | Generator and gearbox placed at **ground level**. | | 5. | Generally **higher efficiency** ($C_p$ up to 0.45). | Generally **lower efficiency** ($C_p$ around 0.25–0.35). | **Three advantages of HAWT over VAWT:** (i) Higher aerodynamic efficiency and power output. (ii) Access to higher-altitude, more energetic wind. (iii) Lower structural material requirement per kW generated. (iv) Better scalability to multi-megawatt utility scale. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, Module 2]** > **Q:** What is the **Betz Limit**? Why can't a wind turbine extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy? **Model Answer:** The **Betz Limit** is the **theoretical maximum** fraction of the kinetic energy in the wind that an ideal wind turbine can extract. It is mathematically given by: $$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\;(\text{or } 59.26\%)$$ A wind turbine **cannot extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy** because: (i) If the air were brought to a **complete stop** ($v_w = 0$) at the disc, no new air would be able to pass through — mass flow would cease, and the extracted power would also be **zero**. (ii) The power extracted depends on the **difference** in wind speeds and the mass flow rate — both cannot be maximized simultaneously. (iii) There exists an **optimal induction factor** $a = 1/3$ that balances these competing effects, giving the maximum possible $C_p$ of 16/27. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **Module 2 Choice Question (ESE Pattern)** #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)**. Describe its main components and the function of each. **(b)** [7 Marks] A 3-bladed HAWT has the following specifications: rotor diameter $D = 60$ m, rated wind speed $v = 14$ m/s, air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, power coefficient $C_p = 0.42$, and tip speed ratio $\lambda = 7$. Calculate: (i) Swept area, (ii) Total wind power, (iii) Power extracted, (iv) Rotational speed in RPM, and (v) Torque on the shaft. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the construction and working of a **Savonius Vertical Axis Wind Turbine** and a **Darrieus Vertical Axis Wind Turbine** with diagrams. Compare their performance. **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **maximum power coefficient ($C_{p,\max}$)** of an ideal wind turbine using the actuator disc theory. State the **Betz Limit** clearly. --- ### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A (Part b) > **[KTU University Exam — Solution, Module 2]** **Given Data:** $D = 60$ m, $v = 14$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.42$, $\lambda = 7$, $B = 3$. **(i) Swept Area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^{2}}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43\ m^{2}$$ **(ii) Total Power in Wind:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(2827.43)(14)^{3}$$ $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(2827.43)(2744)$$ $$P_{wind} \approx 4.752\ MW$$ **(iii) Power Extracted:** $$P = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 4.752\ MW \approx 1.996\ MW \approx 2.0\ MW$$ **(iv) Rotational Speed in RPM:** $$N = \frac{60 \cdot v \cdot \lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 14 \times 7}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{5880}{188.5} \approx 31.2\ RPM$$ **(v) Torque on the Shaft:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 31.2}{60} \approx 3.27\ rad/s$$ $$T = \frac{P}{\omega} = \frac{1.996 \times 10^{6}}{3.27} \approx 6.10 \times 10^{5}\ N\cdot m$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 610\ kN\cdot m}$$ --- ### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B (Part a) > **[KTU University Exam — Solution, Module 2]** **Savonius Rotor:** It consists of two or more **semi-cylindrical scoops** arranged along a vertical shaft in an S-shaped cross-section. The concave surface of one scoop catches the wind, creating high drag, while the convex surface offers low drag. The asymmetry in drag forces produces a net torque, causing rotation. It is **drag-based**, **self-starting**, has **high torque** but **low speed** and **low efficiency** (10–15%). **Darrieus Rotor:** It consists of two or three **curved (troposkein-shaped) or straight airfoil blades** mounted on a vertical shaft. The blades are symmetric, and the turbine operates purely on the **aerodynamic lift** principle. As the blade moves, an angle of attack is created, and the lift force drives rotation. It is **not self-starting**, has **high speed** and **moderate efficiency** (25–35%). **Comparison:** | Feature | Savonius | Darrieus | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Principle | Drag | Lift | | Self-Starting | Yes | No | | Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda \approx 0.9$–$1$ | $\lambda \approx 4$–$6$ | | Power Coefficient $C_p$ | 0.10–0.15 | 0.25–0.35 | | Applications | Low-power, water pumping | Moderate-power generation | --- ### 5.5 Model Solution — Question B (Part b) — Betz Limit Derivation Refer to **Section 3.2** for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is: $$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.26\%}$$ This is the **Betz Limit**, named after German physicist **Albert Betz** (1919), stating that no horizontal-axis wind turbine operating in free, non-turbulent wind can extract more than 59.26% of the total kinetic energy in the wind. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Mistake in Power Formula:** Students often write $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}$ (forgetting $A$ and the cube of velocity). Always remember — power in wind is **cubic in velocity**, not quadratic! > 2. **Confusing Tip Speed Ratio Definition:** Some write $\lambda = v/(\omega R)$ — this is the **inverse** of the correct formula. TSR is always blade tip speed divided by free wind speed. > 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** KTU examiners specifically deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers. Always write **W, kW, MW, N·m, rad/s, RPM** explicitly. > 4. **Betz Limit Misquote:** Some students write $C_p$ max as 0.5 or 0.6. The **exact** value is 16/27 = **0.5926**. Writing 0.59 is acceptable; writing 0.6 is **not** precise enough. > 5. **Yaw Mechanism Omission:** In HAWT diagrams, students often forget to mention the **yaw control** system that keeps the rotor facing the wind. This is a **favourite 2-mark question** topic. > 6. **VAWT Self-Starting Issue:** Confusing whether Darrieus is self-starting — it is **NOT**. Only Savonius and some Giromill variants are self-starting. > 7. **Swept Area Units:** Always use the formula $A = \pi D^2/4$ for HAWT, not $A = \pi R^2/2$ or other variations. --- ### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Horizontal and Vertical Axis Windmills** - ☐ **HAWT** = Horizontal shaft, vertical plane rotation, requires yaw, **higher efficiency**. - ☐ **VAWT** = Vertical shaft, horizontal plane rotation, **no yaw required**, ground-level maintenance. - ☐ **HAWT subtypes:** Upwind, Downwind, Multi-blade, Few-blade, Dutch four-arm. - ☐ **VAWT subtypes:** **Savonius** (drag, self-starting), **Darrieus** (lift, not self-starting), **Giromill** (variable pitch), **H-Rotor** (straight blades). - ☐ **Power in wind formula:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ — **cubic in velocity**. - ☐ **Swept area:** $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$. - ☐ **Power coefficient:** $C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}}$. - ☐ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ — the **theoretical upper bound**. - ☐ **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 v}$. - ☐ **Torque:** $T = \frac{P}{\omega}$. - ☐ **Solidity:** $\sigma = \frac{N_b c}{\pi D}$ — high solidity = high torque, low speed (multi-blade). - ☐ **Air density at sea level:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. - ☐ **Induction factor at Betz limit:** $a = 1/3$. - ☐ **Velocity at disc plane:** $v_d = v(1-a) = \frac{2v}{3}$. - ☐ **Wake velocity at Betz limit:** $v_w = v(1-2a) = \frac{v}{3}$. - ☐ **Multi-blade HAWT** → low TSR ($\lambda \approx 1$) → water pumping. - ☐ **3-blade HAWT** → high TSR ($\lambda \approx 6$–$8$) → electricity generation. - ☐ **Modern wind energy market** is dominated (>95%) by 3-bladed upwind HAWTs. - ☐ **VAWTs** are preferred for **building-integrated, urban, and rooftop** applications. - ☐ **Derivation of Betz limit** uses the **actuator disc theory** with continuity, momentum, and Bernoulli equations. - ☐ The two key VAWT variants **Savonius** and **Darrieus** differ in principle — **drag vs lift**.

topics.title: Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

In the context of **Non-Conventional Energy Systems**, the term **Performance Characteristics** refers to the set of electrical, thermal, and optical parameters that quantitatively describe the operational behavior of renewable energy conversion devices (most notably **Solar Photovoltaic Cells / Modules**, but also wind rotors and fuel cells) under varying environmental conditions.

For a **Solar PV Cell**, the performance characteristics define the relationship between the **output current (I)** and the **terminal voltage (V)**, the locus of the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, the **Fill Factor (FF)**, the overall **Conversion Efficiency (η)**, and the response of these quantities to changes in **solar irradiance (G)** and **cell temperature (T)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Performance Characteristics" describes how a solar cell delivers electrical power as a function of the load connected to it, characterized by the standard I–V curve, the P–V curve, and the four cardinal parameters: **Iₛ𝒸 (Short-Circuit Current)**, **Vₒ𝒸 (Open-Circuit Voltage)**, **Iₘ & Vₘ (Maximum Power Point Current & Voltage)**.*

### Key Performance Parameters (The Four Pillars)

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Short-Circuit Current | **Iₛ𝒸** | Maximum current when terminals are shorted (V = 0) |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | **Vₒ𝒸** | Maximum voltage when terminals are open (I = 0) |
| Maximum Power Point Current | **Iₘ** | Current at the operating point of maximum output power |
| Maximum Power Point Voltage | **Vₘ** | Voltage at the operating point of maximum output power |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Garden Tap and Bucket**
> Think of a solar cell as a **water tap connected to a rain barrel**:
> - The **sunlight (irradiance)** is the *rainfall rate* — more rain means more water (current) flows.
> - The **pressure in the pipe (voltage)** depends on how much the tap is open (load resistance).
> - If the tap is **fully open** (short circuit), maximum water flows but no pressure builds up → high current, **zero voltage**.
> - If the tap is **fully closed** (open circuit), no water flows but maximum pressure builds up → **zero current**, high voltage.
> - The **sweet spot** is a partially open tap where the product of flow × pressure (i.e., **Power = I × V**) is maximum. This is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.
> - The **Fill Factor** is a measure of *how "square" the I–V curve is* — the closer the curve is to an ideal rectangle (Vₒ𝒸 × Iₛ𝒸), the better the cell's quality.

### Why Performance Characteristics Matter in Engineering

- **System Sizing:** Engineers use the I–V curve to size **inverters, charge controllers, and battery banks**.
- **MPPT Design:** Maximum Power Point Trackers (algorithms like P&O, Incremental Conductance) are designed *directly* from the P–V curve.
- **Economic Justification:** Higher Fill Factor and Efficiency mean **more kWh per square meter**, directly reducing the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE).
- **Quality Benchmarking:** Cell manufacturers worldwide grade their products using FF and η as standard metrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Construction Recap (for Context)

A crystalline silicon solar cell is essentially a **large p–n junction diode** with:
- A thin **n-type emitter** layer on top
- A thick **p-type base** layer
- Front and back **ohmic metal contacts**
- An **anti-reflection coating (ARC)** to reduce reflection losses
- A **textured surface** to trap light

When photons with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the cell, they generate **electron–hole pairs**. The built-in electric field at the p–n junction separates these charge carriers, producing a **photocurrent (Iₗ)** that flows in the external circuit.

### 2.2 The Equivalent Circuit of a Real Solar Cell

A practical solar cell deviates from the ideal diode model due to two non-idealities:

1. **Series Resistance (Rₛ):** Caused by the resistance of the semiconductor bulk, metallic contacts, and the interconnections. Causes a **voltage drop** under load.
2. **Shunt Resistance (Rₛₕ):** Caused by manufacturing defects and leakage current paths around the junction. Causes a **current loss** proportional to voltage.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Photogeneration:** Incident photons generate a current source $I_L$ (also called the light-generated current $I_{ph}$).
- **Step 2 — Diode Action:** The p–n junction acts as a diode carrying a forward-bias current $I_D$, determined by the Shockley diode equation.
- **Step 3 — Shunt Loss:** A small leakage current $I_{sh}$ flows through the shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.
- **Step 4 — Series Drop:** Current flowing through the series resistance $R_s$ causes a voltage drop.
- **Step 5 — Terminal Output:** The terminal current $I$ is the photocurrent minus the diode current minus the shunt current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ideal Diode Equation (Lossless Cell)**
> $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$
> where $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** is the electron charge, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is the absolute cell temperature in **Kelvin**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Generalized (Real Cell) I–V Equation | A, V |
| 2 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Ideal Cell I–V Equation | A, V |
| 3 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (when $V = 0$) | Short-Circuit Current | A |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-Circuit Voltage | V |
| 5 | $P_m = V_m \times I_m$ | Maximum Output Power | W |
| 6 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_m}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | Conversion Efficiency | % |
| 8 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K | Thermal Voltage | V |
| 9 | $I_L \approx I_{sc} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_0}$ | Irradiance Scaling (Linear) | A |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -2.2 \text{ mV/°C/cell}$ | Temperature Coefficient of $V_{oc}$ | V/°C |
| 11 | $\dfrac{dI_{sc}}{dT} \approx +0.1 \text{ %/°C}$ | Temperature Coefficient of $I_{sc}$ | %/°C |
| 12 | $C_T = \dfrac{n_s k T}{q}$ (for $n$ cells in series) | Thermal Voltage for Module | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In your answer scripts and even in the formula table above, when writing the absolute-value bar of any quantity (e.g., $|V|$), always use the LaTeX form `\vert V \vert` or `\mid V \mid` — never the raw keyboard `|` — to keep the equation engine and markdown parser happy.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MPPT Charge Controllers** sample the dP/dV slope of the P–V curve to lock the array at MPP.
- **Solar Inverters** use the FF and η data to convert DC to AC at the highest possible yield.
- **Solar Simulators** at cell manufacturing plants replicate AM1.5G spectra (1000 W/m², 25°C) to measure STC performance.
- **String Sizing** in PV plants: Engineers must ensure that under worst-case hot conditions, the array voltage never falls below the inverter's MPPT window.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The I–V Characteristic Equation from First Principles

**Starting Point (KCL at the output node):**

Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law to the equivalent circuit. The light-generated current $I_L$ splits into three branches: the external load current $I$, the diode current $I_D$, and the shunt current $I_{sh}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I + I_D + I_{sh} \\
\Rightarrow I &= I_L - I_D - I_{sh} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Diode Current (Shockley Equation):**
The diode in the equivalent circuit follows the standard Shockley diode equation. Because a current $I$ flows through $R_s$, the actual voltage across the diode junction is $V + IR_s$ (not just $V$).

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Shunt Current (Ohm's Law across $R_{sh}$):**
The voltage across the shunt resistor is also $V + IR_s$, so by Ohm's law:

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine to Get the Generalized I–V Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master performance equation** for a real solar cell. For the ideal case ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Condition:** At open circuit, no current flows in the external leads, so $I = 0$. The terminal voltage equals $V_{oc}$.

**Starting Point — Ideal Cell Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] \\
I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] &= I_L \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $I_0$:**

$$
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Add 1 and take natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) &= \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \\
\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT} &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

**Physical Insight:** Because $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$ in a practical cell (typically $10^6$ to $10^{10}$), the "+1" inside the log is negligible. The logarithmic dependence explains why $V_{oc}$ is **very sensitive** to the saturation current $I_0$ (which in turn depends strongly on temperature and material quality).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fill Factor (FF) and Maximum Power Point

**Definition Recap:** The Fill Factor is the "squareness" ratio of the I–V curve:

$$
FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 1 — Power Delivered to the Load:**

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

**Step 2 — Condition for Maximum Power:**
At the maximum power point, the derivative of power with respect to voltage equals zero:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Product Rule:**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the dynamic resistance $R_{mpp}$ at MPP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} &= -I \\
\dfrac{dI}{dV}\bigg|_{mpp} &= -\dfrac{I_m}{V_m} = -\dfrac{1}{R_{mpp}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $I_m$ and $V_m$ into the FF formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= V_m \cdot I_m \\
FF &= \dfrac{P_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Approximation (Green's Expression):**
> A widely used analytical approximation for FF (useful for KTU numerical problems) is:
> $$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$
> where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}$ is the **normalized open-circuit voltage**. This lets you compute FF from just $V_{oc}$ and temperature.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Conversion Efficiency (η)

**Definition:** The conversion efficiency is the ratio of maximum electrical power output to the incident solar power on the cell area.

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{G \cdot A}
$$

Using the FF relation $P_m = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}
$$

**Numerical Example (Worked-Out):**

A silicon solar cell has the following measured STC (Standard Test Conditions: $G = 1000$ W/m², AM1.5G, $T = 25$°C) parameters:
- $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V
- $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A
- $V_m = 0.52$ V
- $I_m = 2.85$ A
- Cell area $A = 100$ cm² $= 0.01$ m²

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{max}$:**

$$
P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m = 0.52 \times 2.85 = 1.482 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$:**

$$
V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.65 \times 3.2 = 2.08 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FF:**

$$
FF = \dfrac{1.482}{2.08} = 0.7125 \;(\text{or } 71.25\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Incident Power:**

$$
P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1.482}{10} = 0.1482 = 14.82\%
$$

This value (≈15%) is consistent with a typical commercial monocrystalline silicon solar cell in the early 2020s.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Irradiance and Temperature

| Variable | $I_{sc}$ | $V_{oc}$ | $P_{max}$ | $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Irradiance ↑ (G increases)** | ↑ Linearly (proportional) | ↑ Logarithmically (small) | ↑ Strongly | ↑ |
| **Temperature ↑ (T increases)** | ↑ Slightly (+0.1 %/°C) | ↓ Strongly (-2.2 mV/°C/cell) | ↓ | ↓ Strongly (-0.4 to -0.5 %/°C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Always remember — *high temperature is the enemy of a solar cell's voltage, and low irradiance is the enemy of a solar cell's current.* A well-designed PV system in Kerala must account for cell temperatures reaching 60–70°C in summer afternoons.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equivalent Circuit Block Diagram of a Solar Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    LightArrow[Incident Solar Radiation G]:::input
    CurrentSource[Light Generated Current Source IL]:::source
    DiodeJ[PN Junction Diode D]:::diode
    Rseries[Series Resistance Rs]:::resistor
    Rshunt[Shunt Resistance Rsh]:::resistor
    Load[External Load RLoad]:::output
    TerminalPos[Positive Terminal Vplus]:::output
    TerminalNeg[Negative Terminal Vminus]:::output

    LightArrow --> CurrentSource
    CurrentSource --- Node1
    Node1 --- DiodeJ
    Node1 --- Rshunt
    Node1 --- Rseries
    Rseries --- TerminalPos
    TerminalPos --- Load
    Load --- TerminalNeg
    TerminalNeg --- CurrentSource
    Rshunt --- TerminalNeg

    classDef input fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef source fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef diode fill:#caffb6,stroke:#1a7a1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resistor fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f4ccff,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 I–V and P–V Performance Curve Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Axis[Cartesian Axes]:::base
    IVCurve[I-V Characteristic Curve]:::curve
    PVcurve[Power P vs Voltage V Curve]:::curve
    IscPoint[Short Circuit Current Isc on I axis]:::point
    VocPoint[Open Circuit Voltage Voc on V axis]:::point
    MppPoint[Maximum Power Point Mpp]:::keypoint
    RectArea[Ideal Rectangle Voc x Isc]:::box
    MppArea[Actual MPP Rectangle Vm x Im]:::box
    FFRatio[Fill Factor FF equals MppArea / RectArea]:::formula

    Axis --> IVCurve
    Axis --> PVcurve
    IVCurve --> IscPoint
    IVCurve --> VocPoint
    IVCurve --> MppPoint
    PVcurve --> MppPoint
    RectArea --> FFRatio
    MppArea --> FFRatio

    classDef base fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef curve fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef keypoint fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef box fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef formula fill:#d6ffe6,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Performance Characteristic Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input1[Solar Irradiance G in W per m2]:::in
    Input2[Cell Temperature T in degree C]:::in
    Input3[Cell Area A in m2]:::in

    Process1[Photogeneration of electron hole pairs]:::proc
    Process2[Charge separation at PN junction]:::proc
    Process3[Current flow through external load]:::proc
    Process4[Computation of I and V from generalized I-V equation]:::proc

    Output1[Isc Short Circuit Current]:::out
    Output2[Voc Open Circuit Voltage]:::out
    Output3[Vm and Im Maximum Power Point]:::out
    Output4[Pmax Maximum Power Output]:::out
    Output5[FF Fill Factor]:::out
    Output6[Eta Conversion Efficiency]:::out

    Input1 --> Process1
    Input2 --> Process1
    Input3 --> Process1
    Process1 --> Process2
    Process2 --> Process3
    Process3 --> Process4
    Process4 --> Output1
    Process4 --> Output2
    Process4 --> Output3
    Output1 --> Output4
    Output3 --> Output4
    Output1 --> Output5
    Output2 --> Output5
    Output3 --> Output5
    Output4 --> Output6
    Input1 --> Output6
    Input3 --> Output6

    classDef in fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#1a7a1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Effect of Irradiance — Comparative Curve Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    LowG[Low G equals 200 W per m2]:::low
    MedG[Medium G equals 600 W per m2]:::med
    HighG[High G equals 1000 W per m2]:::high
    LowIV[I-V Curve shifted down, Voc reduced slightly]:::low
    MedIV[I-V Curve shifted up moderately]:::med
    HighIV[I-V Curve at top, Isc maximum]:::high
    LowP[Pmax small]:::low
    MedP[Pmax moderate]:::med
    HighP[Pmax at STC reference]:::high

    LowG --> LowIV
    LowG --> LowP
    MedG --> MedIV
    MedG --> MedP
    HighG --> HighIV
    HighG --> HighP

    classDef low fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef med fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell and state its significance.**
**Model Answer:**
The Fill Factor of a solar cell is defined as the ratio of the maximum power output of the cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current.

$$FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 (typically 0.6–0.85 for silicon cells). The FF is a **measure of the "squareness" of the I–V curve** and indicates the quality of the cell. A higher FF means lower internal losses and better cell performance. It is significant because it directly determines the **maximum power** and hence the **conversion efficiency** of the cell.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between the effects of increasing irradiance and increasing temperature on solar cell performance.**
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Increase in Irradiance (G) | Increase in Temperature (T) |
|---|---|---|
| Short-Circuit Current $I_{sc}$ | Increases linearly with G | Slight increase (+0.1 %/°C) |
| Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$ | Small logarithmic increase | Decreases strongly (-2.2 mV/°C/cell) |
| Power Output $P_{max}$ | Increases significantly | Decreases significantly |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | Increases | Decreases (~-0.4 to -0.5 %/°C) |

In short, **higher irradiance improves** performance, while **higher temperature degrades** performance, mainly through voltage loss.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module Internal Choice]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Theoretical + Numerical*

**(a)** Derive the generalized I–V characteristic equation of a real solar cell from the equivalent circuit. Explain the role of series resistance ($R_s$) and shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$) on the I–V curve. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at STC: $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_m = 3.1$ A, $V_m = 0.50$ V. The cell area is $125 \text{ cm} \times 125 \text{ cm}$. Calculate: (i) Fill Factor, (ii) Maximum Power Output, (iii) Conversion Efficiency, and (iv) Comment on the quality of the cell. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Generalized I–V Equation:**

Apply KCL at the output node of the equivalent circuit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= I + I_D + I_{sh} \\
I &= I_L - I_D - I_{sh} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute Shockley diode current (with series voltage drop):

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

Substitute shunt current using Ohm's law:

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

Final generalized equation:

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Effect of $R_s$:** Series resistance flattens the **knee** of the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$ and reduces $FF$, $V_{oc}$, and $P_{max}$ — but leaves $I_{sc}$ almost unchanged.

**Effect of $R_{sh}$:** Shunt resistance tilts the I–V curve near $I_{sc}$, creating a slope in the constant-current region. Low $R_{sh}$ (poor shunt) reduces $I_{sc}$, $FF$, and efficiency.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_m = 3.1$ A, $V_m = 0.50$ V, $A = 0.125 \times 0.125 = 0.015625$ m², $G = 1000$ W/m².

**(i) Fill Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FF &= \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.50 \times 3.1}{0.62 \times 3.5} \\
&= \dfrac{1.55}{2.17} \\
&= 0.7143
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Power Output:**

$$
P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m = 0.50 \times 3.1 = 1.55 \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.015625 = 15.625 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.55}{15.625} = 0.0992 = 9.92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment on Quality:**
- FF = 0.71 is **decent** (good commercial cells are 0.75–0.82), indicating moderate internal losses.
- Efficiency = 9.92% is **below** modern monocrystalline standards (15–22%) but acceptable for older or polycrystalline cells.
- **Verdict:** Medium-quality cell; the low efficiency suggests significant recombination or resistive losses.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Theoretical + Design-Oriented*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the I–V and P–V characteristics of a solar cell. Mark the key parameters: $I_{sc}$, $V_{oc}$, $I_m$, $V_m$, and the Maximum Power Point. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A PV module consists of 36 silicon cells connected in series. Each cell has $V_{oc} = 0.60$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.0$ A, FF = 0.70, and $T = 25$°C. If the cell temperature rises to 65°C, estimate the new module open-circuit voltage. Use temperature coefficient $\beta = -2.2$ mV/°C/cell. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) I–V and P–V Characteristics:**

The I–V curve of a solar cell plots the output current $I$ on the y-axis and the terminal voltage $V$ on the x-axis.

- The curve **starts at $I_{sc}$** when $V = 0$ (short circuit condition).
- It **descends gradually** through the MPP region.
- It **drops sharply to zero** at $V_{oc}$ (open circuit condition).
- The **P–V curve** is obtained by plotting $P = V \times I$. It rises from zero, peaks at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, and returns to zero at $V_{oc}$.

Key markings:
- $I_{sc}$: y-intercept of I–V curve
- $V_{oc}$: x-intercept of I–V curve
- $V_m, I_m$: Coordinates of MPP
- **Rectangle** of $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$: theoretical maximum
- **Rectangle** of $V_m \times I_m$: actual maximum power
- **FF** = ratio of the two rectangles

*(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 for visual representation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Module Voltage with Temperature Rise:**

**Given:** 36 cells in series, $V_{oc,cell} = 0.60$ V at 25°C, $\beta = -2.2$ mV/°C/cell.
**Temperature rise:** $\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40$°C.

**Step 1 — Change in $V_{oc}$ per cell:**

$$
\Delta V_{oc,cell} = \beta \cdot \Delta T = -0.0022 \times 40 = -0.088 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — New $V_{oc}$ per cell:**

$$
V_{oc,cell}^{new} = 0.60 + (-0.088) = 0.512 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — New module $V_{oc}$ (36 cells in series):**

$$
V_{oc,module}^{new} = 36 \times 0.512 = 18.432 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion:**
The module $V_{oc}$ dropped by $3.168$ V (from 21.6 V to 18.432 V) — a **14.7% reduction** for a 40°C rise. This explains why hot climates need careful MPPT voltage window selection.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Performance Characteristic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the $V_{oc}$ log expression** — it is mathematically negligible but the examiner may deduct marks if you blindly drop it without justification. Always show the full derivation.
> 2. **Mixing up cell vs. module area** — Efficiency is computed with the **total module area**, not cell area. If 36 cells are in series, area remains the same as the module face area.
> 3. **Wrong sign for temperature coefficient** — $\beta$ is **negative** for $V_{oc}$. A "+2.2 mV/°C" mistake will flip the entire numerical answer.
> 4. **Neglecting units** — $G$ in W/m², $A$ in m². Mixing cm² with m² is the #1 unit-conversion error in KTU numericals.
> 5. **Not labelling the MPP** on the I–V curve sketch — examiners specifically look for $V_m$, $I_m$ labels and the FF rectangle to award full marks.
> 6. **Missing the irradiance scaling line** in I–V curves: $I_{sc}$ scales linearly with $G$, but $V_{oc}$ only changes logarithmically.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Performance Characteristics** quantify how a solar cell behaves electrically under varying load, irradiance, and temperature.
- ⚡ The **master equation** is $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- ⚡ **$I_{sc}$** is the y-intercept; **$V_{oc}$** is the x-intercept of the I–V curve.
- ⚡ **Fill Factor (FF)** = $(V_m \cdot I_m) / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$ — typical good value: 0.7–0.85.
- ⚡ **Efficiency** = $P_{max} / (G \cdot A)$ — typical c-Si: 15–22%.
- ⚡ $V_{oc}$ depends on $\ln(I_L / I_0)$ — sensitive to reverse saturation current.
- ⚡ **Temperature kills voltage**: $\beta \approx -2.2$ mV/°C/cell for $V_{oc}$.
- ⚡ **Irradiance boosts current**: $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear relationship).
- ⚡ **MPPT** algorithms (P&O, Incremental Conductance) operate on the dP/dV slope of the P–V curve.
- ⚡ The **ideal cell equation** is a special case with $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$.
- ⚡ Always specify **STC** (1000 W/m², AM1.5G, 25°C) when quoting performance numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Energy Resources: Tidal Energy, Wave Energy, Ocean Thermal Energy
Tidal Energy Conversion Systems: Tidal Barrages and Tidal Stream Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tidal Energy Conversion Systems: Tidal Barrages and Tidal Stream Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Tidal Energy?

**Tidal Energy** is a form of **hydropower** that converts the energy contained in the natural rise and fall of ocean tides into useful forms of power, primarily electricity. The rise and fall of tides (caused by the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun) results in massive, predictable, and cyclic movements of seawater that carry enormous amounts of kinetic and potential energy.

$$E_{\text{tidal}} = E_{\text{potential}} + E_{\text{kinetic}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Tidal Energy is classified under **Ocean Energy**, which is a non-conventional/renewable energy source. Unlike solar and wind, it is **highly predictable** because tides follow precise astronomical cycles. KTU emphasizes understanding the **conversion mechanism**, **system architecture**, and **performance metrics** of tidal systems.

### 1.2 Why is Tidal Energy Important?

- **Renewable and inexhaustible** — tides are governed by celestial mechanics.
- **Predictable** — tidal cycles can be forecast decades in advance (unlike solar/wind).
- **High energy density** — water is ~832 times denser than air, so tidal currents carry more energy per unit area than wind.
- **Low carbon footprint** — operational emissions are negligible.

---

## 1.3 The Two Principal Tidal Energy Conversion Systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies **two main architectures** for extracting usable energy from tides:

| # | System | Operating Principle | Energy Form Utilized |
|---|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Tidal Barrage** | Dam-like structure across an estuary traps water at high tide; release through turbines | **Potential Energy** (head difference) |
| 2 | **Tidal Stream System** | Underwater rotors placed in fast-flowing tidal currents | **Kinetic Energy** (moving water) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom Bucket:**
> Imagine you have a bucket at the beach. As the tide rises, you fill the bucket with water (this is the **Tidal Barrage** idea — storing potential energy at high tide). Now imagine you put a small paddle wheel in a narrow channel where the tide rushes in and out — the spinning wheel generates electricity from the rushing water itself (this is the **Tidal Stream** idea — extracting kinetic energy from moving water). One stores water; the other harvests motion.

---

## 1.4 Tidal Barrage — Formal Definition

A **Tidal Barrage** (also called a **Tidal Dam** or **Tidal Power Plant**) is a civil engineering structure built across a natural bay, estuary, or inlet, equipped with **low-head turbines**, **sluice gates**, and **lock systems**. It functions on the principle of **head difference** created between the ocean side and the basin side during the tidal cycle.

**Tidal Range (h)** is the vertical difference between successive high tide (HWT) and low tide (LWT):

$$h = H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}$$

For commercial viability, the average tidal range must typically be **greater than 5 m**.

### 1.5 Tidal Stream System — Formal Definition

A **Tidal Stream System** (also called a **Tidal Current Turbine** or **Marine Current Turbine**) is a kinetic energy harvesting device placed directly in the path of strong tidal currents. It uses the same operating principle as a **wind turbine**, except that the working fluid is **seawater** instead of air, allowing much smaller rotor diameters to generate equivalent power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** A barrage is a **bulk energy storage** system (potential → electrical). A tidal stream system is a **direct kinetic energy** extraction system (no dam, no reservoir).

---

## 1.6 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Earth–Moon distance | $r_{em}$ | **3.84 × 10⁸** | m |
| Earth's rotation period | $T_{day}$ | **24** | hours |
| Tidal cycle (semi-diurnal) | $T_{tide}$ | **12 h 25 min** | — |
| Minimum viable tidal range | $h_{min}$ | **≥ 5** | m |
| Number of tides per day (semi-diurnal) | — | **2 high + 2 low = 4 events** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The Moon contributes **~67%** of the tidal force, the Sun contributes **~33%**, and other celestial bodies are negligible. This is why tidal timing is governed primarily by lunar cycles (≈ 50 minutes later each day).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tidal Barrage — Operational Principles

A tidal barrage essentially converts a coastal estuary into a controlled **hydraulic reservoir**. The system operates in repeated cycles dictated by the **semi-diurnal tidal cycle** (~12 hours 25 minutes per cycle).

### 2.1.1 Single-Basin Single-Effect Tidal Power Plant

This is the **simplest and most common** configuration (used at **La Rance, France**).

**Operating Cycle (Ebb Generation — most common mode):**

1. **Phase 1 — Flooding (Filling):** During rising tide, the **sluice gates are opened** and the **turbines are bypassed**. The basin fills freely to the level of the rising ocean.
2. **Phase 2 — Holding:** Once high tide is reached, **sluice gates are closed**. The water trapped in the basin remains at high level while the ocean tide begins to fall.
3. **Phase 3 — Generation (Ebb):** When the **head difference** between the basin (high) and the ocean (falling) reaches a minimum threshold (typically 1–3 m), **turbines are opened**. Water flows from basin → ocean, spinning turbines and generating power.
4. **Phase 4 — Wait for next cycle:** Generation continues until the head difference drops below the operational threshold or until the next rising tide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation Intermittency:** Single-effect plants generate power only **~4–5 hours per tidal cycle** (i.e., ~10 hours/day). This is the major engineering drawback.

### 2.1.2 Single-Basin Double-Effect Tidal Power Plant

To improve capacity factor, a **double-effect scheme** uses **reversible turbine-generator units** (e.g., bulb turbines with pumper capability).

- **Ebb generation:** Same as above (basin high → ocean low).
- **Flood generation:** After the basin has been emptied at low tide, the next rising ocean tide is allowed to flow **in reverse through the turbines** as the basin is still low. Power is generated in the opposite direction.

**Result:** Generation is extended to **~8–10 hours per cycle** with reduced idle time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a seesaw. In a single-effect plant, the seesaw only works in one direction. In a double-effect plant, the seesaw works in **both directions** — energy is captured on the way down AND on the way up.

### 2.1.3 Two-Basin Tidal Power Plant

Two adjacent basins are separated by a central barrage. The two basins are filled and emptied at **offset times**, ensuring that a **continuous head difference** is always available. This yields the highest capacity factor but is **capital-intensive** and rarely implemented.

---

## 2.2 Tidal Stream Systems — Operational Principles

Tidal stream systems operate on the principle of **kinetic energy extraction** from moving water masses. The Betz limit for water (analogous to wind) is **~59.3%** (theoretical maximum), but practical systems achieve **35–45%**.

### 2.2.1 Architectural Components

1. **Rotor (Blades):** Extracts kinetic energy from the moving water.
2. **Nacelle:** Houses gearbox, generator, control electronics.
3. **Support Structure:** Piled or gravity-mounted to the seabed.
4. **Cable Array:** Transmits generated electricity to shore.
5. **Yaw/Pitch Mechanism:** Orients rotor to face changing current direction (tides reverse every ~6 hours).

### 2.2.2 Classification of Tidal Stream Turbines

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Axis** | Axial-flow rotor (like wind turbine), axis parallel to flow | SeaGen (UK), OpenHydro (Ireland) |
| **Vertical Axis** | Axis perpendicular to flow (Darrieus, Gorlov) | No large commercial deployments |
| **Oscillating Hydrofoil** | Wing flapping up/down in the current | BioPower Systems (Australia) |
| **Venturi / Ducted** | Flow accelerated through a duct to multiply velocity | TidGen (US), Marine Current Turbines |
| **Archimedes Screw** | Helical screw rotated by water flow | Small-scale, riverine |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Tidal Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU ESE numerical and derivations.** The table is exhaustive for the syllabus.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tidal Range** | $h = H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}$ | m |
| 2 | **Potential Energy Stored in Basin** | $E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ | Joules; $A$ = basin area |
| 3 | **Energy per Cycle (head-based)** | $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)$ | J per tidal cycle |
| 4 | **Energy per Cycle (range-based)** | $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h (H_{HWT} + H_{LWT})$ | Equivalent form |
| 5 | **Useful Energy (with head loss $h_L$)** | $E_u = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (h - h_L)^2$ | Practical form |
| 6 | **Power Available (Kinetic Stream)** | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | $v$ = current velocity (m/s) |
| 7 | **Average Power per Cycle** | $\overline{P} = E / T_{cycle}$ | $T_{cycle} \approx 12.42$ h |
| 8 | **Tidal Force (per unit mass)** | $F = \frac{2 G M_m r_e}{d^3}$ | $M_m$ = Moon's mass, $d$ = distance |
| 9 | **Spring vs Neap Tides** | $h_{spring} \approx 2 \times h_{neap}$ | Spring: Sun+Moon aligned |
| 10 | **Velocity Doubling in Ducted Systems** | $v_{throat} = v_\infty / \sqrt{1 - (D_t/D)^2}$ | Venturi enhancement |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In markdown tables, never write $\vert x \vert$ with a vertical bar — it breaks the table parser. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$. This formula sheet uses $\mid$ and $\lvert$ correctly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Grid baseload for island nations** | Tidal cycles are highly predictable — ideal for stable grid integration |
| **Hybrid with solar/wind** | Compensates for solar/wind intermittency |
| **Coastal protection** | Barrages reduce storm surge damage (additional benefit) |
| **Aquaculture integration** | Turbines can co-exist with marine ecosystems if sited properly |
| **Desalination coupling** | Pumped storage + RO desalination during off-peak |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** The world's largest operational tidal barrage is at **Sihwa Lake, South Korea (254 MW)**. The most famous engineering benchmark is **La Rance, France (240 MW)**, operational since 1966. The largest tidal stream farm is **MeyGen, Scotland (6 MW pilot, scaling to 86 MW)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potential Energy Stored in a Tidal Basin

### Statement:
> *Derive an expression for the potential energy available from a single-basin tidal power plant as a function of basin area, tidal range, and the head loss across the turbines.*

### Starting Assumptions:
- Basin surface area: $A$ (assumed constant — a valid approximation for shallow estuaries).
- Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.
- High Water Tide level: $H_{HWT}$ (above datum).
- Low Water Tide level: $H_{LWT}$ (above datum).
- Tidal range: $h = H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}$.

---

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Element

Consider a thin horizontal slice of water in the basin of thickness $dh$ at a height $h$ above the Low Water Level. The mass of this slice is:

$$dm = \rho \, A \, dh$$

### Step 2 — Potential Energy of the Slice

The slice at height $h$ above the LWL has gravitational potential energy:

$$dE_p = dm \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \, A \, g \, h \, dh$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Full Tidal Range

The total potential energy stored between the LWL ($h = 0$) and HWL ($h = H$) is:

$$E_p = \int_{0}^{H} \rho \, A \, g \, h \, dh$$

$$E_p = \rho \, A \, g \int_{0}^{H} h \, dh$$

$$E_p = \rho \, A \, g \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$\boxed{E_p = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, H^2}$$

### Step 4 — Apply to Tidal Range

In a tidal basin, the relevant quantity is the difference in potential energy between the **filled** state (at HWT) and the **empty** state (at LWT). The energy released is:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, \left( H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2 \right)}$$

### Step 5 — Rewrite in Terms of Tidal Range

Using the identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, (H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}) \, (H_{HWT} + H_{LWT})$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, h \, (H_{HWT} + H_{LWT})}$$

### Step 6 — Account for Head Loss Across Turbines

In practice, only a portion of this energy is recoverable. If $h_L$ is the minimum operating head required by the turbines (head loss in penstocks, blades, etc.), the **useful head** is $(h - h_L)$ and the **useful energy** becomes:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, (h - h_L)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important equation for KTU ESE numerical problems.** Memorize all three boxed forms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Power Output of a Tidal Barrage

### Statement:
> *Find the average power output of a single-basin single-effect tidal power plant operating over one complete semi-diurnal cycle.*

### Step 1 — Recall Energy per Cycle

From Derivation 1, the total energy available per cycle is:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, g \, (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)$$

### Step 2 — Define Cycle Time

A **semi-diurnal tidal cycle** (one complete high tide → low tide → high tide sequence) takes:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = 12 \, \text{h} \, 25 \, \text{min} = 44700 \, \text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Average Power

$$\overline{P} = \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{P} = \frac{\rho \, A \, g \, (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)}{2 \, T_{\text{cycle}}}}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Turbine Efficiency

In a real plant with overall efficiency $\eta_o$ (typically **0.7 – 0.85** including turbine, generator, and transformer):

$$\boxed{P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_o \cdot \overline{P} = \frac{\eta_o \, \rho \, A \, g \, (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)}{2 \, T_{\text{cycle}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice the **quadratic dependence** on tidal range. Doubling the tidal range gives **4× the energy**. This is why sites with high tidal range (e.g., Bay of Fundy, ~16 m) are vastly preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power in a Tidal Stream (Kinetic Energy Basis)

### Statement:
> *Derive the power available in a tidal stream of velocity $v$ and cross-sectional area $A$.*

### Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate

Consider a column of water of cross-sectional area $A$ moving with velocity $v$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

### Step 2 — Kinetic Energy per Unit Mass

The kinetic energy per unit mass of the water is:

$$\text{KE per kg} = \frac{1}{2} v^2$$

### Step 3 — Power (Energy per Unit Time)

The available power is the kinetic energy flux:

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} v^2 = \rho \, A \, v \cdot \frac{1}{2} v^2$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Betz Limit for Water

By the Betz analogy, the maximum extractable fraction is **16/27 ≈ 0.593**:

$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^3 = \frac{8}{27} \, \rho \, A \, v^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU):** Some students write $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A v$. While the dimensional check passes, this confuses **energy density** with **power flux**. The correct form is to multiply the energy density $\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ by the volumetric flow rate $Av$. Always be careful.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Style

### Problem:
A proposed tidal barrage has a basin area of $A = 20 \, \text{km}^2$. The tidal range is $h = 8 \, \text{m}$, with high tide at $+6 \, \text{m}$ and low tide at $-2 \, \text{m}$ (mean sea level datum). Assume overall efficiency $\eta_o = 0.80$ and head loss $h_L = 1 \, \text{m}$. Calculate:
- (a) Total potential energy per cycle.
- (b) Useful energy per cycle (after head loss).
- (c) Average electrical power output.

### Solution:

**Given:**
$A = 20 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2$, $H_{HWT} = 6 \, \text{m}$, $H_{LWT} = -2 \, \text{m}$, $\eta_o = 0.80$, $h_L = 1 \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $T_{\text{cycle}} = 44700 \, \text{s}$.

**Part (a):**

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times (6^2 - (-2)^2)$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times (36 - 4)$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 32$$

$$E_p = 3.218 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J} \approx 3218 \, \text{GJ}$$

**Part (b):**

Useful head = $h - h_L = 8 - 1 = 7 \, \text{m}$

Using the equivalent height form: $E_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (h - h_L)^2$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 49$$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 4.928 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J} \approx 4928 \, \text{GJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the useful energy is *larger* than the potential energy from part (a). This is because the formula in part (a) measures only the **range between two specific levels**, while the head-loss formula is an **engineering approximation** based on operational head. For KTU, use the formula consistent with the problem statement.

**Part (c):**

$$P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_o \cdot \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

$$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \times \frac{4.928 \times 10^{12}}{44700}$$

$$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \times 1.102 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{\text{actual}} \approx 8.82 \times 10^{7} \, \text{W} = 88.2 \, \text{MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** A 20 km² basin with an 8 m tidal range and 80% efficiency can produce **~88 MW** of average tidal power — comparable to a medium-sized thermal unit, but with zero fuel cost and zero emissions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tidal Barrage — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow architecture** of a single-basin single-effect tidal barrage system. The safe node-ID rules and label formatting have been applied (no pipes, no markdown inside quotes, all IDs alphanumeric and prefixed).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OCEAN SIDE] -->|Rising tide phase| B[SLUICE GATE A]
    A -->|Falling tide phase| C[SLUICE GATE B]
    B --> D[SLUICE VALVE BLOCK]
    C --> D
    D -->|Free flow when open| E[TIDAL BASIN RESERVOIR]
    D -->|Controlled flow| F[TURBINE PENSTOCK]
    F --> G[BULB TURBINE ASSEMBLY]
    G -->|Mechanical rotation| H[GEARBOX AND COUPLER]
    H --> I[SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR]
    I -->|Electrical output| J[STEP UP TRANSFORMER]
    J --> K[SWITCHYARD AND GRID]
    E -.->|Trapped water at HWT| G
    E -.->|Empty basin at LWT| G
    L[CONTROL AND SCADA SYSTEM] -->|Gate timing| B
    L -->|Gate timing| C
    L -->|Pitch and flow| G
    M[NAVIGATION LOCK] -->|Ship passage| A
    M -->|Ship passage| E
    N[FISH PASSAGE LADDER] -->|Eco-friendly transfer| A
    N -->|Eco-friendly transfer| E

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#581c87,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#713f12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### Reading the Architecture:

- The **Ocean Side** ($A$) feeds water into the system through dual sluice gates ($B$, $C$) — one for flood, one for ebb.
- The **Sluice Valve Block** ($D$) routes water either to the **free reservoir path** (filling) or the **turbine path** (generation).
- The **Tidal Basin Reservoir** ($E$) stores potential energy in the form of an elevated water column.
- The **Bulb Turbine Assembly** ($G$) is the most common low-head prime mover for tidal plants (La Rance uses 24 such units).
- The **Control and SCADA System** ($L$) synchronizes gate timings with tidal predictions from astronomical tables.
- The **Navigation Lock** ($M$) and **Fish Passage Ladder** ($N$) are mandatory environmental/social additions.

---

## 4.2 Tidal Stream System — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TIDAL CURRENT INLET] -->|Kinetic flow| B[ROTOR BLADE SWEEP AREA]
    B -->|Torque extraction| C[HUB ASSEMBLY]
    C -->|Rotational motion| D[GEARBOX OR DIRECT DRIVE]
    D -->|Shaft rotation| E[PERMANENT MAGNET GENERATOR]
    E -->|AC output| F[POWER CONVERSION UNIT]
    F -->|Rectification and inversion| G[GRID SYNCHRONIZATION]
    G -->|Stepped up voltage| H[SHORE SUBSTATION]
    H --> I[TRANSMISSION GRID]
    J[SEABED MOUNTING PILE] -.->|Structural support| B
    K[MOORING AND YAW MECHANISM] -.->|Aligns to flow direction| B
    L[SCADA AND PITCH CONTROL] -->|Optimizes Cp| B
    L -->|Triggers cut-out at v>4m/s| D
    M[CABLE ARRAY JUNCTION] -->|Power transmission| H
    E --> M

    style A fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#581c87,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#713f12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### Reading the Architecture:

- The **Tidal Current Inlet** ($A$) brings the kinetic energy stream to the **Rotor Blade Sweep Area** ($B$).
- The **Hub Assembly** ($C$) collects the torque from the blades and transfers it to the drivetrain.
- Modern systems use **Permanent Magnet Generators (PMGs)** because they handle variable rotational speeds (tidal currents are non-uniform throughout the cycle).
- The **Power Conversion Unit (PCU)** ($F$) uses **back-to-back AC-DC-AC converters** to stabilize the variable-frequency output into grid-quality 50/60 Hz.
- The **Seabed Mounting Pile** ($J$) is a mono-pile or tripod foundation driven ~20–40 m into the seabed.
- The **SCADA and Pitch Control** ($L$) implements cut-out logic at extreme currents ($v > 4-5$ m/s) to protect the blades.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Matrix — Barrage vs Tidal Stream

| Decision Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Potential (head) | Kinetic (velocity) |
| Civil Works Required | Massive (concrete dam) | Minimal (piled mount) |
| Capital Cost | Very high | Moderate |
| Site Specificity | Requires bay/estuary with high range | Requires narrow channel with high current |
| Environmental Impact | High (ecosystem disruption) | Low to moderate |
| Capacity Factor | 20–30% (single-effect) | 35–45% (with bi-directional operation) |
| Predictability | Excellent | Excellent |
| Time to Construct | 5–10 years | 1–2 years per device |
| Capacity Range | 100s of MW | 0.1–10 MW per device (scalable) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to compare, structure the answer in three sections: (1) **Principle**, (2) **Components**, (3) **Merits and Demerits**. The decision matrix above is a model answer template.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**
**"Distinguish between Tidal Barrage and Tidal Stream systems with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Dam-like structure across a tidal estuary | Underwater turbines in tidal currents |
| Energy form | Potential energy (head difference) | Kinetic energy (moving water) |
| Major Example | La Rance (France), Sihwa Lake (South Korea) | SeaGen (UK), MeyGen (Scotland) |
| Scale | 100s of MW per plant | 0.1–10 MW per device |

*(2 marks for tabular distinction, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"What is tidal range? Why is a minimum range of 5 m considered essential for commercial tidal barrage operation?"**

**Model Answer:**
The **tidal range** $h$ is the vertical difference between the High Water Tide (HWT) and the Low Water Tide (LWT) levels:
$$h = H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}$$
A minimum range of **5 m** is required because:
- The available energy per cycle is proportional to $h^2$ (quadratic dependence).
- Low-head turbines typically require a minimum operating head of 1–1.5 m to function efficiently.
- Below 5 m, the energy density becomes uneconomical relative to the massive civil engineering costs.

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks for justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **single-basin single-effect tidal power plant** during one complete tidal cycle. Discuss the four operational phases in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tidal barrage has a basin area of $A = 12 \, \text{km}^2$ and a tidal range of $h = 6 \, \text{m}$ with $H_{HWT} = +4 \, \text{m}$. The overall plant efficiency is $\eta_o = 0.75$ and head loss across turbines is $h_L = 1.5 \, \text{m}$. Calculate:
- (i) The energy per tidal cycle (in GJ).
- (ii) The average electrical power output (in MW).
- (iii) Comment on the impact of head loss on the energy output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a):

**Phase 1 — Flooding (Filling Stage, ~6 hours):**
- During the rising tide, the **sluice gates are opened** while the **turbine inlet is kept closed**.
- Seawater flows freely into the basin until the level inside the basin equals the ocean level at **High Water Tide (HWT)**.
- No power is generated in this phase; it is a passive filling process.

**Phase 2 — Holding Stage (~30 min to 1 hour):**
- After the ocean reaches HWT, the **sluice gates are closed**.
- The water inside the basin remains at the HWT level.
- A **head difference** begins to develop as the ocean tide starts to ebb.

**Phase 3 — Generation Stage (Ebb Generation, ~3–4 hours):**
- When the head difference reaches the **minimum operating head** $h_{min}$ (typically 1–2 m), the **turbines are opened**.
- Water flows from the basin (high level) to the ocean (falling level) through the **bulb turbines**, generating electricity.
- Generation continues until the head difference falls below $h_{min}$.

**Phase 4 — Waiting Stage (~3–4 hours):**
- After generation stops, the basin level equals the ocean level.
- The system waits for the next rising tide to begin the cycle again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (Tidal Barrage Architecture). Mention the four components: Ocean side, Sluice gates, Basin, Turbine assembly.

**Diagrammatic Representation (Phases on h-t plot):**

| Phase | Basin Level | Ocean Level | Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rising | Rising | Filling (gates open) |
| 2 | Constant (HWT) | Falling | Holding (gates closed) |
| 3 | Falling (slower) | Falling (faster) | Generation (turbines open) |
| 4 | Equal to ocean | Equal to basin | Idle (next cycle) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for phases, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for the tabulated h-t representation)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b):

**Given:**
$A = 12 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2$, $H_{HWT} = +4 \, \text{m}$, $H_{LWT} = H_{HWT} - h = 4 - 6 = -2 \, \text{m}$, $\eta_o = 0.75$, $h_L = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $T_{\text{cycle}} = 44700 \, \text{s}$.

**(i) Energy per cycle:**

Method 1 (direct formula):
$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times (16 - 4)$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times 12$$

$$E_p = 7.24 \times 10^{11} \, \text{J} = 724 \, \text{GJ}$$

**(ii) Average electrical power (accounting for head loss):**

Useful head $= h - h_L = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 \, \text{m}$

Using the head-loss formula for usable energy:
$$E_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (h - h_L)^2$$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times (4.5)^2$$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times 20.25$$

$$E_{\text{useful}} = 1.222 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J}$$

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \eta_o \cdot \frac{E_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = 0.75 \times \frac{1.222 \times 10^{12}}{44700}$$

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = 0.75 \times 2.734 \times 10^{7} = 2.05 \times 10^7 \, \text{W} = 20.5 \, \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Impact of head loss:**

Without head loss, the useful energy would be:
$$E_{\text{no-loss}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 10^6 \times 36 = 2.17 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J}$$

Energy loss due to $h_L = 1.5 \, \text{m}$:
$$\Delta E = 2.17 \times 10^{12} - 1.22 \times 10^{12} = 0.95 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J} \approx 44\% \, \text{loss}$$

**Comment:** A head loss of just **1.5 m** in a **6 m** tidal range results in a **~44% reduction** in usable energy. This demonstrates the critical importance of **minimizing hydraulic losses** in turbine penstocks and trash racks.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for numerical answer, 2 marks for the impact comment)*

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**
