**(a)** Explain the principle of **kinetic energy extraction** in a tidal stream system. Compare its performance with a **wind turbine** of equivalent swept area and discuss the role of the **Betz limit**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tidal stream turbine has a rotor diameter $D = 20 \, \text{m}$ and operates in a current of $v = 2.5 \, \text{m/s}$. The coefficient of performance is $C_p = 0.40$. Calculate:
- (i) The swept area and the available kinetic power in the stream.
- (ii) The actual electrical power output assuming generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.92$.
- (iii) What would be the power output if the velocity increased to $3.0 \, \text{m/s}$? Comment on the velocity sensitivity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a):

**Principle of Kinetic Energy Extraction:**

A tidal stream system extracts the **kinetic energy of moving seawater** using a rotor analogous to a wind turbine. The fundamental relationship for available power per unit area is:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3$$

When the rotor intercepts the flow, it extracts a fraction of this power quantified by the **power coefficient** $C_p$:

$$P_{\text{rotor}} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Betz Limit Application:**

By conservation of mass and momentum, the maximum fraction of kinetic energy that **any** fluid-flow turbine can extract is **16/27 ≈ 0.593**. This is known as the **Betz limit** (or Lanchester-Betz limit). For water, the numerical value is the same as for air, but the **absolute power is ~832× higher** because $\rho_{\text{water}} \approx 832 \rho_{\text{air}}$.

**Comparison with a Wind Turbine (Equivalent Swept Area):**

| Parameter | Wind Turbine | Tidal Stream Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid density | $\sim 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ | $\sim 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Power per unit area at 5 m/s | $\sim 76.6 \, \text{W/m}^2$ | $\sim 64{,}062 \, \text{W/m}^2$ |
| Practical $C_p$ | 0.35–0.45 | 0.35–0.45 |
| Blade tip speed limit | ~80 m/s | ~12 m/s (cavitation) |
| Capacity factor | 25–40% | 35–50% (more predictable) |

**Key Engineering Insight:** Although the $C_p$ values are similar, the **same swept area** produces **~836 times more power** in water than in air (because of the density ratio). This is why tidal stream rotors are **much smaller** than wind rotors for the same output.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for principle, 2 marks for Betz, 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for engineering insight)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b):

**Given:**
$D = 20 \, \text{m}$, $v_1 = 2.5 \, \text{m/s}$, $C_p = 0.40$, $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Swept area and available kinetic power:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$P_{\text{available}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times (2.5)^3$$

$$P_{\text{available}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times 15.625 = 2.516 \times 10^6 \, \text{W} = 2.52 \, \text{MW}$$

**(ii) Actual electrical power output:**

$$P_{\text{rotor}} = C_p \cdot P_{\text{available}} = 0.40 \times 2.516 \times 10^6 = 1.006 \times 10^6 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \eta_g \cdot P_{\text{rotor}} = 0.92 \times 1.006 \times 10^6 = 9.26 \times 10^5 \, \text{W} \approx 0.926 \, \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Power at $v_2 = 3.0 \, \text{m/s}$:**

$$P_{\text{available,2}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times (3.0)^3 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times 27 = 4.35 \times 10^6 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{\text{electrical,2}} = \eta_g \cdot C_p \cdot P_{\text{available,2}} = 0.92 \times 0.40 \times 4.35 \times 10^6 = 1.60 \times 10^6 \, \text{W} \approx 1.60 \, \text{MW}$$

**Comment on velocity sensitivity:**

The power ratio is:
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{3.0}{2.5}\right)^3 = (1.2)^3 = 1.728$$

A **20% increase in velocity** results in a **72.8% increase in power**. This is the **cube-law sensitivity**, and it is the single most important design consideration in tidal stream engineering. Small uncertainties in resource assessment translate to large uncertainties in power output.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 2 marks for part (iii) calculation, 1 mark for the cube-law comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $h$ and $H_{HWT}$:** The tidal range $h$ is the *difference* between HWT and LWT, not the absolute level. Writing $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A h^2$ with $h$ as the absolute level is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency:** Energy available $\neq$ energy delivered. Always multiply by $\eta_o$.
> 3. **Forgetting the cube law:** In tidal stream problems, remember $P \propto v^3$. Linear scaling gives **zero marks** on KTU numericals.
> 4. **Mixing barrage and stream formulas:** A barrage uses potential energy (head). A stream uses kinetic energy ($v^3$). **Do not interchange** them.
> 5. **Ignoring the cycle time:** $T_{\text{cycle}} = 44700$ s, not 12 h × 3600 = 43200 s. The difference of 25 minutes is intentional (Moon's orbital lag).
> 6. **Cycle timing for double-effect:** In double-effect plants, energy is harvested in BOTH directions, so the effective cycle time is halved, doubling average power for the same installation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this section before every KTU exam. These are the high-frequency recall items.**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] Tidal Energy = Potential + Kinetic energy of seawater tides
- [ ] Tidal Barrage = Dam-based, potential energy system
- [ ] Tidal Stream = Turbine-based, kinetic energy system
- [ ] Tidal Range = $H_{HWT} - H_{LWT}$ (commercial minimum: 5 m)
- [ ] Semi-diurnal cycle = 12 h 25 min (44,700 s)

### Master Formulas (Mandatory Memorization)
- [ ] $E_{\text{barrage}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (H_{HWT}^2 - H_{LWT}^2)$
- [ ] $E_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A (h - h_L)^2$
- [ ] $P_{\text{stream}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ (available)
- [ ] $P_{\max} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A v^3$ (Betz limit)
- [ ] $\overline{P} = E / T_{\text{cycle}}$ (average)

### Tidal Barrage — Three Configurations
- [ ] **Single-basin single-effect:** Ebb generation only (~10 h/day)
- [ ] **Single-basin double-effect:** Ebb + flood generation (~16 h/day) using reversible bulb turbines
- [ ] **Two-basin:** Two adjacent basins offset in time, continuous generation (rare)

### Tidal Stream — Key Facts
- [ ] Power coefficient $C_p$ practical range: 0.35–0.45
- [ ] Betz limit: 16/27 ≈ 59.3%
- [ ] Water density: 1025 kg/m³ (832× air)
- [ ] Cube law: $P \propto v^3$
- [ ] Cut-out velocity: typically 4–5 m/s (cavitation protection)

### Real-World Benchmarks
- [ ] **La Rance, France** — 240 MW, world's first large-scale (1966)
- [ ] **Sihwa Lake, South Korea** — 254 MW, world's largest
- [ ] **Annapolis Royal, Canada** — 20 MW (Straflo turbine)
- [ ] **MeyGen, Scotland** — World's largest tidal stream farm (86 MW target)

### Advantages of Tidal Energy
- [ ] Predictable (astronomical cycles)
- [ ] Zero fuel cost, zero emissions
- [ ] High energy density
- [ ] Long plant life (60–100 years for barrages)

### Disadvantages of Tidal Energy
- [ ] High capital cost
- [ ] Site-specific (geographic constraints)
- [ ] Environmental impact (barrage ecosystems)
- [ ] Intermittency (single-effect plants)
- [ ] Grid integration challenges (4 cycles per day)
- [ ] Saline corrosion of equipment

### KTU Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Always use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (not 1000)
- [ ] Always use $T_{\text{cycle}} = 44700$ s (not 43200 s)
- [ ] Always check whether the question uses absolute levels or range
- [ ] Always apply efficiency $\eta_o$ before reporting electrical output
- [ ] In stream problems, $P \propto v^3$ is non-negotiable
- [ ] Unit consistency: J for energy, W for power, MW for large plants
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Energy Conversion Systems: Oscillating Water Columns, Point Absorbers, and Overtopping Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wave Energy Conversion Systems: Oscillating Water Columns, Point Absorbers, and Overtopping Devices

# 🌊 Wave Energy Conversion Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wave Energy Conversion (WEC)** refers to the technology used to capture the kinetic and potential energy contained in ocean surface waves and convert it into useful electrical energy, typically through a mechanical power take-off (PTO) system coupled to a generator.

The available **wave power per unit crest width** in deep water is given by:

$$P = \frac{\rho \, g^{2} \, H^{2} \, T}{64 \pi} \quad \text{(W/m)}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of seawater (≈ **1025 kg/m³**), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**), $H$ is the significant wave height, and $T$ is the wave period.

The **specific power** equation can be simplified to the commonly used engineering approximation:

$$P \approx 0.49 \, H^{2} \, T \quad \text{(kW/m)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish between three principal WEC classes — *Oscillating Water Columns (OWC)*, *Point Absorbers*, and *Overtopping Devices* — along with their representative real-world installations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing gently on a plastic bottle half-filled with water, but the air column above the water is forced to escape through a small hole at the top — that escaping air can spin a tiny windmill. This is essentially how an **Oscillating Water Column** works, except that the ocean wave itself does the "blowing" by alternately pushing the water column up and pulling it down inside a hollow chamber.

A **point absorber** is like a small cork bobbing up and down on a swimming pool's surface — the cork moves many times more than the wave's height when its natural frequency is tuned properly (called *heave resonance*).

An **overtopping device** is like a child's wave bucket on a beach — when the wave rushes up and over a ramp, it spills into a raised reservoir, and that elevated water is later released back through a small hydro-turbine, similar to a conventional micro-hydro setup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Wave energy is a *concentrated* form of solar energy. Although waves are diluted along the coastline, the **energy flux** in a typical oceanic wave is roughly **30–70 kW/m of wave crest** — about 20–50 times more powerful than wind or solar per unit surface area.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Wave Energy Converters (Structural Breakdown)

WEC systems are broadly classified based on their position relative to the shoreline and their method of energy extraction:

**A. By Location**
- **Onshore (Shoreline):** Built on the coast or in shallow water. Example: LIMPET, Mutriku, Pico Plant.
- **Nearshore (Intermediate water):** Moored in water depths of 10–25 m.
- **Offshore (Deep water):** Floating or fully submerged. Example: PowerBuoy, Pelamis.

**B. By Orientation & Working Principle**
1. **Oscillating Water Columns (OWC)** – Pneumatic air-driven systems.
2. **Point Absorbers** – Small buoy-type bodies (diameter < wavelength/4) oscillating in heave.
3. **Overtopping Devices** – Wave-fed reservoirs with low-head hydro turbines.
4. **Oscillating Bodies** – Flap/pellet devices (e.g., Oyster, Pelamis).
5. **Attenuators** – Long multi-segment floating bodies aligned with wave direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wave energy is highly **predictable** (compared to wind/solar) because wave climates are determined by long-fetch wind patterns. This gives WEC a higher **capacity factor** (typically **25–40%**) than solar PV (~20%) in similar coastal locations.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave energy density (deep water) | $E = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ | J/m² (energy per unit sea-surface area) |
| 2 | Wave power per unit crest width | $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64\pi}$ | W/m |
| 3 | Simplified engineering form | $P \approx 0.49 \, H^{2} \, T$ | kW/m (when H in m, T in s) |
| 4 | Wave celerity (deep water) | $c = \dfrac{g T}{2\pi}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Wavelength (deep water) | $L = \dfrac{g T^{2}}{2\pi}$ | m |
| 6 | Wave energy flux (group velocity form) | $P = E \cdot c_{g}$ | W/m, where $c_{g} = c/2$ for deep water |
| 7 | Capture width ratio (CWR) | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{P_{absorbed}}{P_{incident} \cdot B}$ | Dimensionless figure of merit |
| 8 | Hydrostatic stiffness (point absorber) | $K_{h} = \rho g A$ | N/m (A = waterplane area) |
| 9 | Heave natural frequency | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{h} + K_{add}}{m + m_{add}}}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | OWC pneumatic power | $P_{OWC} = \eta_{t} \cdot \Delta p \cdot Q$ | W (Δp = air pressure differential, Q = flow) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using the pipe symbol `|` inside any table cell**. For absolute value or magnitude in expressions, use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to preserve the markdown table rendering.

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow — How Wave Power Becomes Electricity

1. **Wave interacts with device** → transfers kinetic + potential energy.
2. **Energy is captured by primary mover** (water column, buoy, ramp).
3. **Conversion to mechanical rotation** via air (Wells turbine) or hydraulic motor.
4. **Power Take-Off (PTO)** couples to a **rotary generator** (induction/synchronous).
5. **Conditioning & grid injection** through power electronics (AC-DC-AC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** OWC installations like the *Mutriku breakwater* (Spain, 296 kW) operate as a "wave farm" supplying grid electricity to the Basque Country; Point absorbers like *OPT's PowerBuoy* are used for offshore navigation buoys and naval applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Wave Power Per Unit Crest Width (Deep Water, Linear Wave Theory)

We begin with the standard Airy (linear) wave theory result that the **total wave energy density** (kinetic + potential) per unit sea-surface area is:

$$E = \frac{1}{16} \, \rho \, g \, H^{2}$$

The **group velocity** (the speed at which wave energy propagates) in deep water is half the phase velocity:

$$c_{g} = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

**Step 1: Power density equation.**
The average wave power transmitted per unit crest width is the product of energy density and group velocity:

$$P = E \cdot c_{g}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the energy density.**
Replacing $E$:

$$P = \left(\frac{1}{16} \, \rho \, g \, H^{2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{gT}{4\pi}\right)$$

**Step 3: Multiply the constants.**
Multiplying the two fractional constants:

$$P = \frac{\rho \, g^{2} \, H^{2} \, T}{64\pi}$$

**Step 4: Simplify numerically.**
Using $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$:

$$P = \frac{(1025)(9.81)^{2} H^{2} T}{64\pi} \approx 490 \, H^{2} T \;\;\text{W/m}$$

**Step 5: Convert to kW/m.**

$$P \approx 0.49 \, H^{2} T \;\;\text{kW/m}$$

This is the engineering rule-of-thumb quoted on most KTU exam sheets.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Performance of the Three WEC Classes

| Parameter | Oscillating Water Column | Point Absorber | Overtopping Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Capture Mechanism** | Pneumatic air compression | Buoy heave motion | Wave ramp + reservoir |
| **Power Take-Off (PTO)** | Wells turbine (bidirectional) | Linear generator / Hydraulic motor | Low-head Kaplan turbine |
| **Typical Size** | 100 kW – 500 kW | 5 kW – 50 kW | 100 kW – 4 MW |
| **Capacity Factor** | 25 – 35 % | 30 – 45 % | 20 – 30 % |
| **Survivability (storms)** | Excellent (rigid structure) | Moderate (needs shut-down) | Good |
| **Real-world Examples** | LIMPET, Mutriku, Pico | PowerBuoy, SeaBeaver | Wave Dragon, TAPCHAN |
| **Main Limitation** | Bidirectional turbine efficiency | Tuning to wave frequency | Reservoir size & overflow |
| **Best Deployment** | Shoreline/cliff | Offshore deep water | Nearshore with calm bay |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A sea has a significant wave height of **H = 2.5 m** and a peak wave period of **T = 8 s**. Estimate (a) the wave power per unit crest length, and (b) the total power across a wave front of crest width **B = 50 m**.

**Solution:**

(a) Using the simplified engineering formula:

$$P = 0.49 \times H^{2} \times T = 0.49 \times (2.5)^{2} \times 8$$

$$P = 0.49 \times 6.25 \times 8 = 24.5 \;\text{kW/m}$$

(b) Total available power:

$$P_{total} = P \times B = 24.5 \times 50 = 1225 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A typical commercial WEC device converts roughly **10–30 %** of this incident power into useful electricity — so for this example, a real installation might generate **120–370 kW** of grid-ready power.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic WEC System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[OCEAN WAVES] --> B[PRIMARY ENERGY CAPTURE]
    B --> B1[Oscillating Water Column]
    B --> B2[Point Absorber Buoy]
    B --> B3[Overtopping Ramp]
    B1 --> C1[AIR TURBINE - Wells Type]
    B2 --> C2[LINEAR OR HYDRAULIC PTO]
    B3 --> C3[LOW HEAD KAPLAN TURBINE]
    C1 --> D[ROTATIONAL MECHANICAL SHAFT]
    C2 --> E[HYDRAULIC ACCUMULATOR]
    E --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> F[ELECTRICAL GENERATOR]
    F --> G[POWER CONDITIONING UNIT]
    G --> H[GRID OR OFF GRID LOAD]
    H --> I[CONTROL AND MONITORING SYSTEM]
    I -.feedback.-> B
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Oscillating Water Column (OWC) Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[OCEAN WAVE] -->|rises| WC[CHAMBER WATER COLUMN]
    WC -->|compresses| AIR[TRAPPED AIR ABOVE]
    AIR -->|high pressure flow| WT[WELLS TURBINE]
    WT -->|rotates| GEN[GENERATOR]
    AIR2[ATMOSPHERIC AIR] -->|low pressure suction| WT
    W2[OCEAN WAVE RECEDES] -->|falls| WC2[WATER COLUMN DROPS]
    WC2 -->|expansion| AIR2
    WT -->|same rotation direction| GEN
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Point Absorber Functional Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[BUOY HEAVE RESPONSE] --> P2{RESONANCE TUNED?}
    P2 -->|yes| P3[LARGE AMPLIFICATION]
    P2 -->|no| P4[SMALL OSCILLATION]
    P3 --> P5[LINEAR GENERATOR or HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[POWER ELECTRONICS]
    P6 --> P7[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT]
    P5 -. PTO damping feedback .- P2
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Overtopping Device Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[OCEAN WAVES] --> O2[WAVE RAMP REFLECTOR]
    O2 -->|elevated water spill| O3[HIGH LEVEL RESERVOIR]
    O3 --> O4[LOW HEAD TURBINE]
    O4 --> O5[GENERATOR]
    O5 --> O6[GRID]
    O3 -.excess overflow.-> O7[SEA RETURN]
```

---

### 4.5 Comparative Layout of the Three Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OWC_GROUP
        A1[Wave] --> A2[OWC Chamber]
        A2 --> A3[Air drives Wells Turbine]
    end
    subgraph PA_GROUP
        B1[Wave] --> B2[Buoy Heaves]
        B2 --> B3[Linear Generator or Hydraulic PTO]
    end
    subgraph OT_GROUP
        C1[Wave] --> C2[Waves Climb Ramp]
        C2 --> C3[Reservoir Drains via Kaplan Turbine]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy converter. Mention any two real-world installations.*

**Model Answer:**

An **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** is a partially submerged hollow structure open to the sea below the waterline and closed at the top except for an air vent housing a **Wells turbine**. As ocean waves enter the column, the water level rises and falls, alternately compressing and expanding the air trapped above it. The bidirectional air flow passes through the **Wells turbine**, which rotates in the same direction regardless of flow direction, driving an electrical generator.

**Two real-world installations:**
1. **LIMPET (Land Installed Marine Power Energy Transmitter)** – Islay, Scotland, 500 kW.
2. **Mutriku breakwater** – Basque Coast, Spain, 296 kW (16 OWC chambers).

*Key advantage:* Bidirectional operation without need for rectification valves.
*Mark allocation: Working (1.5 m) + Sketch (1 m) + Example (0.5 m) = 3 marks.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]**
*Define a 'point absorber' in wave energy context. List any three advantages of point absorbers over other WEC devices.*

**Model Answer:**

A **point absorber** is a wave energy converter whose horizontal dimensions are small compared to the incident wavelength (typically < λ/4). It captures energy primarily through **heave (vertical) oscillation** at or near resonance with the dominant wave frequency.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Resonance amplification:** Can extract significantly more energy than the projected frontal area would suggest.
2. **Modular & scalable:** Units can be deployed in arrays ("wave farms") with simple mooring.
3. **Lower visual impact and cost-effective offshore deployment** in deep water.

*Mark allocation: Definition (1 m) + Advantages (2 m) = 3 marks.*

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 🟢 QUESTION A — *(14 Marks)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the working of an **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** wave energy system. Explain the role and unique features of the **Wells turbine** used in such systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average wave power per unit crest length** in deep water starting from the wave energy density equation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) OWC Working + Wells Turbine

**Schematic (text-based representation):**
```
        ▲ Air vent with Wells Turbine
        │
   ┌────┴────┐
   │  AIR    │   ← Compressed/Expanded Air
   │ SPACE   │
   ├─────────┤   ← Water surface oscillates
   │ WATER   │   ← Ocean water enters chamber
   │ COLUMN  │
   └────┬────┘
        │   ← Open to sea
   ~~~~~│~~~~~   ← Ocean waves
```

**Working:**
- An OWC consists of a **concrete or steel chamber** with its lower end submerged in the sea.
- Waves entering the chamber cause the internal **water surface to oscillate** vertically.
- This oscillation alternately **compresses and expands** the air column above the water.
- The bidirectional airflow is ducted through a **Wells turbine** which drives a generator.

**Wells Turbine — Unique Features:**
1. **Symmetrical airfoil blades** with a small trailing-edge flap (or symmetric profile).
2. Rotates in **the same direction** for both forward and reverse airflow.
3. Exhibits a **self-starting** stall characteristic that limits torque at high flow rates (acts as a passive safety valve).
4. Operates efficiently at moderate pressure drops (**Δp ≈ 1–2 kPa**).

*Mark split: Working (3) + Wells Turbine features (3) + Sketch (1) = 7 marks.*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Derivation of Wave Power

Given:
- Energy density per unit sea-surface area:
  $$E = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$$

- Deep-water group velocity:
  $$c_{g} = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

Average wave power per unit crest width:

$$P = E \cdot c_{g}$$

Substituting:

$$P = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

Multiplying the constants:

$$P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64\pi}$$

Substituting numerical constants ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$):

$$P \approx 0.49 \, H^{2} T \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

*Mark split: Energy density (2) + Group velocity (2) + Substitution (2) + Final form (1) = 7 marks.*

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B — *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **point absorber** wave energy converter with neat block diagram. Discuss any two advantages and one limitation of point absorber systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a sketch, describe the working of an **overtopping wave energy device**. Mention one commercial prototype developed for this technology. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Point Absorber

**Principle:**
A **point absorber** is a buoy-type floating body whose characteristic diameter is small compared to the incident wavelength (D < λ/4). Energy capture occurs through **heave (vertical) oscillation** induced by passing waves.

The buoy is moored to the seabed via a tether connected to a **Power Take-Off (PTO)** system. As waves pass, the buoy oscillates, driving either:
- A **linear generator** directly (e.g., AWS Ocean Energy's Archimedes Waveswing), or
- A **hydraulic pump** that charges an accumulator feeding a secondary generator.

The device is **resonance-tuned** so the natural heave frequency matches the dominant wave frequency, giving large amplification (sometimes > 5× wave amplitude).

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3 above):**
Wave → Buoy → Heave Response → Resonance Tuned → Linear Generator / Hydraulic Cylinder → Power Electronics → Output.

**Two Advantages:**
1. High **capture width ratio** because of resonance amplification.
2. Modular, scalable, and easy to **deploy in arrays**.

**One Limitation:**
1. Highly sensitive to wave frequency mismatch — requires **active tuning** for varying sea states.

*Mark split: Principle (3) + Block diagram (2) + Advantages/Limitation (2) = 7 marks.*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Overtopping Device

**Sketch (text representation):**
```
   ┌───────────┐   ← Raised Reservoir
   │  WATER    │
   │  RESER.   │
   └──┬────┬───┘
      │    │
   ───┘    └───   ← Low-head Kaplan Turbine
      │
   ╔══╧══╗
   ║WAVE ║   ← Reflector ramp
   ║RAMP ║
   ╚══╤══╝
   ~~~│~~~   ← Ocean
```

**Working:**
- Ocean waves strike an inclined **reflector ramp** that funnels water upward into a **raised reservoir** located several metres above mean sea level.
- Water stored in this reservoir is then released back through **low-head Kaplan (or gravity) turbines** to generate electricity.
- The principle is analogous to **conventional micro-hydro**, except that water is supplied by waves rather than rivers.

**Commercial Prototype:**
- **Wave Dragon** (Denmark) — A slack-moored, semi-submerged overtopping device with a rated capacity of up to **4 MW (when deployed in farm form)**.

*Mark split: Sketch (2) + Working (3) + Commercial example (2) = 7 marks.*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1/16 factor** in the energy density equation — students often write $\rho g H^{2}$ instead of $\rho g H^{2}/16$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing phase velocity with group velocity** — wave *energy* travels at group velocity $c_{g} = c/2$ (deep water), not phase velocity $c = gT/2\pi$. This costs 1–2 marks in derivations.
> 3. **Calling the Wells turbine "bidirectional"** — it actually rotates in ONE direction; it just accepts bidirectional *airflow*. Examiners specifically look for this precision.
> 4. **Not mentioning dimension criteria** for point absorbers (D < λ/4) — losing 1 mark in definitions.
> 5. **Mixing up real-world installations** — LIMPET is *onshore Scotland* (Islay), not Spain. Mutriku is Spain.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three principal WEC classes:** Oscillating Water Column, Point Absorber, Overtopping Device.
- ✅ **Energy density in deep water:** $E = \rho g H^{2} / 16$.
- ✅ **Power per unit crest width:** $P = \rho g^{2} H^{2} T / 64\pi$ ≈ **0.49 H²T kW/m**.
- ✅ **Group velocity in deep water:** $c_{g} = c/2 = gT / 4\pi$.
- ✅ **OWC uses Wells turbine** — same rotation for both airflow directions.
- ✅ **Point absorber definition:** D < λ/4, captures energy in heave with resonance.
- ✅ **Overtopping devices** act like low-head hydro — they store wave water in elevated reservoirs.
- ✅ **Capacity factors** are higher than solar (25–40 %).
- ✅ **Key installations:** LIMPET (UK), Mutriku (Spain), Pico (Azores/Portugal), Wave Dragon (Denmark), PowerBuoy (USA).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic: "W-A-P"**
> **W**ells turbine → **O**scillating **W**ater **C**olumn
> **A**mplified heave → **P**oint **A**bsorber
> **P**umped-up reservoir → **O**vertopping
> Think: **"W-A-P – Wells, Amplification, Pumped reservoir"** 🧠

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation of Wave Energy Conversion Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operation of Wave Energy Conversion Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wave Energy Conversion (WEC):** Wave Energy Conversion is the process of extracting the kinetic and potential energy present in ocean surface waves (gravitational sea waves) and transforming it into useful mechanical work — typically through a prime mover coupled to an electrical generator — using devices classified under the broad categories of **Oscillating Water Columns (OWC)**, **Overtopping Devices**, and **Wave-Activated Bodies (WAB)**.

A Wave Energy Conversion System (WECS) consists of three fundamental subsystems working in tandem:

1. **Primary Capture System (PCS):** The hydrodynamic interface that absorbs wave energy.
2. **Power Take-Off (PTO) System:** Converts captured mechanical motion into rotational shaft work.
3. **Control & Conditioning System:** Regulates output, smooths intermittency, and feeds power to the grid.

The energy of a wave has two components:
- **Potential Energy (PE):** Due to the elevated water mass above the mean sea level.
- **Kinetic Energy (KE):** Due to orbital motion of water particles.

For a wave of height **H** and wavelength **λ**, the energy per unit horizontal area (energy density) in deep water is given by:

$$E = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \;\; \text{[J/m}^{2}\text{]}$$

where **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³** is the density of seawater and **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²** is acceleration due to gravity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Ruler" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a long wooden ruler floating on a calm pond.** If you push the end of the ruler down and release it, it bobs up and down repeatedly. This oscillation is exactly how a "point absorber" wave buoy works in the ocean — but instead of your hand providing energy, ocean waves do. The ruler's motion (up/down, side-to-side) is captured by a small generator inside, producing electricity.

Another everyday analogy is a **child's seesaw at the beach**:
- Wave pushes one end up → Potential energy stored.
- Wave pulls it down → Kinetic energy released.
- A hydraulic piston or air turbine converts this rhythmic motion into electricity.

The deeper intuition: **Ocean waves are essentially concentrated, transported solar energy.** Sun heats the equator, generates wind, wind blows over thousands of kilometers of ocean (a "fetch"), and waves accumulate massive amounts of energy. A wave 2 m high carries about **40 kW per meter of crest** — enough to power ~40 homes continuously!

---

## 1.3 Why Wave Energy Matters for Kerala

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala's Strategic Context:** The 590 km Kerala coastline along the Arabian Sea receives consistent **south-west monsoon swells** (June–September) and **pre-monsoon sea breezes** (March–May), making wave power density in the range of **15–25 kW/m**. This positions Kerala as a prime candidate for offshore WEC deployment under India's Deep Ocean Mission.

Standardized metrics used by KTU examiners for wave energy:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Significant wave height | $H_s$ | **1 – 4 m** |
| Wave period | $T$ | **5 – 12 s** |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **40 – 200 m** |
| Wave power (deep water) | $P$ | **10 – 50 kW/m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Ocean Waves

Waves are formed through the following chain:

1. **Wind shear** transfers momentum to the sea surface.
2. **Capillary waves** (ripples) grow into **gravity waves** as fetch and duration increase.
3. Once waves leave the generation zone, they become **swells** — long-period, organized, low-dissipation waves.
4. These swells carry energy across ocean basins with minimal loss (~3% per 1000 km).

For **deep-water gravity waves**, the dispersion relation (linking wavelength and period) is the foundational equation:

$$\omega^{2} = g k \tanh(kd)$$

where:
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ → angular frequency (rad/s)
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ → wavenumber (rad/m)
- $d$ → water depth (m)

In **deep water** ($d > \lambda/2$), $\tanh(kd) \rightarrow 1$, simplifying to:

$$\omega^{2} = g k \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$$

---

## 2.2 Wave Kinematics — Orbital Particle Motion

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a floating ball move in a circle, not horizontally?** Because each water particle in a wave doesn't travel forward — it traces a near-circular orbit in deep water, decaying exponentially with depth.

Particle velocities and displacements in a deep-water sinusoidal wave:

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Horizontal velocity $u$ | $u = \dfrac{H g}{2 \omega} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$ |
| Vertical velocity $w$ | $w = \dfrac{H g}{2 \omega} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$ |
| Wave celerity $c$ | $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ |
| Group velocity $v_g$ | $v_g = \dfrac{c}{2}$ (deep water) |
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{gT^{2}}{2\pi}$ |

The exponential term $e^{kz}$ (with $z$ measured downward, $z<0$) shows that wave motion dies out at depth $\lambda/2$ — a key insight for **submerged WECs** like the Archimedes Wave Swing.

---

## 2.3 Wave Power Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Most Important Equation in Wave Energy:** The power transmitted per unit crest width by a deep-water wave is $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}$. Memorize its form and dependencies.

**Comprehensive Formula Table:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave power per unit crest length | $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}$ | W/m | Deep water, sinusoidal |
| 2 | Simplified power formula | $P \approx H^{2} T$ | kW/m | Engineering approximation |
| 3 | Total wave power per crest | $P_{total} = P \cdot L_{crest}$ | W | $L_{crest}$ = wavefront width |
| 4 | Energy density (deep water) | $E = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ | J/m² | Total = PE + KE |
| 5 | Wave celerity | $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ | m/s | Deep water only |
| 6 | Group velocity | $v_g = \dfrac{c}{2}$ | m/s | Deep water |
| 7 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{gT^{2}}{2\pi}$ | m | Deep water |
| 8 | Celerity (shallow, $d < \lambda/20$) | $c = \sqrt{gd}$ | m/s | Long-wave limit |
| 9 | Capture width ratio | $CWR = \dfrac{P_{captured}}{P_{incident} \cdot B}$ | – | $B$ = device width |
| 10 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{electrical}}{P_{wave}}$ | – | Typically **20 – 40 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Civil and Ocean Engineers use these formulas in: (a) selecting optimum sites for WEC farms, (b) sizing hydraulic PTO rams, (c) designing mooring systems for floating buoys, and (d) estimating LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) of marine renewables for KSEB tender documents.

---

## 2.4 Operational Principle — How Different WEC Devices Operate

A. **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)**

> Imagine blowing across the top of an empty bottle. Air rushes out, then back in. The bottle is an OWC in miniature.

- Waves enter a partially submerged hollow chamber through a sub-surface opening.
- The rising and falling water column compresses/decompresses trapped air above.
- Air rushes through a **Wells turbine** (rotates same direction regardless of air-flow direction).
- Turbine drives a generator.

**Equation of motion (simplified linear):**

$$m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = F_{wave}(t)$$

where $m$ is water inertia, $b$ is damping (PTO + radiation), $k$ is hydrostatic stiffness, and $F_{wave}$ is wave excitation force.

B. **Point Absorber (e.g., PowerBuoy)**

- A buoy (typically 5–10 m diameter) floats at the surface and heaves vertically.
- Internal linear generator converts heave motion directly into electricity.
- High capture factor due to **resonance tuning**: $f_{natural} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ matched to peak wave frequency.

C. **Attenuator (e.g., Pelamis)**

- Long semi-submerged cylindrical segments (~150 m long, 3.5 m diameter) connected by hinged joints.
- Articulated segments bend relative to each other as waves pass.
- Hydraulic rams at the joints pump fluid through motors.

D. **Overtopping Device (e.g., Wave Dragon)**

- Waves ramp up a collector slope and spill into a raised reservoir.
- Head difference between reservoir and sea level drives Kaplan turbines.

E. **Archimedes Wave Swing (AWS)**

- Submerged air-filled cylinder tethered to seabed.
- Wave pressure compresses upper cylinder downward into lower cylinder.
- Linear generator in the gap produces electricity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Wave Energy Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive $E = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Set up the wave surface profile.**

For a deep-water sinusoidal wave propagating in the +x direction:

$$\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

This means the free surface oscillates between $-H/2$ and $+H/2$, giving a total height of $H$.

**Step 2: Compute the potential energy (PE).**

Consider one wavelength. The PE per unit area (per unit crest width) is the work done to lift a water column of height $\eta$ from the still water level:

$$PE = \int_{0}^{\lambda} \rho g \frac{\eta^{2}}{2}\, dx$$

Substituting the wave profile:

$$PE = \int_{0}^{\lambda} \rho g \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{H^{2}}{4} \cos^{2}(kx - \omega t)\, dx$$

$$PE = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8} \int_{0}^{\lambda} \cos^{2}(kx - \omega t)\, dx$$

Using the identity $\cos^{2}\theta = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$, and noting that the integral of $\cos(2\theta)$ over one full wavelength is zero:

$$\int_{0}^{\lambda} \cos^{2}(kx - \omega t)\, dx = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$PE = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{\rho g H^{2} \lambda}{16}$$

PE per unit area:

$$PE_{unit} = \frac{PE}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$$

**Step 3: Compute the kinetic energy (KE).**

For deep-water waves, kinetic energy per unit area equals potential energy per unit area — a famous result from Airy wave theory:

$$KE_{unit} = PE_{unit} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$$

**Step 4: Total energy per unit area.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= PE_{unit} + KE_{unit} \\
&= \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} + \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \\
&= \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Average over a wave period.**

The expression above is **time-dependent**. Averaging over one wave period (using $\langle \cos^{2}(\omega t)\rangle = 1/2$):

$$\boxed{\bar{E} = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \;\; \text{[J/m}^{2}\text{]}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wave Power per Unit Crest Length

**Step 1: Recall that wave power = energy flux.**

$$P = E \cdot v_g$$

In deep water, $v_g = c/2$ (group velocity is half the phase velocity because energy travels at the group velocity, not the phase velocity).

**Step 2: Substitute $E$ and $v_g$.**

$$P = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \cdot \frac{c}{2} = \frac{\rho g H^{2} c}{32}$$

**Step 3: Use the deep-water celerity.**

$$c = \frac{g T}{2\pi}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{g T}{2\pi} \\
&= \frac{\rho g H^{2} \cdot g T}{64 \pi} \\
&= \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values for a standard case.**

Let $H = 2$ m, $T = 8$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$P = \frac{1025 \cdot (9.81)^{2} \cdot (2)^{2} \cdot 8}{64 \pi}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 1025 \cdot 96.2361 \cdot 4 \cdot 8 = 3{,}156{,}540 \;\text{W·units}$$

Dividing by $64\pi \approx 201.06$:

$$P \approx 15{,}696 \;\; \text{W/m} \approx 15.7 \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

This matches the engineering shortcut:

$$P \approx H^{2} T = (2)^{2} \cdot 8 = 32 \;\; \text{kW/m (rough)}$$

(The shortcut gives ~32 kW/m; the exact formula gives ~15.7 kW/m. The shortcut over-estimates because it folds constants into 1.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Capture Width Ratio (CWR)

**Step 1: Definition.**

The capture width ratio is the figure of merit for a WEC:

$$CWR = \frac{P_{absorbed}}{P_{wave\,per\,unit\,width} \cdot B}$$

where $B$ is the physical width of the device perpendicular to the wave direction.

**Step 2: Physical meaning.**

A device with $CWR = 1$ absorbs all the energy crossing its geometric width — an idealized limit. Practical devices reach $CWR = 0.3$ to $0.5$ under optimal tuning.

**Step 3: Power take-off efficiency chain.**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{hydro} \cdot \eta_{PTO} \cdot \eta_{generator}$$

Typical values:

| Stage | Symbol | Range |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrodynamic capture | $\eta_{hydro}$ | 50 – 90 % |
| Mechanical PTO | $\eta_{PTO}$ | 60 – 85 % |
| Generator/electrical | $\eta_{gen}$ | 85 – 95 % |
| **Overall** | $\eta_{overall}$ | **25 – 60 %** |

For example, a 50 kW/m wave crossing a 10 m wide attenuator with $CWR = 0.4$:

$$P_{absorbed} = 50 \times 10 \times 0.4 = 200 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$P_{electric} = 200 \times 0.50 = 100 \;\; \text{kW}$$

This is the energy available after all losses.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A deep-water wave has a significant height of 3 m and a period of 10 s. Compute: (i) wavelength, (ii) wave celerity, (iii) group velocity, (iv) energy density per unit area, and (v) power per unit crest length. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

(i) Wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{g T^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times (10)^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{981}{6.2832} \approx 156.1 \;\; \text{m}$$

(ii) Wave celerity:

$$c = \frac{g T}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 10}{2\pi} = \frac{98.1}{6.2832} \approx 15.6 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

(iii) Group velocity:

$$v_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{15.6}{2} = 7.8 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

(iv) Energy density per unit area:

$$E = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (3)^{2}}{16} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 9}{16}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{90{,}494.25}{16} \\
&\approx 5656 \;\; \text{J/m}^{2} \\
&\approx 5.66 \;\; \text{kJ/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

(v) Power per unit crest length:

$$P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi} = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^{2} \times (3)^{2} \times 10}{64 \pi}$$

Computing numerator: $1025 \times 96.2361 \times 9 \times 10 = 8{,}877{,}770$

$$P = \frac{8{,}877{,}770}{201.06} \approx 44{,}152 \;\; \text{W/m} \approx 44.15 \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

This 3 m, 10 s wave is a **powerful sea state**, typical of Arabian Sea pre-monsoon conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    AIR --> T2[Air Sucked Downward Through Duct]

    TURBINE --> GEN[Rotational Electrical Generator]

```

```mermaid
    S3 -->|Generator Losses 10 percent| S4[Stage 4 - Electrical AC Output 40 percent]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define wave energy conversion. List any four major devices used for it.

**Model Answer:**
Wave energy conversion is the process of extracting the kinetic and potential energy of ocean surface gravity waves and converting it into useful electrical energy via a prime mover and generator. The four major devices are:

1. Oscillating Water Column (OWC) – uses air column and Wells turbine.
2. Point Absorber – floating buoy with heaving motion → linear generator.
3. Attenuator – e.g., Pelamis, articulated multi-segment floating device.
4. Overtopping device – e.g., Wave Dragon, with elevated reservoir.

**(3 Marks – Definition: 1, List: 2)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the wave power formula in deep water and explain how it depends on wave parameters.

**Model Answer:**

$$P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi} \;\; \text{[W/m]}$$

Dependencies:
- $P \propto H^{2}$ → doubling wave height quadruples power.
- $P \propto T$ → longer-period swell carries more energy per unit crest.
- $P \propto \rho$ → seawater (~1025 kg/m³) is denser than fresh water.
- $P \propto g^{2}$ → location-dependent; slightly higher near equator.

**(3 Marks – Formula: 2, Dependencies: 1)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** 
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy conversion system. State the role of the Wells turbine. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A deep-water wave has a height of 2.5 m and a period of 9 s. Calculate (i) wavelength, (ii) group velocity, (iii) energy density per unit area, and (iv) power per unit crest length. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

An **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** is a partially enclosed chamber open to the sea below the waterline. Its operation is as follows:

1. As a wave crest enters, the water inside the chamber rises, compressing the trapped air column above.
2. The compressed air is forced through a duct containing a **Wells turbine**.
3. The Wells turbine spins and drives a generator.
4. As the wave trough follows, the water level inside falls, creating a partial vacuum that sucks air back through the duct.
5. The Wells turbine rotates in the **same direction** for both upward and downward airflow — this is its key advantage.

**Role of the Wells Turbine:** It is an axial-flow turbine with symmetrical aerofoil blades. Its unique feature is that the torque direction is independent of the air-flow direction, eliminating the need for rectification valves.

**Sketch (described in text):**
- A hollow concrete or steel chamber is fixed to the shoreline or seabed.
- A sub-surface opening on the seaward side admits waves.
- An air duct on the upper, landward side houses the Wells turbine and generator.
- Air flows bi-directionally through the duct as waves rise and fall.

**(7 Marks – Sketch: 2, Working: 3, Wells turbine role: 2)**

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $H = 2.5$ m, $T = 9$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{g T^{2}}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{6.2832} = \frac{794.61}{6.2832} \approx 126.5 \;\; \text{m}$$

**(ii) Group velocity:**

$$c = \frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 9}{6.2832} \approx 14.05 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$v_g = \frac{c}{2} = 7.03 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Energy density:**

$$E = \frac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25}{16} = \frac{62{,}857}{16} \approx 3928.6 \;\; \text{J/m}^{2}$$

**(iv) Power per unit crest length:**

$$P = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 96.236 \times 6.25 \times 9}{201.06}$$

$$P = \frac{5{,}548{,}103}{201.06} \approx 27{,}594 \;\; \text{W/m} \approx 27.6 \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

**(7 Marks – Each sub-part: 1.75 ≈ 2)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of (i) a Point Absorber and (ii) an Archimedes Wave Swing (AWS). Compare their hydrodynamic capture principles. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the total energy density of a deep-water gravity wave. Hence show that the wave power per unit crest length is $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Point Absorber:**
- A floating buoy (typically 5–10 m diameter) heaves vertically with the wave motion.
- Internal linear generator converts the relative motion between the buoy and a fixed internal reference (or a counter-weight) directly into electricity.
- Capture is enhanced by **resonance tuning** — natural frequency matched to peak wave frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
- Small device, large energy capture relative to its size (high Capture Width Ratio).

**(ii) Archimedes Wave Swing (AWS):**
- Fully submerged cylindrical device resting on the seabed.
- Upper chamber (air-filled) slides up and down over a lower fixed chamber.
- Wave-induced pressure fluctuations push the upper cylinder down into the lower one.
- A linear generator in the gap produces electricity.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Point Absorber | AWS |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Surface floating | Fully submerged |
| Driving force | Buoyancy + wave elevation | Wave-induced pressure differential |
| Capture mechanism | Heave motion | Compressed air gap |
| Survivability | Vulnerable in storms | Excellent (submerged) |
| Visual impact | High | Low |

**(7 Marks – Each device: 3, Comparison: 1)**

**Model Solution (b):**

See **SECTION 3.1 and 3.2** for the complete derivation.

**Brief recap of key steps:**

1. Wave profile: $\eta(x,t) = \dfrac{H}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$
2. PE per unit area: $\langle PE\rangle = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$
3. KE per unit area = PE per unit area (Airy theory equality): $\langle KE\rangle = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$
4. Total: $E = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{8}$ (instantaneous) → $\bar{E} = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ (time-averaged)
5. Power = energy flux = $E \times v_g = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16} \times \dfrac{c}{2}$
6. With $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$: $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}$ ✓

**(7 Marks – PE derivation: 2, KE derivation: 2, Total: 1, Power: 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** Marks lost when students write $P$ in W/m without showing $H$ in meters and $T$ in seconds consistently.
> 2. **Confusing $c$ and $v_g$.** A common error is to use $c$ instead of $v_g$ in the power formula, doubling the result. Power uses **group velocity**, not phase velocity.
> 3. **Mixing deep-water and shallow-water formulas.** The deep-water approximation $\tanh(kd)\approx 1$ fails when $d < \lambda/2$. State your assumption explicitly.
> 4. **No diagram in Part B (a).** KTU examiners routinely deduct 1–2 marks for missing labeled sketch.
> 5. **Hand-waving the Wells turbine.** Mention "same direction of rotation regardless of airflow direction" — this is the key conceptual point worth marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] Wave energy is a form of indirect solar energy stored in ocean surface gravity waves.
- [ ] Deep-water wave power formula: $P = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{64 \pi}$ [W/m] — **the single most important equation**.
- [ ] Energy density (time-averaged): $\bar{E} = \dfrac{\rho g H^{2}}{16}$ [J/m²].
- [ ] Wavelength (deep water): $\lambda = \dfrac{gT^{2}}{2\pi}$.
- [ ] Celerity (deep water): $c = \dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$; Group velocity $v_g = c/2$.
- [ ] Power ∝ H² and Power ∝ T → bigger, longer waves carry disproportionately more energy.
- [ ] Five major WEC types: **OWC, Point Absorber, Attenuator, Overtopping, AWS**.
- [ ] OWC uses Wells turbine (bidirectional airflow → unidirectional rotation).
- [ ] Point absorbers use resonance tuning for high Capture Width Ratio.
- [ ] AWS is fully submerged — high survivability, low visual impact.
- [ ] Capture Width Ratio (CWR) and overall efficiency ($\eta$) are key performance metrics.
- [ ] Kerala's coastline receives **15–25 kW/m** average wave power — strong potential.
- [ ] Wave energy devices face challenges: intermittency, storm survivability, grid integration, marine ecology.
- [ ] Standard values to remember: $\rho_{seawater} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] Difference between **phase velocity** ($c$) and **group velocity** ($v_g$) is critical — energy travels at $v_g$.
- [ ] Shallow-water limit ($d < \lambda/20$): $c = \sqrt{gd}$ — waves slow down near coast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC): Working Principles and Types (Closed-Cycle, Open-Cycle, Hybrid)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC): Working Principles and Types (Closed-Cycle, Open-Cycle, Hybrid)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy conversion technology that exploits the **natural thermal gradient** existing between the warm surface waters of tropical/sub-tropical oceans and the cold deep waters (typically at depths of 800 m – 1000 m) to generate usable electrical power, mechanical work, or desalinated freshwater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Adapt):** OTEC is a *solar-energy storage system* in which solar radiation incident on the ocean surface is stored as thermal energy in the upper mixed layer of the ocean. This stored thermal energy is then converted into a more useful form (electricity) using the temperature difference between the warm surface water and the cold deep water.

### 1.2 Operational Boundary Conditions

For an OTEC plant to be thermodynamically viable, the following **minimum criteria** must be satisfied:

| Parameter | Threshold Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Surface water temperature ($\theta_w$) | $\geq$ **25 °C** (298 K) | Warm reservoir temperature ($T_H$) |
| Deep water temperature ($\theta_c$) | $\leq$ **5 °C** (278 K) | Cold reservoir temperature ($T_C$) |
| Available $\Delta T$ | $\geq$ **20 °C** (minimum) | Net usable temperature head |
| Geographical latitude | $\leq$ **±35°** from equator | Solar insolation requirement |
| Operating depth for cold water | $\approx$ **1000 m** | Cold pipe suction depth |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ocean's Natural Thermos Flask"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Imagine a Building's Natural Ventilation Stack:**
> 
> Think of OTEC like a tall chimney where warm air rises at the top and cool air sinks at the bottom. The ocean behaves similarly: the **sun-heated top layer** acts like the "warm roof," while the **permanently dark, cold deep layer** acts like the "basement cooler." OTEC is essentially a *heat engine* placed between these two natural reservoirs. We don't burn fuel — we simply let heat *spontaneously flow* from the warm reservoir to the cold one through a working fluid, capturing a small portion (2–3%) of that flow as work. The bigger the temperature gap ($\Delta T$) between the two reservoirs, the more useful work we can squeeze out — just as a steeper waterfall spins a turbine harder than a gentle stream.

### 1.4 Classification of OTEC Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-3 Classification Tag:** The syllabus explicitly mandates study of **three** OTEC architectures:
> 1. **Closed-Cycle OTEC** (also known as the *Anderson Cycle* or *Rankine-style OTEC*)
> 2. **Open-Cycle OTEC** (also known as the *Claude Cycle* or *Vacuum-flash OTEC*)
> 3. **Hybrid OTEC** (combines elements of both closed and open cycles)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Thermodynamic Principle

OTEC is fundamentally governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. The *maximum* (ideal) conversion efficiency is dictated by the **Carnot efficiency**, while real OTEC plants operate at a fraction of this limit.

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

1. **Solar Insolation Capture:** Solar radiation penetrates the ocean's surface layer (0 – 100 m depth), where water — being **optically dense** — absorbs approximately **80 %** of incoming shortwave radiation.
2. **Thermal Stratification:** The absorbed energy creates a stable warm layer (epilimnion) of temperatures around **25 °C – 30 °C**, separated by a sharp thermocline from the cold deep water (**4 °C – 8 °C**).
3. **Working-Fluid Circulation:** A working fluid is caused to *evaporate* using the warm surface water (heat addition) and *condense* using the cold deep water (heat rejection), creating a closed thermodynamic loop.
4. **Turbine Expansion:** The vapor at high pressure is expanded across a turbine, producing **rotational mechanical work**.
5. **Power Take-Off:** The turbine shaft drives an electrical generator, producing grid-quality or off-grid electricity.
6. **Cold-Water Pumping:** A massive volume of cold deep water (CW) must be continuously pumped to the surface through a long **Cold Water Pipe (CWP)** — this constitutes the largest parasitic load of the plant.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Comprehensive OTEC Equation Reference (Memorize for KTU ESE)**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carnot (Ideal) Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | $T$ in **Kelvin**; absolute upper limit |
| 2 | Practical OTEC Efficiency | $\eta_{actual} \approx 0.30 \times \eta_{Carnot}$ | Empirical scaling factor for real losses |
| 3 | Net Power Output | $P_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \cdot Q_H - P_{pump}$ | $Q_H$ = heat absorbed from warm water |
| 4 | Heat Transfer Rate | $Q_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})$ | $\dot{m}_w$ in kg/s, $c_{p,w} \approx 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | Cold Water Lift Penalty | $P_{pump} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot g \cdot h / \eta_{pump}$ | $h$ = pipe depth (≈ **1000 m**), $\eta_{pump}$ ≈ 0.85 |
| 6 | Maximum Theoretical $\eta_{Carnot}$ for OTEC | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{278}{298} \approx 6.7\,\%$ | For $\Delta T = 20$ °C reference case |
| 7 | Realistic Plant $\eta$ | $\eta_{real} \approx 2\,\% - 3\,\%$ | After turbine + generator + pump losses |
| 8 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{\Delta h_{turbine}}$ | For Open-Cycle OTEC design |
| 9 | Fresh Water Yield (Open-Cycle) | $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} - \dot{m}_{flash\ loss}$ | Open-cycle by-product (≈ 0.15 % of warm flow) |
| 10 | Cold Water Pipe Diameter | $D_{CWP} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}_{cw}}{\pi \rho_w v_{max}}}$ | $v_{max}$ ≈ 2–3 m/s to limit biofouling |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING:** In all KTU board examinations, examiners deduct marks when students confuse the symbols $\Delta T$ (temperature *difference* in °C or K) with absolute temperatures $T_H, T_C$ (which **must** be in Kelvin when used in Carnot's formula). Always convert: $T[K] = \theta[°C] + 273.15$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Island Nations of the Pacific:** The Pacific Islands (Hawaii, Tahiti, Kiribati, Philippines) host the world's only operational OTEC pilots because they possess year-round **23 °C – 28 °C** surface waters within easy coastal access to deep cold water.
- **Closed-Cycle OTEC Plants:** Used for **baseload electricity generation** in tropical island grids with high renewable penetration goals.
- **Open-Cycle OTEC Plants:** Doubly useful for **desalinated drinking water** production in arid coastal zones (e.g., Kawaihae, Hawaii plant).
- **Hybrid Systems:** Optimized for **co-generation** of power + freshwater, making them attractive for **dual-purpose tropical resort / agricultural complexes**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the OTEC Theoretical Efficiency (Carnot Basis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark question** under Module 3. Memorize every step below.

#### Statement of the Problem
Given an OTEC system operating between warm surface water at $T_H$ and cold deep water at $T_C$, derive the maximum thermodynamic efficiency using the **Carnot cycle** framework.

#### Step-by-Step Mathematical Development

**Step 1 — Define the Thermal Reservoirs**
The warm ocean surface water acts as the **high-temperature reservoir** at temperature $T_H$ (in K), and the cold deep ocean water acts as the **low-temperature reservoir** at temperature $T_C$ (in K).

**Step 2 — Invoke the Carnot Heat Engine Postulate**
For any *reversible* heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs, the efficiency is given by the well-known **Carnot relation**:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

where $W_{net}$ is the net work output, $Q_H$ is the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir, and $Q_C$ is the heat rejected to the cold reservoir.

**Step 3 — Apply the Reversible Heat Transfer Ratio**
For a reversible (i.e., infinitely slow, frictionless) process, the ratio of heat exchanges equals the absolute temperature ratio:

$$\frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Efficiency Expression**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of Available Temperature Difference**
Using the algebraic identity $T_H - T_C = \Delta T$ and noting that $T_H = T_C + \Delta T$, we rewrite:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_C + \Delta T} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_C + \Delta T}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Substitution for a Reference OTEC Site**
For a typical tropical OTEC site:
- $T_H = 298$ K (25 °C surface water)
- $T_C = 278$ K (5 °C deep water)
- $\Delta T = 20$ K

Compute the Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{278}{298} = \frac{20}{298} \approx 0.0671 = 6.71\,\%$$

**Step 7 — Apply Empirical Correction Factor for Real Systems**
Real OTEC plants suffer from turbine irreversibilities, heat-exchanger losses, and pumping penalties. A well-accepted empirical correction factor is **≈ 0.30** of Carnot:

$$\eta_{real,OTEC} \approx 0.30 \times 6.71\,\% \approx 2.01\,\%$$

**Step 8 — Net Power Output Calculation**
For a warm water mass flow rate of $\dot{m}_w = 1{,}000{,}000$ kg/hr (≈ 277.8 kg/s):

$$Q_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T = 277.8 \times 4.186 \times 20 \approx 23{,}260\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

$$P_{net} = \eta_{real} \cdot Q_H = 0.0201 \times 23{,}260 \approx 467.5\ \text{kW (electric)}$$

This is consistent with realistic per-module OTEC outputs of **0.4 – 0.5 MW** per 1 m³/s of warm water throughput.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cold-Water-Pipe Pumping Penalty

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This derivation justifies why deep cold water is the **#1 cost driver** of OTEC.

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate Relation**

$$\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{\pi D_{CWP}^2}{4} \cdot v_{cw}$$

where $v_{cw}$ is the cold-water velocity inside the pipe.

**Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate of Cold Water**

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \rho_w \cdot \dot{V}_{cw} \quad \text{with} \quad \rho_w \approx 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Ideal Hydraulic Power Required to Lift Cold Water**

$$P_{pump,ideal} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ and $h = 1000$ m for deep-cold-water uptake.

**Step 4 — Account for Pump Mechanical-Electrical Efficiency**

$$P_{pump} = \frac{\dot{m}_{cw} \cdot g \cdot h}{\eta_{pump}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**
For $\dot{m}_{cw} = 5 \times 10^5$ kg/s (large commercial plant):

$$P_{pump} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 1000}{0.85} \approx 5.77\ \text{GW (gross input)}$$

This illustrates that **sub-sea pumping** is the dominant parasitic load, and it is precisely the reason OTEC plants require **massive economies of scale** to remain viable.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Type-Wise Comparison of OTEC Cycles

| Parameter | Closed-Cycle OTEC | Open-Cycle OTEC | Hybrid OTEC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working Fluid | Ammonia (NH₃) / R-134a / Propane | Seawater itself (steam) | NH₃ + seawater for fresh water |
| Evaporator Pressure | High (~ 8 – 10 bar) | Very low (~ 0.03 bar vacuum) | Combination |
| Turbine Type | Standard axial-flow turbine | Low-pressure axial turbine | Both |
| Fresh Water By-Product | No | **Yes** (≈ 0.15 % of warm flow) | Yes |
| Turbine Size | Compact | Massive (volumetric flow huge) | Medium |
| Heat Exchanger Material | Titanium (corrosion-resistant) | Direct contact (no HX needed) | Titanium + vacuum vessel |
| Net Plant Efficiency | ~ 2.5 % | ~ 1.7 % | ~ 2.0 % |
| Capital Cost Driver | Heat exchangers | Vacuum chamber + CWP | Both |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **High** | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs below are **purely alphanumeric and letter-prefixed** (e.g., `evapA`, `turbineB`). Reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are never used as node IDs. Double-quoted labels contain only **raw uppercase / alphanumeric text**, with no markdown or HTML formatting.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Cycle OTEC (Anderson Cycle)

```mermaid
  WarmResO -->|Heat QH transfer| WFC[Working Fluid in Heat Engine]

  WFC -->|Net Work Wnet| ElectricalO[Electrical Power Output]
```
