<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Q: Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). State the minimum temperature difference required for an OTEC plant to operate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** OTEC is a renewable energy technology that converts the thermal energy stored in warm surface ocean water into electricity by exploiting the temperature difference with cold deep ocean water, using a heat engine operating on the Rankine or Carnot cycle.
- **Minimum ΔT (1 mark):** The minimum temperature difference required is **20 °C** between the warm surface water (≥ 25 °C) and the cold deep water (≤ 5 °C) at a depth of approximately **1000 m**.

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`
**Q: Differentiate between Closed-Cycle and Open-Cycle OTEC systems in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Working Fluid:** In Closed-Cycle OTEC, a low-boiling-point fluid such as ammonia (NH₃) or R-134a is circulated in a sealed loop, whereas in Open-Cycle OTEC, seawater itself is used as the working fluid and is flash-evaporated under vacuum.
2. **By-Product:** Open-Cycle OTEC produces **fresh water** as a natural by-product when the evaporated steam condenses, whereas Closed-Cycle OTEC produces **only electricity** and no fresh water.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations: each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, broken into sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either (a) OR (b)** of the chosen question. Full-step model solutions are provided below for self-evaluation.

---

#### **Question B1 — Option (a):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]`

**Q: Explain with neat block diagrams the working principle of a Closed-Cycle OTEC system. Also derive the expression for the Carnot efficiency of an OTEC plant operating between surface water at 28 °C and deep water at 6 °C.**

**(a) Working Principle of Closed-Cycle OTEC — 7 Marks**

The closed-cycle OTEC system, also known as the **Anderson Cycle**, uses a low-boiling-point working fluid (typically ammonia, NH₃) confined in a sealed loop. The operational sequence is as follows:

1. **Heat Addition (Evaporator):** Warm surface seawater (≈ 28 °C) is pumped through the shell side of an **evaporator heat exchanger**. The working fluid (NH₃) absorbs heat and **boils** at high pressure (≈ 8 – 10 bar), converting entirely to high-pressure vapor.
2. **Turbine Expansion:** The high-pressure NH₃ vapor expands through an **axial-flow turbine**, producing rotational mechanical work. The turbine drives an electrical generator.
3. **Heat Rejection (Condenser):** The expanded vapor, now at low pressure, enters the **condenser**, where cold deep seawater (≈ 6 °C from 1000 m depth) absorbs the latent heat and re-condenses the NH₃ into a liquid.
4. **Pump Action:** The liquid NH₃ is pressurized by a **feed pump** and returned to the evaporator, completing the closed loop.

**Block Diagram:** See Mermaid diagram 4.2 above for the architecture. Key components: **Warm S.W. → Evaporator → Turbine → Generator → Condenser → Pump → back to Evaporator**, with **Cold S.W.** entering the condenser.

**(b) Derivation of Carnot Efficiency — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**
$T_H = 28 + 273 = 301$ K
$T_C = 6 + 273 = 279$ K

**Step 2 — Apply Carnot's Efficiency Equation:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{279}{301}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{301 - 279}{301} = \frac{22}{301} \approx 0.0731 = 7.31\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Real Plant Estimate:**
With empirical factor 0.30:

$$\eta_{real} \approx 0.30 \times 7.31\,\% \approx 2.19\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Using °C instead of Kelvin in Carnot's formula. Always convert!
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Omitting the empirical real-efficiency correction. Examiners expect it for design justification.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Failing to **label the block diagram** with component names. KTU valuators specifically look for the arrows' directions and labels.

---

#### **Question B1 — Option (b):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]`

**Q: With the help of a block diagram, describe the Open-Cycle OTEC system. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.**

**(a) Working Principle of Open-Cycle OTEC — 7 Marks**

The open-cycle OTEC, also known as the **Claude Cycle**, uses **seawater itself** as the working fluid — no separate heat exchanger is required for evaporation. The sequence is:

1. **Flash Evaporation:** Warm surface seawater enters a **flash evaporator** maintained at very low pressure (≈ 0.03 bar, near-vacuum). The sudden pressure drop causes a portion of the seawater to **flash-evaporate** into low-pressure steam.
2. **Turbine Expansion:** The low-pressure steam drives an **axial-flow low-pressure turbine** coupled to a generator.
3. **Condensation & Fresh Water Recovery:** The exhaust steam enters a **surface condenser** cooled by cold deep seawater pumped from 1000 m depth. Upon condensation, it yields **fresh potable water** — a valuable by-product.
4. **Discharge:** Non-condensable gases (air) are vented; the cooled brine and condensed freshwater are separated and discharged.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Mermaid diagram 4.3. The cycle is **open** because seawater enters and exits as a single-pass flow, unlike the closed NH₃ loop.

**(b) Advantages and Disadvantages — 7 Marks**

**Advantages:**
1. **Dual Output:** Produces both electricity and **fresh potable water**, ideal for arid island nations.
2. **No Toxic Working Fluid:** Uses seawater only, eliminating ammonia-related toxicity or leak hazards.
3. **Simpler Heat Exchanger:** Direct-contact flash evaporation removes the need for expensive titanium heat exchangers used in closed cycles.
4. **Vacuum-Driven:** Naturally exploits the low-pressure physics, requiring no external compressor.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Enormous Volume Flow:** Steam occupies ~ **30,000 × larger volume** per kg than liquid, requiring a **massive turbine** diameter.
2. **Vacuum Maintenance:** Maintaining < 0.05 bar in the flash chamber is technically challenging due to seawater ingress of air.
3. **Lower Real Efficiency:** Only ~ **1.5 % – 2 %** net, slightly lower than closed-cycle OTEC.
4. **High Capital Cost of CWP:** Cold Water Pipe still dominates project economics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (for Option b):**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Confusing the term "open cycle" with "open loop" in the Carnot sense. Explain clearly that *open* refers to the working fluid being seawater rather than a sealed refrigerant.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention **fresh water as a co-product** — examiners specifically test this awareness.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read Before Each Exam):**

- OTEC is a **solar derivative** — the sun is the *ultimate* energy source; ocean is merely the storage medium.
- **Minimum ΔT = 20 °C** is the universally cited operational threshold.
- Closed-Cycle OTEC = **Anderson Cycle** = NH₃ working fluid = **no fresh water**.
- Open-Cycle OTEC = **Claude Cycle** = seawater as working fluid = **fresh water co-product**.
- **Hybrid OTEC** = combines closed-cycle power + open-cycle desalination.
- Carnot efficiency **must use Kelvin** temperatures: $\eta = 1 - T_C / T_H$.
- Reference values: $\eta_{Carnot} \approx 6.7\,\%$, $\eta_{real} \approx 2 - 3\,\%$.
- **Cold Water Pipe (CWP)** depth ≈ **1000 m**; CWP is the largest capital cost.
- **Best sites:** Tropics within ±35° latitude — Hawaii, Tahiti, Philippines, Maldives.
- Working-fluid alternatives for closed cycle: **NH₃, R-134a, R-22, propane** — all low-boiling-point refrigerants.
- Titanium is the **standard heat-exchanger material** due to seawater corrosion.
- The **turbine** in closed-cycle is conventional Rankine-type; in open-cycle it is a **low-pressure axial turbine** with very large blade diameter.
- **Pure cold-water pumping parasitic load** can exceed 30 % of gross power — a key design trade-off.
- Land-based OTEC plants (Hawaii, 2015) demonstrated **> 100 kW** continuous output; offshore deep-ocean plants remain in pilot phase.
- **KTU Tag-Style:** Questions on this topic appear under Module 3 and frequently test the **derivation of Carnot efficiency** and **comparison of cycles**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy from Biomass: Principles of Bio-Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy from Biomass: Principles of Bio-Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass (KTU Syllabus Definition):** Biomass is any organic matter derived from living or recently living organisms — including agricultural crops, forestry residues, animal wastes, municipal solid waste (MSW), aquatic plants, and dedicated energy crops — that can be converted into useful forms of energy (heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous fuels) through biochemical, thermochemical, or physico-chemical conversion routes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bio-Conversion (KTU Module 3 Anchor Definition):** Bio-conversion is the controlled transformation of solid/liquid biomass feedstocks into high-grade secondary energy carriers (biogas, producer gas, bioethanol, biodiesel, charcoal, pyrolysis oil, etc.) by exploiting biological, thermal, or chemical pathways. The choice of route is governed by feedstock moisture content, carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio, particle size, and the desired end-use fuel.

The three master pathways recognised by the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

| Pathway Family | Driving Agent | Typical Moisture Range | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biochemical** | Micro-organisms / enzymes | High (50–95 %) | Biogas, ethanol, compost |
| **Thermochemical** | Heat (>300 °C) | Low (<15 %) | Heat, syngas, bio-oil, char |
| **Physico-chemical** | Chemical catalysts / solvents | Variable | Biodiesel, pellets, furfural |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of biomass bio-conversion like cooking the same ingredient three different ways to get three completely different meals.** 🥦
>
> - **Boiling it gently in a sealed pot (Biochemical route):** Microbes "eat" the wet biomass and "burp" out a flammable gas called **biogas** — just like yeast eats sugar and burps out the bubbles in bread (and alcohol in wine).
> - **Roasting it dry in a kiln (Thermochemical route):** When you heat dry biomass without oxygen (or with limited oxygen), the carbon and hydrogen are rearranged into producer gas, charcoal, or bio-oil — the way a barbecue converts wood into glowing charcoal and smoke.
> - **Squeezing & chemically reacting the oil (Physico-chemical route):** When the biomass already contains oil (like seeds of Jatropha or soybean), a chemical reaction with an alcohol converts the thick oil into thin, engine-friendly biodiesel — analogous to converting raw cream into butter through churning.

### 1.3 Why Bio-Conversion is a Pillar of Non-Conventional Energy Systems

- **Carbon-Neutral Cycle:** The CO₂ released during conversion is re-absorbed by the next generation of plants, closing the **carbon loop** within a human lifetime.
- **Decentralised Energy:** Small-scale digesters and gasifiers (5 kW – 5 MW) are ideal for **rural Kerala** villages where grid reliability is poor.
- **Waste-to-Wealth:** Kerala generates approximately **10,000 tonnes/day** of municipal solid waste and **4.5 million tonnes/year** of agricultural residues — all of which are convertible feedstocks.
- **Multi-Product Output:** A single biogas plant simultaneously produces **fuel**, **fertiliser** (digestate slurry), and **manure** — a triple revenue stream.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Biochemical Conversion Pathway

The biochemical pathway harnesses the metabolic machinery of micro-organisms to degrade complex organics. It is feasible **only when moisture content is high**, because water is the medium in which microbes operate.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — Hydrolysis:** Long-chain polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are first broken into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases).
- **Step 2 — Acidogenesis:** Acidogenic bacteria convert the monomers into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.
- **Step 3 — Acetogenesis:** VFAs are further oxidised into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
- **Step 4 — Methanogenesis:** Strict anaerobes (Methanobacterium, Methanococcus) convert acetic acid and H₂/CO₂ into **CH₄** and CO₂ — this is the rate-limiting step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Operating Envelope for Anaerobic Digesters:**
> - **pH:** 6.8 – 7.4 (methanogens die below 6.5)
> - **Temperature:** Mesophilic 30 – 40 °C **or** Thermophilic 50 – 60 °C
> - **C/N Ratio:** **25 – 30 : 1** (ideal). Below 20 → ammonia toxicity; above 35 → slow reaction.
> - **Retention Time:** 15 – 40 days (mesophilic); 10 – 20 days (thermophilic).

### 2.2 The Thermochemical Conversion Pathway

Thermochemical conversion requires **dry** feedstocks and uses heat as the prime mover.

- **Combustion (Direct Burning):** Complete oxidation in excess O₂. Used for steam generation and electricity. Efficiency typically **60 – 85 %** in modern boilers.
- **Gasification:** Partial oxidation at **800 – 1200 °C** with controlled air/steam supply. Output is **producer gas** (CO + H₂ + N₂ + CH₄ + CO₂). Cold-gas efficiency **70 – 80 %**.
- **Pyrolysis:** Heating **without oxygen** at **400 – 800 °C**. Products are **bio-char (solid)**, **bio-oil (liquid)**, and **pyro-gas (gas)** in varying proportions depending on temperature and heating rate.
- **Torrefaction:** A mild form of pyrolysis at **200 – 320 °C** that produces a coal-like solid fuel with higher energy density and hydrophobicity.

### 2.3 The Physico-Chemical Conversion Pathway

- **Transesterification (Biodiesel):** Vegetable oil / animal fat + alcohol (methanol/ethanol) + catalyst (NaOH/KOH) → **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** + Glycerol.
- **Hydrolysis (Cellulosic Ethanol):** Cellulose + H₂O (acid/enzyme) → Glucose → Fermentation → Ethanol.
- **Briquetting / Pelletisation:** Mechanical compaction of loose biomass into dense cubes or pellets (density 600 – 1200 kg/m³) for easy transport and combustion.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric CH₄ from glucose | $\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2}$ | — | Theoretical yield ≈ 0.37 m³ CH₄/kg glucose |
| 2 | Heating value of biogas | $HV_{mix} = \sum x_i \, HV_i$ | $x_i$ = mole/vol fraction; $HV_i$ = MJ/m³ | Biogas ≈ **22 MJ/m³** (60 % CH₄) |
| 3 | Calorific value of dry biomass | $GCV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | Ultimate analysis (mass %) | Wood ≈ **20 MJ/kg** |
| 4 | Air-Fuel ratio (combustion) | $A/F = \dfrac{4.31\,C + 4.34\,H + 1.12\,S - 0.43\,O}{100}$ | Ultimate analysis (%) | Biomass ≈ **5 – 7 : 1** |
| 5 | C/N ratio | $C/N = \dfrac{\%\,C\,(feedstock)}{\%\,N\,(feedstock)}$ | Mass percentages | Ideal 25 – 30 |
| 6 | Gasifier equivalence ratio | $ER = \dfrac{(O/F)_{actual}}{(O/F)_{stoich}}$ | Mass O₂ : mass fuel | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| 7 | Cold-gas efficiency | $\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas}\, \times\, HV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}\, \times\, HV_{fuel}} \times 100\%$ | kg/s, MJ/kg | 70 – 80 % |
| 8 | Biodiesel yield | $Y_{bd} = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100\%$ | kg biodiesel / kg oil | 90 – 98 % |
| 9 | Theoretical biogas yield (Boyle's) | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.40\,V_{VS}}{1.04}$ | $V_{VS}$ = kg volatile solids/day | 0.385 m³/kg VS |
| 10 | Methane fraction correlation | $\%\,CH_4 = 0.92\,(S/I) - 0.28$ | S/I = substrate/inoculum ratio | Empirical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU Numerical Problems:** Always convert ultimate analysis percentages to mass fractions (divide by 100) before substituting into the Dulong/GCV formula. Energy values must be in **MJ/kg** for mass-basis or **MJ/m³** for volume-basis; do not mix the two.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **India's SATAT Scheme (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** targets **5,000 CBG (Compressed Biogas) plants** by 2025 — directly leveraging the anaerobic-digestion bio-conversion route.
- **Kerala's NDBB Programme** (National Dairy Development Board) has installed over **50,000 household biogas plants** in Wayanad, Idukki, and Palakkad.
- **Oil-palm biomass gasification** in Malaysia and Indonesia now powers 5 – 25 MW grid-connected plants using empty fruit bunches (EFB).
- **Second-generation (2G) ethanol** plants in India use rice-straw hydrolysis — a physico-chemical route — to produce 50 – 60 million litres/year.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Biogas Yield from Glucose (Buswell's Equation)

The Buswell equation gives the theoretical methane yield from any organic substrate of the general formula $\mathrm{C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d}$.

**Starting with glucose (the simplest carbohydrate) $\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6}$:**

$$\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow a\,CH_4 + b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O}$$

Apply atom balance:
- **Carbon:** $6 = a + b$
- **Hydrogen:** $12 = 4a + 2c$
- **Oxygen:** $6 = 2b + c$

Solve the linear system:

From Carbon: $b = 6 - a$.

Substitute into Oxygen:

$$6 = 2(6-a) + c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 6 = 12 - 2a + c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 2a - 6$$

Substitute into Hydrogen:

$$12 = 4a + 2(2a - 6) = 4a + 4a - 12 = 8a - 12$$

$$8a = 24 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = 3$$

Back-substitute:

$$b = 6 - 3 = 3$$
$$c = 2(3) - 6 = 0$$

Therefore the balanced biochemical reaction is:

$$\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** One mole of glucose (180 g) produces **3 moles of CH₄ (≈ 67.2 L at STP)** and **3 moles of CO₂ (≈ 67.2 L)**. Hence the biogas is intrinsically 50:50 CH₄:CO₂ on a molar basis for pure glucose — but real digesters achieve 55 – 65 % CH₄ due to additional H₂-utilising methanogenesis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heating Value of Biogas Mixture

The calorific value of a gaseous mixture is the **mole-fraction-weighted sum** of the individual component calorific values.

$$HV_{mix} = \sum_{i} x_i \, HV_i$$

For a typical biogas with 60 % CH₄, 35 % CO₂, 3 % H₂S, 2 % N₂:

$$HV_{mix} = (0.60)(35.8) + (0.35)(0) + (0.03)(23.0) + (0.02)(0)$$

$$HV_{mix} = 21.48 + 0 + 0.69 + 0 = 22.17\;\mathrm{MJ/m^3}$$

Therefore, biogas at 60 % CH₄ has a calorific value ≈ **22.17 MJ/m³**, often rounded to **22 MJ/m³** in KTU numerical answers.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Dulong's Formula — Higher Calorific Value of Solid Biomass

Starting from the empirical correlation for coal and adapted for biomass:

$$GCV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$$

where C, H, S, O, N, A are the ultimate analysis mass percentages.

**Worked example:** A sample of coconut coir pith (abundant in Kerala) has ultimate analysis: C = 47 %, H = 5.5 %, O = 42 %, N = 0.5 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 4.9 %.

Substitute:

$$GCV = (0.3491)(47) + (1.1783)(5.5) + (0.1005)(0.1) - (0.1034)(42) - (0.0151)(0.5) - (0.0211)(4.9)$$

Step-wise evaluation:

$$GCV = 16.41 + 6.48 + 0.01 - 4.34 - 0.0076 - 0.10$$

$$GCV = 18.45\;\mathrm{MJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Always show the substitution line-by-line. Examiners award **partial credit** for correct substitution of each term, even if arithmetic slips occur.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Air-Fuel Ratio for Biomass Combustion

The stoichiometric oxygen requirement per kg of dry biomass is:

$$O_{stoich} = \dfrac{2.67\,C + 8\,H + S - O}{100}\;\;\;(\mathrm{kg\;O_2/kg\;fuel})$$

Corresponding stoichiometric air (since air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass):

$$A_{stoich} = \dfrac{O_{stoich}}{0.232} = \dfrac{4.31\,C + 4.34\,H + 1.12\,S - 0.43\,O}{100}$$

For the same coconut coir pith:

$$A_{stoich} = \dfrac{(4.31)(47) + (4.34)(5.5) + (1.12)(0.1) - (0.43)(42)}{100}$$

$$A_{stoich} = \dfrac{202.57 + 23.87 + 0.112 - 18.06}{100} = \dfrac{208.49}{100}$$

$$A_{stoich} \approx 2.08\;\mathrm{kg\;air/kg\;fuel}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Actual Air Supplied** = Stoichiometric Air × %Excess Air. Combustion of biomass typically uses **30 – 50 % excess air** to ensure complete burnout, so actual $A_{actual} \approx 2.7 - 3.1$ kg/kg.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Methane Yield via Boyle's Volatile Solids Method

For a mixed substrate, the empirical Boyle correlation gives:

$$Y_{CH_4} = 0.385\,\,V_{VS} \;\;\;(\mathrm{m^3\;CH_4\;at\;STP\;per\;kg\;VS\;added})$$

where $V_{VS}$ = volatile solids (kg/day) loaded into the digester.

**Worked Example:** A 50 m³ digester is loaded with **200 kg/day** of cattle dung having 80 % VS content.

Daily VS load:

$$V_{VS} = 200 \times 0.80 = 160\;\mathrm{kg\;VS/day}$$

Daily CH₄ produced:

$$Y_{CH_4} = 0.385 \times 160 = 61.6\;\mathrm{m^3\;CH_4/day}$$

Assuming 60 % CH₄ content in biogas:

$$\text{Total biogas} = \dfrac{61.6}{0.60} \approx 102.7\;\mathrm{m^3/day}$$

Energy output (using 35.8 MJ/m³ for pure CH₄):

$$E = 61.6 \times 35.8 = 2205\;\mathrm{MJ/day} \approx 612\;\mathrm{kWh/day}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define biomass and list any four biomass resources available in Kerala.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Biomass is any organic material of recent biological origin, excluding fossil fuels, that can be converted into useful energy via biochemical, thermochemical, or physico-chemical routes.

**Four Kerala biomass resources (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Coconut residues** (coir pith, shell, frond, husk) — Kerala produces ~7,500 million coconuts/year.
2. **Rice straw** — abundant in Kuttanad and Palakkad paddy belts.
3. **Animal dung** — cattle & goat manure from Wayanad dairy farms.
4. **Aquatic weeds / water hyacinth** — invasive species in Vembanad Lake.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Municipal solid waste from Kochi & Trivandrum corporations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Distinguish between anaerobic digestion and pyrolysis as bio-conversion routes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular comparison preferred):**

| Feature | Anaerobic Digestion | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|
| Driving agent | Micro-organisms | Heat (no oxygen) |
| Operating temperature | 30 – 60 °C | 400 – 800 °C |
| Moisture requirement | High (>50 %) | Low (<15 %) |
| Main products | Biogas (CH₄+CO₂) + digestate | Bio-oil, bio-char, pyro-gas |
| Reaction time | 15 – 40 days | Seconds – minutes |
| Energy efficiency | 30 – 60 % (conversion) | 70 – 85 % |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Bio-Conversion Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing mass-basis (MJ/kg) with volume-basis (MJ/m³)** calorific values — instant 3-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ultimate-analysis percentages to fractions** before substituting into Dulong's formula.
> 3. **Skipping atom-balance steps** in Buswell-stoichiometry derivations — examiners reward systematic balance.
> 4. **Confusing GCV and NCV** (Net Calorific Value): $NCV = GCV - 0.212\,H - 0.0245\,M$, where H and M are hydrogen and moisture mass %.
> 5. **Writing "combustion" instead of "gasification" or vice-versa** in process-flow diagrams — the temperature and ER (equivalence ratio) windows are different.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the various bio-conversion routes available for converting biomass into useful energy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Bio-conversion routes are categorised into three families based on the conversion mechanism: biochemical, thermochemical, and physico-chemical.

**Biochemical Route (2 marks):**
- **Anaerobic digestion:** Decomposition of organic matter by microbes in oxygen-free environment at 30 – 60 °C. Yields **biogas (50 – 65 % CH₄)** and nutrient-rich digestate slurry.
- **Alcohol fermentation:** Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) converts sugars into ethanol at 25 – 35 °C. Used for bioethanol from sugarcane/molasses.
- **Aerobic composting:** In presence of O₂, microbes convert organic waste into humus-rich compost.

**Thermochemical Route (2 marks):**
- **Combustion:** Direct burning of dry biomass in excess air — produces heat for boilers, kilns, steam turbines.
- **Gasification:** Partial oxidation at 800 – 1200 °C with controlled air/steam — produces **producer gas** (CO + H₂).
- **Pyrolysis:** Heating without oxygen at 400 – 800 °C — yields bio-oil, bio-char, and pyro-gas.

**Physico-Chemical Route (1 mark):**
- **Transesterification** of vegetable oils with alcohol (methanol) and catalyst (NaOH) yields **biodiesel (FAME)**.
- **Briquetting** of loose biomass into dense pellets (density 600 – 1200 kg/m³).

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 above.

---

**(b)** A biomass sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 40 %, N = 1 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 2.5 %. Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula and the stoichiometric air required for complete combustion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall Dulong's formula (1 mark):**

$$GCV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$GCV = (0.3491)(50) + (1.1783)(6) + (0.1005)(0.5) - (0.1034)(40) - (0.0151)(1) - (0.0211)(2.5)$$

$$GCV = 17.455 + 7.070 + 0.050 - 4.136 - 0.015 - 0.053$$

$$GCV = 20.371\;\mathrm{MJ/kg} \approx 20.37\;\mathrm{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air formula (1 mark):**

$$A/F = \dfrac{4.31\,C + 4.34\,H + 1.12\,S - 0.43\,O}{100}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$A/F = \dfrac{(4.31)(50) + (4.34)(6) + (1.12)(0.5) - (0.43)(40)}{100}$$

$$A/F = \dfrac{215.5 + 26.04 + 0.56 - 17.2}{100} = \dfrac{224.9}{100}$$

$$A/F = 2.249\;\mathrm{kg\;air/kg\;fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Final statement (1 mark):** The biomass has a GCV of **20.37 MJ/kg** and requires **2.25 kg of air per kg of fuel** for stoichiometric combustion.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Set B, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With the help of Buswell's stoichiometric equation, derive the theoretical methane yield per kg of glucose. Also comment on the limitations of this yield in real digesters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write unbalanced reaction (1 mark):**

$$\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 + H_2O}$$

**Step 2 — Assign stoichiometric coefficients (1 mark):**

$$\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow a\,CH_4 + b\,CO_2 + c\,H_2O}$$

**Step 3 — Apply atom balance (2 marks):**
- C: $6 = a + b$
- H: $12 = 4a + 2c$
- O: $6 = 2b + c$

Solving simultaneously: $a = 3$, $b = 3$, $c = 0$.

**Step 4 — Final balanced equation (1 mark):**

$$\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute yield (1 mark):** Molecular weight of glucose = 180 g/mol. 1 mole gives 3 moles CH₄ = 3 × 22.4 L = **67.2 L CH₄** at STP. Per kg glucose: $1000 \times 67.2 / 180 = \mathbf{373\;L\;CH_4/kg}$.

**Step 6 — Limitations in real digesters (1 mark):**
- Inhibitory compounds (ammonia, VFAs, H₂S) lower actual yield to **200 – 250 L CH₄/kg glucose**.
- Pathogens (toxins, temperature shocks) reduce methanogen activity.
- Non-ideal mixing and dead-zones reduce conversion efficiency.

---

**(b)** A biogas plant receives **400 kg/day** of mixed waste with **75 % volatile solids** content. Using Boyle's correlation, estimate the daily methane production and the equivalent electrical energy output if the biogas drives a generator set with **35 % electrical efficiency**. Assume CH₄ fraction = **60 %** and HV of CH₄ = **35.8 MJ/m³**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute VS load (1 mark):**

$$V_{VS} = 400 \times 0.75 = 300\;\mathrm{kg\;VS/day}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boyle's correlation (1 mark):**

$$Y_{CH_4} = 0.385 \times 300 = 115.5\;\mathrm{m^3\;CH_4/day}$$

**Step 3 — Total biogas from CH₄ fraction (1 mark):**

$$\text{Biogas} = \dfrac{115.5}{0.60} = 192.5\;\mathrm{m^3/day}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal energy content (1 mark):**

$$E_{thermal} = 115.5 \times 35.8 = 4134.9\;\mathrm{MJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Electrical energy output (1 mark):**

$$E_{elec} = 4134.9 \times 0.35 = 1447.2\;\mathrm{MJ/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh (1 mark):** Using 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ:

$$E_{elec} = \dfrac{1447.2}{3.6} \approx 402\;\mathrm{kWh/day}$$

**Step 7 — Final statement (1 mark):** The plant produces **115.5 m³/day of CH₄** and generates approximately **402 kWh/day** of electrical energy — enough to power ~40 rural Kerala households at 10 kWh/day each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Energy from Biomass: Principles of Bio-Conversion**

- ✅ **Biomass** = recent organic matter (≠ fossil fuels); Kerala has coconut, rice straw, dung, aquatic weeds.
- ✅ **Three bio-conversion families:** Biochemical (wet), Thermochemical (dry), Physico-chemical (oil-bearing).
- ✅ **Anaerobic digestion stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis (rate-limiting).
- ✅ **Buswell's balanced equation for glucose:** $\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2}$ → **373 L CH₄/kg glucose** (theoretical).
- ✅ **Optimal digester pH:** 6.8 – 7.4; **C/N ratio:** 25 – 30 : 1; **Mesophilic T:** 30 – 40 °C.
- ✅ **Dulong's GCV formula** uses ultimate analysis mass % — remember to multiply each % by its coefficient and sum.
- ✅ **Air-Fuel ratio** for biomass ≈ **2 – 6 kg/kg**; Excess air 30 – 50 % typical.
- ✅ **Boyle's correlation:** $Y_{CH_4} = 0.385\,V_{VS}$ (m³ CH₄ / kg VS).
- ✅ **Biogas calorific value ≈ 22 MJ/m³** at 60 % CH₄; pure CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³.
- ✅ **Pyrolysis window:** 400 – 800 °C, no O₂ → bio-oil / bio-char / pyro-gas.
- ✅ **Gasification window:** 800 – 1200 °C, ER = 0.2 – 0.4 → producer gas.
- ✅ **Transesterification:** Oil + Methanol + NaOH → **FAME (Biodiesel) + Glycerol** (90 – 98 % yield).
- ✅ **Carbon-neutral loop:** CO₂ from bio-conversion ↔ CO₂ absorbed by next plant generation.
- ✅ **GCV vs NCV:** Always subtract latent heat of water vapour ($0.212\,H + 0.0245\,M$) for NCV.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas Production)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas Production)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a sequence of biochemical processes by which microorganisms break down biodegradable organic material in the **complete absence of oxygen**, producing a renewable gaseous fuel known as **biogas** (primarily a mixture of methane, $\text{CH}_4$, and carbon dioxide, $\text{CO}_2$) along with a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate**.

The generalized stoichiometric combustion of the methane component is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -890 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This is the core process studied under **bio-energy conversion pathways** in KTU Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Anaerobic digestion is classified under **Biochemical Conversion** of biomass, distinctly different from Thermo-chemical conversion (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis). The product biogas is a *secondary energy carrier*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach"

Think of anaerobic digestion as the **industrial replication of a cow's rumen (its first stomach chamber)**.

- A cow chews grass, swallows it, and the microbes in its oxygen-free stomach break it down over hours/days, releasing methane (which the cow burps out).
- An **anaerobic digester** is essentially a sealed, heated, stirred, oxygen-excluded "industrial cow stomach" placed inside a steel or concrete tank.
- We feed it food waste, cow dung, sewage, or crop residue → microbes digest it → methane-rich biogas is collected from the top of the sealed tank.

This makes anaerobic digestion a **closed-loop waste-to-energy** technology that simultaneously:
1. Generates combustible fuel (biogas)
2. Produces organic fertilizer (digestate)
3. Reduces greenhouse gas emissions (captured $\text{CH}_4$ instead of released)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| Calorific value of pure methane | **55.5 MJ/kg** |
| Calorific value of biogas (60% CH₄) | **≈ 22–25 MJ/m³** |
| Density of methane (STP) | **0.717 kg/m³** |
| Optimum digester pH | **6.8 – 7.4** |
| Optimum C/N ratio | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** |
| Mesophilic temperature range | **30 °C – 40 °C** |
| Thermophilic temperature range | **50 °C – 60 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (SRC):** All gas volumes in KTU problems are measured at **STP: 0 °C (273.15 K) and 1.01325 bar**, unless otherwise stated. Methane yield is reported in **Nm³ CH₄ per kg VS** (Normal cubic meter methane per kilogram Volatile Solids added).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Bio-Chemical Stages

Anaerobic digestion is **not a single reaction** — it is a four-stage microbial food chain. Each stage is performed by a different group of bacteria, and they must live in symbiosis.

### **Stage 1: Hydrolysis**
- Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into **monomers** (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
- Performed by **extracellular enzymes** (amylases, proteases, lipases) secreted by hydrolytic bacteria.
- **Rate-limiting step** for feedstocks rich in lignocellulose or fats.

$$\text{Polymers} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{hydrolases}} \text{Monomers (sugars, AA, fatty acids)}$$

### **Stage 2: Acidogenesis (Fermentation)**
- Monomers are converted into **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** like acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid, plus $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, and alcohols.
- Performed by **acidogenic bacteria**.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$

### **Stage 3: Acetogenesis**
- VFAs (longer than acetic acid) and alcohols are oxidized into **acetic acid**, $\text{H}_2$, and $\text{CO}_2$.
- Performed by **acetogenic bacteria**, which require very low partial pressure of hydrogen ($< 10^{-4}$ atm) to remain thermodynamically favorable.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COO}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$$

### **Stage 4: Methanogenesis**
- The final and most sensitive stage. Two pathways exist:

| Pathway | Reaction | Bacteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Acetoclastic** (≈ 70% of CH₄) | $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$ | *Methanosarcina, Methanosaeta* |
| **Hydrogenotrophic** (≈ 30% of CH₄) | $\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | *Methanobacterium, Methanococcus* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the four stages matter for KTU:** Methanogens are extremely sensitive to pH, temperature, ammonia, and VFAs. A sudden surge in Stage 1-2 output (overloading) causes VFA accumulation → pH drop → **digester souring (failure)**. This is a guaranteed 7- or 14-mark question topic.

## 2.2 Why Anaerobic Digestion Works — The Thermodynamic "Why"

The methanogens harvest only **~5–15%** of the original organic energy for cell synthesis; the rest is converted to biogas. This is why AD is energetically *very efficient* compared to aerobic composting (where ~60% of energy is lost as heat).

The Gibb's free energy change of methanogenesis is highly negative (spontaneous):

$$\Delta G^\circ = -131 \text{ kJ/mol} \text{ (for acetoclastic methanogenesis)}$$

## 2.3 Operating Parameters — The KTU "Must-Know" List

| Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Failure Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 30–40 °C | 50–60 °C | Below 20 °C → digestion stops |
| **pH** | 6.8 – 7.4 | 6.8 – 7.4 | < 6.3 → methanogens die |
| **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)** | 15 – 30 days | 10 – 20 days | Too short → washout |
| **SRT (Solid Retention Time)** | 20 – 40 days | 10 – 20 days | < SRT_min → cell washout |
| **OLR (Organic Loading Rate)** | 1 – 4 kg VS/m³·d | 2 – 6 kg VS/m³·d | Too high → VFA accumulation |
| **C/N ratio** | 20 – 30 : 1 | 20 – 30 : 1 | < 15 → ammonia toxicity; > 35 → N deficiency |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{CH}_4} = Y_s \cdot m_{\text{VS,added}}$ | $Y_s$ = methane yield, $m_{\text{VS}}$ = mass volatile solids | Nm³ CH₄, kg VS |
| 2 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{VS,added}}}{V_{\text{digester}} \cdot t}$ | $V$ = digester volume, $t$ = time | kg VS/m³·d |
| 3 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{\text{digester}}}{Q}$ | $Q$ = feed flow rate | days |
| 4 | $E_{\text{biogas}} = V_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = thermal conversion efficiency | MJ or kWh |
| 5 | $\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} = 0.60 \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4}$ | Assumes 60% CH₄ fraction | MJ/m³ |
| 6 | $m_{\text{CH}_4} = V_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot \rho_{\text{CH}_4}$ | $\rho_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.717$ kg/m³ | kg |
| 7 | $P_{\text{thermal}} = \dot{V}_{\text{biogas}} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{biogas}}$ | Continuous power output | kW (thermal) |
| 8 | $P_{\text{electrical}} = P_{\text{thermal}} \cdot \eta_{\text{CHP}}$ | $\eta_{\text{CHP}} \approx 0.30\text{–}0.40$ | kW (electric) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** AD plants are deployed globally for:
> - **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** management in cities like Delhi, Stockholm, Singapore.
> - **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** for sludge stabilization (e.g., Cochin Smart City STP).
> - **Dairy farm waste** → cow-dung based family-size **Khadi & Village Industries Commission (KVIC)** biogas plants in rural Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Biogas Energy Output from a Continuous-Feed Digester

**Problem statement (typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
A dairy farm operates a **100 m³ CSTR digester** with a daily feed of **1500 kg of cattle dung** containing **80% moisture and 18% VS (of total solids)**. The digester runs at mesophilic temperature (35 °C) and produces a methane yield of **0.25 Nm³ CH₄ per kg VS added**. Methane fraction in biogas is **62%**. Combined Heat & Power (CHP) unit has electrical efficiency of **35%** and heat efficiency of **45%**. Calculate the daily (i) volume of biogas produced, (ii) electrical energy output, and (iii) thermal energy output.

---

### **Step 1 — Compute Total Solids (TS) fed per day**

We are told moisture is 80%, so total solids (dry matter) fraction = **20%**.

$$\text{TS}_{\text{added}} = m_{\text{feed}} \times (1 - \text{moisture fraction})$$

$$\text{TS}_{\text{added}} = 1500 \text{ kg} \times (1 - 0.80)$$

$$\text{TS}_{\text{added}} = 1500 \times 0.20 = 300 \text{ kg TS/day}$$

*This converts wet feed mass to dry mass basis.*

### **Step 2 — Compute Volatile Solids (VS) fed per day**

Volatile Solids (VS) are the organic fraction of TS. Given as 18% of TS:

$$\text{VS}_{\text{added}} = \text{TS}_{\text{added}} \times \text{VS fraction}$$

$$\text{VS}_{\text{added}} = 300 \text{ kg} \times 0.18 = 54 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

*The VS fraction represents the biodegradable organic load.*

### **Step 3 — Compute Daily Methane Volume**

Apply the specific methane yield $Y_s = 0.25$ Nm³ CH₄/kg VS:

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = Y_s \times \text{VS}_{\text{added}}$$

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.25 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kg VS} \times 54 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

$$V_{\text{CH}_4} = 13.5 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}$$

### **Step 4 — Compute Daily Biogas Volume**

Biogas contains 62% CH₄ (by volume), so the total biogas is larger:

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{V_{\text{CH}_4}}{\text{CH}_4 \text{ fraction}}$$

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = \frac{13.5}{0.62}$$

$$V_{\text{biogas}} = 21.77 \text{ Nm}^3 \text{ biogas/day}$$

### **Step 5 — Calorific Value of Biogas**

The Calorific Value of pure methane is $\text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4} = 35.88 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$ (this is the value at STP). For a 62% CH₄ mixture:

$$\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} = 0.62 \times 35.88 = 22.25 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$

### **Step 6 — Total Chemical Energy in Biogas (per day)**

$$E_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{biogas}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{biogas}}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 21.77 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day} \times 22.25 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 484.4 \text{ MJ/day}$$

### **Step 7 — Electrical Energy Output**

Apply electrical efficiency of CHP unit ($\eta_e = 0.35$):

$$E_{\text{electrical}} = E_{\text{total}} \times \eta_e$$

$$E_{\text{electrical}} = 484.4 \times 0.35 = 169.5 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$E_{\text{electrical}} = \frac{169.5}{3.6} = 47.09 \text{ kWh/day}$$

### **Step 8 — Thermal Energy Output**

Apply thermal efficiency of CHP unit ($\eta_t = 0.45$):

$$E_{\text{thermal}} = E_{\text{total}} \times \eta_t = 484.4 \times 0.45 = 218.0 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Converting: $E_{\text{thermal}} = 60.55 \text{ kWh/day}$

### **Final Answer Summary**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Daily biogas production | **21.77 Nm³/day** |
| Daily methane production | **13.5 Nm³ CH₄/day** |
| Daily electrical energy | **47.09 kWh/day** |
| Daily thermal energy | **60.55 kWh/day** |
| Total energy recovered | **169.5 + 218.0 = 387.5 MJ/day** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sizing a Digester from Target OLR

**Problem statement:** Design a digester to treat **10 tonnes/day of vegetable market waste** (VS = 12% of wet mass) at an **Organic Loading Rate (OLR) of 3 kg VS/m³·d**. Compute the required digester volume.

### **Step 1 — Compute daily VS load**

$$\text{VS}_{\text{added}} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \times 0.12 = 1200 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

### **Step 2 — Apply the OLR definition**

By definition:

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{\text{VS}_{\text{added}}}{V_{\text{digester}}}$$

Rearranging for $V_{\text{digester}}$:

$$V_{\text{digester}} = \frac{\text{VS}_{\text{added}}}{\text{OLR}}$$

$$V_{\text{digester}} = \frac{1200 \text{ kg VS/day}}{3 \text{ kg VS/m}^3\cdot\text{d}} = 400 \text{ m}^3$$

### **Step 3 — Retention time check**

Assuming $Q = 10$ m³/day liquid feed equivalent:

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ days}$$

This is within the mesophilic safety range (15–30 days minimum for mixed waste), so the design is acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Digester Types

| Digester Type | HRT | OLR Suitability | Mixing | Best Feedstock | Construction Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | 40–60 d | Low | None | Farmyard manure, FOG | Low |
| **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor)** | 15–30 d | 1–4 kg VS/m³·d | Mechanical / gas recirculation | Slurries < 10% TS | Medium |
| **Plug Flow** | 20–30 d | 2–5 kg VS/m³·d | Piston-like flow | Cattle dung, fibrous slurries | Medium |
| **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)** | 4–12 h (for wastewater) | Very high | Upflow | Soluble wastewater | High |
| **Fixed Dome (KVIC model)** | 30–50 d | 1–2 kg VS/m³·d | None | Cattle dung | Low (for rural) |
| **Floating Drum (PRAI model)** | 30–50 d | 1–2 kg VS/m³·d | None | Cattle dung | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU key takeaway:** Always specify whether a reactor operates at **mesophilic** or **thermophilic** regime in numerical answers, because methane yield, OLR, and HRT limits differ. Examiner deduction of 1–2 marks is common for not specifying temperature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Stage Biochemical Pathway (Functional Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FEEDSTOCK\nComplex Polymers\nCarbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids"] --> B["STAGE 1 HYDROLYSIS\nHydrolytic Bacteria\nAmylases Proteases Lipases"]
    B --> C["STAGE 2 ACIDOGENESIS\nAcidogenic Bacteria"]
    C --> D["STAGE 3 ACETOGENESIS\nAcetogenic Bacteria"]
    D --> E["STAGE 4 METHANOGENESIS\nMethanogenic Archaea"]
    E --> F["BIOGAS OUTPUT\n60 to 70 percent CH4\n30 to 40 percent CO2"]
    A --> G["WATER H2O"] --> B
    D --> H["VFAs Acetate H2 CO2"] --> E
    E --> I["DIGESTATE OUTPUT\nNutrient rich slurry"]
```

> **Reading guide:** Node IDs are alphanumeric. The flow goes from raw biomass (left) through the four biological stages to biogas (right) and digestate (bottom right). The H₂O and VFAs nodes show the intermediate flow of water and intermediate acids required by Stages 1 and 4 respectively.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a CSTR Biogas Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["FEED PREPARATION UNIT"]
        S1["Collection Pit\nMixing of dung water\nParticle size reduction"]
    end
    subgraph A2["DIGESTION UNIT CSTR"]
        S2["Digester Tank\nSealed Oxygen free\nHeated to 35 to 55 C"]
        S3["Mechanical Mixer\nSlow speed agitator\nGas recirculation option"]
    end
    subgraph A3["GAS HANDLING UNIT"]
        S4["Gas Holder\nFloating dome\nPressure regulation"]
        S5["Purification\nH2S removal by iron oxide\nMoisture trap and silica gel"]
    end
    subgraph A4["ENERGY CONVERSION UNIT"]
        S6["CHP Engine\nGas turbine or IC engine\n35 percent electrical efficiency"]
        S7["Flare Stack\nEmergency gas burner\nSafety vent"]
    end
    subgraph A5["OUTPUT UTILIZATION"]
        S8["Electricity to Grid\nHot water to digester heating\nDigested slurry to farm field"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 <--> S3
    S2 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S4 --> S7
    S6 --> S8
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a block-level functional architecture, not a P&ID. It shows the *information/flow architecture* through five sub-systems: Feed Prep → Digestion → Gas Handling → Energy Conversion → Utilization. Note the **feedback loop**: heat from CHP is recycled to keep the digester at mesophilic temperature — this is the hallmark of an energy-self-sufficient AD plant.

## 4.3 Process State Machine — Digester Failure Recovery

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STABLE
    STABLE --> OVERLOADED: OLR exceeds design OR sudden VFA spike
    OVERLOADED --> SOUR: pH drops below 6.3
    SOUR --> STABLE: Stop feeding for 24 to 48 h
    SOUR --> FAILED: Methanogens washout irreversible
    FAILED --> [*]
    STABLE --> FOAMING: Excessive protein OR overmixing
    FOAMING --> STABLE: Add antifoam OR reduce mixing
    STABLE --> AMMONIA_TOX: Free NH3 exceeds 1500 mg per L
    AMMONIA_TOX --> STABLE: Dilute feedstock OR reduce temperature
```

> **Reading guide:** This state diagram is essential for KTU viva and 14-mark questions on "operational issues in AD plants". The student must remember the **three primary failure modes**: souring, foaming, ammonia toxicity — and the corresponding recovery action.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion and write the chemical reaction for methanogenesis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four stages of anaerobic digestion are:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down by extracellular enzymes into soluble monomers like sugars, amino acids, and fatty acids. *(1 mark)*

2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are fermented by acidogenic bacteria into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), alcohols, $\text{CO}_2$, and $\text{H}_2$. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols are oxidized by acetogens into acetic acid, $\text{H}_2$, and $\text{CO}_2$. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Methanogenesis** — Methanogenic archaea convert the intermediates to methane. The two main reactions are: *(1 mark)*

$$\text{Acetoclastic: } \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{Hydrogenotrophic: } \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the C/N ratio in anaerobic digestion? Why is a C/N ratio of 20:1 to 30:1 considered optimal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**C/N ratio** is the mass ratio of **Carbon to Nitrogen** present in the biodegradable feed. *(1 mark)*

- Microbes need both carbon (for energy) and nitrogen (for protein synthesis). Methanogens require a C/N of **20:1 to 30:1** for balanced growth. *(1 mark)*
- If **C/N is too low** (e.g., 5:1 — excess nitrogen like pure urine), free ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) is released, which is **toxic** to methanogens.
- If **C/N is too high** (e.g., 60:1 — high carbon like sawdust), nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient, slowing microbial growth, and VFAs accumulate causing pH drop. *(1 mark)*

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four stages of anaerobic digestion with the help of chemical equations, the bacterial groups involved, and the products formed at each stage. Discuss why methanogenesis is the most sensitive stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A community biogas plant in rural Kerala receives **2000 kg/day of mixed kitchen waste and cow dung** having a moisture content of **75%** and VS = **20% of TS**. The digester operates at mesophilic conditions with a methane yield of **0.30 Nm³ CH₄/kg VS**, and biogas contains **65% CH₄**. Calculate:
   - (i) Daily mass of Volatile Solids added.
   - (ii) Daily methane and biogas volume produced.
   - (iii) Daily electrical energy generated by a CHP unit operating at 32% electrical efficiency. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

- **Hydrolysis (1.5 marks):** Complex insoluble polymers → soluble monomers. Bacteria: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*. Enzymes: cellulases, amylases, proteases, lipases. Example: $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow n\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$.

- **Acidogenesis (1.5 marks):** Sugars, amino acids → VFAs + alcohols + $\text{H}_2$ + $\text{CO}_2$. Bacteria: *Clostridium*, *Bifidobacterium*. Equation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$.

- **Acetogenesis (1.5 marks):** VFAs → acetate + $\text{H}_2$ + $\text{CO}_2$. Bacteria: *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas*. Equation: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COO}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$.

- **Methanogenesis (1.5 marks):** Acetate + $\text{H}_2$/$\text{CO}_2$ → $\text{CH}_4$. Two pathways: acetoclastic (70%) and hydrogenotrophic (30%).

- **Why methanogenesis is most sensitive (1 mark):** Methanogens have the **slowest growth rate** (doubling time 2–25 days), are highly **pH-sensitive** (optimum 6.8–7.4, fail below 6.3), and are **inhibited** by ammonia, sulfide, VFAs, and heavy metals. A small environmental disturbance can cause population washout.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Daily VS added:**
- Moisture = 75% → Total Solids = 25% of feed.
- $\text{TS} = 2000 \times 0.25 = 500$ kg TS/day.
- $\text{VS} = 500 \times 0.20 = 100$ kg VS/day. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Daily methane and biogas volume:**
- $V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.30 \times 100 = 30$ Nm³ CH₄/day. *(1.5 marks)*
- $V_{\text{biogas}} = \dfrac{30}{0.65} = 46.15$ Nm³/day. *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Daily electrical energy:**
- $\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} = 0.65 \times 35.88 = 23.32$ MJ/Nm³.
- $E_{\text{total}} = 46.15 \times 23.32 = 1076.2$ MJ/day. *(1 mark)*
- $E_{\text{electrical}} = 1076.2 \times 0.32 = 344.4$ MJ/day $= \dfrac{344.4}{3.6} = 95.66$ kWh/day. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the design and operational parameters of a **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** biogas digester. Explain the role of HRT, SRT, OLR, pH, and temperature in determining digester stability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A continuously operated CSTR digester with volume **500 m³** receives a feed of **30 m³/day** containing **60 kg VS/m³**. If the methane yield coefficient is **0.28 Nm³ CH₄/kg VS added** and the methane content in biogas is **60%**, determine:
   - (i) The Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT).
   - (ii) The Organic Loading Rate (OLR).
   - (iii) The daily methane and total biogas production.
   - (iv) Comment on whether the digester is in a stable mesophilic operating range. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

- **CSTR description (1.5 marks):** A fully mixed, mechanically agitated, heated, sealed tank with continuous feed and discharge. The contents are assumed **completely homogeneous** — outlet concentration = inside concentration.

- **HRT (1 mark):** $\text{HRT} = V/Q$ — average time feed liquid spends in digester. Affects organic matter degradation: longer HRT → more conversion but smaller digester throughput.

- **SRT (1 mark):** $\text{SRT} = V \cdot X / (Q \cdot X)$ — average time microbes stay inside. **Must be greater than the minimum doubling time** of methanogens to prevent washout. For mesophilic: SRT ≥ 15 days.

- **OLR (1 mark):** kg VS per m³ digester volume per day. Optimum 1–4 kg VS/m³·d for mesophilic CSTR. Beyond this, VFA accumulation and pH crash.

- **pH (1 mark):** Mesophilic methanogens prefer **6.8 – 7.4**. Below 6.3 → toxicity. Above 7.6 → free ammonia toxicity.

- **Temperature (1 mark):** Mesophilic (35 °C) is more stable and energy-efficient; thermophilic (55 °C) gives higher kinetics but is less stable and consumes more heat for the digester.

- **Stability criteria (0.5 mark):** Stable operation = constant pH 6.8–7.4, VFA/alkalinity ratio < 0.3, methane content > 55%.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) HRT:**
- $\text{HRT} = V/Q = 500/30 = 16.67$ days. *(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) OLR:**
- $\text{VS}_{\text{added}} = Q \times C_{\text{VS}} = 30 \times 60 = 1800$ kg VS/day.
- $\text{OLR} = 1800/500 = 3.6$ kg VS/m³·d. *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Daily methane and biogas:**
- $V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.28 \times 1800 = 504$ Nm³ CH₄/day. *(1.5 marks)*
- $V_{\text{biogas}} = 504 / 0.60 = 840$ Nm³/day. *(1 mark)*

**(iv) Stability comment (1.5 marks):**
- HRT = 16.67 days → within mesophilic safe range (15–30 days) ✓
- OLR = 3.6 kg VS/m³·d → within mesophilic optimum (1–4) ✓
- Conclusion: **The digester is operating in a stable mesophilic range**, but is **near the upper OLR limit**. A small surge in VS concentration or feed flow could cause VFA accumulation and pH drop. Recommend monitoring VFA/alkalinity ratio and providing a 24-h feed buffer tank.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $V_{\text{biogas}}$ when the question asked for $V_{\text{CH}_4}$, or use **m³** instead of **Nm³** (Normal cubic meter at STP).
> 2. **Forgot to convert moisture → TS → VS** (–2 marks): If the problem gives feed on a wet basis, you must walk through the TS and VS conversion explicitly. Examiner will not give partial credit for an incomplete chain.
> 3. **Missing temperature specification (–1 mark):** Always state "operates at mesophilic 35 °C" or "thermophilic 55 °C" in your answer.
> 4. **Conflating HRT and SRT (–1 mark):** HRT applies to *liquid* residence; SRT applies to *microbial solids* residence. They are equal only in CSTR with no biomass recycle.
> 5. **Skipping the dividing step in biogas calculation:** Forgetting to divide methane volume by CH₄ fraction is the single most common error in energy problems.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** = biological breakdown of organic matter *without oxygen* → produces **biogas** (60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂) and **digestate** (fertilizer).
- ⭐ **Four stages (in order):** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. Each is performed by a *different microbial community*.
- ⭐ **Methanogens** are the slowest-growing (doubling time 2–25 days) and the **rate-limiting** group. Methanogenic failure = digester failure.
- ⭐ **Optimum operating window:** pH 6.8–7.4, T = 35 °C (mesophilic) or 55 °C (thermophilic), C/N = 20:1 to 30:1.
- ⭐ **HRT** (hydraulic) ≠ **SRT** (solids/microbial). In CSTR they are equal; in UASB/AF, SRT >> HRT.
- ⭐ **OLR** (kg VS/m³·d) is the **primary design parameter** — once chosen, digester volume = VS_added/OLR.
- ⭐ **Methane yield** is reported as **Nm³ CH₄ per kg VS added**. Typical values: cow dung 0.20–0.30, kitchen waste 0.30–0.50, sewage sludge 0.10–0.20.
- ⭐ **Energy formula chain (must memorize):** $V_{\text{biogas}} = V_{\text{CH}_4}/f_{\text{CH}_4}$ → $E_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{biogas}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{biogas}}$ → $E_{\text{electric}} = E_{\text{total}} \times \eta_e$ → divide by 3.6 to convert MJ → kWh.
- ⭐ **CV of pure methane = 35.88 MJ/Nm³ (or 55.5 MJ/kg)**. For biogas, scale by CH₄ fraction.
- ⭐ **Three failure modes** in real plants: **souring (low pH), foaming (excess protein), ammonia toxicity (low C/N)**. Each has a known corrective action.
- ⭐ **CHP** = Combined Heat & Power. Typical $\eta_e = 30\text{–}40\%$, $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 40\text{–}50\%$, with heat recovery often used to keep the digester warm (energy self-sufficiency).
- ⭐ **KTU-typical numerical answer format:** Always show: (1) assumption of temperature regime, (2) TS and VS conversion chain, (3) explicit substitution with units, (4) final answer in a boxed/tabulated form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
