## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or microalgal lipids. It is produced primarily through a chemical process called **transesterification**, in which a triglyceride molecule reacts with a short-chain alcohol (typically methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst to yield **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** and glycerol as a by-product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Biodiesel is a clean-burning, non-toxic, biodegradable, and renewable alternative fuel that can be used in conventional Compression Ignition (CI) engines either in its pure form (B100) or as a blend with petroleum diesel (B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of biodiesel like **converting raw honey into sugar syrup**. The raw honey (vegetable oil) is thick, viscous, and hard to pour — it won't flow well through a diesel engine's fuel injection system. By chemically "splitting" it using alcohol and a catalyst (like adding water and heat to honey), we get a thin, easily flowing liquid (biodiesel) that engines can use efficiently. The "leftover" glycerol is like the wax/sediment that settles at the bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Biodiesel Matters Today:**
> - Global biodiesel production exceeded **45 billion liters** (2023).
> - Reduces **CO₂ emissions by 78%** compared to fossil diesel (lifecycle basis).
> - **Renewable**, **biodegradable**, and **non-toxic**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Biodiesel Engineering

| Parameter | Standard Range | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Density** | 860 – 900 kg/m³ | Affects fuel injection mass |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | 3.5 – 5.0 cSt (at 40°C) | Critical for atomization |
| **Cetane Number** | 45 – 65 | Ignition quality measure |
| **Flash Point** | > 130°C | Safe storage and transport |
| **Calorific Value** | 37 – 40 MJ/kg | Energy release upon combustion |
| **Oxygen Content** | 10 – 12% | Aids cleaner combustion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Manufacturing Routes for Biodiesel

There are **four principal methods** for producing biodiesel from oils/fats. Understanding each is critical for KTU board examinations.

#### Method 1: Direct Use and Blending
- Vegetable oils are used directly or blended (20%, 50%) with diesel.
- **Limitation:** High viscosity (~30-40 cSt) causes poor atomization, carbon deposits, and engine knocking.
- *Not preferred industrially.*

#### Method 2: Microemulsions
- Oils are mixed with alcohols (ethanol/methanol) and surfactants to form microemulsions.
- Improves viscosity, but long-term use causes injector coking.

#### Method 3: Thermal Cracking (Pyrolysis)
- Oils are heated to **300°C – 800°C** in absence of air, breaking long hydrocarbon chains.
- Produces a wide range of products; expensive to control.

#### Method 4: Transesterification (Most Common) ⭐
- The **industry-standard** method due to simplicity, low cost, and high yield.

### 2.2 Transesterification: The Core Process

Transesterification is a **three-step reversible reaction** where triglyceride + alcohol → diglyceride → monoglyceride → glycerol + 3 moles of FAME.

**Stoichiometry:**
- 1 mole triglyceride : **3 moles alcohol** (molar ratio)
- Catalyst (NaOH or KOH): **0.5% – 1%** by weight of oil
- Temperature: **60°C – 65°C** (just below methanol boiling point of 64.7°C)
- Reaction time: **1 – 4 hours**

### 2.3 The Transesterification Reaction

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{ROH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3 \, \text{R'COOR} + \text{Glycerol}$$

Where:
- R = alkyl group of the alcohol (CH₃ for methanol)
- R' = long-chain fatty acid hydrocarbon chain (C₁₂ – C₂₂)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| Molar ratio of alcohol to oil | $n_{ROH} = 3 \times n_{oil}$ | mol/mol | Stoichiometric balance |
| % Excess methanol | $\%\text{excess} = \frac{n_{actual} - n_{stoich}}{n_{stoich}} \times 100$ | % | Yield optimization |
| Density of biodiesel | $\rho_{bio}$ | kg/m³ | ASTM D1298 |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$ | cSt (mm²/s) | ASTM D445 |
| Cetane Number (CN) | Empirical: ignition delay | dimensionless | ASTM D613 |
| Calorific Value (CV) | $CV = \frac{Q_{comb}}{m_{fuel}}$ | MJ/kg | ASTM D240 |
| Acid Value | $AV = \frac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{m_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g | Oil quality check |
| Saponification Value | $SV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{m_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g | Average mol. weight |
| Iodine Value | $IV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{thio} \times 12.69}{m_{sample}}$ | g I₂/100g | Degree of unsaturation |
| Biodiesel Yield | $\%\text{Yield} = \frac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTE for KTU students:** Always remember the standard **3:1 molar ratio** of alcohol to oil. Most board exam numerical problems hinge on this relationship.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diesel Engine Retrofit:** Biodiesel (B20) can be used in existing diesel engines without modification.
- **Cold Flow Improvement:** Required for northern climates.
- **Oxidative Stability:** Improved via antioxidants (BHT, BHA).
- **Glycerol By-product:** Used in pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and food industries — adds economic viability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Methanol Requirement

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the amount of methanol required to completely transesterify **1000 kg of soybean oil**, given:
- Average molecular weight of soybean oil triglyceride = **885.4 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of methanol (CH₃OH) = **32.04 g/mol**
- Plant uses **20% excess methanol** to drive equilibrium forward.

---

**Step 1: Calculate moles of triglyceride in 1000 kg of oil.**

$$m_{oil} = 1000 \, \text{kg} = 1{,}000{,}000 \, \text{g}$$

$$M_{oil} = 885.4 \, \text{g/mol}$$

$$n_{oil} = \frac{m_{oil}}{M_{oil}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{885.4} = 1129.66 \, \text{mol}$$

**Logic:** The total mass of oil is divided by its molar mass to get the number of moles, which is the foundation for stoichiometric calculation.

---

**Step 2: Apply the 3:1 stoichiometric ratio.**

From the balanced equation:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3 \, \text{Methanol} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

$$n_{methanol,stoich} = 3 \times n_{oil} = 3 \times 1129.66 = 3388.98 \, \text{mol}$$

**Logic:** Each triglyceride molecule requires 3 methanol molecules to release 3 FAME molecules.

---

**Step 3: Account for 20% excess methanol.**

$$n_{actual} = n_{stoich} \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$n_{actual} = 3388.98 \times 1.20 = 4066.78 \, \text{mol}$$

**Logic:** Excess methanol shifts Le Chatelier's equilibrium to the right, increasing FAME yield to > 95%.

---

**Step 4: Convert moles of methanol to mass.**

$$m_{methanol} = n_{actual} \times M_{methanol}$$

$$m_{methanol} = 4066.78 \times 32.04 = 130{,}300 \, \text{g} \approx \mathbf{130.3 \, kg}$$

**Logic:** Multiplying moles by molar mass gives the practical weight of methanol that must be charged to the reactor.

---

**Step 5: Calculate biodiesel yield (FAME produced).**

Assume conversion efficiency = **96%**.

$$m_{FAME} = m_{oil} \times 0.96 = 1000 \times 0.96 = 960 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\%\text{Yield} = \frac{960}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{96\%}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Cetane Number Significance

The **Cetane Number (CN)** is mathematically related to ignition delay (ID):

$$ID = \frac{CN_{ref,min} \times ID_{ref} \times 100}{CN_{actual}}$$

For petroleum diesel: $CN_{ref} = 45 - 55$, $ID_{ref} = 2.5$ ms.

**For Biodiesel (typical CN = 50–60):**

$$ID_{bio} = \frac{50 \times 2.5}{55} = 2.27 \, \text{ms}$$

**Logic:** Higher CN = Shorter ignition delay = Better combustion quality.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Characteristics of Biodiesel vs Diesel

| Property | Petroleum Diesel | Biodiesel (B100) | Jatropha Biodiesel | Coconut Biodiesel |
|----------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 830 | 880 | 875 | 870 |
| Viscosity at 40°C (cSt) | 2.5 | 4.5 | 4.8 | 3.5 |
| Cetane Number | 48 | 55 | 52 | 65 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 70 | 150 | 145 | 165 |
| Calorific Value (MJ/kg) | 43 | 37.2 | 38.5 | 36.5 |
| Sulfur Content (wt%) | 0.05 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 | < 0.001 |
| Oxygen Content (%) | 0 | 11 | 11 | 10 |
| Acid Value (mg KOH/g) | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher viscosity of biodiesel necessitates fuel preheating in colder climates. Lower calorific value results in ~7-10% higher fuel consumption for equivalent mileage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Biodiesel Production Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Vegetable Oil Feedstock] --> B[Pre-treatment Tank]
    B --> C[Degumming & Filtration]
    C --> D[Acid Esterification Stage]
    D --> E[Transesterification Reactor 60C]
    F[Methanol Storage Tank] --> G[Mixer Vessel]
    H[NaOH or KOH Catalyst] --> G
    G --> E
    E --> I[Gravity Settling Separator]
    I --> J[Upper Layer Biodiesel FAME]
    I --> K[Lower Layer Glycerol By-product]
    J --> L[Water Washing Column]
    L --> M[Drying Unit Vacuum]
    M --> N[Quality Testing Lab]
    N --> O[Pure Biodiesel B100 Storage]
    O --> P[Blending Unit B20 B50]
    P --> Q[Distribution to Engines]
    K --> R[Glycerol Purification Unit]
    R --> S[Pharmaceutical Grade Glycerol]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#d4f4dd
    style K fill:#ffd6d6
    style O fill:#d4f4dd
```

### 4.2 Process Architecture: Transesterification Reactor Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Oil Inlet 1000 kg/hr] --> R2[Reactor Vessel]
    M1[Methanol Inlet 130 kg/hr] --> R2
    C1[Catalyst NaOH 10 kg/hr] --> R2
    R2 --> R3[Heating Jacket 60C]
    R3 --> R4[Stirrer Motor]
    R4 --> R5[Sampling Port]
    R5 --> R6[Reaction Time 2 hours]
    R6 --> R7[Outlet to Separator]

    style R2 fill:#ffeb99
    style R6 fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 Process Flow Summary

The production of biodiesel follows this **7-stage architecture**:

1. **Feedstock Collection & Pre-treatment** — Degumming, water removal
2. **Acid Esterification** — Reduces FFA (Free Fatty Acid) content if oil is acidic
3. **Alkaline Transesterification** — Main reaction with NaOH/KOH catalyst
4. **Phase Separation** — Gravity separation of glycerol (bottom) and FAME (top)
5. **Water Washing** — Removes residual catalyst and soap
6. **Drying** — Vacuum drying to remove moisture (< 500 ppm)
7. **Quality Testing & Storage** — GC, viscosity, flash point, CN analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Always label the three layers after transesterification — (1) FAME layer on top, (2) Soap/aqueous layer in middle, (3) Glycerol layer at bottom.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
Define biodiesel. List any four advantages of biodiesel over petroleum diesel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Biodiesel is a renewable, biodegradable, mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or microalgal lipids. It is produced by transesterification of triglycerides with short-chain alcohols in the presence of a catalyst.
>
> **Advantages (1.5 marks — any four):**
> 1. **Renewable and biodegradable** — Carbon-neutral lifecycle.
> 2. **Lower emissions** — Reduces CO, unburnt HC, and particulate matter.
> 3. **Higher cetane number** (~55 vs 48) — Better combustion quality.
> 4. **Higher flash point** (>130°C) — Safer handling.
> 5. **Better lubricity** — Reduces engine wear.
> 6. **Ultra-low sulfur content** — Cleaner exhaust.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
What is transesterification? Write the chemical reaction for the production of biodiesel from triglyceride.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Transesterification (1.5 marks):** Transesterification is the chemical process in which an alcohol (typically methanol) reacts with a triglyceride in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH/KOH) to produce fatty acid methyl esters (FAME/biodiesel) and glycerol as a by-product.
>
> **Reaction (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{R'COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$
>
> Where: Triglyceride + Methanol → FAME + Glycerol

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> (a) Explain the manufacturing process of biodiesel by transesterification in detail with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)
> (b) Compare the characteristics of biodiesel and petroleum diesel. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Manufacturing Process of Biodiesel by Transesterification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-treatment (1 mark):**
The crude vegetable oil is first filtered and degummed to remove phospholipids, water, and impurities. The free fatty acid (FFA) content is checked. If FFA > 2%, an **acid esterification** pretreatment is performed with H₂SO₄ and methanol to convert FFAs into FAME.

**Step 2 — Catalyst Preparation (1 mark):**
Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) or potassium hydroxide (KOH) is dissolved in methanol to form **sodium methoxide (CH₃ONa)**. Typical catalyst loading: **0.5% – 1%** by weight of oil.

**Step 3 — Transesterification Reaction (2 marks):**
The triglyceride and methanol are fed into a stirred tank reactor maintained at **60°C – 65°C** for **1 – 4 hours** with continuous stirring at 300–600 rpm. The reaction is reversible, so excess methanol (typically 20%) is used to drive the equilibrium toward FAME production.

**Step 4 — Phase Separation (1 mark):**
The reaction mixture is transferred to a settling tank. Due to density difference, **glycerol (denser)** settles at the bottom and **FAME (lighter)** floats on top. The two layers are separated.

**Step 5 — Purification (1 mark):**
The crude FAME is washed with warm water to remove residual catalyst, soap, and glycerol, then vacuum-dried to remove moisture (< 500 ppm).

**Step 6 — Quality Testing (1 mark):**
The purified biodiesel is tested for density, viscosity, flash point, cetane number, acid value, and water content per ASTM standards before storage.

> **Block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1 above).**

**(b) Comparison: Biodiesel vs Petroleum Diesel (7 marks):**

| Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Petroleum Diesel |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 880 | 830 |
| **Viscosity at 40°C (cSt)** | 4.5 | 2.5 |
| **Cetane Number** | 55 | 48 |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | 150 | 70 |
| **Calorific Value (MJ/kg)** | 37.2 | 43 |
| **Oxygen Content (%)** | 11 | 0 |
| **Sulfur Content (wt%)** | < 0.001 | 0.05 |
| **Renewability** | Renewable | Non-renewable |
| **Biodegradability** | Highly (95% in 28 days) | Slow |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Biodiesel has inferior viscosity and calorific value but superior cetane number, flash point, and environmental compatibility.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative):** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> (a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the production of biodiesel from Jatropha oil. (7 marks)
> (b) Discuss the important fuel properties of biodiesel used in engine applications. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Production of Biodiesel from Jatropha Oil (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Jatropha Seed Collection & Oil Extraction (1.5 marks):**  
Jatropha seeds contain **30–40%** non-edible oil. Seeds are crushed in expellers; oil is filtered and stored.

**Step 2 — Acid Pretreatment (1.5 marks):**  
Since Jatropha oil has high FFA (3–15%), it is first treated with H₂SO₄ (1% by weight) and methanol at 60°C for 1 hour to convert FFAs to FAME.

**Step 3 — Alkaline Transesterification (2 marks):**  
Pre-treated oil + methanol + NaOH (1% wt) → Reactor at 60°C, 600 rpm, 2 hours. Molar ratio oil:methanol = 1:6 (20% excess).

**Step 4 — Separation & Purification (1.5 marks):**  
After 2 hours, settle in decanter; separate glycerol (bottom) and FAME (top). Wash FAME with hot water; dry under vacuum.

**Step 5 — Storage (0.5 marks):**  
Pure Jatropha biodiesel (JME) is stored in closed tanks with nitrogen blanketing to prevent oxidation.

**Flow Diagram:**
```
Jatropha Seeds → Oil Expeller → Degumming → Acid Esterification →
Alkaline Transesterification → Phase Separation → Water Washing → 
Drying → Jatropha Biodiesel (JME)
```

**(b) Important Fuel Properties of Biodiesel (7 marks):**

1. **Cetane Number (CN)** — Measure of ignition quality. Biodiesel has CN 50-65, higher than diesel. (1 mark)
2. **Kinematic Viscosity** — Affects atomization. Biodiesel ~4.5 cSt at 40°C, close to diesel. (1 mark)
3. **Flash Point** — Biodiesel > 130°C, making it safer for transport. (1 mark)
4. **Calorific Value** — ~37 MJ/kg, 7-10% lower than diesel → higher fuel consumption. (1 mark)
5. **Acid Value** — Should be < 0.5 mg KOH/g to prevent engine corrosion. (0.5 mark)
6. **Iodine Value** — Indicates unsaturation. High values → poor oxidative stability. (0.5 mark)
7. **Saponification Value** — Used to calculate average molecular weight of fatty acids. (0.5 mark)
8. **Cloud Point & Pour Point** — Indicate low-temperature operability. (0.5 mark)
9. **Oxidative Stability** — Critical for long-term storage. (0.5 mark)
10. **Water & Sediment Content** — Should be < 0.05% by volume per EN ISO 12937. (0.5 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong Stoichiometry:** Students often write **1:1** instead of **3:1** alcohol-to-oil ratio. This costs **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Transesterification with Esterification:** Transesterification involves a triglyceride + alcohol; esterification is acid + alcohol.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Catalyst:** NaOH/KOH is mandatory. Without it, the reaction is extremely slow.
> 
> 4. **Temperature Mistake:** Writing boiling point of methanol (64.7°C) instead of reaction temperature (60–65°C) shows conceptual weakness.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring Excess Methanol:** The 20% excess methanol is critical to push equilibrium; missing this loses **1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Diagram without Labels:** KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabelled flow diagrams. Always label **inputs, outputs, temperatures, and catalysts**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Biodiesel = FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester)** — produced via transesterification.
- ✅ **3:1 molar ratio** of alcohol (methanol) to triglyceride is the stoichiometric foundation.
- ✅ **Reaction Temperature: 60–65°C** (just below methanol boiling point).
- ✅ **Catalyst: NaOH or KOH** (alkaline catalysis preferred); **H₂SO₄** for high-FFA oils (acid esterification pretreatment).
- ✅ **Reaction Time: 1–4 hours** with continuous stirring.
- ✅ **By-product: Glycerol** (~10% by mass) — useful in pharmaceutical industry.
- ✅ **Density of Biodiesel: 860–900 kg/m³** (vs diesel 830 kg/m³).
- ✅ **Viscosity: 3.5–5 cSt** (vs diesel 2.5 cSt) — affects atomization.
- ✅ **Cetane Number: 45–65** (vs diesel 48) — biodiesel has better ignition.
- ✅ **Calorific Value: ~37 MJ/kg** (vs diesel 43 MJ/kg) — ~10% lower.
- ✅ **Flash Point: >130°C** (vs diesel 70°C) — safer fuel.
- ✅ **Oxygen Content: 10–12%** — aids complete combustion, lower emissions.
- ✅ **Sulfur: < 0.001%** — virtually sulfur-free, prevents acid rain.
- ✅ **Feedstock Generations:** 1st — edible oils; 2nd — Jatropha, Karanja; 3rd — microalgae.
- ✅ **ASTM D6751 / EN 14214** — International quality standards.
- ✅ **B20 blend** is most commonly used in unmodified engines.
- ✅ **Challenges:** NOx increase, cold flow issues, oxidative stability, food-vs-fuel debate.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Renewable, biodegradable (95% in 28 days), non-toxic, lubricity improver.
- ✅ **Equation to memorize:** $\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{R'COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Biodiesels: Manufacture and Characteristics**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Production Methods: Electrolysis, Thermochemical Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Production Methods: Electrolysis, Thermochemical Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Hydrogen Production Methods: Electrolysis & Thermochemical Processes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Hydrogen is the lightest, most abundant chemical element in the universe. As an **energy carrier** (not a primary energy source), hydrogen stores and delivers energy produced from other sources. When combusted or used in a fuel cell, it produces only **water** as the byproduct, making it a **zero-carbon-emission** fuel critical to the global hydrogen economy and decarbonization strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrogen is classified under Module 4 of Non-Conventional Energy Systems because it represents a **clean secondary energy vector** capable of bridging intermittent renewable sources (solar/wind) with industrial, transportation, and grid-scale energy demands.

**Fundamental Properties of Molecular Hydrogen (H₂):**

| Property | Value (Standard Conditions) |
|----------|------------------------------|
| Molecular Weight | **2.016 g/mol** |
| Density (Gas, STP) | **0.0899 kg/m³** |
| Boiling Point | **−252.87 °C (20.28 K)** |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **119.96 MJ/kg** |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **141.88 MJ/kg** |
| Energy Content by Mass | ~3× gasoline |
| Flame Velocity | 2.8 m/s |
| Flammability Range | 4–75 % (in air) |

---

### 1.2 Hydrogen Production Routes — Overview

Hydrogen can be produced via multiple pathways, each categorized by the **color code** convention adopted by the hydrogen industry:

| Color Code | Production Method | Carbon Footprint |
|------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Grey H₂** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | High CO₂ emissions |
| **Blue H₂** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Low CO₂ emissions |
| **Green H₂** | **Electrolysis powered by Renewables** | Near-zero CO₂ |
| **Pink H₂** | Electrolysis powered by Nuclear | Near-zero CO₂ |
| **Turquoise H₂** | Methane Pyrolysis | Solid Carbon byproduct |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 4, the focus is exclusively on **electrolysis** and **thermochemical cycles**, both of which qualify as **green/pink hydrogen** production technologies under the Indian National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023).

---

### 1.3 Electrolysis — Definition

**Formal Definition:**
Electrolysis is an **electrochemical process** that decomposes water molecules into **hydrogen gas (H₂)** at the cathode and **oxygen gas (O₂)** at the anode by passing a **direct electric current (DC)** through water containing a suitable electrolyte.

**Overall Reaction (Universal for All Electrolyzer Types):**

$$H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$$

---

### 1.4 Thermochemical Processes — Definition

**Formal Definition:**
Thermochemical hydrogen production involves the **multi-step thermal decomposition of water** through a closed-loop series of chemical reactions. Heat (typically from a **concentrated solar power (CSP)** plant or a **high-temperature nuclear reactor**) drives intermediate reactions at temperatures ranging from **500 °C to 2500 °C**, ultimately regenerating the reactant chemicals and producing pure hydrogen and oxygen.

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Instantly Clear

> **🪔 The Lantern Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a sealed lantern filled with water (the lantern = the electrolyzer/thermochemical reactor). Inside the lantern, you strike a match (electrical energy OR high-temperature heat). The water inside splits into two invisible gases — one rises to the top (Hydrogen, the lightest gas in the universe) and one stays at the bottom (Oxygen). You collect each gas through separate pipes. When you later burn the hydrogen in a fuel cell, it recombines with oxygen to re-form water, releasing the energy you put in — like a rechargeable battery, but using gases instead of metals.

---

### 1.6 Historical Context & KTU Relevance

- **First Electrolyzer:** Built in **1800** by William Nicholson and Anthony Carlisle.
- **First Thermochemical Cycle:** The **Iron-Chlorine cycle** proposed by Funk in 1966.
- **KTU Relevance:** Aligns with India's **National Hydrogen Mission (2023)** targeting **5 million tonnes of green hydrogen production by 2030**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrolysis — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 The Electrochemical Principle

An electrolyzer cell consists of three primary components:

1. **Anode (Positive Electrode):** Where **oxidation** occurs — water is oxidized to produce O₂.
2. **Cathode (Negative Electrode):** Where **reduction** occurs — water/H⁺ is reduced to produce H₂.
3. **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor that completes the circuit without electron transport.

**Half-Reactions (in Acidic Medium — Applicable to PEM Electrolyzers):**

$$2H^+_{(aq)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} \quad \text{(Cathodic Reduction, } E^0 = 0.00\text{ V)}$$

$$H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} + 2H^+_{(aq)} + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anodic Oxidation, } E^0 = -1.229\text{ V)}$$

**Half-Reactions (in Alkaline Medium — Applicable to Alkaline Electrolyzers):**

$$2H_2O_{(l)} + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} + 2OH^-_{(aq)} \quad \text{(Cathodic Reduction)}$$

$$2OH^-_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} + H_2O_{(l)} + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anodic Oxidation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** The choice of electrolyte determines the operating temperature, current density, and purity of hydrogen produced — a critical design parameter in industrial electrolyzer selection.

---

### 2.1.2 Thermodynamic Voltage Requirements

**Three Critical Voltages Define Electrolyzer Operation:**

| Voltage Type | Symbol | Value | Physical Meaning |
|--------------|--------|-------|------------------|
| **Reversible (Thermodynamic Minimum)** | $V_{rev}$ | **1.229 V** | Minimum voltage at 25 °C, 1 atm based on Gibbs free energy |
| **Thermoneutral Voltage** | $V_{tn}$ | **1.481 V** | Voltage at which no net heat is exchanged with surroundings (based on enthalpy) |
| **Actual Operating Voltage** | $V_{cell}$ | 1.8 – 2.5 V | Real voltage including all irreversibilities (overpotentials + ohmic losses) |

**The Thermodynamic Origin:**

The reversible cell voltage is derived from **Gibbs Free Energy** at standard conditions:

$$V_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$$

where:
- $\Delta G^0 = 237.2$ kJ/mol (Gibbs free energy of water formation)
- $n = 2$ (electrons transferred per mole of H₂)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant)

The thermoneutral voltage is derived from **Enthalpy**:

$$V_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF}$$

where:
- $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol (Higher Heating Value of H₂)

---

### 2.1.3 Types of Electrolyzers — Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks comparative questions on electrolyzer technologies. Master this table thoroughly.

| Feature | **Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL)** | **PEM Electrolyzer (PEMEL)** | **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEL)** |
|---------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Electrolyte** | Liquid KOH (25–30 wt%) | Proton Exchange Membrane (Nafion™) | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) ceramic |
| **Operating Temperature** | 60–90 °C | 50–80 °C | **700–850 °C** |
| **Operating Pressure** | 1–30 bar | Up to 70 bar | 1–15 bar |
| **Current Density** | 0.2–0.6 A/cm² | 0.6–2.0 A/cm² | 0.3–1.0 A/cm² |
| **Cell Voltage** | 1.8–2.4 V | 1.8–2.2 V | 1.0–1.5 V |
| **H₂ Purity** | 99.5–99.9% | **99.99%** | 99.9% |
| **Efficiency (HHV)** | 63–70% | 65–75% | **75–85%** |
| **Capital Cost** | **Lowest** | High | Highest |
| **Dynamic Response** | Slow | **Fast (seconds)** | Very Slow (thermal inertia) |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | **9 (Commercial)** | 7–8 | 5–6 (Pilot) |
| **Best Coupling** | Steady grid/solar | Intermittent renewables | Industrial waste heat + nuclear |

---

### 2.1.4 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

**Faraday's First Law (1833):**
The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is **directly proportional** to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated (grams)
- $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C)
- $Q$ = electric charge (Coulombs)
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

**Faraday's Second Law:**
When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights**.

$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}$$

---

### 2.1.5 Electrolyzer Efficiency Calculations

Three efficiency definitions are critical for KTU problems:

**1. Faraday Efficiency (Current Efficiency) — $\eta_F$:**

$$\eta_F = \frac{\text{Actual H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Theoretical H}_2 \text{ from Faraday's Law}} \times 100\%$$

**2. Voltage Efficiency — $\eta_V$:**

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{rev}}{V_{cell}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.229}{V_{cell}} \times 100\%$$

**3. Overall Energy Efficiency — $\eta_{overall}$:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \times \eta_V = \frac{V_{rev}}{V_{cell}} \times \frac{\text{Actual H}_2}{\text{Theoretical H}_2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** PEM electrolyzers can reach voltage efficiencies of ~70% at full load, while SOECs achieve higher (>80%) due to favorable thermodynamics at high temperature — the **Nernst equation** dictates that $V_{rev}$ decreases at elevated temperatures.

---

## 2.2 Thermochemical Hydrogen Production — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Why Thermochemical Cycles?

Direct thermal decomposition of water requires temperatures above **2500 °C**:

$$H_2O \xrightarrow{>2500°C} H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

This is **impractical** due to material limitations. Thermochemical cycles achieve the same end result at **500–1000 °C** by introducing intermediate reactions whose net sum equals water splitting.

**General Form of an n-step Thermochemical Cycle:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta H_i = \Delta H_{H_2O} = 285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

---

### 2.2.2 Major Thermochemical Cycles — Comparative Analysis

| Cycle Name | Steps | Max Temperature | Efficiency | Status |
|------------|-------|-----------------|------------|--------|
| **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I)** | 3 | 900 °C | 50–60% | Mature pilot |
| **Hybrid Sulfur (HyS / Westinghouse)** | 2 (1 electrolysis) | 900 °C | 43–52% | Pilot |
| **Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl)** | 4–5 | 550 °C | 40–50% | Pilot |
| **Iron-Chlorine (Fe-Cl)** | 3 | 800 °C | 35–45% | Lab |
| **Zinc-Zinc Oxide (Zn-ZnO)** | 2 | 2000 °C | 45–55% | Research |
| **Cerium Oxide (CeO₂)** | 2 | 2000 °C | ~40% | Lab |

---

### 2.2.3 Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle — Step-by-Step Detail

The S-I cycle, developed by **General Atomics (1970s)**, is the most studied thermochemical cycle globally. It involves three reactions:

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, 20–120 °C):**

$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$$

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 800–900 °C):**

$$H_2SO_4 \xrightarrow{900°C} H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Iodide Decomposition (Endothermic, 300–500 °C):**

$$2HI \xrightarrow{450°C} H_2 + I_2$$

**Net Reaction:**

$$H_2O \longrightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** Notice that I₂ and SO₂ are **regenerated** in Steps 2 and 3 — they are recycled. This is a **closed-loop** cycle where only H₂O is consumed and H₂ + O₂ are produced.

---

### 2.2.4 Hybrid Sulfur (HyS) Cycle — The Electrolysis Step

The HyS cycle (also called the **Westinghouse cycle**) replaces the entire HI decomposition step with a **single electrochemical step**:

**Step 1 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (800–900 °C):**

$$H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

**Step 2 — SO₂ Electrolysis (80–120 °C):**

$$SO_2 + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{Electrolysis} H_2SO_4 + H_2$$

**Net Reaction:**

$$H_2O \longrightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The HyS cycle operates at **lower voltage (~0.6 V)** compared to direct water electrolysis (1.23 V) because the SO₂ oxidation reaction is thermodynamically easier. This translates to **lower electricity consumption** by up to **40%**.

---

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Reversible cell voltage | Volts |
| 2 | $V_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^0}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage | Volts |
| 3 | $\Delta G^0 = 237.2$ kJ/mol | Std. Gibbs energy of H₂O formation | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol | Std. Enthalpy of H₂O formation | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $n = 2$ | Electrons transferred per mol H₂ | — |
| 6 | $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Faraday's constant | C/mol |
| 7 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law — mass liberated | grams |
| 8 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Faraday efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\eta_V = \dfrac{V_{rev}}{V_{cell}} \times 100$ | Voltage efficiency | % |

| 11 | $W_{elec} = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t$ | Electrical energy consumed | Joules |

| 13 | $\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot \eta_F}{n \cdot F} \cdot \dfrac{RT}{P}$ | Volumetric H₂ production rate | m³/s |
| 14 | $V_T = \dfrac{V_{rev}(T)}{V_{rev}(298)} = 1 - \dfrac{(T-298)\cdot \Delta S}{nF}$ | Temperature correction of $V_{rev}$ | Volts |
| 15 | $\eta_{cycle} = \dfrac{HHV_{H_2}}{Q_{in} + W_{in}}$ | Thermochemical cycle efficiency | % |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reversible Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

### Objective
Derive the **minimum reversible voltage** ($V_{rev}$) required to split one mole of liquid water into hydrogen and oxygen at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm).

**Starting Thermodynamic Identity:**

The change in Gibbs free energy during an electrochemical reaction relates to the cell potential through:

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}$$

where:
- $\Delta G$ = change in Gibbs free energy (J/mol)
- $n$ = number of moles of electrons transferred in the balanced reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol
- $E_{cell}$ = cell potential (Volts)

**Step 1 — Write the Half-Reactions**

At the cathode (reduction):
$$2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2 \quad \text{with } E^0_{cathode} = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

At the anode (oxidation):
$$H_2O \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad \text{with } E^0_{anode} = -1.229 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Combine to Get the Overall Reaction**

$$H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$$

Each mole of H₂ produced involves the transfer of **2 moles of electrons**, so:

$$n = 2$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Standard Gibbs Free Energy**

From standard thermodynamic tables for the water-splitting reaction at 25 °C, 1 atm:

$$\Delta G^0 = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} = +237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$$

The positive sign confirms water splitting is **non-spontaneous** — energy must be supplied.

**Step 4 — Solve for the Reversible Voltage**

Rearranging the fundamental relation:

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{n \cdot F}$$

Substituting values (note the negative sign cancels the positive $\Delta G^0$):

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{(+237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rev} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation**

This is the **thermodynamic floor**: no electrolyzer, regardless of design, can split water with less than **1.229 V** at 25 °C. Real cells operate at 1.8–2.5 V due to irreversibilities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermoneutral Voltage from Enthalpy

### Objective
Derive the voltage at which the electrolyzer operates **without net heat exchange** with its surroundings (i.e., all the required energy comes from electricity alone).

**Starting Identity:**

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$$

For the water-splitting reaction at standard conditions:
- $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol (Higher Heating Value)
- $\Delta G^0 = 237.2$ kJ/mol
- $T = 298.15$ K
- $T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G = 285.8 - 237.2 = 48.6$ kJ/mol

**Step 1 — Apply the Voltage-Energy Relation**

Since enthalpy also drives the reaction, we can directly relate it to a voltage:

$$V_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$V_{tn} = \frac{285{,}800 \text{ J/mol}}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$

$$V_{tn} = \frac{285{,}800}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{V_{tn} = 1.481 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

If the cell operates at $V_{cell} < 1.481$ V, heat must flow **into** the cell from the surroundings to sustain the reaction. If $V_{cell} > 1.481$ V, heat is **released** by the cell.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass of Hydrogen Produced by Faraday's Law

### Problem Statement
An alkaline electrolyzer operates at a current of **1000 A** for **8 hours**. Calculate:
1. The mass of hydrogen produced (assuming Faraday efficiency = 95%)
2. The volume of hydrogen at STP
3. The electrical energy consumed at $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V

### Step 1 — Given Data
- $I = 1000$ A
- $t = 8 \text{ hours} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800$ s
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- $n = 2$ electrons per mole H₂
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $\eta_F = 0.95$
- $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V

### Step 2 — Theoretical Mass via Faraday's First Law

The total charge transferred:

$$Q = I \times t = 1000 \text{ A} \times 28{,}800 \text{ s} = 2.88 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$

The theoretical moles of H₂:

$$n_{H_2, theo} = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^7}{192{,}970}$$

$$n_{H_2, theo} = 149.25 \text{ mol}$$

The theoretical mass of H₂:

$$m_{theo} = n_{H_2, theo} \times M_{H_2} = 149.25 \times 2.016 = 300.9 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3 — Actual Mass (with Faraday Efficiency)

$$m_{actual} = m_{theo} \times \eta_F = 300.9 \times 0.95 = \boxed{285.85 \text{ g} \approx 0.286 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 4 — Volume at STP

At STP, 1 mole of ideal gas occupies **22.414 L**:

$$V_{H_2} = n_{H_2, actual} \times 22.414 \text{ L/mol} = (149.25 \times 0.95) \times 22.414$$

$$V_{H_2} = 141.79 \times 22.414 = \boxed{3178 \text{ L} = 3.178 \text{ m}^3}$$

### Step 5 — Electrical Energy Consumed

$$W = V_{cell} \times I \times t = 2.0 \times 1000 \times 28{,}800 = \boxed{5.76 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 57.6 \text{ MJ}}$$

### Step 6 — Specific Energy Consumption

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{W}{m_{actual}} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^7 \text{ J}}{0.286 \text{ kg}} = \boxed{2.014 \times 10^8 \text{ J/kg} = 55.94 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Overall Electrolyzer Efficiency

### Using the values from Derivation 3:

**Step 1 — Voltage Efficiency**

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{rev}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{1.229}{2.0} = 0.6145 = 61.45\%$$

**Step 2 — Faraday Efficiency**

$$\eta_F = 95\% = 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \times \eta_V = 0.95 \times 0.6145 = \boxed{58.38\%}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Content of Produced Hydrogen (HHV basis)**

$$E_{H_2} = 0.286 \text{ kg} \times 141.88 \text{ MJ/kg} = 40.58 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Energy In vs Energy Out**

$$\eta_{energy} = \frac{E_{H_2}}{W_{input}} = \frac{40.58}{57.6} = 70.45\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the Difference:** $\eta_{overall} = 58.38\%$ uses $V_{rev}$ (Gibbs basis), while $\eta_{energy} = 70.45\%$ uses HHV (enthalpy basis). Both are valid representations — KTU questions must specify which one is being asked.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Thermochemical Cycle Efficiency (S-I Cycle)

### Problem Statement
A Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) thermochemical cycle receives $Q_{in} = 600$ kJ of high-temperature heat at 900 °C and uses $W_{in} = 50$ kJ of work (for pumping, separation) per mole of H₂ produced. Calculate the cycle efficiency.

### Step 1 — Energy Input

$$Q_{in} + W_{in} = 600 + 50 = 650 \text{ kJ/mol H}_2$$

### Step 2 — Energy Output (HHV of H₂)

$$E_{H_2} = 285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Step 3 — Cycle Efficiency

$$\eta_{cycle} = \frac{E_{H_2}}{Q_{in} + W_{in}} = \frac{285.8}{650} = \boxed{43.97\%}$$

### Step 4 — Comparison

If $Q_{in}$ came from a CSP plant with 40% solar-to-thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{solar-to-H_2} = \eta_{solar} \times \eta_{cycle} = 0.40 \times 0.4397 = \boxed{17.6\%}$$

---

| **Primary Energy Input** | Electricity | High-temperature heat |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 — Definition Type `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term **"thermochemical cycle"** in the context of hydrogen production. Why are multi-step cycles preferred over direct thermal decomposition of water?

**Model Answer:**

A thermochemical cycle is a **closed-loop series of chemical reactions** that uses high-temperature heat (typically 500–1000 °C) to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Intermediate chemicals (such as I₂, SO₂, H₂SO₄) are continuously regenerated.

**Why preferred over direct splitting:**
- Direct thermal splitting requires temperatures **above 2500 °C**, exceeding the melting point of all known engineering materials and causing severe thermal losses.
- Thermochemical cycles reduce the peak temperature to **500–1000 °C**, achievable using concentrated solar power or high-temperature nuclear reactors.
- They enable **higher overall efficiency** by harvesting both high-grade and low-grade heat (process integration).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that thermochemical cycles are **closed-loop** — the intermediate reactants must be regenerated, otherwise it is not a true cycle.

---

### Question 2 — Concept Type `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **Faraday efficiency** and **voltage efficiency** of an electrolyzer. Which one is more sensitive to current density?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Faraday Efficiency ($\eta_F$) | Voltage Efficiency ($\eta_V$) |
|--------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Ratio of actual H₂ produced to theoretical H₂ | Ratio of reversible voltage to actual cell voltage |
| **Formula** | $\eta_F = m_{actual}/m_{theoretical}$ | $\eta_V = V_{rev}/V_{cell}$ |
| **Cause of Loss** | Parasitic losses — gas crossover, side reactions | Overpotentials — activation, concentration, ohmic |
| **Sensitivity to Current Density** | **Less sensitive** (mostly > 95%) | **Highly sensitive** — increases exponentially |

**More sensitive:** **Voltage efficiency** is more sensitive to current density because overpotentials (especially activation overpotential) grow rapidly with increasing current density per the **Butler-Volmer equation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q (a)** Derive the expression for the **reversible cell voltage** of a water electrolyzer from Gibbs free energy. State all assumptions. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** A PEM electrolyzer operates at 250 A current and 2.1 V cell voltage for 10 hours. Calculate:
   1. Mass of H₂ produced (assume Faraday efficiency = 98%)
   2. Overall efficiency of the electrolyzer (HHV basis)
   3. Specific energy consumption in kWh/kg H₂ (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Reversible Cell Voltage

**Given:**
- Reaction: $H_2O_{(l)} \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$
- $\Delta G^0 = 237.2$ kJ/mol (at 25 °C, 1 atm)
- $n = 2$ (electrons per mole H₂)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Step 1 — Identify the fundamental relation between free energy and cell voltage:**

$$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for cell voltage:**

$$E_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to standard conditions:**

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$V_{rev} = -\frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rev} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Standard temperature (25 °C) and pressure (1 atm)
2. Reversible (no overpotentials, no current flow)
3. Pure liquid water as reactant
4. Ideal gas behavior for H₂ and O₂
5. No concentration polarization

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Don't forget the **negative sign** — without it, the equation yields a negative voltage, which is unphysical for electrolysis. The sign convention: for a **spontaneous** reaction (galvanic cell), $\Delta G < 0$ gives $E > 0$. For **non-spontaneous** electrolysis, we apply an external voltage > 1.229 V.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution

**Given:**
- $I = 250$ A
- $V_{cell} = 2.1$ V
- $t = 10 \text{ hours} = 36{,}000$ s
- $\eta_F = 0.98$
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- $HHV_{H_2} = 141.88$ MJ/kg

**Sub-Part 1 — Mass of H₂ Produced**

Total charge:
$$Q = I \times t = 250 \times 36{,}000 = 9.0 \times 10^6 \text{ C}$$

Theoretical moles of H₂:
$$n_{theo} = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^6}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 46.64 \text{ mol}$$

Theoretical mass:
$$m_{theo} = 46.64 \times 2.016 = 94.03 \text{ g}$$

Actual mass:
$$m_{actual} = 94.03 \times 0.98 = \boxed{92.15 \text{ g} = 0.09215 \text{ kg}}$$

**Sub-Part 2 — Overall Efficiency (HHV basis)**

Energy input:
$$W_{in} = V_{cell} \times I \times t = 2.1 \times 250 \times 36{,}000 = 1.89 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

Energy output (HHV of H₂ produced):
$$E_{out} = m_{actual} \times HHV = 0.09215 \text{ kg} \times 141.88 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$$
$$E_{out} = 1.308 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

Overall efficiency:
$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{E_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{1.308 \times 10^7}{1.89 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 69.2\%}$$

**Sub-Part 3 — Specific Energy Consumption**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{W_{in}}{m_{actual}} = \frac{1.89 \times 10^7 \text{ J}}{0.09215 \text{ kg}}$$

$$\text{SEC} = 2.051 \times 10^8 \text{ J/kg}$$

Convert to kWh/kg (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):

$$\boxed{\text{SEC} = 56.97 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) thermochemical cycle** with all three reaction steps. State the maximum temperature required and identify the heat source. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** Compare the **Alkaline, PEM, and Solid Oxide** electrolyzers based on electrolyte, operating temperature, efficiency, and dynamic response. Justify which type is best suited for coupling with **intermittent solar PV**. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Sulfur-Iodine Cycle

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (20–120 °C):**

$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow 2HI_{(aq)} + H_2SO_{4(aq)}$$

Two immiscible phases are formed — an acid phase (H₂SO₄) and an iodide phase (HI).

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (800–900 °C):**

$$H_2SO_4 \xrightarrow{900°C} H_2O + SO_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

This endothermic step supplies the O₂ byproduct and regenerates SO₂.

**Step 3 — HI Decomposition (300–500 °C):**

$$2HI \xrightarrow{450°C} H_2 + I_2$$

This step yields pure H₂ and regenerates I₂ for the Bunsen reaction.

**Net Reaction:**

$$H_2O \longrightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

**Maximum Temperature:** **900 °C**

**Heat Source Options:**
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** — solar tower with heliostat field
- **High-Temperature Gas-cooled Reactor (HTGR)** — Generation IV nuclear

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Alkaline (AEL) | PEM (PEMEL) | Solid Oxide (SOEL) |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| Electrolyte | KOH liquid (25–30%) | Nafion membrane | YSZ ceramic |
| Temperature | 60–90 °C | 50–80 °C | 700–850 °C |
| Efficiency (HHV) | 63–70% | 65–75% | 75–85% |
| Dynamic Response | Slow (minutes) | **Fast (seconds)** | Very Slow (hours) |

**Best for Solar PV Coupling: PEM Electrolyzer**

**Justification:**
1. **Fast dynamic response** — matches the rapid fluctuations in solar PV output (clouds, sunrise/sunset).
2. **Cold start capability** — no thermal warm-up required.
3. **High current density** — smaller footprint for distributed PV installations.
4. **High H₂ purity** — eliminates need for downstream purification in many cases.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often choose SOEL due to its highest efficiency. However, SOEL's **slow thermal response** makes it unsuitable for **intermittent** renewables. It is best coupled with **baseload** nuclear or steady industrial heat.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Areas in KTU Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Missing Units:** Always write kJ/mol, MJ/kg, kWh/kg explicitly. Marks are deducted for dimensionless answers.
> 2. **Wrong Sign in $V_{rev}$:** The negative sign in $V_{rev} = -\Delta G / nF$ is critical. Forgetting it gives a physically meaningless negative voltage.
> 3. **Confusing HHV vs LHV:** Indian exams and Indian engineering standards typically use **HHV (141.88 MJ/kg)** unless specified otherwise. Don't interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting Faraday Efficiency:** If the problem gives $\eta_F \neq 100\%$, students often forget to multiply and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **S-I Cycle Steps Order:** The order is **Bunsen → H₂SO₄ decomposition → HI decomposition**. Reversing them loses marks.
> 6. **Volume Calculation:** STP volume = **22.414 L/mol** (older IUPAC) or **22.711 L/mol** (newer). Always state which standard you use.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section for last-minute exam revision.**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Constants
- $\Delta G^0_{H_2O} = 237.2$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta H^0_{H_2O} = 285.8$ kJ/mol
- $V_{rev} = 1.229$ V
- $V_{tn} = 1.481$ V
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $HHV_{H_2} = 141.88$ MJ/kg
- $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg

### 🔑 Must-Know Half-Reactions
- **Cathode (acidic):** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$
- **Anode (acidic):** $H_2O \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- **Cathode (alkaline):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$
- **Anode (alkaline):** $2OH^- \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^-$

### 🔑 Must-Know Cycle Steps (S-I)
1. Bunsen Reaction at 20–120 °C
2. H₂SO₄ Decomposition at 900 °C
3. HI Decomposition at 450 °C

### 🔑 Must-Know Electrolyzer Differences
- **Alkaline:** Cheapest, mature, slow response → grid baseload
- **PEM:** Fastest response, expensive → intermittent renewables
- **SOEC:** Highest efficiency, high temperature → industrial heat/nuclear

### 🔑 Must-Know Efficiencies
- $\eta_V = V_{rev} / V_{cell} = 1.229 / V_{cell}$
- $\eta_F = m_{actual} / m_{theoretical}$
- $\eta_{overall} = \eta_F \times \eta_V$ (Gibbs basis)
- $\eta_{HHV} = E_{H_2,HHV} / W_{input}$ (enthalpy basis)

### 🔑 Must-Know Faraday's Law
$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

### 🔑 Quick Numerical Shortcuts
- 1 kg H₂ contains **33.33 kWh** (LHV) or **39.4 kWh** (HHV) of energy
- 1 Nm³ H₂ = **3.54 kWh** (LHV basis)
- Electrolyzers typically consume **50–55 kWh/kg H₂**

### 🔑 Real-World Indian Context (For Bonus Marks)
- **National Green Hydrogen Mission** (launched Jan 2023)
- Target: **5 MMT/year** green H₂ production by 2030
- **SIGHT** (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) — financial incentives
- Pilot projects by **NTPC, IOCL, Adani, Reliance**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Transportation: Compressed Gas, Liquid Hydrogen, Metal Hydrides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage and Transportation: Compressed Gas, Liquid Hydrogen, Metal Hydrides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen Storage and Transportation — The Foundational Problem

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Hydrogen storage and transportation refers to the engineered methodologies and physical systems employed to safely contain hydrogen gas in a usable, retrievable, and economically viable form, while delivering it from the point of production (electrolysis, steam methane reforming, biomass gasification) to the point of end-use (fuel cells, internal combustion engines, industrial processes). Because molecular hydrogen (H₂) possesses an exceptionally low ambient-condition density of approximately **0.0899 kg/m³** and a low molecular weight of **2.016 g/mol**, it cannot be practically used in its atmospheric state and must be densified through one of three primary routes: **compression, liquefaction, or chemical/physical bonding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Focus):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes three principal storage vectors — *Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)*, *Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)*, and *Metal Hydride Storage* — along with the engineering physics, thermodynamics, and material science governing each route. The Department of Energy (DOE) targets for on-board vehicular storage are **gravimetric ≥ 6.5 wt%** and **volumetric ≥ 70 g H₂/L** by 2030.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Suitcases"

Imagine you need to transport **1 kg of feathers** from one city to another. You have three suitcases:

| Suitcase | Method | Real-World Analogy |
|----------|--------|--------------------|
| **Suitcase A: Vacuum Compression** | Compressed Gas (CGH₂) | You crush the feathers under enormous pressure inside a strong steel cylinder — the volume shrinks, but the suitcase walls must be incredibly thick. |
| **Suitcase B: Deep Freeze** | Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) | You cool the feathers to **−252.87 °C** until they liquefy — the suitcase becomes much smaller, but you need a perfect thermos that constantly fights heat leak. |
| **Suitcase C: Chemical Sponge** | Metal Hydrides | You soak the feathers into a chemical sponge (a metal alloy) that *chemically traps* the hydrogen inside its crystalline lattice — the sponge looks solid, but it is full of stored fuel. |

Each "suitcase" represents a fundamentally different physics principle: **mechanical compression, cryogenic phase change, and reversible chemisorption** respectively.

---

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen Storage is the Hardest Engineering Problem in the Hydrogen Economy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hydrogen Storage Trilemma**
> A practical storage system must simultaneously satisfy three often-conflicting requirements:
> 1. **High gravimetric density** (kg of H₂ per kg of system) — important for aerospace and mobile applications.
> 2. **High volumetric density** (kg of H₂ per m³ of system) — important for urban vehicles and confined spaces.
> 3. **Low energy input for release** — release temperature, pressure, and kinetics must be practical.

No single storage technology currently dominates all three metrics — this is the central research challenge addressed in KTU Module 4.

---

## 1.4 Physical and Thermodynamic Constants (Standard Reference)

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Hydrogen molecular mass | M(H₂) | **2.016 × 10⁻³** | kg/mol |
| Density of H₂ at STP | ρ_STP | **0.0899** | kg/m³ |
| Critical temperature of H₂ | T_c | **33.19** | K |
| Critical pressure of H₂ | P_c | **1.297** | MPa |
| Boiling point of H₂ at 1 atm | T_b | **20.271** | K (−252.879 °C) |
| Lower heating value | LHV | **119.96** | MJ/kg |
| Higher heating value | HHV | **141.88** | MJ/kg |
| Energy required to liquefy H₂ | W_liq | **≈ 10–13** | kWh/kg |
| Self-ignition temperature | T_ign | **585** | °C |
| Flammability range in air | Φ | **4 – 75** | vol% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Embrittlement Warning:** Hydrogen atoms readily diffuse into the crystal lattice of ferrous and titanium alloys, causing **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)** — a catastrophic loss of ductility and tensile strength. All storage vessels must use **H₂-compatible alloys** (e.g., 316L stainless steel, aluminum 6061-T6 with polymer liners).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Operating Principle
Hydrogen is mechanically compressed using multi-stage reciprocating or diaphragm compressors and stored in **Type I → Type IV** high-pressure vessels. The governing equation is the **Ideal Gas Law**, with real-gas corrections via the **compressibility factor Z** at high pressure.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Feed compression:** Hydrogen enters a 1st-stage compressor at ~**20–30 bar** and is compressed to **200–450 bar** in a 2nd or 3rd stage.
2. **Intercooling:** Between stages, the gas is cooled to avoid adiabatic temperature spikes (T_ad ≈ **900 K** if uncooled).
3. **Storage:** Gas is delivered to a Type IV tank (carbon-fiber wrapped polymer liner) at **350 bar (5,076 psi)** or **700 bar (10,153 psi)**.
4. **Dispensing:** A pressure regulator reduces the tank pressure to the fuel-cell or engine inlet requirement (~**2–10 bar**).

### 2.1.3 Compressed Hydrogen Tank Classification (ISO/TS 15869)

| Tank Type | Liner Material | Reinforcement | Max Pressure | Application |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| **Type I** | Steel (or aluminum) | None (all-metal) | 175–200 bar | Stationary industrial storage |
| **Type II** | Steel | Hoop-wrapped composite | 260–300 bar | Limited mobile, trailers |
| **Type III** | Aluminum (or steel) | Fully wrapped composite | 350–450 bar | Light-duty FCEV prototypes |
| **Type IV** | Polymer (HDPE) | Fully wrapped carbon fiber | **700 bar (standard for FCEVs)** | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A **Type IV 700-bar tank** stores approximately **5–6 kg H₂**, giving a volumetric density of **~ 40 g H₂/L** and gravimetric density of **~ 5.7 wt%** (system-level).

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle
Hydrogen is liquefied through the **Hampson-Linde cycle** (Joule-Thomson expansion with regenerative cooling) at **20.271 K** and stored in **super-insulated cryogenic dewars**. Since LH₂ has a density of **70.8 kg/m³** (≈ 800× STP gas density), it offers the highest *volumetric* storage density of the three methods.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Pre-cooling:** H₂ gas is first cooled to ~**80 K** using liquid nitrogen (LN₂) heat exchangers.
2. **Ortho-to-Para conversion:** Normal hydrogen at room temperature is **75% ortho** and **25% para**. Without conversion, the spontaneous ortho → para reaction releases **527 kJ/kg** of heat — a catastrophic heat source in a cryogenic tank. Catalytic converters (e.g., Fe(OH)₃) drive this conversion pre-liquefaction.
3. **Joule-Thomson expansion:** Gas is expanded through throttling valves; temperature drops due to the **Joule-Thomson coefficient** (μ_JT ≈ **−0.03 K/bar** for H₂ at room temp).
4. **Liquefaction:** Final cooling below **33.19 K** (critical T) condenses H₂ to liquid.
5. **Storage in dewar:** Double-walled vacuum super-insulated vessel with multi-layer insulation (MLI) — typically **30–60 layers of aluminized Mylar**.

### 2.2.3 Boil-Off Loss (BOL) Phenomenon
Even with perfect insulation, ambient heat leak causes continuous evaporation:

$$\dot{m}_{BOL} = \frac{Q_{leak}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{(UA)_{eff} \cdot (T_{amb} - T_{LH_2})}{h_{fg}}$$

- $h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization) ≈ **445.6 kJ/kg**
- Modern road-tankers achieve BOL of **0.1–0.3 %/day**
- For a 5,000 kg LH₂ tanker on a 5-day journey, ~ **7.5 kg** is lost (a non-trivial economic penalty).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently state the boiling point as "−253 °C" — the exact value is **20.271 K = −252.879 °C**. KTU examiners may deduct 0.5 marks for unit/rounding errors.

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Metal Hydride Storage

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Operating Principle
Hydrogen atoms are absorbed into the interstitial sites of a metal or intermetallic alloy lattice, forming a reversible chemical compound:

$$M_{(s)} + \frac{x}{2} H_{2(g)} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{desorption}]{\text{absorption}} MH_{x(s)} + \Delta H$$

The reaction is **exothermic** during absorption and **endothermic** during desorption. Equilibrium is governed by **Pressure-Composition Isotherms (PCI curves)** and the **van't Hoff equation**.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Activation:** Fresh alloy surfaces are passivated by an oxide layer. First-time heating to **300–400 °C** under H₂ pressure is required to crack this layer — called *"activation"*.
2. **Absorption (Hydriding):** Cool the activated alloy to operating T; introduce H₂ at 1–30 bar. The reaction is **exothermic** (heat must be removed).
3. **Storage (Plateau Region):** The Pressure-Composition Isotherm exhibits a flat **plateau** at a constant equilibrium pressure P_eq — this is the usable storage region.
4. **Desorption (Dehydriding):** Apply heat (or reduce P). The reaction is **endothermic**. Heat is typically supplied via engine coolant or a dedicated heat exchanger.

### 2.3.3 Classical Examples of Metal Hydrides

| Alloy / Hydride | Formula | wt% H₂ | T (1 bar P_eq) | Application |
|-----------------|---------|--------|----------------|-------------|
| Lanthanum Nickel | LaNi₅H₆ | **1.4** | 25 °C | Ni-MH batteries, small stationary |
| Iron Titanium | FeTiH₂ | **1.9** | 25 °C | Commercial "Hy-STOR" tanks |
| Magnesium | MgH₂ | **7.6** | 290 °C | High-temp stationary, R&D |
| Sodium alanate | NaAlH₄ | **7.5** | 100–200 °C | Complex hydride (R&D) |
| Ti-V-Cr alloy (bcc) | TiVCrH₄ | **2.6** | 25 °C | Next-gen automotive |

---

## 2.4 The Van't Hoff Equation (Master Equation for Metal Hydrides)

The equilibrium pressure of a metal-hydride system is thermodynamically linked to temperature through the **van't Hoff relation**:

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

A linear plot of $\ln P_{eq}$ versus $1/T$ yields:
- **Slope** = $\Delta H / R$ → reaction enthalpy (kJ/mol H₂)
- **Intercept** = $-\Delta S / R$ → reaction entropy (J/mol·K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For a useful ambient-temperature hydride, $\Delta H \approx$ **30–45 kJ/mol H₂** is ideal — too high and desorption is hard; too low and the plateau pressure at room T is sub-atmospheric.

### 2.4.1 Engineering Example
For FeTiH₂, $\Delta H = -28.1$ kJ/mol H₂ and $\Delta S = -106$ J/(mol·K). At 298 K:

$$P_{eq} = \exp\left(\frac{-28100}{8.314 \times 298} - \frac{-106}{8.314}\right) = \exp(11.34 + 12.75) = \exp(24.09) \approx 3.1 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This extremely high calculated P_eq is because FeTiH₂ actually has **two plateau regions**. The first plateau at low pressure (~1–10 bar) is the engineering-relevant one. The full thermodynamic treatment must consider the two-step reaction: **FeTi + 0.5H₂ → FeTiH**, then **FeTiH + 0.5H₂ → FeTiH₂**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $PV = nRTZ$ | Z = compressibility factor | — | Real H₂ at high P |
| 2 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{PVM}{RTZ}$ | H₂ mass in tank | kg | Compressed gas capacity |
| 3 | $w_{comp} = \dfrac{nRT}{\eta}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Isothermal compression work | J | Compressor power |
| 4 | $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8$ | Liquid H₂ density | kg/m³ | LH₂ volume calc |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{BOL} = \dfrac{(UA)(T_{amb} - T_{LH_2})}{h_{fg}}$ | Boil-off mass rate | kg/s | LH₂ tank loss |
| 6 | $E_{liq} \approx 10\text{–}13$ kWh/kg | Liquefaction energy | kWh/kg | LH₂ efficiency |
| 7 | $\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | Van't Hoff equation | — | Metal hydride T–P |
| 8 | $wt\% = \dfrac{x \cdot M_H}{M_{MH_x}} \times 100$ | Gravimetric H₂ content | % | Metal hydride comparison |
| 9 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy | J/mol | Reaction spontaneity |
| 10 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat of hydride bed | J | Thermal management |
| 11 | $P = P_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius (kinetics) | Pa | Absorption rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula (8) is the universal yardstick for comparing metal hydrides. The KTU board expects students to compute wt% for a given hydride formula as a 2-mark sub-question. Formula (3) appears almost every year in compressor-work problems.

---

## 2.6 Comparative Engineering Trade-off Matrix

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ | Metal Hydride |
|-----------|----------------|-----|---------------|
| Volumetric density (g/L) | **40** | **70.8** | 50–150 (varies) |
| Gravimetric density (wt%) | 5.7 (system) | **7.5** (pure LH₂) | 1.4–7.6 |
| Operating T (°C) | Ambient | **−253** | 25–350 |
| Operating P (bar) | 350 / 700 | 1 (atm, boil-off driven) | 1–30 |
| Energy to store (kWh/kg) | **1.0–1.5** | **10–13** | 1.5–2.5 |
| Safety concern | High pressure | Cryogenic + H₂ | Pyrophoric dust |
| Boil-off / leak | Slow leak | **Yes (BOL)** | Negligible |
| Refill time | 3–5 min | 10–15 min | 30–60 min (kinetics-limited) |
| KTU 2024 priority | **Highest (industry standard)** | Aerospace, long-haul | **R&D high (Module 4 weightage)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Hydrogen Stored in a CGH₂ Tank

**Given:** A Type IV tank has internal volume $V = 0.15 \text{ m}^3$ (typical automotive FCEV), filled to $P_2 = 700 \text{ bar} = 70 \text{ MPa}$, at $T = 298 \text{ K}$. Initial pressure $P_1 = 1 \text{ atm} = 0.1013 \text{ MPa}$. Compressibility factor $Z \approx 1.45$ at 700 bar (NIST data for H₂).

**Find:** Mass of H₂ stored.

### Step 1 — Apply the Real Gas Equation

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{PM}{RTZ}$$

This comes from $PV = ZnRT \Rightarrow n = \frac{PV}{ZRT}$, then $m = nM = \frac{PVM}{ZRT}$.

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{(70 \times 10^6) \times 0.15 \times (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314) \times 298 \times 1.45}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator Evaluation

$$70 \times 10^6 \times 0.15 = 1.05 \times 10^7$$

$$1.05 \times 10^7 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1168 \times 10^4$$

### Step 4 — Denominator Evaluation

$$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.572$$

$$2477.572 \times 1.45 = 3592.479$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{21168}{3592.479} = 5.89 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Verification

This matches Toyota Mirai's published tank capacity of **5.6 kg at 700 bar** in a 122 L tank — confirming the validity of the calculation. The small difference is due to tank inefficiency (10–15% unusable residual H₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Insight:** This 7-mark derivation typically awards 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution and arithmetic, 1 mark for unit check, and 1 mark for physical interpretation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compressor Work for Isothermal Compression of H₂

**Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 700$ bar, $n = 1$ kmol of H₂, $T = 298$ K, isothermal efficiency $\eta = 0.65$.

### Step 1 — Work Done by an Ideal Reversible Isothermal Compression

For a closed system undergoing isothermal reversible compression:

$$w_{rev} = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V \, dP = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{nRT}{P} \, dP$$

### Step 2 — Perform the Integration

$$w_{rev} = nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = nRT \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

For 1 kmol of H₂:

$$nRT = 1000 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 2{,}477{,}572 \text{ J} = 2477.572 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{700}{1}\right) = \ln(700) = 6.551$$

$$w_{rev} = 2477.572 \times 6.551 = 16{,}232.5 \text{ kJ per kmol}$$

### Step 4 — Per kg of H₂

$$w_{rev,per\_kg} = \frac{16232.5}{2.016} = 8053 \text{ kJ/kg} = 8.053 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Account for Real Compressor Efficiency

$$w_{actual} = \frac{w_{rev}}{\eta} = \frac{8.053}{0.65} = 12.39 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to kWh

$$E_{compression} = \frac{12.39}{3.6} = 3.44 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This means **~ 3.44 kWh of electricity is required per kg of H₂** to compress it to 700 bar. With H₂ LHV = 33.33 kWh/kg, this represents a **~ 10.3% energy penalty** — much smaller than the 30–40% penalty for liquefaction, which is why CGH₂ dominates the automotive market.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gravimetric Hydrogen Density of a Metal Hydride

**Given:** A candidate alloy has the formula $LaNi_5H_6$. Compute the wt% H₂.

### Step 1 — Compute Molar Masses

$$M(La) = 138.91 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$M(Ni) = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$M(H) = 1.008 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 2 — Molar Mass of $LaNi_5H_6$

$$M(LaNi_5H_6) = 138.91 + 5(58.69) + 6(1.008)$$

$$= 138.91 + 293.45 + 6.048 = 438.41 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Hydrogen in 1 mol

$$m_H = 6 \times 1.008 = 6.048 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4 — wt% Calculation

$$wt\% = \frac{6.048}{438.41} \times 100 = 1.38\%$$

This matches the engineering table value of **~ 1.4 wt%** — confirming the formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the $x$ in the numerator of Formula (8). For $MgH_2$: wt% = $\frac{2 \times 1.008}{24.305 + 2(1.008)} \times 100 = 7.66\%$ — this is the *theoretical maximum*; in practice only **~ 6.5–7.0%** is reversible.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equilibrium Pressure of a Metal Hydride (Van't Hoff Application)

**Given:** For the alloy $FeTiH_2$:
- $\Delta H = -28.1$ kJ/mol H₂ = $-28100$ J/mol
- $\Delta S = -106$ J/(mol·K)
- Find the desorption temperature required to release H₂ at 1 bar.

### Step 1 — Write the Van't Hoff Equation

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

### Step 2 — Set P_eq = 1 bar and Solve for 1/T

At $P_{eq} = 1$ bar, $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for T

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

$$T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \frac{-28100}{-106} = 265.1 \text{ K}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Celsius

$$T = 265.1 - 273.15 = -8.05 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** FeTiH₂ wants to release H₂ at atmospheric pressure at **−8 °C**, which is below ambient. At room temperature (25 °C), the equilibrium pressure is *higher* than 1 bar, meaning FeTi absorbs H₂ readily at room T. The two-step nature of FeTiH formation modifies this real value to ~**+25 °C at 1 bar**, demonstrating that PCI plateau analysis is essential.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Boil-Off Loss of an LH₂ Road Tanker

**Given:** A 5,000 kg LH₂ road tanker has effective overall heat transfer coefficient $UA = 30$ W/K. Ambient $T_{amb} = 30$ °C = 303.15 K. Liquid H₂ temperature $T_{LH_2} = 20.27$ K. Latent heat $h_{fg} = 445.6$ kJ/kg.

### Step 1 — Heat Leak Rate

$$\dot{Q}_{leak} = (UA)(T_{amb} - T_{LH_2}) = 30 \times (303.15 - 20.27) = 30 \times 282.88 = 8486.4 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Boil-Off Mass Rate

$$\dot{m}_{BOL} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{leak}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{8486.4 \text{ J/s}}{445600 \text{ J/kg}} = 0.01905 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Daily Boil-Off

$$\dot{m}_{BOL,day} = 0.01905 \times 86400 = 1646 \text{ kg/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a *gross* over-estimate because modern dewars have $UA \approx$ **1–3 W/K**, not 30 W/K. KTU problems often use *idealized* $UA$ values. With $UA = 2$ W/K, $\dot{m}_{BOL,day} = 109.7$ kg/day, i.e., **2.19% per day** for a 5,000 kg tanker.

### Step 4 — Boil-Off as % per Day

$$BOL\% = \frac{109.7}{5000} \times 100 = 2.19\%/\text{day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark:** LH₂ tankers are designed for **≤ 0.3%/day BOL**. Any value above 1%/day in a KTU problem typically indicates a poorly insulated vessel or an unrealistic $UA$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Exam-Oriented Problem Sets

| Problem Category | Key Formula | Common Mistake | Mark Allocation |
|------------------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Tank capacity | $m = PVM/(RTZ)$ | Forgetting Z at high P | 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for Z |
| Compressor work | $w = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)/\eta$ | Using P in different units | 1 mark unit consistency |
| Liquefaction energy | $E \approx 10\text{–}13$ kWh/kg | Confusing with HHV | 1 mark |
| Boil-off rate | $\dot{m} = UA \Delta T / h_{fg}$ | ΔT in wrong units | 1 mark |
| wt% metal hydride | $wt\% = xM_H / M_{MH_x}$ | Forgetting factor of 2 | 2 marks |
| Van't Hoff | $\ln P = \Delta H/(RT) - \Delta S/R$ | Sign of ΔH | 1 mark |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Storage — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Source<br>Electrolysis or SMR] --> B{Storage Mode Selection}
    B --> C[CGH2 Route<br>350 to 700 bar]
    B --> D[LH2 Route<br>minus 253 C]
    B --> E[Metal Hydride Route<br>Reversible Chemisorption]
    C --> C1[Multi-Stage Compressor<br>1 to 700 bar]
    C1 --> C2[Type IV Tank<br>Carbon Fiber Polymer Liner]
    C2 --> C3[Pressure Regulator<br>Output 2 to 10 bar]
    C3 --> C4[Fuel Cell or Engine]
    D --> D1[Ortho-Para Converter<br>Fe OH 3 Catalyst]
    D1 --> D2[Hampson Linde Liquefier<br>Joule Thomson Expansion]
    D2 --> D3[Super Insulated Dewar<br>MLI 30 to 60 Layers]
    D3 --> D4[Cryogenic Pump<br>or Pressure Dispense]
    D4 --> D4B[Fuel Cell or Engine]
    E --> E1[Alloy Activation<br>300 to 400 C Initial]
    E1 --> E2[Hydriding Reactor<br>Exothermic Absorption]
    E2 --> E3[Heat Exchanger<br>Desorption Heating]
    E3 --> E4[Fuel Cell or Engine]
    C4 --> F[End Use Application]
    D4B --> F
    E4 --> F
```

## 4.2 Pressure-Composition Isotherm (PCI) Block Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region 1<br>Alpha Phase<br>Solid Solution] --> B[Region 2<br>Plateau Region<br>Alpha plus Beta Coexistence]
    B --> C[Region 3<br>Beta Phase<br>Hydride Saturated]
    A1[P very low<br>less than 1 bar] --> A
    B1[P constant at P_eq<br>Desirable Storage Region] --> B
    C1[P rises steeply<br>Hydride saturated] --> C
    D[Temperature T1 Low<br>Plateau P High] --> B
    E[Temperature T2 High<br>Plateau P Higher] --> B
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — CGH₂ Refueling Station

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input H2 at 20 bar from Electrolyzer] --> P2[Buffer Tank 1<br>50 bar]
    P2 --> P3[Compressor Stage 1<br>50 to 200 bar]
    P3 --> P4[Intercooler<br>Cooling to 313 K]
    P4 --> P5[Compressor Stage 2<br>200 to 700 bar]
    P5 --> P6[High Pressure Storage<br>Type IV Cascade]
    P6 --> P7[Dispenser Hose<br>to Vehicle]
    P7 --> P8[Vehicle Tank<br>Type IV 700 bar]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A3 --> A1

```
