# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Mark-Loss Triggers:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in P–V–T equations** — Converting bar to Pa is the most frequent 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing gravimetric vs. volumetric density** — A 700-bar tank is *volumetrically inferior* to LH₂ but *gravimetrically comparable*.
> 3. **Forgetting the compressibility factor Z at 700 bar** — Using ideal gas gives ~ 30% error.
> 4. **Sign of ΔH in van't Hoff** — Absorption is exothermic (ΔH < 0); desorption is endothermic.
> 5. **Not accounting for ortho–para conversion** in LH₂ — A 3-mark question almost always appears on this.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Compare compressed gaseous hydrogen (CGH₂) and liquid hydrogen (LH₂) storage methods in terms of (i) volumetric density, (ii) energy input, and (iii) major engineering challenge."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (20.27 K) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Volumetric density** | ~ **40 g/L** | ~ **70.8 g/L** (superior) |
| **Energy input** | ~ **1.0–1.5 kWh/kg** | ~ **10–13 kWh/kg** |
| **Major challenge** | High-pressure tank safety & cost | Boil-off loss & ortho–para conversion |

Hence, **CGH₂** is preferred for short-range FCEVs (Toyota Mirai); **LH₂** is preferred for long-haul trucks and aerospace (NASA, Airbus).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Define metal hydride and explain the role of the Pressure-Composition Isotherm (PCI) in selecting a hydride for hydrogen storage."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **metal hydride** is a reversible chemical compound formed when a metal or intermetallic alloy absorbs hydrogen into its crystal lattice: $M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$.

The **PCI curve** plots equilibrium pressure $P_{eq}$ versus hydrogen-to-metal atomic ratio (H/M) at a fixed temperature. It exhibits three distinct regions:
1. **α-phase region** — solid solution of H in the metal lattice (low H content, P rises steeply).
2. **Plateau region** — coexistence of α and β phases, where P is nearly constant — this is the **engineering-usable storage region**.
3. **β-phase region** — saturated hydride (P rises sharply).

The plateau pressure at the operating temperature determines the *charge/discharge* conditions. For example, LaNi₅H₆ has a plateau near **2 bar at 25 °C**, ideal for near-ambient applications. The **van't Hoff equation** relates plateau pressure to temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the mass of hydrogen stored in a high-pressure tank using the real gas equation. A Type IV tank of volume **0.12 m³** is filled with H₂ to **700 bar** at **298 K**. Calculate the mass of H₂ stored, given the compressibility factor **Z = 1.45**, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), and M(H₂) = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the four types of compressed hydrogen storage tanks. Discuss the engineering rationale for the selection of Type IV tanks for fuel-cell electric vehicles. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the Real Gas Equation** (1 mark)

$$PV = ZnRT \quad \text{where} \quad n = \frac{m}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for mass m** (1 mark)

$$m = \frac{PVM}{ZRT}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values** (1 mark)

$$m = \frac{(70 \times 10^6) \times 0.12 \times (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator** (1 mark)

$$70 \times 10^6 \times 0.12 = 8.4 \times 10^6$$

$$8.4 \times 10^6 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 16{,}934.4$$

**Step 5 — Denominator** (1 mark)

$$1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 1.45 \times 2477.572 = 3592.48$$

**Step 6 — Final result with unit check** (1 mark)

$$m = \frac{16934.4}{3592.48} = 4.71 \text{ kg H}_2$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation** (1 mark)
The value (4.71 kg) is consistent with published FCEV tank capacities of 4.4–6.0 kg, confirming the calculation and validating the importance of including $Z$ (using Z = 1 would over-estimate mass by ~ 45%).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Diagram + classification table (3 marks):**

| Type | Liner | Reinforcement | Pressure |
|------|-------|---------------|----------|
| I | Steel | None | 175–200 bar |
| II | Steel | Hoop composite | 260–300 bar |
| III | Aluminum | Full composite | 350–450 bar |
| IV | Polymer (HDPE) | Full carbon fiber | 700 bar |

**Why Type IV for FCEVs — 4 key points (4 marks):**

1. **Highest gravimetric density** — carbon fiber is 5× stronger than steel at 1/5 the weight. Type IV achieves **~ 5.7 wt%** system-level vs. Type I at ~ 1.0 wt%.
2. **No hydrogen embrittlement** — the polymer (HDPE) liner is H₂-impermeable, eliminating the embrittlement failure mode of Type I/II steel.
3. **Cycling safety** — carbon-fiber wrap distributes hoop stress uniformly, allowing 10,000+ pressure cycles at 700 bar.
4. **Automotive fuel-cell operating pressure** — fuel cells require 700 bar inlet to achieve meaningful energy density and reasonable vehicle range (500+ km).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the storage of hydrogen in metal hydrides. With the help of a Pressure-Composition Isotherm (PCI) curve, describe the absorption and desorption process. Derive the **van't Hoff equation** relating equilibrium pressure to temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the metal hydride **LaNi₅H₆**, compute the gravimetric hydrogen density (wt%). If the enthalpy of formation is **−30.1 kJ/mol H₂** and the entropy change is **−108 J/(mol·K)**, determine the desorption temperature at which the plateau pressure equals 1 bar. Comment on the suitability of LaNi₅H₆ for ambient-temperature hydrogen storage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Storage Principle (2 marks):**
Hydrogen is stored by reversible chemisorption in a metal lattice: $M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$. The reaction is **exothermic** during absorption (heat must be removed) and **endothermic** during desorption (heat must be supplied).

**PCI Curve Description (2 marks):**

A typical PCI plot has three regions:
- **α-phase (left):** H dissolves in metal; P rises with H content.
- **Plateau (middle):** α + β coexist; P nearly constant = **P_eq(T)**.
- **β-phase (right):** saturated hydride; P rises sharply.

**Derivation of van't Hoff equation (3 marks):**

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0$$

For the reaction $M + \frac{x}{2}H_2 \rightarrow MH_x$, $\Delta G$ is also linked to P:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + \frac{x}{2}RT \ln P_{H_2}$$

Setting $\Delta G = 0$:

$$\frac{x}{2}RT \ln P_{eq} = -\Delta G^0 = T\Delta S^0 - \Delta H^0$$

For per mol H₂ basis (redefine):

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} - \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation** — a straight line when $\ln P_{eq}$ is plotted against $1/T$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Molar mass of LaNi₅H₆ (1 mark):**

$$M = 138.91 + 5(58.69) + 6(1.008) = 438.41 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — wt% H₂ calculation (1 mark):**

$$wt\% = \frac{6 \times 1.008}{438.41} \times 100 = 1.38\%$$

**Step 3 — Set up van't Hoff at P_eq = 1 bar (1 mark):**

At $P_{eq} = 1$ bar, $\ln P_{eq} = 0$:

$$T = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} = \frac{-30100}{-108} = 278.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to °C (1 mark):**

$$T = 278.7 - 273.15 = 5.55 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on suitability (3 marks):**

LaNi₅H₆ has the following properties that make it **highly suitable for ambient-temperature storage**:
- A desorption temperature of ~ 5.5 °C at 1 bar is *below* ambient (25 °C) → at room temperature, the plateau pressure is *above* 1 bar → the alloy **absorbs hydrogen spontaneously at moderate pressure** (1–5 bar) at 25 °C.
- The wt% of 1.38% is low for *automotive* use but **acceptable for stationary and Ni-MH battery applications**.
- Kinetics are fast (fully activated at 25 °C in 30 minutes).
- Cycling stability is excellent (10,000+ cycles with < 10% capacity loss).
- **Limitation:** wt% is too low for FCEV range requirements; MgH₂ or complex hydrides (NaAlH₄) are preferred for vehicles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Module 4 Storage & Transportation**

### ✅ Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)
- Use **PV = ZnRT** at high pressure (Z ≈ 1.45 at 700 bar).
- **Type IV 700-bar tank** is the FCEV industry standard.
- Compressor work: $w = \dfrac{nRT}{\eta}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$.
- Typical energy penalty: **1.0–1.5 kWh/kg** (~ 3–5% of LHV).
- Risk: **hydrogen embrittlement** — use 316L stainless steel or polymer liners.

### ✅ Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)
- Boiling point: **20.271 K = −252.879 °C** (not −253 °C).
- Density: **70.8 kg/m³**.
- **Ortho–para conversion** is mandatory before liquefaction (catalyst: Fe(OH)₃).
- Liquefaction energy: **10–13 kWh/kg** (30–40% of LHV).
- Boil-off loss (BOL): $\dot{m}_{BOL} = \dfrac{UA \Delta T}{h_{fg}}$, typical ≤ 0.3%/day.
- Used in aerospace (NASA) and long-haul cryogenic trucks.

### ✅ Metal Hydrides
- Reaction: $M + \dfrac{x}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x$ (ΔH < 0 for absorption).
- **PCI curve** has three regions: α-phase, plateau, β-phase.
- **Van't Hoff equation:** $\ln P_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} - \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$.
- Ideal ΔH for ambient T: **30–45 kJ/mol H₂**.
- LaNi₅H₆: 1.4 wt% — good for stationary, Ni-MH batteries.
- FeTiH₂: 1.9 wt% — used in commercial Hy-STOR systems.
- MgH₂: 7.6 wt% — best *theoretical* but needs 290 °C.
- wt% formula: $wt\% = \dfrac{xM_H}{M_{MH_x}} \times 100$ (do not forget to include x).

### ✅ Universal Constants to Memorize
- R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- M(H₂) = **2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol**
- ρ_H₂ STP = **0.0899 kg/m³**
- ρ_LH₂ = **70.8 kg/m³**
- T_b = **20.271 K**
- LHV = **119.96 MJ/kg**, HHV = **141.88 MJ/kg**

### ✅ KTU Examiner's Top 5 Trap Questions
1. "Why is LH₂ not used in everyday FCEVs?" → Liquefaction energy penalty.
2. "Why must H₂ be cooled below −240 °C before final J-T expansion?" → H₂ has a negative J-T coefficient at room T; positive only below ~ 200 K.
3. "Why is Type II not preferred for FCEVs?" → Limited to 300 bar, heavy.
4. "Calculate wt% for MgH₂." → 7.66%.
5. "What is the *practical* (not theoretical) wt% of MgH₂?" → 6.5–7.0% due to oxide passivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cells: Working Principle and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells: Working Principle and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel (most commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) into **direct current (DC) electricity**, with water and heat as the primary by-products. The conversion bypasses the intermediate steps of combustion and mechanical rotation, yielding a theoretical efficiency (based on Gibbs free energy) significantly higher than conventional heat engines.

The key differentiator from a battery is that a **fuel cell does not run down or require recharging**; it produces electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied — hence it is a **"continuous energy generator"** rather than an energy storage device.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Fuel Cell = A Battery That Never Runs Out, Powered by Gas"**
>
> Imagine a rechargeable battery that you never have to plug in to recharge. Instead, you simply pour hydrogen "soda" into one side and let air flow through the other side. As long as the soda keeps flowing, electricity keeps coming out, just like water keeps flowing from a tap until you turn it off. The waste product is **pure drinking water** — clean enough, in fact, for astronauts in the Apollo missions to drink!

### Core Distinguishing Metrics

- **Standard Cell Potential (H₂/O₂ cell): E° = 1.229 V** at 25 °C (298 K)
- **Faraday's Constant: F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹**
- **Universal Gas Constant: R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Theoretical Efficiency Limit: η_theoretical = ΔG°/ΔH° ≈ 83%** (for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C based on HHV)
- **Lower Heating Value Efficiency (LHV basis): η ≈ 94%–98%**

### Why Fuel Cells Matter in the KTU 2024 Energy Context

- Zero greenhouse-gas emissions (water-only by-product) when pure hydrogen is used.
- Modular, scalable (W to MW range).
- High part-load efficiency (unlike thermal plants).
- Silent operation — suitable for urban and indoor applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** is used as the reference architecture to illustrate the principle.

1. **Fuel Delivery:** Pressurized hydrogen gas (H₂) is supplied to the **anode** side through flow-field channels.
2. **Oxidant Delivery:** Air (or pure O₂) is supplied to the **cathode** side.
3. **Anode Half-Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):** A platinum catalyst splits H₂ into protons and electrons.
   $$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
4. **Charge Separation:** Protons (H⁺) migrate through the **proton-exchange membrane** (a hydrated polymer electrolyte such as Nafion). Electrons are forced to take the **external circuit** because the membrane is electronically insulating.
5. **Power Delivery:** Electrons flowing through the external load constitute the **useful direct current**.
6. **Cathode Half-Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):** Protons, electrons, and oxygen combine at the cathode.
   $$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
7. **Net Reaction:** Combining the two half-reactions gives the overall cell reaction.
   $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

### 2.2 Theoretical Cell Voltage and the Nernst Equation

The reversible (open-circuit) voltage of a fuel cell is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{cell} = E^°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E^°_{cell}$ = standard-state reversible cell voltage (**1.229 V** for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel (= 2 for H₂)
- $P_{H_2}$, $P_{O_2}$, $P_{H_2O}$ = partial pressures (atm) of the gases
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96,485 C mol⁻¹**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.3 Polarization Losses (The "Why" Real Cells Don't Hit 1.229 V)

The actual operating voltage $V_{cell}$ falls below $E_{cell}$ due to three irreversible losses:

$$V_{cell} = E_{cell} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

| Loss Type | Symbol | Physical Cause | Dominant Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation Loss** | $\eta_{act}$ | Slow electrode kinetics (slow ORR) | Low current |
| **Ohmic Loss** | $\eta_{ohm} = iR$ | Ionic & electronic resistance | Mid current |
| **Concentration Loss** | $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass-transport limitations | High current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Tafel Equation describes the activation loss:
> $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$
> where $\alpha$ is the charge-transfer coefficient and $i_0$ is the exchange current density. The ORR has a notoriously low $i_0$, which is why platinum catalysts are essential.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E°_{cell} = \frac{\Delta G°}{nF}$ | Standard reversible voltage from Gibbs free energy |
| 2 | $\eta_{HHV} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H_{HHV}} \approx 83\%$ | HHV-based theoretical efficiency |
| 3 | $\eta_{LHV} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H_{LHV}} \approx 94\%$ | LHV-based theoretical efficiency |
| 4 | $E_{cell} = E° - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$ | Nernst equation (cell voltage under load conditions) |
| 5 | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln(i/i_0)$ | Activation polarization (Tafel) |
| 6 | $\eta_{ohm} = iR_{internal}$ | Ohmic polarization (linear in current) |
| 7 | $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Power output of a single cell |
| 8 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Power output of a fuel-cell stack |
| 9 | $Q = nF$ | Total charge per mole of electrons (Coulomb's law of electrolysis) |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{P}{\eta_{cell} \cdot LHV}$ | Hydrogen mass flow rate for given power |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Transportation:** PEMFCs power Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, and city buses.
- **Space:** NASA's Apollo and Space Shuttle missions used **Alkaline Fuel Cells**.
- **Stationary Power:** PAFC and MCFC units provide combined heat and power (CHP) for hospitals and data centers.
- **Portable Electronics:** DMFC technology powers laptops and military field equipment.
- **Grid-scale Storage:** Reversible (unitized) fuel cells support renewable integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Efficiency of the H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

We begin from fundamental thermodynamics. The **maximum useful electrical work** extractable from a chemical reaction at constant T and P equals the **Gibbs free energy change**.

**Step 1 — Overall reaction:**

$$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

**Step 2 — Look up the standard thermodynamic data at 298 K:**

- Standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water: $\Delta G°_f = -237.2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- Higher Heating Value (combustion enthalpy of H₂): $\Delta H°_{HHV} = -285.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- Lower Heating Value (water leaves as vapor): $\Delta H°_{LHV} = -241.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Apply the Gibbs relationship for reversible cell voltage:**

$$E°_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G°}{nF}$$

Substituting $\Delta G° = -237.2 \times 10^3 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$:

$$E°_{cell} = -\frac{-237.2 \times 10^3}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$E°_{cell} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E°_{cell} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the HHV-based theoretical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{HHV} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°_{HHV}} = \frac{237.2}{285.8}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{HHV} \approx 0.8299 = 82.99\%}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the LHV-based theoretical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°_{LHV}} = \frac{237.2}{241.8}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{LHV} \approx 0.9809 = 98.09\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway:** A fuel cell can theoretically recover **~83%** of hydrogen's chemical energy as electricity — vastly superior to a Carnot engine (typically <45% for practical steam plants) — without any moving parts.

### 3.2 Solved Numerical Example (Model KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A PEMFC operates at 60 °C with H₂ at 1.5 atm and O₂ at 0.21 atm (air), producing liquid water. The actual operating cell voltage is **0.70 V** at a current density of **0.6 A cm⁻²**, and the cell area is **250 cm²**.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) The reversible Nernst voltage $E_{cell}$ at operating conditions
> (b) The total polarization loss per cell
> (c) The power output of a 100-cell stack

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

- $T = 60 + 273 = 333 \text{ K}$
- $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- $P_{H_2} = 1.5 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm}$, $P_{H_2O} \approx 1 \text{ atm}$ (condensed)
- $E° = 1.229 \text{ V}$, $V_{cell} = 0.70 \text{ V}$, $i = 0.6 \text{ A cm}^{-2}$, $A = 250 \text{ cm}^2$

**Step 2 — Compute the Nernst correction term.**

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 333}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2{,}768.6}{192{,}970} = 0.01435 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reaction quotient Q.**

$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times (0.21)^{1/2}}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 0.4583} = \frac{1}{0.6875} = 1.4545$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Nernst Equation.**

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 - (0.01435) \ln(1.4545)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 - (0.01435)(0.3747)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.00538$$

$$\boxed{E_{cell} \approx 1.2236 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Total polarization loss.**

$$\eta_{total} = E_{cell} - V_{cell} = 1.2236 - 0.70$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{total} \approx 0.5236 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 6 — Stack current.**

$$I = i \times A = 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \text{ A}$$

**Step 7 — Stack power output.**

$$P_{stack} = n_{cells} \times V_{cell} \times I = 100 \times 0.70 \times 150$$

$$\boxed{P_{stack} = 10{,}500 \text{ W} = 10.5 \text{ kW}}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp. | Efficiency (LHV) | Power Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** | Polymer Membrane (Nafion) | 60–100 °C | 50–60% | 1 W – 250 kW | Vehicles, portable power |
| **AFC** | KOH solution (alkaline) | 60–250 °C | 50–70% | 1 kW – 100 kW | Space (Apollo), military |
| **PAFC** | Liquid H₃PO₄ | 150–220 °C | 40–50% | 50 kW – 10 MW | Stationary CHP |
| **MCFC** | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | 600–700 °C | 50–60% | 100 kW – 10 MW | Large power plants |
| **SOFC** | YSZ (solid ceramic) | 600–1000 °C | 50–65% | 1 kW – 100 MW | Stationary, hybrid cycles |
| **DMFC** | Polymer Membrane | 60–130 °C | 30–40% | 1 W – 1 kW | Portable electronics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PEMFC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2TANK[Hydrogen Tank H2] -->|Pressurized H2| ANODEFLOW[Anode Flow Field]
    AIRFAN[Air Blower O2 Supply] -->|Filtered Air| CATHODEFLOW[Cathode Flow Field]

    ANODEFLOW --> ANODE[Anode Catalyst Layer Pt]
    CATHODEFLOW --> CATHODE[Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt]

    ANODE -->|HOR Reaction: H2 to 2H+ + 2e-| MEMBRANE[Proton Exchange Membrane Nafion]
    MEMBRANE -->|H+ ions migrate| CATHODE
    ANODE -->|Electrons forced external path| EXTLOAD[External Load DC Power]

    EXTLOAD -->|Return electrons via wire| CATHODE

    CATHODE -->|ORR Reaction: 2H+ + 1/2 O2 + 2e- to H2O| WATER[Water Output]
    ANODE --> HEAT1[Heat Byproduct]
    CATHODE --> HEAT2[Heat Byproduct]

    HEAT1 --> COOLER[Cooling Loop]
    HEAT2 --> COOLER
    WATER --> CONDENSER[Water Condenser]
    CONDENSER --> DRYOUT[Exhaust]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Fuel Cell System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FUELPROCESSING["FUEL PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> A2[Pressure Regulator]
        A2 --> A3[Humidifier]
    end

    subgraph OXIDANTPROCESSING["OXIDANT PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1[Air Filter] --> B2[Blower / Compressor]
        B2 --> B3[Humidifier]
    end

    subgraph ELECTROCHEMICALCORE["ELECTROCHEMICAL CORE"]
        C1[Anode] --> C2[Electrolyte Membrane]
        C2 --> C3[Cathode]
        C1 -.Electrons.-> D1[External Load DC Bus]
        D1 -.Return Path.-> C3
    end

    subgraph TMS["THERMAL MANAGEMENT SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1[Coolant Plates] --> E2[Radiator / Heat Exchanger]
    end

    subgraph POWERCOND["POWER CONDITIONING SUBSYSTEM"]
        F1[DC-DC Converter] --> F2[Inverter for AC Loads]
        F2 --> F3[Grid or Load Bank]
    end

    A3 --> C1
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D1 --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The four subsystems — *Fuel Processing, Oxidant Processing, Electrochemical Core, and Thermal Management* — together form a **complete fuel cell power plant**. In real engineering systems (e.g., Toyota Mirai), the *Power Conditioning* subsystem converts the raw DC bus output into the AC form required by traction motors or the grid.

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Operating Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOW[Low Temperature 60 to 250 C PEMFC, AFC, DMFC, PAFC] --> MID[High Temperature 600 to 1000 C MCFC, SOFC]
    MID --> APPS[Applications Scale Up with Temperature]
    LOW --> APPS
    APPS --> SPACETC[Space and Vehicles]
    APPS --> STATIONARY[Stationary CHP and Grid]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a fuel cell. How is it different from a conventional battery?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that directly converts the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel and oxidant into DC electricity, with water and heat as by-products. **Difference from a battery (key points):**
1. A battery is a **closed energy storage system** that discharges until reactants are exhausted; a fuel cell is an **open energy conversion system** that operates as long as fuel/oxidant are supplied.
2. A battery requires **recharging** after use; a fuel cell requires only **refueling**.
3. Battery electrodes are **consumed/replenished internally**; in a fuel cell, electrodes are merely **catalytic surfaces** and remain unchanged.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Write the half-cell and overall reactions of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell. Mention the standard cell voltage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Anode (HOR):** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- **Cathode (ORR):** $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
- **Overall:** $H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$
- **Standard Cell Voltage:** $E°_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ at 25 °C.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 — Question A (14 Marks)]**
> **Q3. (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the Nernst voltage of an H₂/O₂ fuel cell. A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with H₂ at 2 atm, O₂ at 1 atm, and produces liquid water. Calculate the reversible cell voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Working Principle of PEMFC (7 marks):**

1. **Construction:** A PEMFC consists of (i) an anode, (ii) a cathode, (iii) a proton-exchange membrane (Nafion) sandwiched between them, (iv) catalyst layers (platinum on carbon) at both electrode-membrane interfaces, and (v) bipolar flow-field plates with gas channels. **(1 mark)**

2. **Fuel Supply:** Pressurized, humidified hydrogen is fed to the anode side through the flow-field channels. **(1 mark)**

3. **Oxidant Supply:** Humidified air/oxygen is fed to the cathode side. **(0.5 marks)**

4. **Anode Reaction (HOR):** At the platinum catalyst, hydrogen molecules dissociate into protons and electrons:
   $$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
   **(1 mark)**

5. **Charge Transport:** The Nafion membrane conducts only **H⁺ ions** (proton hopping through hydrated sulfonate groups). Electrons are blocked by the membrane and forced through the **external load**, generating DC current. **(1.5 marks)**

6. **Cathode Reaction (ORR):** Electrons returning through the external circuit combine with protons and oxygen at the cathode:
   $$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
   **(1 mark)**

7. **By-products and Cooling:** Water and heat are removed via the cathode exhaust and coolant plates respectively. Pure water can be recovered for drinking or humidifier feed. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Nernst Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the Gibbs free energy for the overall cell reaction, the maximum reversible electrical work per mole of electrons is given by:
$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For non-standard conditions, the chemical potentials of the gases must be accounted for. Using the relation $\mu_i = \mu_i° + RT\ln(P_i)$, the change in Gibbs free energy becomes:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT\ln(Q)$$

where $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$ is the reaction quotient.

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE°_{cell} + RT\ln(Q)$$

Dividing throughout by $-nF$:

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q) = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)}$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: $T = 80 + 273 = 353 \text{ K}$, $P_{H_2} = 2 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 1 \text{ atm}$, $P_{H_2O} = 1 \text{ atm}$ (liquid water), $n = 2$.

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{2 \times 1^{1/2}} = 0.5$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 - (0.01521)\ln(0.5) = 1.229 - (0.01521)(-0.6931)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.01054$$

$$\boxed{E_{cell} \approx 1.240 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that increasing the partial pressure of H₂ **above 1 atm raises the reversible voltage** — this is why high-pressure operation is favored in automotive fuel cell systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)]**
> **Q4. (a)** Compare the construction, electrolyte, operating temperature, efficiency, and applications of any **five** major types of fuel cells. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the three major polarization losses in a fuel cell with a typical polarization curve (V vs I). Mention practical strategies to reduce each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Comparison of Five Fuel Cell Types (7 marks):**

| # | Feature | PEMFC | AFC | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrolyte | Polymer (Nafion) | KOH (liquid) | H₃PO₄ (liquid) | Molten carbonates | YSZ (solid ceramic) |
| 2 | Mobile Ion | H⁺ | OH⁻ | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| 3 | Operating Temp. | 60–100 °C | 60–250 °C | 150–220 °C | 600–700 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| 4 | Efficiency (LHV) | 50–60% | 50–70% | 40–50% | 50–60% | 50–65% |
| 5 | Applications | Vehicles, portable | Space (Apollo) | Stationary CHP | Large power plants | Grid, hybrid cycles |

**Construction highlights:** PEMFC and PAFC use liquid electrolytes held in a matrix; AFC uses circulating KOH; MCFC uses molten Li/K carbonates requiring Ni electrodes; SOFC uses a fully solid ceramic stack enabling high-temperature operation and internal reforming. **(7 marks awarded for comprehensive tabular comparison and notes)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Polarization Losses (7 marks):**

**1. Activation Loss (η_act):** Caused by the slow kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Dominant at low current densities. **Strategies to reduce:** Use higher platinum loading, develop Pt-alloy or non-precious metal catalysts, raise operating temperature (PEMFC up to ~80 °C), and increase reactant partial pressures.

**2. Ohmic Loss (η_ohm):** Caused by ionic resistance in the electrolyte and electronic resistance in electrodes and current collectors. Dominant in the linear mid-current region. Follows Ohm's law:
$$\eta_{ohm} = iR_{internal}$$
**Strategies to reduce:** Use thinner membranes with higher conductivity, optimize bipolar-plate contact resistance, use metallic interconnects, and operate at higher temperature (increases ionic conductivity).

**3. Concentration Loss (η_conc):** Caused by mass-transport limitations when reactants are consumed faster than they can be supplied. Dominant at high current densities. **Strategies to reduce:** Optimize flow-field channel geometry, increase operating pressure, use higher catalyst activity to lower required current density, and improve GDL (gas diffusion layer) porosity.

**Polarization Curve Description:**
The V–I curve starts at $E° = 1.229 \text{ V}$ (open-circuit), drops sharply due to activation loss, enters a near-linear ohmic region, then plummets at high current due to concentration loss. **(2 marks for the curve description and labeling)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mobile ion** in each fuel cell type is the **single most common marking deduction** (typically –2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing $E°$ with operating V** — students often quote 1.229 V as "cell voltage" without acknowledging losses. Always distinguish theoretical (Nernst) vs. actual (terminal) voltage.
> 3. **Units error:** $RT/nF$ must be expressed in **Volts** (do NOT leave in J/C without unit justification).
> 4. **Polarization curve**: V must be on **Y-axis** and I (or current density) on **X-axis**; reversing these costs –1 mark.
> 5. **Theoretical efficiency is based on Gibbs free energy**, not Carnot efficiency. Mixing the two loses 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A fuel cell is an **open** electrochemical converter, not a storage device like a battery.
- ✅ Standard reversible voltage of H₂/O₂ cell = **1.229 V** at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- ✅ Overall reaction: $H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ — only water is produced.
- ✅ Theoretical efficiency (HHV basis) = **~83%**; (LHV basis) = **~98%** — far above Carnot limit.
- ✅ **Nernst Equation:** $E_{cell} = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$ — must be memorised.
- ✅ Three polarization losses: **activation (Tafel), ohmic, concentration** — label V–I curve correctly.
- ✅ PEMFC and AFC = **low temperature**; PAFC = medium; MCFC and SOFC = high temperature.
- ✅ PEMFC uses Nafion membrane, mobile ion **H⁺**; AFC uses KOH, mobile ion **OH⁻**; MCFC uses **CO₃²⁻**; SOFC uses **O²⁻**.
- ✅ **Apollo missions used AFCs** — a frequently asked KTU question.
- ✅ Power stack equation: $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$.
- ✅ Hydrogen mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{cell} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$.
- ✅ **Applications:** PEMFC → vehicles; AFC → space; PAFC → CHP; MCFC/SOFC → grid; DMFC → portable.
- ✅ **E° decreases with temperature rise**, but actual operating voltage often **increases** due to reduced activation loss — a KTU favourite trick question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization and Durability of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterization and Durability of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: CHARACTERIZATION AND DURABILITY OF FUEL CELLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characterization of a Fuel Cell** is defined as the systematic experimental and analytical procedure used to quantify the electrochemical performance, efficiency, and operational behavior of a fuel cell under controlled conditions. It involves the measurement of voltage-current (polarization) relationships, power output, electrochemical impedance, and transient response to determine the cell's suitability for a given application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Durability of a Fuel Cell** refers to the ability of the fuel cell to maintain its performance (voltage, power output, and efficiency) over an extended period of operation under specified environmental and load conditions. It is typically quantified as the **rate of voltage degradation per 1000 hours (μV/hr)** or the total number of operational hours before the cell voltage drops below a defined end-of-life threshold.

**Key Performance Metrics Used in KTU Syllabus:**
- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Theoretical maximum voltage with no load
- **Current Density (j):** Current per unit active electrode area (A/cm²)
- **Power Density (P):** Power per unit active area (W/cm²)
- **Area Specific Resistance (ASR):** Resistance per unit area (Ω·cm²)
- **Round-Trip Efficiency:** Electrical energy out / Chemical energy in

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Battery Health Check" Concept:**
>
> Imagine you purchased a rechargeable torchlight. **Characterization** is like taking the torchlight to a laboratory to test:
> 1. How bright is it at full charge? (Maximum power density)
> 2. How does brightness change as the battery drains? (Polarization curve)
> 3. How fast can it deliver current? (Power density)
> 4. What is its internal resistance? (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy)
>
> **Durability** is like tracking how the torchlight performs after 500, 1000, and 2000 charge-discharge cycles. Does it still light up brightly, or has it dimmed? The "dimming rate" is analogous to the **degradation rate** (μV/hr) of a fuel cell.
>
> In fuel cells, just like a torchlight losing capacity over time, the catalyst degrades, membranes get contaminated, and electrodes corrode — all contributing to gradual performance loss.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following universally accepted constants and benchmarks are used in fuel cell characterization:

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485$ **C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure:** $T = 298.15$ **K**, $P = 1$ **atm**
- **Hydrogen Lower Heating Value (LHV):** $119.96$ **MJ/kg**
- **Hydrogen Higher Heating Value (HHV):** $141.88$ **MJ/kg**
- **DOE 2025 Durability Target (PEMFC):** **5,000 hours** for passenger vehicles, **30,000 hours** for stationary applications
- **End-of-Life Voltage Threshold:** Typically **80%** of rated voltage or a fixed drop (e.g., **10%** from beginning of life)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Always quote Faraday's constant as $F = 96,485$ C/mol and use $T = 298$ K for standard-state Nernst equation calculations unless stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Polarization Curve — The Heart of Fuel Cell Characterization

The **polarization curve** (also called the **V-i curve** or **I-V characteristic curve**) is the single most important diagnostic plot in fuel cell characterization. It plots **cell voltage ($V_{cell}$)** on the y-axis against **current density ($j$)** on the x-axis.

### 2.2 Three (or Four) Distinct Loss Regions

The polarization curve is divided into characteristic regions, each dominated by a specific type of irreversible loss:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The General Fuel Cell Voltage Equation:**
>
> $$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$
>
> where each term corresponds to a specific loss mechanism explained below.

#### Region 1: Activation Losses (Low Current Density Region)

- **Cause:** Sluggish kinetics of the electrochemical reactions, especially the **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)** at the cathode.
- **Why it occurs:** A portion of the cell voltage is consumed to overcome the activation energy barrier of the reactions.
- **Mathematical Form (Tafel Equation):**
- **Physical Meaning:** Dominant at low current densities (0 to ~100 mA/cm²). The voltage drops sharply initially.

#### Region 2: Ohmic Losses (Intermediate Current Density Region)

- **Cause:** Resistance to the flow of ions through the **electrolyte/membrane** and resistance to the flow of electrons through the **electrode materials, current collectors, and interconnects**.
- **Why it occurs:** Every conductor has finite resistance; ohmic losses follow **Ohm's Law** (linear with current).
- **Mathematical Form:** $\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot R_{ohmic}$
- **Physical Meaning:** Dominant in the linear, mid-range region of the polarization curve. The slope of this linear region is the **Area Specific Resistance (ASR)**.

#### Region 3: Concentration (Mass Transport) Losses (High Current Density Region)

- **Cause:** Inability to supply reactants (H₂ or O₂) to the electrode surface fast enough, or to remove products (water) efficiently.
- **Why it occurs:** At very high current densities, the reactant concentration at the catalyst surface drops to near zero, causing a sharp voltage drop.
- **Mathematical Form:**
- **Physical Meaning:** Dominant at high current densities (>500 mA/cm²). The curve drops sharply toward the **limiting current density ($j_L$)**.

#### Region 4 (Optional): Internal Currents / Fuel Crossover Losses

- **Cause:** Small parasitic currents from **fuel crossover** (H₂ diffusing through the membrane to the cathode side) and **internal short-circuiting** through the electrolyte.
- **Why it occurs:** Even at OCV, the actual voltage is lower than the thermodynamic Nernst voltage due to these losses.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nernst Open Circuit Voltage (H₂/O₂ cell) | $E_{Nernst} = 1.229 - 0.85 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15) + 4.3085 \times 10^{-5} T \ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | V | Theoretical max voltage |
| Activation Loss (Tafel) | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = A \ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | V | Slow kinetics |
| Tafel Slope | $A = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}$ | V/decade | Slope of activation region |
| Ohmic Loss | $\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot R_{ohmic} = j \cdot ASR$ | V | Linear in j |
| Concentration Loss | $\eta_{conc} = - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | V | Mass transport limit |
| Power Density | $P = V_{cell} \times j$ | W/cm² | Useful output |
| Maximum Power Density | $P_{max} = V_{max} \times j_{max}$ | W/cm² | Peak performance |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482}$ (for HHV) or $\frac{V_{cell}}{1.253}$ (for LHV) | dimensionless | Thermodynamic efficiency |
| Charge Transfer Resistance | $R_{ct} = \frac{RT}{nFj_0}$ | Ω·cm² | From EIS |
| Double Layer Capacitance | $C_{dl} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | F | From EIS |
| Degradation Rate | $\Delta V_{\%} = \frac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{V_{initial}} \times 100$ | % or μV/hr | Durability metric |
| End-of-Life Hours | $t_{EOL} = \frac{V_{initial} - V_{EOL}}{k}$ | hours | k = degradation rate |
| Activation Energy (Arrhenius) | $k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | varies | Temperature dependence |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Pitfall:** In the markdown table above, absolute value notation has been written as $\vert x \vert$ — **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown table cells as it breaks the table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polarization curves** are used by fuel cell manufacturers (e.g., **Ballard, Plug Power, Toyota, Hyundai**) to compare different membrane types, catalyst loadings, and operating conditions.
- **EIS measurements** are critical for diagnosing **faulty membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs)** without dismantling the cell.
- **DOE targets** for fuel cell durability drive research funding and technology roadmaps in countries like the US, EU, Japan, South Korea, and India.
- **Durability testing** under **Accelerated Stress Tests (AST)** allows prediction of 5,000+ hour lifetimes in a few hundred hours of lab time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for the H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

The overall electrochemical reaction in a PEMFC is:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (l)$$

**Step 1: Write the standard Gibbs Free Energy Change**
At standard conditions (298.15 K, 1 atm), the Gibbs free energy of formation for liquid water is $\Delta G_f^\circ = -237.13$ kJ/mol.

**Step 2: Calculate the Standard Cell Potential ($E^\circ$)**

$$E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

where $n = 2$ electrons are transferred per molecule of H₂.

**Step 3: Substitute the Values**

$$\begin{aligned}
E^\circ &= -\frac{-237.13 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}} \\
&= \frac{237130}{192970} \\
&\approx 1.229 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Generalize to Non-Standard Conditions (Nernst Equation)**
The Nernst equation relates the cell potential to the partial pressures of reactants/products:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{Nernst} &= E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \\
&= E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: For an Operating PEMFC with Liquid Water Product ($a_{H_2O} = 1$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{Nernst} &= 1.229 - \frac{8.314 \times T}{2 \times 96485} \ln \left( \frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} \right) \\
&= 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times T}{2 \times 96485} \ln \left( P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Insert Typical Operating Values**
Assuming $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, $T = 353$ K (80°C):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{Nernst} &= 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} \ln(1) \\
&= 1.229 + 0.0152 \times 0 \\
&= 1.229 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign conversion logic: $-\ln(1/Q) = +\ln(Q)$, which is why the expression flips from the standard form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tafel Equation for Activation Losses

**Step 1: Start with the Butler-Volmer Equation**
For a single-electron transfer reaction ($n = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
j &= j_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{(1-\alpha) F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the High-Overpotential Approximation**
For large $\eta_{act}$ (negative overpotential at the cathode), the second exponential becomes negligible:

$$j \approx j_0 \exp\left( \frac{\alpha F \eta_{act}}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Overpotential**
Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right) &= \frac{\alpha F \eta_{act}}{RT} \\
\eta_{act} &= \frac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Convert to the Common-Log (Base-10) Form**
Since $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\eta_{act} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha F} \log_{10}\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right) = A \log_{10}\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right)$$

where $A = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha F}$ is the **Tafel slope** (V/decade).

**Step 5: Numerical Example**
For a PEMFC at 80°C with $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485} \\
&= \frac{6761.7}{48242.5} \\
&\approx 0.0702 \text{ V/decade} \\
&\approx 70 \text{ mV/decade}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Power Density Point

**Step 1: Define the Cell Voltage in the Ohmic-Dominated Region**
For simplicity, assume only ohmic losses dominate in the linear region:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - j \cdot R_{ohmic}$$

**Step 2: Write the Power Density Equation**

$$P = V_{cell} \times j = (E_{Nernst} - j \cdot R_{ohmic}) \times j$$

**Step 3: Differentiate P with Respect to j and Set to Zero**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dj} &= E_{Nernst} - 2j \cdot R_{ohmic} = 0 \\
j_{max} &= \frac{E_{Nernst}}{2 R_{ohmic}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Voltage at Maximum Power**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{max} &= E_{Nernst} - j_{max} \cdot R_{ohmic} \\
&= E_{Nernst} - \frac{E_{Nernst}}{2 R_{ohmic}} \cdot R_{ohmic} \\
&= \frac{E_{Nernst}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Maximum Power Density**

$$P_{max} = V_{max} \times j_{max} = \frac{E_{Nernst}}{2} \times \frac{E_{Nernst}}{2 R_{ohmic}} = \frac{E_{Nernst}^2}{4 R_{ohmic}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** At maximum power transfer, **half the open-circuit voltage is lost** to internal resistance. This is a direct application of the **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** from basic circuit theory.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Degradation Rate Calculation

**Step 1: Define the Degradation Rate ($k$)**
The degradation rate is defined as the voltage loss per unit time:

$$k = \frac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{t_{operating}}$$

**Step 2: Express in Standard Units (μV/hr)**

$$k = \frac{(V_{initial} - V_{final}) \times 10^6}{t_{operating}} \quad [\mu V/hr]$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example**
- Initial voltage at 0.5 A/cm²: $V_{initial} = 0.700$ V
- Final voltage after 1,000 hours: $V_{final} = 0.680$ V
- Operating time: $t = 1000$ hr

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{(0.700 - 0.680) \times 10^6}{1000} \\
&= \frac{0.020 \times 10^6}{1000} \\
&= 20 \text{ μV/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Predict End-of-Life Time**
If the end-of-life threshold is $V_{EOL} = 0.600$ V:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{EOL} &= \frac{V_{initial} - V_{EOL}}{k \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{0.700 - 0.600}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{0.100}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 5000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) — Randles Circuit Model

**Step 1: Identify the Equivalent Circuit Elements**
A simplified Randles circuit for a single electrode has:
- $R_s$ = Solution/electrolyte resistance (ohmic)
- $R_{ct}$ = Charge transfer resistance (kinetic)
- $C_{dl}$ = Double-layer capacitance (interface)

**Step 2: Write the Total Impedance**

$$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} - j \frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Real and Imaginary Parts**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{Re} &= R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2} \\
Z_{Im} &= - \frac{\omega R_{ct}^2 C_{dl}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Nyquist Plot Behavior**
- At very high frequencies ($\omega \to \infty$): $Z \to R_s$ (intercept with real axis)
- At very low frequencies ($\omega \to 0$): $Z \to R_s + R_{ct}$ (second intercept)
- The **semicircle diameter** $= R_{ct}$, which decreases as the reaction becomes more facile.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Characterization Techniques

| **Technique** | **What it Measures** | **Output** | **Information Gained** | **Typical Duration** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polarization Curve (V-i)** | Steady-state voltage vs. current | V vs. i curve | Activation, ohmic, and concentration losses | 1–4 hours |
| **Power Density Curve** | Power output vs. current | P vs. i curve | Maximum power point, optimal operating point | 1–4 hours |
| **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** | AC impedance vs. frequency | Nyquist / Bode plot | Charge transfer resistance, double-layer capacitance, mass transport | 30 min – 2 hours |
| **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)** | Current response to cyclic potential sweep | I vs. V plot | Electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), catalyst activity | 10 – 30 min |
| **Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV)** | Current response to linear potential ramp | I vs. V plot | Fuel crossover, internal currents, OCV losses | 5 – 15 min |
| **Accelerated Stress Test (AST)** | Long-term voltage decay under stress | V vs. time curve | Durability, lifetime prediction | 100 – 2000 hours |
| **Galvanostatic / Potentiostatic Hold** | Voltage / current at fixed load | V-t or i-t curve | Steady-state performance, degradation kinetics | Hours to days |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "polarization curve" in the context of a fuel cell. List the three main regions of loss on a typical polarization curve.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> A **polarization curve** (or **V-i curve**) is a plot of **cell voltage (V)** as a function of **current density (i, A/cm²)** drawn under steady-state operating conditions. It is the primary diagnostic tool used in fuel cell characterization to assess cell performance.
>
> **Three Loss Regions (1 mark):**
> 1. **Activation loss region** — dominant at low current density (caused by sluggish ORR kinetics)
> 2. **Ohmic loss region** — dominant in the linear, mid-range region (caused by membrane and contact resistance)
> 3. **Concentration loss region** — dominant at high current density (caused by mass transport limitations)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by the "durability" of a fuel cell? State the typical DOE durability target for PEM fuel cells used in light-duty vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> **Durability** is the ability of a fuel cell to maintain its performance (cell voltage, power output) over a prolonged period of operation under specified operating conditions. It is typically quantified as the **rate of voltage decay in μV/hr** or as the **total hours of operation before end-of-life**.
>
> **DOE Target (1 mark):**
> For **light-duty passenger vehicles**, the DOE 2025 target is **5,000 hours** of operation, corresponding to approximately 150,000 miles of driving. For **stationary applications**, the target is **30,000 to 40,000 hours**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the construction and working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) with a neat labeled diagram. **Discuss the electrochemical reactions at the anode and cathode.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a typical polarization curve, explain the **three major loss mechanisms** in a fuel cell. Derive the **Tafel equation** for activation overpotential and calculate the Tafel slope at 80°C for a PEMFC with charge transfer coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction of PEMFC (3 marks):**
- A PEMFC consists of a **proton-conducting polymer membrane** (typically **Nafion**) sandwiched between two **catalyst layers** (anode and cathode) made of platinum nanoparticles supported on carbon.
- The **membrane electrode assembly (MEA)** is placed between two **bipolar plates** with **flow channels** for gas distribution and current collection.
- **Gaskets** prevent gas leakage, and **end plates** provide mechanical compression.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Hydrogen is fed to the **anode** side; oxygen (or air) is fed to the **cathode** side.
- At the anode, H₂ is split into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻) by the platinum catalyst.
- Protons migrate through the **proton exchange membrane** to the cathode; electrons flow through the external circuit, producing **electrical current**.
- At the cathode, protons, electrons, and oxygen combine to form **water and heat**.

**Electrochemical Reactions (2 marks):**

**Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction - HOR):**
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

**Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction - ORR):**
$$\frac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O \quad E^\circ = 1.23 \text{ V}$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad E^\circ_{cell} = 1.23 \text{ V}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Three Loss Mechanisms (3 marks):**

As detailed in Section 2.2:
1. **Activation losses** — slow reaction kinetics, dominant at low current density
2. **Ohmic losses** — resistance of membrane and electrodes, linear with current
3. **Concentration losses** — mass transport limitation of reactants, dominant at high current density

**Derivation of Tafel Equation (2 marks):**

Starting from the Butler-Volmer equation and applying the high-overpotential approximation (as derived in Section 3.2):

$$\eta_{act} = A \log_{10}\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right) = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha F} \log_{10}\left( \frac{j}{j_0} \right)$$

**Calculation of Tafel Slope (2 marks):**

Given: $T = 80^\circ C = 353$ K, $\alpha = 0.5$, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485} \\
&= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{48242.5} \\
&= \frac{6761.7}{48242.5} \\
&\approx 0.0702 \text{ V/decade} \\
&\approx 70.2 \text{ mV/decade}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Define **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)**. Explain the **Randles equivalent circuit** and its use in fuel cell diagnosis. Describe how **charge transfer resistance ($R_{ct}$)** is obtained from a **Nyquist plot**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **durability** of a fuel cell. List and briefly explain **four major degradation mechanisms** in a PEM fuel cell. A fuel cell operates at 0.5 A/cm² with an initial voltage of 0.720 V. After 1,500 hours, the voltage drops to 0.690 V. Calculate: (i) the degradation rate in μV/hr, and (ii) the predicted end-of-life time if the end-of-life threshold is 0.600 V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of EIS (1 mark):**
> **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** is a non-destructive diagnostic technique that measures the **complex impedance** ($Z = Z_{Re} - j Z_{Im}$) of an electrochemical system over a wide range of **AC frequencies** (typically 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz). A small sinusoidal AC perturbation is applied, and the resulting current response is analyzed to extract kinetic and transport parameters.

**Randles Equivalent Circuit (3 marks):**

The Randles circuit is the simplest model for a single electrode-electrolyte interface. It consists of:
- $R_s$ — **Solution resistance** (purely ohmic, frequency-independent)
- $R_{ct}$ — **Charge transfer resistance** (in parallel with $C_{dl}$)
- $C_{dl}$ — **Double-layer capacitance** (representing charge accumulation at the interface)

**Use in Fuel Cell Diagnosis (2 marks):**
- EIS separates the **ohmic**, **kinetic**, and **mass transport** contributions to the total cell impedance.
- It is used to monitor **catalyst degradation** (via increasing $R_{ct}$), **membrane drying** (via changing $C_{dl}$), and **electrode flooding** (via low-frequency loops).
- It is non-destructive and can be performed **in situ** during operation.

**Extraction of $R_{ct}$ from Nyquist Plot (1 mark):**
- The Nyquist plot displays $-Z_{Im}$ vs. $Z_{Re}$.
- The high-frequency intercept on the real axis gives $R_s$.
- The **diameter of the semicircle** equals $R_{ct}$.
- Low-frequency features (second arc) indicate mass transport resistance.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition of Durability (1 mark):**
**Durability** is the ability of a fuel cell to maintain its rated performance over an extended operational period under specified environmental and load conditions.

**Four Major Degradation Mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Platinum Catalyst Dissolution and Agglomeration:**
   - Pt nanoparticles dissolve at high electrode potentials (>0.9 V), migrate, and re-deposit as larger particles.
   - This reduces the **Electrochemically Active Surface Area (ECSA)** and increases activation losses.

2. **Carbon Support Corrosion:**
   - The carbon catalyst support oxidizes to CO₂ at potentials above ~1.0 V, especially during start-up/shut-down transients.
   - This causes **loss of catalyst support**, catalyst detachment, and increased mass transport resistance.

3. **Membrane Degradation:**
   - Chemical attack by **radical species (•OH, •OOH)** formed from gas crossover, especially under low humidity and high temperature.
   - Leads to membrane thinning, pinhole formation, and **gas crossover**, reducing OCV and causing cell failure.

4. **Bipolar Plate Corrosion:**
   - Metallic plates (e.g., stainless steel) form passive oxide layers in the acidic, humid fuel cell environment.
   - This increases **contact resistance** and can release metal ions that poison the catalyst.

**Numerical Calculations (2 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{initial} = 0.720$ V
- $V_{final} = 0.690$ V
- $t = 1500$ hours
- $V_{EOL} = 0.600$ V

**Step 1: Calculate the Degradation Rate (i)**

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{t} \\
&= \frac{0.720 - 0.690}{1500} \\
&= \frac{0.030}{1500} \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V/hr} \\
&= 20 \text{ μV/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate End-of-Life Time (ii)**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{EOL} &= \frac{V_{initial} - V_{EOL}}{k} \\
&= \frac{0.720 - 0.600}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{0.120}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 6000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tafel slope and Tafel equation:** The Tafel **equation** is the full expression; the Tafel **slope** is the constant multiplier $A = \frac{2.303 RT}{\alpha F}$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in Nernst equation:** Many students write $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ instead of $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$. The sign of the $\ln Q$ term is **negative** by convention.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch in degradation rate:** Express the answer in **μV/hr**, **not** in V/hr or V/1000 hr. Examiners expect the standard **μV/hr** unit.
>
> 4. **Confusing $j_0$ and $j_L$:** The **exchange current density** ($j_0$) characterizes the intrinsic reaction rate (small = sluggish kinetics). The **limiting current density** ($j_L$) is the maximum sustainable current (determined by mass transport). Both appear in Tafel-type equations but mean very different things.
>
> 5. **Forgetting $n = 2$ in PEMFC:** For the H₂/O₂ reaction, $n = 2$ electrons per molecule. Using $n = 1$ or $n = 4$ is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Drawing polarization curve without labeling axes and regions:** Always label the x-axis ("Current Density, A/cm²"), y-axis ("Cell Voltage, V"), and clearly mark the three loss regions. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting temperature in Tafel slope calculation:** The Tafel slope depends on temperature. Always show the temperature in Kelvin, not Celsius.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characterization and Durability of Fuel Cells**

- **Polarization Curve (V-i curve):** The most important diagnostic plot; three loss regions: activation (low j), ohmic (mid j), concentration (high j).
- **Tafel Equation:** $\eta_{act} = A \log_{10}(j/j_0)$; Tafel slope $A = \frac{2.303 RT}{\alpha F} \approx 70$ mV/decade at 80°C for PEMFC.
- **Ohmic Loss:** $\eta_{ohmic} = j \times R_{ohmic}$; slope of the linear region is the Area Specific Resistance (ASR).
- **Concentration Loss:** $\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln(1 - j/j_L)$; dominant near the limiting current.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$; for H₂/O₂, $E^\circ = 1.229$ V at 25°C.
- **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** At $P_{max}$, $V_{cell} = E/2$ and $j = E/(2R_{ohmic})$.
- **EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy):** AC technique, Randles circuit, $R_{ct}$ = diameter of Nyquist semicircle, $R_s$ = high-frequency intercept.
- **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV):** Measures ECSA; integrates the H-adsorption/desorption peak area.
- **Degradation Rate:** $k = \frac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{t}$ in **μV/hr**; DOE target ≤ 10 μV/hr for automotive PEMFC.
- **End-of-Life Criterion:** Typically 10% voltage loss or voltage below 80% of rated value.
- **Four Major Degradation Mechanisms:** Pt dissolution/agglomeration, carbon support corrosion, membrane chemical/mechanical degradation, bipolar plate corrosion.
- **Accelerated Stress Test (AST):** DOE and FCCJ protocols predict 5,000+ hour lifetimes in 200–2,000 hours of lab testing.
- **DOE 2025 Durability Targets:** 5,000 hr (automotive), 30,000 hr (stationary).
- **Key Constants:** $F = 96,485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $E^\circ_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229$ V.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Conventional Energy System Applications** refer to the practical engineering deployment, integration, and utilization of renewable energy technologies (solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, ocean, and hydrogen-based systems) across residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and transportation sectors. These applications convert naturally replenished energy fluxes into usable forms — **electrical, thermal, mechanical, or chemical** — while maintaining ecological balance and long-term sustainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> Applications of non-conventional energy systems encompass the design, sizing, and operational integration of solar thermal (water heating, space heating, drying, distillation), solar photovoltaic (standalone, grid-tied, hybrid), wind (water pumping, standalone/grid-connected generation), biomass (biogas plants, gasifiers, biodiesel), geothermal (direct use, heat pumps, power plants), ocean (tidal barrage, OTEC, wave energy), and hydrogen/fuel cell systems.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart village** that is completely self-sufficient. The sun heats the water for the morning bath (solar thermal). Solar panels on the roof power the lights and fans (solar PV). A windmill pumps water from the well for irrigation (wind energy). Cow dung from the farm is fed into a digester that produces cooking gas (biogas). The hot rocks deep beneath the earth warm the greenhouse in winter (geothermal). The ocean tides spin a turbine to light up the streetlamps at night (tidal). All these systems work together like instruments in an **orchestra**, each one playing its part to provide clean energy without burning fossil fuels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Applications Matter in KTU Examinations:**
> This topic is a high-weightage synthesis module where students must recall, classify, and explain the end-use deployment of every energy source studied in Modules 1–3. Approximately **15–20% of ESE marks** test the application mapping of each renewable system.

### Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Solar Constant (GSC):** $\mathbf{1367 \, W/m^2}$ (Total solar irradiance perpendicular to sun's rays at the outer atmosphere).
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH):** Equivalent number of hours per day with **1000 W/m²** irradiance.
- **Wind Power Density:** **1 kW/m²** is the commercial viability threshold.
- **Calorific Value of Biogas:** **18–25 MJ/m³** (≈ 55% methane content).
- **Geothermal Gradient:** **25–30 °C/km** of depth (normal gradient).
- **Tidal Range (Commercial):** Minimum **5 m** between high and low tide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Applications

The applications of non-conventional energy systems can be logically classified into **four energy conversion domains**:

- **Thermal Applications:** Solar water heaters, solar cookers, solar dryers, solar stills, solar ponds, geothermal heat pumps, biomass combustion.
- **Electrical Applications:** Solar PV systems, wind turbines, geothermal power plants, OTEC, tidal barrages, fuel cells.
- **Mechanical Applications:** Windmills for water pumping, biomass gasifier engines.
- **Chemical Applications:** Hydrogen production via electrolysis, biodiesel synthesis, biogas (methane) generation.

### 2.2 Application Logic Tree

- **Solar Energy Applications**
  - *Solar Thermal* → Flat Plate Collector (FPC), Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC), Solar Concentrator (Parabolic Dish, Scheffler), Solar Still, Solar Dryer, Solar Cooker, Solar Pond.
  - *Solar Photovoltaic* → Standalone PV, Grid-Tied PV, Hybrid PV-Wind, Solar Lantern, Solar Water Pumping, Solar Home System (SHS).
  - *Solar Passive Architecture* → Trombe Wall, Greenhouse, Sunspace.
- **Wind Energy Applications**
  - *Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)* → Grid-connected power generation (Megawatt scale).
  - *Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)* → Darrieus, Savonius (off-grid / urban).
  - *Windmills* → Mechanical water pumping (multiblade traditional design).
- **Biomass Energy Applications**
  - *Biochemical Conversion* → Biogas plant (digestion), ethanol fermentation.
  - *Thermochemical Conversion* → Combustion, Pyrolysis, Gasification.
  - *Transesterification* → Biodiesel production.
  - *Direct Combustion* → Rice husk boilers, bagasse cogeneration.
- **Geothermal Energy Applications**
  - *Direct Use* → Space heating, greenhouse heating, aquaculture.
  - *Ground Source Heat Pump (GSHP)* → Residential/commercial heating & cooling.
  - *Power Generation* → Dry steam, flash steam, binary cycle plants.
- **Ocean Energy Applications**
  - *Tidal Energy* → Tidal barrage, tidal stream (kinetic).
  - *Wave Energy* → Oscillating Water Column (OWC), point absorbers.
  - *Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)* → Closed cycle (Rankine), Open cycle (Claude), Hybrid.
- **Hydrogen & Fuel Cell Applications**
  - *Production* → Electrolysis (using solar/wind), biomass gasification.
  - *Storage* → Compressed gas, liquid H₂, metal hydrides.
  - *End Use* → PEMFC vehicles, stationary power backup, portable electronics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Application / System | Governing Formula | Key Variables | Units | Practical Utility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Solar Water Heater (FPC) | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, [S - U_L (T_i - T_a)]$ | $A_c$: collector area, $F_R$: heat removal factor, $U_L$: loss coefficient | W, m², °C | Domestic & industrial hot water sizing |
| 2 | Solar PV Array Sizing | $E_{load} = \dfrac{P_{peak} \times PSH \times \eta_{system}}{1}$ | $P_{peak}$ in W, PSH in hrs | Wh/day | Off-grid system design |
| 3 | Wind Power Extracted | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^3 \, C_p$ | $\rho$: air density (1.225 kg/m³), $A$: swept area, $C_p \le 0.593$ (Betz) | W | Turbine selection & capacity estimation |
| 4 | Biogas Yield (Boyle's Model) | $G = B_o \times SV_o \times MCF$ | $B_o$: biogas yield constant (0.24 m³/kg VS), $SV_o$: volatile solids, $MCF$: methane correction factor | m³/day | Digester volume sizing |
| 5 | Geothermal Heat Pump (COP) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_{hot}}{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}$ | $T$ in Kelvin | dimensionless | Heating efficiency evaluation |
| 6 | Tidal Power (Barrage) | $P_{tidal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, A \, g \, R^2$ | $\rho_w$: water density (1025 kg/m³), $R$: tidal range | W | Barrage plant capacity |
| 7 | Wave Energy (Sinnko) | $P_{wave} = \dfrac{\rho_w \, g^2}{64 \, \pi} \, H_s^2 \, T_e$ | $H_s$: significant wave height, $T_e$: wave period | W/m | Wave device rating |
| 8 | OTEC Efficiency | $\eta_{OTEC} = \dfrac{T_{warm} - T_{cold}}{T_{warm}}$ | Surface temp ≈ 25 °C, deep ≈ 5 °C | dimensionless | OTEC feasibility check |
| 9 | Hydrogen Heating Value | $HHV_{H_2} = 142 \, MJ/kg$ | — | MJ/kg | Fuel cell input calculation |
| 10 | Biodiesel Stoichiometry | $Triglyceride + 3\,ROH \rightarrow Glycerol + 3\,R'COOR$ | Mole ratio = 3:1 | — | Transesterification yield |
| 11 | Solar Pond (Stability) | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial z} < 0$ (Non-convective gradient) | Salinity gradient zones | °C/m | Salt gradient pond operation |
| 12 | PV Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | $FF$: fill factor (0.7–0.85) | % | Module performance rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Memorize the **Betz Limit (0.593)** and the **Solar Constant (1367 W/m²)** — these are guaranteed 1–2 mark questions in any module on renewable applications.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distributed Rural Electrification:** Solar Home Systems (SHS) and micro-wind turbines bring power to off-grid villages (e.g., India's Saubhagya scheme).
- **Industrial Process Heat:** Scheffler dishes and parabolic troughs supply steam at **150–300 °C** for textile and dairy industries.
- **Cogeneration:** Bagasse-based combined heat and power (CHP) plants supply electricity and steam to sugar mills.
- **Hydrogen Mobility:** Green hydrogen from solar-PV electrolysis fueling **PEMFC buses** and trucks (zero-emission transport).
- **District Cooling/Heating:** GSHP networks for entire townships (e.g., Stockholm's GeoCity).
- **Coastal Power:** France's La Rance tidal barrage (**240 MW**) demonstrates commercial-scale ocean energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wind Power Application Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A small wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **4 m** operating at a wind speed of **10 m/s**. If the Betz coefficient is achieved and air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, compute the maximum power output. If the turbine is used for water pumping, estimate the daily energy available and the volume of water that can be lifted to a height of **20 m** (assume pump-motor efficiency = 50%).

**Step 1 — Swept Area of the Rotor**

The swept area is the circular area traced by the blade tips.

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \, D^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \times (4)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 16}{4} = 4\pi
$$

$$
A = 4 \times 3.1416 = 12.5664 \, m^2
$$

*Logic:* The rotor disc area determines how much kinetic energy the wind can transfer to the blades per unit time.

**Step 2 — Maximum Power Extraction (Betz Equation)**

$$
P_{max} = C_{p,Betz} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^3
$$

Substituting the Betz optimum $C_p = 0.593$:

$$
P_{max} = 0.593 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 12.5664 \times (10)^3
$$

$$
P_{max} = 0.593 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 12.5664 \times 1000
$$

$$
P_{max} = 0.593 \times 7696.92 = 4564.27 \, W \approx 4.56 \, kW
$$

*Logic:* Only 59.3% of the wind's kinetic energy can be extracted; the rest must remain in the flow to keep air moving downstream.

**Step 3 — Daily Energy Available**

Assuming the turbine operates effectively for **8 hours** at the rated wind speed (typical daily average):

$$
E_{daily} = P_{max} \times t = 4564.27 \times 8 = 36514.16 \, Wh
$$

$$
E_{daily} \approx 36.51 \, kWh
$$

*Logic:* Energy = Power × Time; this is the total mechanical energy available at the rotor shaft per day.

**Step 4 — Useful Energy at Pump Shaft**

Applying the pump-motor efficiency $\eta = 0.50$:

$$
E_{useful} = \eta \times E_{daily} = 0.50 \times 36.51 = 18.255 \, kWh
$$

*Logic:* The pump-motor system has friction and electrical losses; only half the rotor energy reaches the water.

**Step 5 — Volume of Water Lifted**

The potential energy needed to lift a mass $m$ of water to height $h$ is $PE = m \, g \, h$. Since $m = \rho_w \times V$:

$$
E_{useful} = \rho_w \, V \, g \, h
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{\rho_w \, g \, h}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{18.255 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 20}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{18255}{196200} = 0.0930 \, m^3
$$

$$
V \approx 93.0 \, \text{litres per day}
$$

*Logic:* A small wind-electric water pump can lift about 93 litres daily to a 20 m head — sufficient for small-scale irrigation or livestock watering.

### 3.2 Derivation: Solar Photovoltaic Array Sizing for a Home

**Problem Statement:**
A household has a daily electrical load of **5 kWh**. The location receives an average of **5 Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** per day. The system efficiency is **75%** (accounting for inverter, charge controller, battery, and wiring losses). Determine the required PV array size in kWp.

**Step 1 — Required Energy Output from PV Modules**

$$
E_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\eta_{system}}
$$

$$
E_{PV} = \dfrac{5}{0.75} = 6.667 \, kWh/day
$$

*Logic:* The PV modules must over-produce to compensate for system losses downstream.

**Step 2 — Required PV Array Power**

$$
P_{peak} = \dfrac{E_{PV}}{PSH}
$$

$$
P_{peak} = \dfrac{6.667}{5} = 1.333 \, kWp
$$

*Logic:* Under standard test conditions (STC), 1 kWp produces 1 kWh per peak sun hour. To produce 6.667 kWh with 5 PSH, we need approximately 1.33 kWp of installed capacity.

**Step 3 — Number of Modules**

Assuming each standard module is **330 Wp**:

$$
N = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{P_{module}} = \dfrac{1333}{330} = 4.04
$$

Therefore, **5 modules** (rounded up) are required.

*Logic:* Always round up to the next whole module; fractional modules cannot be installed in practice.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application-to-Technology Cross-Reference

| End-Use Sector | Solar PV | Solar Thermal | Wind | Biomass | Geothermal | Ocean | Hydrogen/FC |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Domestic Lighting** | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | — | — | ✔ |
| **Water Heating** | — | ✔ | — | — | ✔ | — | — |
| **Cooking** | — | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | — | — |
| **Space Heating/Cooling** | — | ✔ | — | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| **Water Pumping** | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | — | — | — |
| **Grid Power Generation** | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Transportation** | — | — | — | ✔ (biofuel) | — | — | ✔ (FCV) |
| **Industrial Process Heat** | — | ✔ | — | ✔ | ✔ | — | — |
| **Drying (Agriculture)** | — | ✔ | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Desalination** | — | ✔ | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Standalone Telecom** | ✔ | — | ✔ | — | — | — | ✔ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
